positions.title: Omniscience precludes action
positions.description: An all-knowing being cannot act because action presupposes ignorance. If one knows with certainty what will happen, one cannot coherently attempt to influence what will happen and therefore cannot intend to act.
positions.text_evidence: MASTER: NO. ACTION PRESUPPOSES IGNORANCE. IF YOU KNOW WITH CERTAINTY, WHAT IS GOING TO HAPPEN, YOU CANNOT COHERENTLY ATTEMPT TO INFLUENCE WHAT IS GOING TO HAPPEN AND YOU CAN THEREFORE CANNOT INTENT TO ACT.

positions.title: Omnipotence precludes feeling
positions.description: An all-powerful being cannot experience feelings. Only a being capable of being changed can experience feelings, and if a being is incapable of being changed, it cannot feel.
positions.text_evidence: MASTER: NO. ONLY A BEING THAT IS CAPABLE OF BEING CHANGED CAN EXPERIENCE FEELINGS. AND IF A BEING IS CAPABLE OF BEING CHANGED, THEN IT IS NOT CAPABLE OF FEELING.

positions.title: Immutability precludes self-change
positions.description: A being incapable of being changed cannot change itself. To change oneself is to be changed, albeit by oneself. A being that cannot be changed cannot be changed by anything, including itself.
positions.text_evidence: MASTER: NO, IT IS NOT. TO BE CHANGE ONESELF IS TO BE CHANGED, ALBEIT BY ONSELF. AND, OBVIOUSLY, A BEING THAT CANNOT BE CHANGED CANNOT BE CHANGED BY ANYTHING, INCLUDING ITSELF.

positions.title: Action requires self-change
positions.description: To act is to change oneself. A being that cannot change itself cannot act.
positions.text_evidence: MASTER: NO, IT CANNOT, SINCE TO ACT IS TO CHANGE ONESELF.

positions.title: Omnipotence entails impotence
positions.description: If a being is all-powerful (omnipotent), it is completely impotent. The concept of an all-powerful being is incoherent because such a being could neither act nor change.
positions.text_evidence: STUDENT: SO IF A BEING IS ALL-POWERFUL, IT IS COMPLETELY IMPOTENT. MASTER: CORRECT.

positions.title: Traditional divine attributes are incoherent
positions.description: The concepts of an all-knowing being and an all-powerful being are both incoherent. To the extent we conceptualize God as all-knowing and all-powerful, we are conceptualizing Him incoherently.
positions.text_evidence: MASTER: CORRECT. STUDENT: AND FOR SIMILAR REASONS, WE ARE ALSO TO CONCLUDE THAT THE CONCEPT OF AN ALL-KNOWING BEING IS INCOHERENT.

positions.title: The universe is counter-entropic through life
positions.description: While the physical universe is entropic (disintegrating according to thermodynamics), biological reality is counter-entropic - organisms grow and assume increasingly sophisticated energy-efficient forms. This counter-entropic tendency is even stronger at the psychological level.
positions.text_evidence: MASTER: TO THE EXTENT THAT THE WORLD FALLS WITHIN THE SCOPE OF THE DISCIPLINE OF PHSYICS, IT IS ENTROPIC... BUT NOTICE THAT, TO THE EXTENT THE WORLD FALLS WITHIN THE SCOPE OF BIOLOGY, IT IS COUNTER-ENTROPIC.

positions.title: Consciousness maximizes energy efficiency
positions.description: Mental/psychological dimensions make organisms more energy-efficient than they would otherwise be. Psychological growth is a particularly extreme way of increasing an organism's level of energy-efficiency.
positions.text_evidence: STUDENT: RIGHT. TO THE EXTENT THAT AN ORGANISM HAS A MENTAL DIMENSION, IT IS MORE ENERGY-EFFICIENT THAN IT WOULD OTHERWISE, PSYCHOLOGICAL GROWTH BEING A PARTICULARLY EXTREME WAY OF INCREASING AN ORGANISM'S LEVEL OF ENERGY-EFFICIENCY.

positions.title: Consciousness is inherent in existence itself
positions.description: It is inherent in existence that existence be conscious. The universe is disintegrating (lacking consciousness) only to the extent it is ceasing to be what it is. To the extent the universe is growing and becoming what it truly is, it has consciousness. Therefore, there is ipso facto a God - the universe itself, which is pulsating with life and thought rather than being cold and mechanical.
positions.text_evidence: MASTER: SO YOU SEE, MY SON, IT IS ONLY TO THE EXTENT THAT THE UNIVERSE IS DISINTEGRATING---THAT IT IS CEASING TO BE WHAT IT IS—THAT IT LACKS CONSCIOUSNESS; AND CONTRARIWISE, TO THE EXTENT THAT THE UNIVERSE IS GROWING—THAT IT IS BECOMING WHAT IT TRULY IS---IT HAS CONSCIOUSNESS.

positions.title: Logic as the study of formal truth
positions.description: Logic is the study of formal truth. A formal truth is a statement that holds entirely in virtue of its own structural properties, such as 'it is either raining or it isn't' or 'there are continuous functions that cannot be differentiated at any point.'
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: It is the study of formal truth... A formal truth is a statement that holds entirely in virtue of its own structural properties.

positions.title: Mathematical logic studies statement-functions
positions.description: Mathematical logic is logic done with mathematical concepts and techniques. The central concept of mathematics is function (a rule assigning objects to objects). Mathematical logic studies statement-functions - functions that assign statements to statements - while other branches study numerical functions.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: Mathematical logic studies statement-functions. Most other branches of mathematics study numerical functions.

positions.title: Negation preserves incompatibility
positions.description: The negation of a statement is incompatible with that statement - they cannot simultaneously be true. To properly negate a statement, put 'it is not the case that' in front of it. The negation of 'someone is tall' is 'nobody is tall' (not 'someone is not tall'), since the latter can be true simultaneously with the former.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: The negation of a statement is incompatible with that statement... The negation of someone is tall is it is not the case that someone is tall; in other words, nobody is tall.

positions.title: Truth-functional operators defined by truth tables
positions.description: Conjunction, disjunction, and negation are truth-functional: whether the output sentence is true depends only on whether input sentences are true. 'P and Q' is true iff both P and Q are true. 'P or Q' is false iff both P and Q are false. 'Not-P' is true iff P is false.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: 'and', 'or', and 'not' are very special, since they are truth-functional, meaning that whether or not the output sentence is true depends only on whether or not input sentences are true.

positions.title: Non-truth-functional operators exist
positions.description: 'Because' and epistemic operators like 'Jim knows that' are non-truth-functional. Even if P and Q are both true, 'P because Q' isn't necessarily true. Similarly, given only that P is true, it doesn't follow that 'Jim knows that P' is true.
positions.text_evidence: MAX: Because even if P and Q are both true, 'P because Q' isn't necessarily true... Given only that P is true, it does not follow that 'Jim knows that Mary is in Alaska' is true.

positions.title: Recursive functions define logical systems
positions.description: Each logical function (negation, conjunction, disjunction) is defined for its own outputs - you can negate negations, conjoin conjunctions, etc., indefinitely. This means each function is recursive. A system of logic is a recursively defined statement class, and logic is the study of such classes. This is what it means to say logic is the study of formal truth.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: Notice that each of these functions if defined for its own outputs... this means that each of these functions is recursive... a system of logic is a recursively defined statement class. And logic is the study of such classes.

positions.title: All functions reduce to statement-functions
positions.description: While there are many types of functions (numerical, class-to-class, property-to-property), all functions are ultimately statement-functions, even though some appear not to be. This is a deep claim about the foundations of logic and mathematics.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: That all functions are statement-functions, even though some appear not to be. MAX: I don't understand. Dr. K: You will by the end of this podcast-series.

positions.title: Determinism defined as universal causation
positions.description: Determinism is the doctrine that nothing is uncaused. If determinism is correct, how the world is now determines exactly how it will be in the future, and how it was in the past determines exactly how it is now.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: Determinism is the doctrine that nothing is uncaused. MAX: So if determinism is correct, then how the world is now determines exactly how it will be in the future.

positions.title: Indeterminism entails some chaos
positions.description: If indeterminism is true, the universe is chaotic to a non-null degree. A single uncaused event makes indeterminism correct, regardless of how minute that event is or how orderly the rest of the universe.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: If there is a single uncaused event, then indeterminism is correct, even if the universe is otherwise completely deterministic. And it doesn't matter how minute that event is.

positions.title: Indeterminism threatens freedom more than determinism
positions.description: Indeterminism is more of a threat to freedom than determinism. Determinism is not a threat to freedom but is actually necessary for it. Indeterminism is as fatal to freedom as determinism is necessary for it.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: Indeterminism is more of a threat to freedom than determinism. In fact, determinism is not a threat to freedom. It is in fact necessary for it. Determinism is as necessary for freedom as indeterminism is fatal to freedom.

positions.title: External indeterminism doesn't increase freedom
positions.description: If the external world (outside our bodies) were indeterministic while our minds/bodies remained deterministic, we would not be more free. In a highly indeterministic world where baseballs might turn into hawks, we would have less control and thus less freedom.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: would we be more free in W than we are here? MAX: No; if anything, less free. At least in our world, there are dependable mechanisms. So we can control it. But in W, we can't even do that.

positions.title: Internal indeterminism doesn't increase freedom
positions.description: If our nervous systems were indeterministic, we wouldn't be more free. An indeterministic nervous system means current state fails to determine later states, so decisions don't reliably lead to actions. Knowledge at one time wouldn't reliably influence decisions later.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: So to the extent that your nervous system is indeterministic, its condition at time t leaves it open whether I will make the requisite bodily movements... Your berry-knowledge won't necessarily lead to the right decision unless the mechanisms that convert knowledge into intentions are intact.

positions.title: Mechanisms require determinism
positions.description: Where there are mechanisms, there is determinism. Where there is indeterminism, there are no mechanisms. A mechanism is something that can be relied on to produce an output of a certain kind when given an input of a certain kind. Mechanisms exist only to the extent the universe is deterministic.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: A mechanism is something that can be relied on to produce on output of a certain kind, when given an input of a certain kind... Where there are mechanisms, there is determinism. Where there is indeterminism, there are no mechanisms.

positions.title: Indeterministic minds are incoherent
positions.description: The more indeterministic a mind is, the more incoherent it is - its future states fail to cohere with present states, present with past. An indeterministic mind is no mind at all, but rather a series of mutually isolated minds, each existing only for an instant and therefore not existing at all.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: The more its future condition fails to cohere with its present condition... it is therefore no mind at all, but rather a series of mutually isolated minds... Since its earlier states fail to cohere with its later states... So it exists only for an instant. And therefore doesn't exist at all.

positions.title: Indeterminism prevents composite objects
positions.description: In an indeterministic universe, composite objects like billiard balls couldn't exist. The trillions of micro-particles would not be kept in place by deterministic laws and would go their own ways, causing the object to disintegrate or never form in the first place. Even atoms require deterministic laws among their parts to exist.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: To the naked eye, a billiard ball looks like a single indivisible object. But it consists of trillions of micro-particles interacting with each one another... if those interactions weren't governed by deterministic laws... each particle would go its own way... and the billiard ball would powder into nothingness.

positions.title: Freedom requires appropriate mental state responsiveness
positions.description: Inner freedom consists in one's current mental state embodying appropriate responsiveness to previous mental states, and future mental states embodying the right sort of responsiveness to present mental states. Freedom is about having a mind whose mental states determine one another in the right way, not about having undetermined mental states.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: So this inner freedom you are discussing---one has it to the extent that one's current mental state embodies the appropriate sort of responsiveness to one's previous mental states... possession of this inner freedom is not about having a mind whose mental states don't determine one another; it is about having a mind whose mental states determine one another in the right way.

positions.title: Freedom as causal integration
positions.description: A person is free if their mind is constitutive of the right sort of causal integration. Freedom requires both determinism (causal integrity) and the correct kind of causal structure, but determinism alone is not sufficient for freedom.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: Yes, a person is free if his mind is constitutive of the right sort of causal integration. MAX: And my view is that a person is not free unless his mind is constitutive of the right sort of causal integration, but I don't grant that a person is free if that condition is satisfied.

positions.title: Responsibility requires agent-constitutive causation
positions.description: For an action to be free is for the agent to be responsible for it. For an agent to be responsible is for agent-constitutive forces to be responsible for it, which means for them to cause it. Prior to the agent's existence, we cannot speak of the agent being made to do anything. Once in existence, being made to do something consists in agent-constitutive forces being directed contrary to the agent's objectives; doing something freely consists in those forces aligning with the agent's objectives.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: For an action on Stalin's part to be free is for Stalin to be responsible for it. And for Stalin to be responsible for it is for Stalin-constitutive forces to be responsible for it... And until such forces, such Stalin-constitutive forces, are in existence, we cannot talk of Stalin's being made to do this or that.

positions.title: Control over mind is prerequisite for freedom
positions.description: If a person does not have control over their future mental states, they do not have inner freedom. Someone who cannot govern their own mind is not free, similar to someone with a degenerative neural disorder who cannot govern their body. True freedom requires the ability to make judgments and decisions appropriately responsive to past experiences and controlled by logic and evidence.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: If a person does not have control over his future mental states, does he have this inner freedom you speak of? MAX: No. If a person cannot govern his own mind, he is not free.

positions.title: Power presupposes freedom
positions.description: There is a distinction between freedom and power. Power is the ability to convert freedom into desired results. Power presupposes the existence of freedom and must be distinguished from it. A rock has power (effects) but not freedom or the ability to do things in the relevant sense.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: There is freedom, and there is power. Power is the ability to convert freedom into desired results. But power presupposes the existence of freedom, and must therefore be distinguished from it.

positions.title: Counterfactuals as epistemic probability relations
positions.description: Counterfactuals are not statements about states of affairs in alternative worlds, but rather express relations of probabilification holding among statements. They register confirmation relations among propositions - the degree of credibility that certain propositions confer on other propositions. The factualist (possible worlds) analysis makes counterfactuals incoherent.
positions.text_evidence: Counterfactuals don't really say anything about events or anything concrete... Counterfactuals merely register confirmation relations among propositions.

positions.title: Factualist analysis of counterfactuals fails
positions.description: The factualist (possible worlds) analysis of counterfactuals is untenable. It requires metaphysically possible worlds with same laws but different facts, which is typically logically impossible. In deterministic worlds, any single event difference requires massive past differences. In indeterministic worlds, counterfactuals lose their truth conditions.
positions.text_evidence: The factualist view is not tenable... Two deterministic worlds cannot differ from each other with respect to a single state of affairs unless they differ with respect to a series of states of affairs extending back into the furthest recesses of time.

positions.title: Possible worlds analysis requires circular mixture of determinism
positions.description: The factualist analysis requires different counterfactuals to be validated by different mixtures of determinism and indeterminism. But all counterfactuals must be evaluated relative to the same world with the same laws. This requires our world to simultaneously exhibit incompatible mixtures of determinism and indeterminism, which is absurd.
positions.text_evidence: The factualist needs the world to be simultaneously characterized by different mixtures of determinism and indeterminism. But this is an incoherent requirement.

positions.title: Counterfactuals are crypto-probability propositions
positions.description: 'If A had been the case, then B would have been the case' means: The proposition K and A gives a high probability to B, where K is the conjunction of propositions we know to be true, minus the proposition that A and B were not the case.
positions.text_evidence: if A had been the case, then B would have been the case means: The proposition K and P(A) gives a high probability to P(B), where K is the conjunction of propositions we know to be true, minus the proposition that A and B were not the case.

positions.title: Probability is epistemic not metaphysical
positions.description: Probability is an epistemic, not a metaphysical, notion. Probability is a logical relation between propositions, not a property of states of affairs. When we say 'there is a 5% probability that Socrates had four kidneys,' we are saying something about epistemic possibility given our data, not about metaphysical possibility.
positions.text_evidence: It is clear, then, that probability is an epistemic, not a metaphysical, notion. Probability is a logical relation between propositions; it is not a property of states of affairs.

positions.title: False propositions probabilify other propositions
positions.description: False propositions probabilify other propositions just like true ones. 'The moon is made of cheese' is false, but it still gives very high probability to 'there is a piece of cheese that weighs more than 100 lbs.' 'Kennedy didn't die in '63' is false but still gives probability to other propositions.
positions.text_evidence: It must be kept in mind that false propositions probabilify other propositions just like true ones. The proposition the moon is made of cheese is false, but that doesn't stop it from giving a very high degree of probability (essentially 100%) to the proposition there is a piece of cheese that weighs more than 100 lbs.

positions.title: Laws cannot be defined except via counterfactuals
positions.description: There is widespread agreement that the concept of a law of nature cannot be defined except in terms of counterfactuals - a law is inter alia something that supports counterfactuals. This creates circularity for the factualist analysis, which must understand counterfactuals in terms of laws.
positions.text_evidence: the possible-worlds analysis of counterfactuals is guilty of covert circularity. For, thus analyzed, counterfactuals can only be understood in terms of laws of nature... But there is widespread agreement that the concept of a law cannot itself be defined except in terms of the notion of a counterfactual (a law is inter alia something that supports counterfactuals).

positions.title: Probability definable from actual events only
positions.description: The relevant kind of probability can be defined wholly in terms of what has happened (not what would happen and not even what must happen in a nomic sense). This avoids the circularity problem in analyzing counterfactuals.
positions.text_evidence: I also argue that the relevant kind of probability can be defined wholly in terms of what has happened (not what would happen and not even what must happen in a nomic sense). So my analysis isn't guilty of any kind of circularity.

positions.title: Indeterminism destroys counterfactual truth conditions
positions.description: If the macroscopic world is non-negligibly indeterministic, the factualist position is nullified. If identical initial conditions lead to an outcome only X% of the time, then the counterfactual 'if I'd turned the key, the car would have started' lacks truth value - at most we can say there's an X% chance it starts.
positions.text_evidence: if the macro-world here is indeterministic, then the factualist position is quite definitely false... The most one can say is: there is a 54% chance that it starts in w. Really, the proposition in w, under the circumstances described, the car starts seems to lack truth-value.

positions.title: Worlds are not sets of propositions
positions.description: Worlds are not sets of propositions. Our world is something which makes propositions be true or false. A set of propositions does not make propositions be true or false. The set <snow is white, grass is green, penguins fly> doesn't make any of those propositions true or false - it exists regardless of their truth values.
positions.text_evidence: Our world is not a set of propositions. Our world is something which makes sets of propositions be true or false. Any world deserving of the name is something which makes propositions be true or false. A set of propositions does not make propositions be true or false.

positions.title: Weak factualism is incoherent
positions.description: Weak factualists (who identify worlds with sets of propositions) hold an incoherent position. They deny that counterfactuals merely register probabilification relations among propositions, and they deny that counterfactuals register facts about other concrete worlds. But these are the only coherent options.
positions.text_evidence: the weak-factualist denies that counter-factuals merely register relations of probabilification (or entailment) among propositions. And he denies that counter-factuals register facts relating to other worlds (in any genuine sense of 'world'). So his position is completely incoherent.

positions.title: Maximal consistent sets useless for counterfactuals
positions.description: Alternative worlds as maximal consistent sets of propositions are absolutely useless for analyzing counterfactuals. Such sets would need to contain propositions covering every possible question, but including all such detail defeats the purpose of counterfactual analysis.
positions.text_evidence: alternative worlds, in the sense of maximal consistent sets of propositions, are absolutely useless for analyzing counter-factuals.

positions.title: Counterfactual truth independent of empirical discoveries
positions.description: The truth of counterfactuals is not contingent on empirical discoveries about the balance of determinism and indeterminism in nature. 'If Jones had struck the window, it would have shattered' doesn't become false if scientists discover unexpected mixtures of determinism. This shows factualist analysis is wrong.
positions.text_evidence: surely the truth of (4) is not contingent on the outcome of such inquiries. So the factualist has mis-analyzed (4).

positions.title: Discipline-relative counterfactual evaluation
positions.description: What a biologist considers counterfactually possible differs from what a physicist considers counterfactually possible because they relativize statements to different data sets within their disciplinary scopes. This may reduce to differences in what propositions are known by practitioners in different fields.
positions.text_evidence: What a biologist considers counterfactually possible differs from what a physicist would consider counterfactually possible... the physicist knows certain things that the biologist does not and that the economist knows certain things that the historian does not.

positions.title: Critique of Humean analysis of laws
positions.description: The Humean analysis of laws as regularities is viciously circular. If states of affairs are themselves causal processes (as they must be), then defining causation as regular concomitance of states of affairs is circular. Additionally, temporal succession itself appears to be a causal notion, making the analysis doubly circular.
positions.text_evidence: Hume says that laws are regularities involving certain kinds of states of affairs. But this presupposes that the notion of a state of affairs is a non-causal one. For obviously if states of affairs are themselves causal processes, then Hume's analysis becomes viciously circular.

positions.title: States of affairs are causal processes
positions.description: It is part of our concept of a state of affairs (excluding completely instantaneous ones) that such things are causal sequences. States of affairs cannot be understood as non-causal. This undermines Humean attempts to define causation in terms of regularities among states of affairs.
positions.text_evidence: It seems to me part of our concept of a state of affairs (leaving aside completely instantaneous ones) that such things are causal sequences.

positions.title: Temporal priority defined causally
positions.description: Temporal succession is itself a causal notion. A standard definition: x precedes y iff some possible (or actual) causal sequence connects x to y. Given that forces are deformations of space-time and no two events are totally causally isolated, x precedes y iff there is some causal process starting with x that ends with y.
positions.text_evidence: A standard definition of temporal priority is this: x precedes y iff some possible causal sequence (e.g., a beam of light) connects x to y... So it seems to me that x precedes y iff there is some causal process starting with x that ends with y.

positions.title: Obsessive beliefs are pseudo-beliefs
positions.description: Obsessive beliefs are pseudo-beliefs - if someone obsessionally believes that such and such, what they actually believe is the opposite. Obsessive hatred represents lingering attachment that won't be admitted. Obsessing that someone is listening to phone calls expresses fear that no one cares.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: if someone obsesses about his hatred of so and so, that is because he is not yet done with so and so. He is still attached to that person... if someone obsessionally believes that such and such, it is because what he actually believes is the opposite of such and such.

positions.title: Compulsive actions are pseudo-actions
positions.description: Compulsive acts are pseudo-actions - if one compulsively does such and such, one's real intention is to do the opposite. Compulsive avoidance of an ex-girlfriend indicates wanting to see her. Compulsive doting on mother actually expresses hatred for her.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: If one compulsively does such and such, it is because one's real intention is to do the opposite of such and such. If a given person compulsively avoids his ex-girlfriend, it is because he wants to see her.

positions.title: OCD essence is ambivalence
positions.description: The essence of OCD is ambivalence - simultaneously loving and hating the same object. The compulsively doting son both loves and hates his mother. Because he loves her, his desire not to be with her must express itself circuitously through compulsive behaviors.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: the essence of OCD is ambivalence. The compulsively doting son both loves and hates his mother. He loves her, and therefore feels obliged to have lunch with her on Sunday. But he also hates her.

positions.title: Doing and undoing in OCD
positions.description: The obsessional engages in 'doing and undoing' (Freud) - does something, then undoes it, then undoes the undoing. Driving to mother's house, then turning back for wallet, then back again for stove. This represents the stalemate between love and hatred, with each emotion expressing itself through the oscillating behavior.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: it is because of this emotional polarity within him that the obsessional engages in what Freud refers to as 'doing and undoing.' He does a given thing. Then he undoes it. And then undoes his undoing of it.

positions.title: OCD behavior as overdetermined
positions.description: OCD behaviors are overdetermined (Freud) - they serve multiple unconscious functions simultaneously. Back-and-forth driving serves both to express ambivalence toward mother and to discharge sexual frustration through piston-like repetitive motion mimicking the sexual act.
positions.text_evidence: MAX: So it is 'overdetermined', to use another expression of Freud's. Dr. K: Precisely... And it also has to do with his undischarged libido.

positions.title: Symbolic representation in OCD symptoms
positions.description: OCD symptoms involve symbolic representation of unconscious desires and conflicts. Fear about stove being left on represents undischarged libido; the stove symbolizes passionate desire. Driving behavior symbolizes sexual act. 'Fear' the stove will explode is really desire that genital region will 'explode.'
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: The stove represents undischarged libido... What he wants is for the stove to explode. The stove represents his passionate desire for his girlfriend. His 'fear' the stove will explode is really his desire that he---meaning his genital region---will, so to speak, 'explode.'

positions.title: Intentions vs desires in action explanation
positions.description: There is a crucial distinction between desires and intentions in explaining action. Intentions are grounded in one's personality; desires need not be. There are free-floating desires that are 'in one, but not of one.' Counterfeit intentions exist where conscious intention differs from unconscious intention.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: Intentions are grounded one's personality. Desires need not be so grounded. There are free floating desires: desires that are in one, but not of one.

positions.title: Compulsive actions achieve opposite of conscious intention
positions.description: The end-result of a compulsively performed action is the opposite of the consciously intended result and coincides with the unconsciously intended result. Compulsive acts of kindness toward mother don't affect her like real kindness but like acts of hostility. Taking mother to endless doctors demoralizes her and weakens her immune system.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: the end-result of a compulsively performed action is the opposite of the consciously intended result---and therefore coincides with the unconsciously intended result... [Compulsive kindness] affects her in the same way as acts of hostility. For that is what they are.

positions.title: Authentic action requires alignment of values and desires
positions.description: Authentic actions must embody both values and desires in alignment. Actions driven by desire without values are instinctual reactions (real but not actions). Actions driven by values without desire are sublimations (actions but not real). Compulsive acts split into two half-actions - one all instinct, one all values - and thus aren't really anything.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: Actual actions must embody beliefs as well as desire. But also necessary that the values in question be in alignment with the desires in question... Desire-driven acts that do not embody values have the authenticity of veritable actions, but not the requisite sublimity, and value-driven acts that do not embody desire have the requisite sublimity of veritable actions... The former are real, but they aren't actions. The latter are actions, but they aren't real.

positions.title: Awareness without consciousness in the obsessional
positions.description: The obsessional is aware of underlying dynamics (mother's treachery, sexual frustration) without being consciously aware. One can be aware of something at the level of unconsciousness. The person doesn't quite see irritating behaviors for what they are even unconsciously, but is surely aware at some level.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: This young man obviously is not consciously aware of his mother's treachery. But he is aware of it. He probably does not quite see it for what it is, even at the level of unconsciousness. But he is surely aware of it.

positions.title: Single ultimate determinant despite overdetermination
positions.description: Despite overdetermination, behavior may have a single ultimate determinant. The son's behavior is overdetermined by both ambivalence toward mother and sexual frustration, but ultimately the sexual frustration is primary - ambivalence exists because mother causes sexual frustration. The two causes operate as a single cause in this context.
positions.text_evidence: MAX: ultimately, his behavior has only determinant, this being his sexual frustration... He is ambivalent towards his mother because she is causing him to be sexually frustrated. So his ambivalence and his sexual frustration are, at least in this context, operating as a single cause. Dr. K: Admirably well put, Max.

positions.title: Consciousness of hatred eliminates compulsions
positions.description: When unconscious hatred becomes conscious, associated compulsions vanish. The compulsiveness exists precisely because the true feelings are unconscious. Once awareness reaches consciousness, the need for disguised expression through compulsion disappears.
positions.text_evidence: MAX: What happens when it [unconscious hatred] becomes conscious? Dr. K: What happens is that any associated compulsions vanish.

positions.title: Compulsive behavior as leaking not acting
positions.description: In compulsive acts, the actor isn't truly acting - rather, sadism or hatred is 'leaking out' of them. Although compulsive acts discharge negative emotions, it isn't the person doing the discharging. The beneficent side is intentional but lacks emotional support, making it behavior one 'undergoes' rather than 'engages in.'
positions.text_evidence: MAX: even though those actions, for lack of a better word, do discharge Max's hatred of his mother, it isn't Max who is doing the discharging. It is rather that his sadism is leaking out of him... it almost fails to be his behavior. It is almost more appropriate to describe it as behavior that he undergoes than it is to describe it as behavior that he engages in.

positions.title: Deracinated values possess the agent
positions.description: When values are deracinated (lacking instinctual basis), it is less appropriate to describe them as values that the agent has than as values that have the agent. The agent is possessed by external value structures rather than authentically holding values integrated with their desires.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: given how deracinated his values are---how lacking in any instinctual basis---it is less appropriate to describe them as values that he has than it is to describe them as values that have him.

positions.title: Unconscious awareness distinct from conscious awareness
positions.description: There are levels of awareness: conscious awareness and unconscious awareness. One can be aware of something (like a mother's meddlesome behavior) without being consciously aware of it. This unconscious awareness is genuine awareness, though it may not involve seeing things fully for what they are.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: This young man obviously is not consciously aware of his mother's treachery. But he is aware of it.

positions.title: Compulsive time management as defense
positions.description: Compulsive punctuality (showing up two hours early) serves as a hedge against the unconscious wish not to show up at all. Being 'hyper on time' insulates against the desire to avoid the obligation entirely. This is another form of doing and undoing.
positions.text_evidence: MAX: He may be compulsively on time—hyper on time, as it were—as a way of insulating himself against his wish not to show up at all. Dr. K: Precisely.

positions.title: True agency requires ideals not just passion
positions.description: True agency is about fulfillment of ideals (not necessarily ethical, but ideals of some kind), not mere desire satisfaction. Where there is desire without values, there is passion (in both contemporary and etymological senses) but not action. An instinctual reaction driven purely by desire is not authentic action.
positions.text_evidence: Dr. K: True agency is about the fulfillment of ideals---not necessarily ethical ideals, but ideals of some kind or other---and where there are no values there are ipso facto no ideals... Where there is desire without values, there is passion---in both the contemporary and the etymological senses of the word---but not action.

positions.title: Probability must be interpreted causally, not statistically
positions.description: When we say the probability of an event is X%, this statement must be interpreted causally, not statistically. What is being said is that, given the laws of physics/nature and the construction of the system, it is a possibility (with degree X) that the event will occur, whether or not it ever actually occurs. Probability is determined by causal mechanisms and laws, not by observed frequencies.
positions.text_evidence: When it is said that that probability of a computer-malfunction is greater than 0%, the statement being made is to be interpreted causally, not statistically. What is being said is that, the laws of physics being what they are and the construction of the computer being what it is, it is a possibility, albeit one that may never be actualized, that brand-new/well-constructed/etc. computers malfunction. (Chapter 7, Conclusion)

positions.title: Statistics are evidence of probabilities but not themselves probabilities
positions.description: Statistics (observed frequencies) are evidence for what the probabilities are, but they are not identical with probabilities. The actual probability is determined by the underlying causal/nomic structure, and statistics can be misleading if they happen to diverge from that structure due to coincidence. Two worlds with identical laws have identical probabilities even if their statistics differ radically.
positions.text_evidence: Statistics are evidence of probabilities; statistics are not themselves probabilities. According to FT [Frequency Theory], statistics are identical with probabilities; therefore, FT is false. Also, statistics are only as truth-conducive as they are consistent with the causal mechanisms that generate them. (Chapter 7, 'Evidence of probabilities vs. actual probabilities')

positions.title: The W1/W2 thought experiment refutes the Frequency Theory of Probability
positions.description: Consider W1 and W2, two worlds with identical laws where computers have probability P of malfunctioning. In W1, the statistics match this probability. In W2, by sheer coincidence, no computer ever malfunctions (0% frequency). Frequency Theory says W2-probability = 0%, but this is false. The actual probability in both worlds is P (determined by the laws), even though the statistics differ. This refutes Frequency Theory.
positions.text_evidence: In W2, the likelihood that a given computer will behave in a program-compliant doesn't exceed the likelihood that a given computer in W1 will do so: the two probabilities coincide. But in W2 the previously-described rash of coincidences obscures the deterministic, and therefore that probabilistic, structure of events. (Chapter 7, 'Probability in relation to higher-order mechanisms')

positions.title: Probability remains constant even when statistics are misleading due to coincidence
positions.description: In a world with the same biological laws as ours, but where by sheer coincidence smokers never get cancer, doctors would be wrong to say smoking is harmless. The probability that a smoker will get cancer in that world is the SAME as in our world, because the probability is determined by the biological laws (which are identical), not by the observed frequencies (which differ).
positions.text_evidence: In a world governed by the same biological---and non-biological laws as ours, but in which by sheer coincidence smokers never got cancer/emphysema, doctors would be no less wrong than they would in our world to tell their patients that smoking was harmless... the probability, in that world, that a given smoker would succumb to cancer/emphysema would coincide with the corresponding probability in our world. (Chapter 7, 'Evidence of probabilities vs. actual probabilities')

positions.title: The Frequency Theory of Probability is false
positions.description: The Frequency Theory of Probability (which identifies probabilities with observed frequencies) is false. Probabilities are determined by laws and causal mechanisms, not by statistical patterns. Identical laws yield identical probabilities regardless of statistical divergences, and different causal structures yield different probabilities regardless of statistical similarities.
positions.text_evidence: The Frequency Theory of Probability (FT) tells us that, in W2, the probability of a computer's never malfunctioning is 100%... This is plainly false. In each universe, the likelihood of computer-error is the same, even though, in W1 the likelihood seems high and, in W2, the likelihood seems low. (Chapter 7, 'Statistical trends reflective of, but distinct from, probabilities')

positions.title: Worlds with identical laws have identical probabilities regardless of statistical divergences
positions.description: If two worlds W1 and W2 have the same laws of nature, they have the same probabilistic structure - the same probabilities for all events - even if their actual statistical frequencies happen to diverge dramatically due to coincidence. Probability supervenes on laws/causal mechanisms, not on observed patterns.
positions.text_evidence: In each universe, the likelihood of computer-error is the same, even though, in W1 the likelihood seems high and, in W2, the likelihood seems low... What is going on is that, in W1, the statistics are consistent with the deterministic-probabilistic structure of the operative laws, whereas in W2 they are not. (Chapter 7, 'Statistical trends reflective of, but distinct from, probabilities')

positions.title: Worlds with different causal/nomic structures have different probabilities even if event-sequences are identical
positions.description: If universe U1 is governed by laws while universe U2 is governed by pure chance (or different laws), then the probabilities of events in U1 and U2 are DIFFERENT, even if the actual sequence of events that occurs in each universe is identical. This is because probability is determined by the underlying causal/nomic structure, not by the pattern of events. U1's events have probabilities determined by laws; U2's events have probabilities determined by chance (or by different laws).
positions.text_evidence: Derived from the W1/W2 argument and the conclusion that 'probability... is to be interpreted causally, not statistically.' If probability depends on causal/nomic structure, then different causal/nomic structures entail different probabilities regardless of event-patterns. This directly refutes epistemic or statistical interpretations of probability.

positions.title: Socrates executed for wartime disruption, not truth-seeking
source: WORK-038 (Why Was Socrates Executed? Part I)
positions.thesis: Socrates was executed for being disruptive during Athens' civil war, not for seeking truth. He caused Athenians to question moral beliefs that undergirded patriotism precisely when Athens most needed political unity.
key_arguments: Athens was in bitter civil war when Socrates was active
Confusing political discussions during wartime assumed to be agit-prop
Socrates' questioning undermined patriotism when most needed
He was guilty of being disruptive, weakening political resolve
Real reason for execution = reason executioners gave, not martyrdom narrative
positions.text_evidence: Socrates caused Athenians to question their pre-existing moral beliefs, which undermined their patriotism at a time when Athens most needed it... Socrates appears to have been guilty of that charge, with the consequence that he weakened political resolve precisely when it was most needed.

positions.title: Philosophical questioning can undermine necessary political unity
source: WORK-038 (Why Was Socrates Executed? Part I)
positions.thesis: While truth is generally preferable to fiction, philosophical questioning of foundational beliefs can weaken necessary social cohesion, especially during crises requiring unified action.
key_arguments: Day-to-day functioning of society requires some positions be uncritically accepted
Socrates targeted precisely these necessary acceptances
Result was disastrous consequences for Athens
Criticism may be valid but still harmful if poorly timed
positions.text_evidence: The day-to-day functioning of a society requires that some positions be uncritically accepted; and Socrates appears to have targeted those acceptances, with disastrous consequences for Athens.

positions.title: Truth out of context as damaging as falsehood
source: WORK-038 (Why Was Socrates Executed? Part I)
positions.thesis: Truths taken out of context, or made available to people who don't know how to contextualize them, can be as damaging as falsehoods and lead to confusion that enables acceptance of falsehoods.
positions.consistency: Pragmatic epistemology - truth requires proper context
key_arguments: Truth in general preferable to fiction
But decontextualized truths can be damaging
People without contextualizing capacity misuse truths
Confusion from decontextualized truth leads to falsehood acceptance
positions.text_evidence: Truths taken out of context, or made available to people who don't know how to put them into context, can be as damaging as falsehoods and are likely to lead to confusion, which in turn is likely to lead to acceptance of falsehoods.

positions.title: Socrates sought power through intellectual influence
source: WORK-038 (Why Was Socrates Executed? Part II)
positions.thesis: Socrates' philosophical activity was a strategy to gain power not through official positions but by installing his worldview at the top through grooming power-adjacent elites. He wanted to be the state, not serve it.
key_arguments: Socrates targeted generals, high-born youth, political players - not regular folk
Strategic selection indicates power-seeking, not pure truth-seeking
Refused official roles because he wanted to rule, not be middle management
Built infrastructure: moral theory, political theory, epistemology
Groomed men who might reach top: Critias, Alcibiades, etc.
Was building a pipeline to install his system
positions.text_evidence: He didn't roam the city talking to farmers or carpenters or regular folk. He talked to generals. To high-born young men. To political players. That wasn't an accident. That was a strategy... He wanted to be the state.

positions.title: Philosopher-king doctrine as mission statement, not abstract ideal
source: WORK-038 (Why Was Socrates Executed? Part II)
positions.thesis: The philosopher-king doctrine was not an abstract ideal but Socrates' mission statement - a concrete plan for installing someone like himself in power. That's why he was executed: his answers, if acted on, would have made him king.
positions.consistency: Demystifying interpretation of Platonic political theory
key_arguments: Ideal regime ruled by philosopher = someone like Socrates
Not abstract ideal but mission statement
Execution was not martyrdom for truth but elimination for trying to win
Others wanted to be king or keep things kingless while staying near top
Socrates was answering questions in ways that would make him king
positions.text_evidence: The ideal regime, in his view, would be ruled not by a soldier, or a priest, or a merchant—but by a philosopher. Not just any philosopher: someone like him... Not an abstract ideal. A mission statement.

positions.title: Socrates operationally trained oligarchic coup leaders
source: WORK-038 (Why Was Socrates Executed? Part III)
positions.thesis: Socrates didn't merely 'lay groundwork' for oligarchic coups - he operationally trained the men who tried to take over Athens (Critias, Alcibiades, Charmides). He was assembling a team, not innocently thinking.
key_arguments: Critias, Alcibiades, Charmides were 'his people'
Not theoretical influence but operational training
Wasn't innocent thinker whose ideas were misused
Was actively assembling a team for power seizure
Connection to Thirty Tyrants not coincidental
positions.text_evidence: He didn't 'lay groundwork.' He trained the men who tried to take over. Critias, Alcibiades, Charmides. These were his people. That's not theoretical. That's operational.

positions.title: Socrates delegitimized authority with replacement theory
source: WORK-038 (Why Was Socrates Executed? Part III)
positions.thesis: Socrates wasn't just exposing ignorance for sport - he was methodically delegitimizing existing authority while building a replacement system where 'someone who knows' (the philosopher) should rule instead of generals, priests, or wealthy.
key_arguments: Publicly, methodically delegitimized authority of those in charge
Had replacement theory ready: philosopher should be king
Not the general, not the priest, not the wealthy - the man of reason
Made theory explicit and built it through training elites
Stayed away from public office to avoid propping up existing system
Wanted different system where he wouldn't be on sidelines
positions.text_evidence: He wasn't just exposing ignorance. He was delegitimizing the authority of the people in charge, publicly, methodically. And he wasn't doing it for sport. He had a replacement theory... One where someone like him—someone who knows—should rule.

positions.title: The Republic as conservative propaganda disguised as political philosophy
positions.thesis: Plato's Republic is fundamentally conservative propaganda designed to rationalize aristocratic power and prevent revolution, not genuine political philosophy seeking justice.
key_arguments: Written by aristocrat with vested interest in maintaining power
Presents ideal society as one where aristocrats keep power
Detailed class distinctions designed to limit inter-class interaction
All done under pretext of designing 'just' society
Actual objective: curb dogs of revolution
Political work of propaganda, not philosophy
positions.text_evidence: What Plato is discussing in the Republic is not the philosopher's ideal society, or some abstract notion of justice, or the human good. What Plato is discussing is the class-relations of Athenian society, and he is doing so in such a way as to rationalize the existing class-divisions.

positions.title: Plato's extreme rationalism is political, not epistemological
positions.thesis: Plato's radical epistemological claim that observables are unreal 'shadows' while only pure thought accesses reality is not genuine epistemology but political belief presented as epistemological belief, driven by fear of political change.
key_arguments: Claim that what we observe consists of 'shadows' while realities known only through pure thought is absurd
Obviously false that nothing ephemeral/observable is real
Otherwise reasonable philosopher holds extreme implausible views - why?
Plato presenting political beliefs as epistemological beliefs
Deeply afraid of political change, wants Athens to stay as is
Spurns change by claiming it's 'unreal' - mere 'shadows'
positions.text_evidence: What is this about? Why did such an otherwise reasonable philosopher have such extreme and implausible views? The reason is that Plato is presenting political beliefs as epistemological beliefs. Plato is deeply afraid of political change.

positions.title: Plato's Good/Real equivalence as coded social stability doctrine
positions.thesis: Plato's linking of 'the Good' with 'the real' makes no literal sense (since bad things are real) but makes perfect sense as code: 'good/real' = what conduces to social stability, 'bad/shadows' = what threatens social stability.
positions.consistency: Decoding of Platonic metaphysics as political theory
key_arguments: Obviously false literally: bedbugs as real as swans, pain as real as pleasure
Idea that bad things are 'unreal' is non-starter
But Plato speaking in code about society, not reality per se
'Good/real' = socially stabilizing
'Bad/shadows' = socially threatening
'Just society' = galley slaves in dungeon, aristocrats enjoying the deck
Cave allegory decoded: those in cave = workers who should stay there
positions.text_evidence: In the Republic, Plato also links 'the Good' with 'the real': which makes no sense if taken literally... But Plato is really speaking in code... The 'good'--i.e. the 'real'--is what conduces to social stability; and 'the bad'--i.e. the 'mere shadows'--are what threaten social stability.

positions.title: Plato's epistemology rationalizes his social position
positions.thesis: Plato's epistemological views make little sense as epistemology but make perfect sense as attempts to rationalize and preserve his privileged social position against threatening changes.
key_arguments: Conservative epistemology serves reactionary political agenda
Not enough to see/feel/touch - 'high priest of intellect' must approve
Translation: before social change allowed, aristocrats must decide
Epistemology deployed as gatekeeping mechanism
Intellectual authority used to block social change
positions.text_evidence: Plato's epistemological views make little sense if taken as epistemological views, but they make plenty of sense if taken as attempts to rationalize his social position.

positions.title: Descartes' epistemology responds to Church authority crisis
positions.thesis: Descartes' epistemological project was fundamentally an attempt to cope with social unrest caused by science stripping Catholic Church of credibility. He sought new certainties to replace discredited Church certainties.
key_arguments: When Descartes wrote, science was just emerging
Every scientific advance revealed Church doctrine as wrong
Enormous source of anxiety given Church's thousand-year domination
Church had provided certainties that proved false
Descartes took on himself to find new certainties
Did epistemology to cope with threatening changes, like Plato
positions.text_evidence: Advances in knowledge were quickly stripping the Catholic Church of all credibility... This had to have been an enormous source of anxiety for Europeans, Descartes included... The Church had provided certainties. Those certainties proved false. So Descartes took it upon himself to find new certainties.

positions.title: Extreme rationalism correlates with anxiety about social change
positions.thesis: Both Plato and Descartes advocate extreme rationalism (overplaying role of thought, underplaying observation) because both were uneasy about social changes - Plato unambivalently hostile, Descartes ambivalently accepting.
positions.consistency: General principle about rationalism and social anxiety
key_arguments: Both philosophers advocate particularly extreme rationalism
Both responded to unease about social changes with rationalism-driven skepticism about the observable
Plato celebrated supposed unreality of observable (complete antipathy to change)
Descartes attempted to overcome it (mixed feelings about change)
Degree of rationalism tracks degree of anxiety about change
positions.text_evidence: Both Philosophers advocate particularly extreme forms of rationalism... The reason for this, I would conjecture, is that both philosophers were uneasy about the changes in question—Descartes ambivalently so, and Plato unambivalently so.

positions.title: Plato's political philosophy replaces world with thought-world
positions.thesis: Plato's system was completely a priori - he wasn't engaging actual world but proposing to replace it with his own thought-world having little reality or justification outside his thought-space.
positions.consistency: Critique of philosophical method in political theory
key_arguments: Plato paradigm case of political philosopher
System completely a priori
Claimed to reform world but really replacing it with his thought-world
Thought-world had little reality outside his thought-space
Not in any real way engaging actual world
positions.text_evidence: Consider Plato, as he is a paradigm case of a political philosopher. Plato's system was completely a priori... He was really talking about how to replace the world with a world of his own—a world that had little reality, and little justification, outside of his own thought-space.

positions.title: Philosopher-king doctrine is incoherent
positions.thesis: The idea that society should be ruled by philosopher-king is incoherent because societies must be run by sapient doers, not sapient thinkers - by people who engage world through action, not by those who flee into intellectual constructions.
key_arguments: Societies run by sapient doers, not sapient thinkers
Leaders must engage world, not flee into intellectual constructions
Therefore not to be run by philosophers (who are thinkers/fleers)
Should be run by intelligent people, but only philosopher-like to extent of being intelligent
Philosopher qua philosopher disqualified for rule
positions.text_evidence: The incoherent idea that society should be ruled by a philosopher king— incoherent, because societies are to be run by sapient doers, not sapient thinkers--- by people who do not flee from the world into intellectual constructions.

positions.title: Marx's political philosophy as destructive a priori fantasy
positions.thesis: Marx explicitly proposed destroying existing social structure and replacing it with system having no real basis in reality (totally fabricated), which when implemented led to unprecedented loss of life and opportunity.
key_arguments: Explicitly about destroying existing structure
Replacement system had no real basis in reality
Totally fabricated basis
Implementation led to unprecedented loss of life
Implementation led to unprecedented loss of opportunity
Textbook case of failure to engage actual political realities
positions.text_evidence: Marx... was talking, quite explicitly, about destroying the existing social structure and replacing it with one that had no real basis---other than a totally fabricated one---in reality, and which, when it was implemented, led to loss of life and opportunity on an unprecedented scale.

positions.title: Empiricizing philosophy enables world-engagement
positions.thesis: Locke and Hume's empiricism represented attempt to make philosophy engage the world. Their political philosophies succeeded precisely to extent they empiricized/anti-philosophized philosophy.
key_arguments: Locke/Hume/Smith created modern free-market economics
Made extremely reasonable points that were enormously successful when implemented
Hard-core empiricists whose agenda was to empiricize philosophy
Criticism of existing philosophy: failed to engage world
Objective: reinvent discipline to engage world
Political philosophies good precisely because almost anti-philosophical
positions.text_evidence: Locke and Hume were hard-core empiricists, their whole agenda as philosophers being to empiricize philosophy. Their criticism of existing philosophy was that it failed to engage the world... Their political philosophies—which were very good—did engage the world; but to the extent that they did, they were almost anti-philosophical.

positions.title: Empiricism collapses into skepticism/autistic withdrawal
positions.thesis: The empiricist project ultimately defeats itself: arch-empiricism (knowledge only of direct sensory evidence) collapses into pure skepticism (knowledge only of conscious states), representing the most complete autistic withdrawal from reality.
key_arguments: Arch-empiricism: don't know it without direct sensory evidence
Empiricism rejects inference rules that would enable positing existence beyond experience
Therefore collapses into view we can know nothing except our conscious states
Pure skepticism = most complete conceivable autistic withdrawal
Attempt to engage world through empiricism ends in maximum withdrawal
Ironic outcome of empiricist project
positions.text_evidence: Empiricism collapses into the view that we can have knowledge of nothing except our own conscious states... So in their attempt to make philosophy engage the world, Locke and Hume empiricized philosophy. But in empiricizing philosophy, Locke and Hume made philosophy collapse into pure skepticism, pure skepticism being the most compete conceivable autistic withdrawal from reality.

positions.title: Material Conditional Does Not Capture "If-Then" Meaning
positions.text: The symbol "→" in classical logic (material conditional) does not mean "if...then" in natural language. It merely means "not both P and not-Q" and is strictly truth-conditional, lacking the modal/entailment force of natural language conditionals.

positions.title: Natural Language Conditionals Are Modal
positions.text: "If P, then Q" in natural language means "Q follows from P" or "not-Q is incompatible with P" - a modal notion involving possibility and impossibility, not reducible to truth-functional logic.

positions.title: The Paradoxes of Material Implication Are Not Genuine Paradoxes
positions.text: The so-called "paradoxes of validity" (e.g., that a false statement entails anything) are not real paradoxes but arise from misconstruing "P→Q" as meaning "if P, then Q" when it actually only means "not (P and not-Q)".

positions.title: Relevance Logic Misguided in Approach
positions.text: Attempts by relevance logicians (Belnap, Anderson) to create truth-conditional approximations of natural language "if...then" are misconceived, as they try to model a fundamentally modal notion using truth-conditional methods.

positions.title: Classical Logic Implicitly Requires Non-Truth-Conditional Conditionals
positions.text: Classical logic is implicitly committed to a non-truth-conditional conception of conditionality, as the Law of Non-contradiction depends on the modal notion that no statement can be compatible with its own negation.

positions.title: Utilitarianism Is Self-Defeating
positions.text: Utilitarianism undermines itself because it presupposes moral bonds and commitments (like those that make Erskin's family deserving of money) while simultaneously permitting violation of those same kinds of bonds when they conflict with utility maximization.

positions.title: Kantian Ethics Implicit in the Concept of Desert
positions.text: The concept of what people "deserve" inherently presupposes a Kantian ethical framework where people are ends-in-themselves and not mere means. Without this, the concept of desert loses meaning.

positions.title: Promise-Keeping Cannot Be Overridden by Utility Calculations
positions.text: If it is morally permissible to break one promise for the sake of greater utility, then the moral bonds that create all obligations (including those that make certain people "deserve" help) are undermined. The permission to violate commitments destroys the ground of all moral desert.

positions.title: Marriage and Parenthood as Moral Commitments
positions.text: Marriage and parenthood constitute special moral commitments that create obligations not owed to others. These commitments are of the same kind as promises and cannot be selectively enforced without contradiction.

positions.title: The Greatest Good Presupposes Anti-Utilitarian Ethics
positions.text: The utilitarian goal of "the greatest good for the greatest number" presupposes a world where people can trust each other, exercise autonomy, and not be defrauded - but this presupposes exactly the kind of non-consequentialist ethics that utilitarianism rejects.

positions.title: Moral Worth of Persons Is Precondition for Any "Greater Good"
positions.text: There cannot coherently be a "greater good" in a world where people lack inherent moral worth and can be treated as mere means. The concept of a greater good depends on people having the kind of value that prohibits treating them merely as means.

positions.title: Consequentialism Generally Is Self-Undermining
positions.text: All forms of consequentialism face the same problem as utilitarianism: they permit actions that undermine the very conditions (trust, autonomy, respect for persons) that make their goals valuable.

positions.title: Sensory Perception Is Reliable
positions.text: We can trust our senses as sources of knowledge. Skeptical arguments against sensory perception fail.

positions.title: Cartesian Skepticism About Senses Can Be Refuted
positions.text: Descartes' skeptical arguments about sensory perception and the evil demon hypothesis can be successfully refuted.

positions.title: We Have Certain Knowledge
positions.text: Despite skeptical challenges, there are things we can know with certainty.

positions.title: The Problem of Induction Has a Solution
positions.text: Hume's problem of induction can be resolved. (Note: Kuczynski's broader work argues for a rationalist a priori solution)

positions.title: Gettier Cases Show Justification Requires Non-Accidental Connection to Truth
positions.text: Gettier's examples demonstrate that justified true belief is insufficient for knowledge. Knowledge requires that the connection between justification and truth not be accidental.

positions.title: Testimony Requires Independent Verification for Knowledge
positions.text: For testimony-based belief to constitute knowledge, one must have independent evidence of the testifier's reliability. Testimony alone is insufficient.

positions.title: We Have Access to Absolute Truth
positions.text: There exists such a thing as absolute truth, and we can know it. Relativism about truth is false.

positions.title: Uncertainty Has Philosophical Value
positions.text: Recognition of uncertainty and the limits of knowledge has important philosophical value (despite our ability to know some things absolutely).

positions.title: Bostrom's Simulation Argument Deserves Serious Consideration
positions.text: Nick Bostrom's argument that we may be living in a computer simulation raises genuine epistemological questions worthy of philosophical analysis.

positions.title: Mind-Body Dualism Has Strong Arguments
positions.text: There are compelling arguments for substance dualism - the view that mind and body are fundamentally different kinds of substances.

positions.title: Materialism Faces the Hard Problem of Consciousness
positions.text: Materialist accounts of mind face severe difficulties explaining subjective conscious experience (the "hard problem").

positions.title: Nagel's Argument Against Materialism Is Sound
positions.text: Thomas Nagel's famous argument (from "What Is It Like to Be a Bat?") successfully refutes materialist theories of mind.

positions.title: Subjective Experience Cannot Be Reduced to Physical Facts
positions.text: The qualitative, subjective nature of conscious experience (qualia) cannot be fully captured or explained by physical/functional descriptions.

positions.title: The Explanatory Gap Is Real
positions.text: There is a genuine explanatory gap between physical/functional descriptions of brain states and phenomenal consciousness that materialist theories cannot bridge.

positions.title: Dennett's Eliminativism About Consciousness Is False
positions.text: Daniel Dennett's attempt to eliminate or explain away phenomenal consciousness fails. Consciousness is real and cannot be explained in purely functional/behavioral terms.

positions.title: Wittgenstein on Language and Thought Contains Important Insights
positions.text: Wittgenstein's views on the relationship between language and thought raise important philosophical questions about meaning and understanding.

positions.title: Referential-Attributive Distinction Is Not Russellian
positions.text: The distinction between referential and attributive uses of descriptions requires a non-Russellian treatment. Russell's theory of descriptions cannot adequately capture this distinction.

positions.title: The Hypothetico-Deductive Method Has Serious Problems
positions.text: The hypothetico-deductive method of scientific confirmation faces significant philosophical difficulties that undermine its status as the primary method of scientific reasoning.

positions.title: Values Play an Essential Role in Science
positions.text: Value judgments are not eliminable from scientific practice. Science necessarily involves value assumptions that go beyond purely empirical matters.

positions.title: Modern Science Has Distinctive Epistemic Features
positions.text: Modern scientific method differs fundamentally from pre-modern approaches to knowledge in ways that have important philosophical implications.

positions.title: Early Modern Scientific Thought Represents Philosophical Breakthrough
positions.text: The scientific revolution of the early modern period (Galileo, Newton, etc.) represents a fundamental shift in philosophical thinking about nature and knowledge.

positions.title: Moore's Proof of an External World Fails
positions.text: G.E. Moore's famous proof of an external world (by holding up his hands) is unsuccessful and does not refute skepticism about the external world.

positions.title: There Is an External World
positions.text: Despite problems with Moore's proof, we can establish that an external world exists.

positions.title: Descartes' Conception of Self Is Defensible
positions.text: Descartes' view of what he is (a thinking thing) can be defended, though aspects require modification.

positions.title: Standard Arguments for God's Existence Fail
positions.text: The traditional arguments for God's existence (cosmological, teleological, ontological) are unsuccessful.

positions.title: There Are Genuine Paradoxes Relating to God
positions.text: The concept of God as traditionally understood generates genuine paradoxes (e.g., omnipotence paradox, problem of evil) that create serious philosophical difficulties.

positions.title: God Is Not Necessary for Morality
positions.text: Morality does not depend on God's existence. Ethical truths and obligations exist independently of whether God exists.

positions.title: God's Foreknowledge Conflicts with Moral Responsibility
positions.text: If God has complete foreknowledge of all future events, human beings cannot be genuinely morally responsible for their actions. Divine foreknowledge and human free will are incompatible.

positions.title: Agnosticism Is More Defensible Than Theism
positions.text: Given the arguments for and against God's existence, agnosticism is a more philosophically defensible position than belief in God.

positions.title: Religious Institutions Face a Fundamental Paradox
positions.text: Religious institutions face an inherent paradox in attempting to systematize and institutionalize what is supposed to be a matter of personal faith and individual spiritual experience.

positions.title: Clifford Right About Epistemic Duty, William James Wrong
positions.text: W.K. Clifford's view that it is wrong to believe anything on insufficient evidence is correct, while William James' defense of the "will to believe" in religious matters is misguided.

positions.title: Dawkins' Critique of Religion Has Merit But Also Problems
positions.text: Richard Dawkins' arguments against religion contain valuable criticisms but also exhibit philosophical weaknesses.

positions.title: Wollstonecraft and Marx Correct About Religion's Social Function
positions.text: Mary Wollstonecraft and Karl Marx were right that religion serves problematic social functions, though their views differ in details.

positions.title: Galileo Was Right to Challenge Religious Authority on Scientific Matters
positions.text: Galileo was justified in asserting the autonomy of scientific investigation against religious authority.

positions.title: Searle's Chinese Room Refutes Strong AI
positions.text: John Searle's Chinese Room argument successfully demonstrates that mere computation/symbol manipulation is insufficient for genuine understanding and consciousness.

positions.title: Turing Test Is Not a Good Test of Intelligence
positions.text: The Turing Test (Imitation Game) is not an adequate test for genuine intelligence or consciousness. Behavioral indistinguishability from humans does not establish presence of mind.

positions.title: Free Will Is Compatible with Determinism
positions.text: Free will and determinism are compatible (compatibilism). We can act freely even if our actions are determined by prior causes.

positions.title: Hume, Frankfurt, and Holbach on Freedom Raise Important Issues
positions.text: The views of Hume, Harry Frankfurt, and Baron d'Holbach on personal freedom and responsibility raise important questions about the nature of human agency.

positions.title: Neuroscience Does Not Eliminate Free Will
positions.text: Recent neuroscientific findings (e.g., Libet experiments) do not actually disprove the existence of free will.

positions.title: Operant Conditioning Does Not Disprove Free Will
positions.text: The existence of operant conditioning and behavioral modification does not establish that we lack free will.

positions.title: Consciousness and Free Will Are Compatible
positions.text: There is no conflict between the phenomenology of consciousness and the existence of free will.

positions.title: We Have Access to Our Own Mental States Through Introspection
positions.text: We have privileged first-person access to our own mental states through introspection. This access is direct and generally reliable.

positions.title: Apes Lack Key Cognitive Capacities Humans Possess
positions.text: Despite significant cognitive abilities in great apes, humans possess distinctive cognitive capacities (likely related to language and abstract thought) that apes lack.

positions.title: Mill's Harm Principle Defensible
positions.text: John Stuart Mill's harm principle (that liberty should only be restricted to prevent harm to others) is fundamentally sound.

positions.title: Liberty Has Intrinsic Value
positions.text: Personal freedom and liberty have inherent value, not merely instrumental value for producing other goods.

positions.title: Hobbes, Marx, Rousseau, Nietzsche Each Identify Central Problems
positions.text: Hobbes' analysis of the state of nature, Marx's critique of capitalism, Rousseau's social contract theory, and Nietzsche's critique of conventional morality each identify important truths about politics and society.

positions.title: Free Markets Have Both Virtues and Vices
positions.text: Free market systems produce important benefits (efficiency, innovation, wealth creation) but also generate serious problems (inequality, exploitation, market failures).

positions.title: Legal Freedom and Substantive Freedom Can Conflict
positions.text: There is a tension between legal/formal freedom (absence of coercion) and substantive freedom (actual ability to pursue one's goals). The free market can maximize the former while limiting the latter.

positions.title: Mill and von Hayek Make Important Contributions to Economic Philosophy
positions.text: Both Mill's and Friedrich von Hayek's analyses of free market economics contain valuable insights, despite their different emphases.

positions.title: Founding Fathers Had Complex Views on Democracy
positions.text: The American founding fathers both supported and opposed democratic elements. The Constitution was neither simply democratic nor simply anti-democratic but mixed.

positions.title: Plato's Republic Prefigures Totalitarianism
positions.text: Plato's ideal state in the Republic shares disturbing features with modern totalitarian regimes (echoing Karl Popper's famous critique).

positions.title: Freedom of Speech Has Limits
positions.text: While freedom of speech is valuable and important, it is not absolute. There are legitimate limits to free expression.

positions.title: Freedom of Speech Deserves Strong Defense
positions.text: Despite legitimate limits, freedom of speech requires robust defense against attempts to restrict it for bad reasons.

positions.title: Prejudice Functions as Psychological Resistance
positions.text: Prejudice and bigotry serve psychological functions (such as maintaining self-esteem, creating in-group solidarity, projecting unacceptable impulses onto out-groups).

positions.title: Freudian Analysis Illuminates Prejudice
positions.text: Psychoanalytic concepts (sublimation, projection, displacement) help explain the psychological roots of prejudice and discrimination.

positions.title: College Admissions Should Consider Standardized Tests
positions.text: Standardized tests should continue to play a role in college admissions despite their limitations, as they provide valuable information about academic preparation.

positions.title: Wealth Redistribution Can Be Legitimate
positions.text: Under certain conditions, government redistribution of wealth is morally justified.

positions.title: Drug Legalization Has Economic Justification
positions.text: There are strong economic arguments for legalizing currently prohibited drugs.

positions.title: Environmental Regulation Preferable to Carbon Taxes
positions.text: Direct environmental regulations are superior to carbon tax schemes for addressing environmental problems.

positions.title: Minimum Wage Laws Are Economically Harmful
