positions.text: Minimum wage laws, despite good intentions, produce negative economic consequences that harm those they intend to help.

positions.title: American Entry into WWI Had Economic Justification
positions.text: The United States' entry into World War I can be justified on economic grounds.

positions.title: US Was a World Leader 1946-1991
positions.text: The United States functioned as a genuine world leader during the Cold War period, despite problematic aspects of its foreign policy.

positions.title: Colonialism Differs from Imperialism
positions.text: Colonialism and imperialism, while related, are distinct phenomena that need to be analyzed separately.

positions.title: Soviet Union Collapse Had Multiple Causes
positions.text: The collapse of the Soviet Union resulted from a combination of economic, political, and social factors rather than any single cause.

positions.title: Late-Term Abortions Raise Serious Ethical Issues
positions.text: Late-term abortions present more difficult ethical questions than early-term abortions, requiring careful philosophical analysis.

positions.title: Suicide Raises Complex Philosophical Questions
positions.text: Schopenhauer's analysis of suicide and related philosophical discussions show that the ethics of suicide is more complex than commonly assumed.

positions.title: Corporate Executives Should Be Held Criminally Liable for Workplace Deaths
positions.text: When corporate negligence leads to deaths or injuries in the workplace, executives should face criminal rather than merely civil penalties.

positions.title: Euthanasia and Medical Ethics Require Careful Analysis
positions.text: End-of-life medical decisions, including euthanasia, raise complex ethical issues that demand rigorous philosophical examination.

positions.title: Business Ethics Faces "Endgame" Scenarios
positions.text: Business people regularly face ethical dilemmas where maintaining ethical principles seems to conflict with competitive success, requiring philosophical analysis of whether ethics and business success are compatible.

positions.title: Camus and Schopenhauer Share Views on Meaning of Life
positions.text: Albert Camus and Arthur Schopenhauer reach similar conclusions about the meaninglessness or absurdity of life, despite different philosophical frameworks.

positions.title: Camus and Nagel on Life's Meaning Converge
positions.text: Camus' absurdism and Thomas Nagel's analysis of life's meaning share important insights about the human condition.

positions.title: Camus' Hero Is Rebel Without Traditional Cause
positions.text: Camus' vision of authentic existence involves rebellion against absurdity without appeal to traditional religious or metaphysical meaning.

positions.title: Schopenhauer on Human Suffering Remains Relevant
positions.text: Schopenhauer's analysis of human suffering and the will contains insights that remain philosophically important.

positions.title: Schopenhauer's Metaphysics Has Fractal Structure
positions.text: Schopenhauer's conception of reality involves a fractal-like structure where the same patterns repeat at different levels.

positions.title: Nietzsche and Schopenhauer on Meaning Differ Importantly
positions.text: While both Nietzsche and Schopenhauer deny traditional religious meaning, their alternative visions differ significantly.

positions.title: Sartre's Later Work Inferior to Earlier Work
positions.text: Sartre's later Marxist writings are philosophically weaker than his earlier existentialist work.

positions.title: Socrates' Execution Involved Multiple Factors
positions.text: Socrates was executed not simply for his philosophical views but due to a complex combination of political, social, and philosophical factors.

positions.title: Pre-Socratics Identify Problem of the One and the Many
positions.text: The pre-Socratic philosophers identified a fundamental metaphysical problem about unity and plurality that remains important.

positions.title: Plato's Cave Allegory Addresses Epistemology and Metaphysics
positions.text: Plato's allegory of the cave makes important points about both the nature of knowledge and the structure of reality.

positions.title: Aristotle's Virtue Ethics Has Enduring Value
positions.text: Aristotle's approach to ethics through virtue and character development provides an important alternative to consequentialism and deontology.

positions.title: Aristotle's Conception of Good Life Remains Relevant
positions.text: Aristotle's analysis of eudaimonia (flourishing/the good life) offers insights applicable to contemporary questions about wellbeing.

positions.title: Aristotle on Virtue More Defensible Than Kant
positions.text: Aristotle's virtue-based approach to ethics is philosophically superior to Kant's duty-based approach in important respects.

positions.title: Kant's Moral Philosophy Has Significant Strengths
positions.text: Kant's ethical theory, based on the categorical imperative and treating persons as ends, captures important moral truths.

positions.title: Kant's Formula of Humanity Captures Core of Morality
positions.text: Kant's principle of treating humanity always as an end and never merely as a means expresses a fundamental moral truth.

positions.title: Locke, Aristotle, and Kant on Virtue Can Be Synthesized
positions.text: The virtue theories of Locke, Aristotle, and Kant can be partially integrated despite their differences.

positions.title: Bentham and Mill on Pleasure Differ Importantly
positions.text: Jeremy Bentham's quantitative hedonism and John Stuart Mill's qualitative hedonism represent significantly different approaches to utilitarian ethics.

positions.title: Mill vs. Hobbes on Liberty Reveals Deep Disagreement
positions.text: Mill's defense of liberty and Hobbes' more restricted view of freedom reflect fundamentally different conceptions of human nature and politics.

positions.title: Rousseau, Goldman, and von Hayek Offer Competing Views of Ideal State
positions.text: Different political philosophers (Rousseau's general will, Emma Goldman's anarchism, Hayek's spontaneous order) present competing but each partially valid visions.

positions.title: Sociology of Knowledge Has Philosophical Importance
positions.text: Sociological factors in knowledge production raise important epistemological questions about objectivity and justification.

positions.title: Cartesian Certainty Possible But Limited
positions.text: We can achieve the kind of certainty Descartes sought, but only for a limited range of propositions.

positions.title: There Are Things We Know with Certainty
positions.text: Despite skeptical challenges, there are propositions we can know with absolute certainty (e.g., "I exist," "I am thinking").

positions.title: Global Warming Evidence Is Compelling
positions.text: The scientific evidence for anthropogenic climate change is strong enough to warrant serious concern and policy responses.

positions.title: Evolution Is Well-Established Scientific Fact
positions.text: The theory of evolution by natural selection is supported by overwhelming evidence and is as well-established as any scientific theory.

positions.title: Fiction Has Important Purpose
positions.text: Fictional narratives serve important functions beyond mere entertainment, including moral education, emotional development, and exploring possibilities.

positions.title: Modern Art Raises Philosophical Questions
positions.text: Works of modern art raise important philosophical questions about the nature of art, beauty, and aesthetic value.

positions.title: Language and Thought Are Intimately Connected
positions.text: Language and thought are deeply interconnected, though the exact nature of their relationship requires careful analysis.

positions.title: Intensionality and Modality Raise Pre-Semantic Issues
positions.text: Questions about intensional contexts and modal operators raise important pre-semantic considerations about meaning.

positions.title: Higher Education Has Distinctive Business Model
positions.text: Universities operate according to a distinctive business model that differs fundamentally from ordinary commercial enterprises.

positions.title: Graduation and Retention Rates May Not Matter Most
positions.text: Contrary to common assumptions, graduation and retention rates may not be the most important metrics for evaluating educational institutions.

positions.title: Universities Sell Degrees, Not Instruction
positions.text: The fundamental product universities offer is credentials (degrees) rather than education or instruction per se.

positions.title: Knowledge Management Principles Don't Apply to Traditional Universities
positions.text: Standard knowledge management principles used in business cannot be straightforwardly applied to traditional university structures.

positions.title: Digitization of Education Often Advisable
positions.text: Many forms of education can and should be digitized when possible for efficiency and accessibility.

positions.title: Alternative Education Models Should Be Explored
positions.text: Alternative models of higher education (like the hypothetical "DMO" described) deserve serious consideration as improvements over traditional university structures.

positions.title: Education Should Emphasize Instruction Over Selection
positions.text: Educational institutions should focus more on providing quality instruction to students than on functioning as gatekeepers selecting the most talented.

positions.title: Consequentialism Self-Defeating Generally
positions.text: The critique of utilitarianism extends to all forms of consequentialism: they undermine the conditions that make their goals valuable.

positions.title: Deontological Constraints Are Rationally Required
positions.text: Deontological constraints (such as prohibitions on treating persons as mere means) are not arbitrary restrictions on utility maximization but rational requirements of coherent moral thinking.

positions.title: Moral Bonds and Commitments Foundational
positions.text: Relationships involving trust, commitment, and special obligations (friendship, family, promises) are foundational to morality rather than being overrideable by impersonal utility calculations.

positions.title: Good Employee Treatment Not Always Profitable
positions.text: While treating employees well can sometimes increase profitability, there are cases where ethical treatment of workers reduces short-term profits, creating genuine ethical dilemmas for businesses.

positions.title: Corporate Social Responsibility More Than Profit Maximization
positions.text: Contra Milton Friedman, the social responsibility of business extends beyond mere profit maximization to stakeholders beyond shareholders.

positions.title: Money Can Function Like a Drug
positions.text: The pursuit of money can exhibit addiction-like psychological patterns that raise ethical questions about capitalism and human motivation.

positions.title: Parable of the Sadhu Reveals Ethical Failures
positions.text: The famous business ethics case "Parable of the Sadhu" reveals how people can fail to act ethically in group situations even when they would act ethically individually.

positions.title: Sublimation Remains Relevant Concept
positions.text: Freud's concept of sublimation (redirecting instinctual energy into socially acceptable outlets) remains psychologically and philosophically relevant.

positions.title: Failure to Sublimate Explains Some Pathologies
positions.text: The "Peter Pan syndrome" and related psychological patterns can be understood as failures of proper sublimation of impulses.

positions.title: Attachment Theory Illuminates Mental Illness
positions.text: Attachment theory provides important insights into the development and nature of various mental illnesses.

positions.title: Evolutionary Psychology Explains Why We Lie
positions.text: Lying and deception have evolutionary explanations related to adaptive strategies in social competition and cooperation.

positions.title: Internal vs. External Motivation Distinction Important
positions.text: The distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic motivation has important implications for psychology, education, and workplace management.

positions.title: Prejudice Has Deep Psychological Roots
positions.text: Prejudice and discrimination are not mere ignorance but have deep psychological roots in ego defense mechanisms and social identity formation.

positions.title: Feminist Theory Identifies Patriarchal Structures
positions.text: Feminist analysis successfully identifies systematic patterns of male dominance in social structures.

positions.title: Critical Thinking Faces Systematic Traps
positions.text: There are identifiable patterns of fallacious reasoning that systematically impede critical thinking and rational discourse.

positions.title: Free Market Creates Contradictions
positions.text: Free market capitalism generates internal contradictions that create economic instability and social problems.

positions.title: Marxist Economics Has Valid Insights
positions.text: Despite problems with Marxist political programs, Marxist economic analysis identifies real features of capitalism.

positions.title: Commodity Fetishism Is Real Phenomenon
positions.text: Marx's concept of commodity fetishism (treating social relations as relations between things) captures a real feature of market economies.

positions.title: Knowledge Capital Deserves Recognition
positions.text: Knowledge and information should be recognized as forms of capital deserving economic analysis.

positions.title: Technology-Caused Job Shortages May Require Welfare State
positions.text: If technology produces structural unemployment, universal welfare programs may become economically necessary.

positions.title: Philosophy Has Value Beyond Practical Application
positions.text: Philosophy is valuable not primarily for practical applications but for expanding understanding, reducing dogmatism, and exploring possibilities.

positions.title: Philosophical Uncertainty Has Value
positions.text: Recognition of the limits of our knowledge and the value of uncertainty is itself an important philosophical insight.

positions.title: Graduate-Level Philosophy Examines More Complex Issues
positions.text: Graduate philosophical work differs from undergraduate work in depth, complexity, and rigor of analysis rather than just in subject matter.

positions.title: Wittgenstein's Private Language Argument Is Flawed
positions.text: Wittgenstein's argument against private language fails because private rules can have enforcement mechanisms through natural consequences (e.g., eating poisonous berries if one misinterprets one's own code), not just social enforcement.

positions.title: Thought Not Inherently Linguistic
positions.text: Thought is not inherently linguistic. There exists visual thought, musical thought, and other non-linguistic forms of thinking. Wittgenstein was wrong to identify thought with language use.

positions.title: Thought Required for Language Understanding
positions.text: Thought is logically prior to language understanding. One must be able to think in order to understand or operate with semantic rules. Therefore, thought cannot be constituted by linguistic rule-following.

positions.title: Wittgenstein's Rule-Following Argument Fails
positions.text: The rule-following argument (that all genuine rules must be social/public) is false because private rules can have real consequences that constitute genuine enforcement without social mechanisms.

positions.title: Late-Term Abortions Specifically Are Unethical
positions.text: Late-term (third trimester) abortions are ethically wrong because neurobiological evidence shows late-term fetuses have psychological characteristics similar to newborn infants, making them persons with rights.

positions.title: Personhood Depends on Psychological Capacities
positions.text: Whether something is a person (and thus has moral rights) depends on whether it has relevant psychological capacities such as consciousness, pain sensitivity, and other mental features.

positions.title: Neurobiological Evidence Relevant to Abortion Ethics
positions.text: Medical/neurobiological evidence about fetal development is directly relevant to and decisive for ethical questions about abortion.

positions.title: "Future Like Ours" Argument Has Force
positions.text: Don Berkich's argument that fetuses have "a future like ours" and abortion robs them of this future provides a legitimate basis for opposition to abortion.

positions.title: Moore's Proof Begs the Question
positions.text: G.E. Moore's attempt to prove an external world by holding up his hands begs the question against the skeptic because the skeptic would not grant the existence of hands as mind-independent objects.

positions.title: Question-Begging Broader Than Premise-Conclusion Identity
positions.text: An argument begs the question not only when the conclusion is identical to a premise, but when the premises would not be granted by one's opponent or when accepting the premises requires already accepting the conclusion.

positions.title: Sophisticated Beliefs Are Distillations of Lower-Level Beliefs
positions.text: Beliefs like "there exists an external world" are sophistications or distillations of lower-level beliefs like "there exists a hand here." The fact that children have the latter without the former doesn't show independent plausibility.

positions.title: Non-Question-Begging Arguments Against Skepticism Possible
positions.text: Unlike Moore's proof, some arguments against skepticism don't beg the question, such as arguing from the existence of mental states plus causation principles to the existence of external causes of those mental states.

positions.title: Nagel's Bat Argument Succeeds Against Materialism
positions.text: Nagel's argument that complete knowledge of bat physiology would not give knowledge of what it's like to be a bat successfully refutes materialist accounts of consciousness.

positions.title: Physiological Knowledge Insufficient for Psychological Knowledge
positions.text: Complete knowledge of an organism's physical/physiological facts does not automatically give knowledge of that organism's phenomenal psychological states (what it's like to be that organism).

positions.title: Intensional Fallacy Response to Nagel Fails
positions.text: The objection that Nagel commits an intensional fallacy (like arguing water isn't H2O because one can know something is water without knowing it's H2O) fails because complete chemical knowledge gives complete knowledge of water, but complete physiological knowledge doesn't give complete knowledge of consciousness.

positions.title: Psychology Not Reducible to Physiology
positions.text: Psychology (what it's like to have certain experiences) cannot be reduced to or identified with physiology, even if they are in some sense correlated.

positions.title: Schopenhauer Correct That Life Involves Suffering
positions.text: Schopenhauer's analysis that life fundamentally involves suffering and that pleasure/happiness are unstable while pain is stable is essentially correct.

positions.title: Life's Meaning Requires Some Strife
positions.text: "Life without pain has no meaning" - some degree of adversity or challenge is necessary for life to have meaning (Schopenhauer's insight).

positions.title: Acceptance of Life's Negativity Enables Its Positive Aspects
positions.text: Both Camus and Schopenhauer agree that accepting what is negative or difficult in life is necessary for gaining what is positive from it.

positions.title: Camus' Absurdism Differs from Schopenhauer's Pessimism
positions.text: While both Camus and Schopenhauer have seemingly negative views of life, they differ fundamentally: Schopenhauer focuses on suffering, Camus on lack of pre-given meaning; and Camus sees lack of meaning as potentially liberating.

positions.title: Search for Pre-Given Meaning Prevents Finding Meaning
positions.text: Searching for what happiness or meaning consists of prevents one from being happy or living meaningfully (insight shared by Camus and Schopenhauer).

positions.title: Lack of Pre-Given Meaning Enables Freedom
positions.text: The fact that life doesn't have a pre-existing meaning is not purely negative; it allows human freedom to create meaning rather than being constrained by predetermined purposes (Camus).

positions.title: Professional Ethics Violations Include Breach of Confidentiality
positions.text: Violations of professional confidentiality (like discussing client information with others without consent) constitute serious ethical breaches regardless of motive.

positions.title: Professional Relationships Create Ethical Boundaries
positions.text: Romantic relationships between professionals and staff members can create ethical problems when they tempt violations of professional duties like confidentiality.

positions.title: Private Conduct Matters When It Affects Professional Duties
positions.text: While private conduct (like marital infidelity) may be morally problematic, it becomes professionally relevant primarily when it leads to breaches of professional responsibilities.

positions.title: Gettier Successfully Refutes JTB Analysis of Knowledge
positions.text: Edmund Gettier successfully showed that justified true belief is not sufficient for knowledge through his counterexamples.

positions.title: Knowledge Requires Non-Accidental Justification-Truth Connection
positions.text: The Gettier cases show that knowledge requires the connection between justification and truth to be non-accidental, not merely that both be present.

positions.title: No-False-Belief Condition Insufficient
positions.text: The amendment that knowledge = JTB with no false beliefs in the justification chain is insufficient, as shown by Goldman's fake barn case.

positions.title: Goldman's Fake Barn Shows Limits of No-False-Belief Amendment
positions.text: In Goldman's fake barn case, Jerry has JTB based on no false beliefs but arguably lacks knowledge due to environmental luck.

positions.title: Knowledge May Be Unanalyzable
positions.text: Williamson's claim that knowledge is a primitive, unanalyzable concept may be correct given the failure of various analyses.

positions.title: Assumption That All Concepts Are Analyzable Is Probably False
positions.text: The assumption that all concepts can be analyzed in terms of more basic concepts is almost certainly false; some concepts must be primitive.

positions.title: Knowledge Need Not Be Accompanied by Self-Knowledge
positions.text: One can know something without knowing that one knows it (contra certain ordinary language analyses). Meta-knowledge involves additional concepts beyond first-order knowledge.

positions.title: Radford's Knowledge-Without-Belief Claim Is Spurious
positions.text: Radford's claim that Jean knew but didn't believe that James I died in 1625 is wrong; Jean both knew and believed but lacked meta-knowledge.

positions.title: Subconscious Knowledge Counts as Belief
positions.text: Williamson's argument succeeds: When someone "guesses" based on subconscious memory, they both believe and know, even if not consciously aware.

positions.title: Bostrom's Simulation Argument Deserves Serious Consideration
positions.text: Nick Bostrom's argument that we may be living in an ancestor simulation run by posthuman civilizations raises genuine philosophical issues.

positions.title: Computational Substrate Independence Is Plausible
positions.text: The "comp" thesis that conscious experiences can be associated with any system implementing the right computational structures is plausible and marks an important difference from mere physical processes like hurricanes.

positions.title: Simulation Hypothesis Creates Epistemological Problems
positions.text: If fsim ≈ 1 (most observers with human-type experiences are in simulations), this creates significant epistemological challenges about knowing we're not simulated.

positions.title: Bostrom's "No Info" Assumption Goes Beyond What Stated
positions.text: Bostrom's "no info" assumption that we can't distinguish simulated from biological experiences assumes more than stated - it assumes something like Byrne's skeptical premise about indistinguishability.

positions.title: Experience Machine Is Bad for Multiple Reasons
positions.text: Nozick's experience machine thought experiment shows that we value not just experiences but also actually doing things and being certain kinds of people.

positions.title: Bostromian Simulation May Be Less Bad Than Experience Machine
positions.text: Living in a Bostromian ancestor simulation might not be as bad as Nozick's experience machine, depending on whether one values being "real" in a fundamental sense.

positions.title: Ethical Relativism Is False
positions.text: Ethical relativism (that what's ethical depends on culture) is false; there are universal moral truths that transcend cultural variation.

positions.title: Informed Consent Has Universal Value
positions.text: The principle of informed consent (patients must have relevant information to make medical decisions) has universal validity across cultures.

positions.title: Cultural Practices Must Be Evaluated by Universal Standards
positions.text: When cultural practices conflict with universal values like health and wellbeing, universal values should take precedence.

positions.title: Surface Cultural Values May Conflict with Deeper Universal Values
positions.text: Cultural injunctions (like Navajo prohibition on negative talk) may embody empirically false beliefs about how to actualize deeper, universal values shared across cultures.

positions.title: Medical Obligations Transcend Cultural Variations
positions.text: A physician's obligation to maximize patient health and minimize suffering is universal and not culturally relative, even if cultures impose conflicting obligations.

positions.title: Universal Values Exist Despite Cultural Differences
positions.text: While cultures differ in some values, certain values (like protecting life and health) are truly universal, though not universally complied with.

positions.title: Withholding Health-Critical Information Is Always Wrong
positions.text: A medical doctor in any culture who withholds health-critical information from patients is failing in professional duties.

positions.title: Mill Affords Broader Liberty Than Hobbes
positions.text: Mill's political philosophy affords citizens a maximally expansive set of liberties based on the harm principle, while Hobbes restricts liberty severely to prevent anarchy.

positions.title: Hobbes' Pessimistic View of Human Nature Justified by Historical Context
positions.text: Hobbes' dim view of human nature and need for strong authority was influenced by witnessing the English Civil War and its anarchic violence.

positions.title: Economic Freedom Requires Political Freedom
positions.text: Mill understood that political freedom (speech, assembly, religion) is necessary for economic prosperity, not just freedom to transact.

positions.title: Minimal Government Compatible with Social Order
positions.text: Contra Hobbes, minimal government intervention (beyond preventing harm) does not lead to anarchy but rather to prosperity and flourishing.

positions.title: Adam Smith's Insights Support Mill's Liberty Principle
positions.text: Adam Smith's demonstration that free economic exchange creates prosperity supports Mill's broader argument for maximal liberty.

positions.title: Excessive Legal Restriction Harms Economy and Society
positions.text: Laws that do more than prevent harm tend to create a sluggish economy, bloated government, and depressed populace.

positions.title: Hobbesian Stability Concerns Overstated
positions.text: Hobbes' fear that freedoms of speech, assembly, and religion lead to civil war and anarchy are overstated; such freedoms are compatible with stability.

positions.title: Clear and Present Danger Standard for Liberty Restrictions
positions.text: The law should only restrict liberty to prevent "clear and present dangers," not mere anticipations or possibilities (Mill's view).

positions.title: Truth Is Correspondence
positions.text: Truth is a property of representations (beliefs, statements) that consists in those representations corresponding to or accurately depicting reality.

positions.title: Truth Requires Representations
positions.text: Truth is not a property of the world itself but a relation between representations and the world. Without representations, there would be no truth (though there would be states of affairs).

positions.title: Truth Is Objective in Important Sense
positions.text: While truth requires representations (subject-dependent), once representations exist, whether they are true is determined by the world, not by us (objective in relevant sense).

positions.title: Inference to Best Explanation as Epistemic Method
positions.text: We determine whether beliefs are true by assessing whether they minimize anomalies and coincidences relative to alternatives. Beliefs that best explain available data are most likely true.

positions.title: We Cannot Compare Beliefs Directly with Reality
positions.text: We cannot directly compare beliefs with the realities they represent; we only know reality through our beliefs, so justification involves coherence and explanatory power.

positions.title: Anti-Skeptical Argument from Coherence
positions.text: We can know we're not dreaming/hallucinating because the skeptical hypothesis (mind creating all experiences ex nihilo) posits more miracles/unexplained facts than realism does.

positions.title: Mental Coherence Indicates External Reality
positions.text: The fact that our mental states are internally coherent and practically useful indicates they represent external realities whose mutual relations the relations of our experiences shadow.

positions.title: Global Skepticism Posits Too Many Miracles
positions.text: If all experiences arise ex nihilo in the mind without external support, this would be a vast collection of unexplained miracles. Hypotheses that minimize miracles are to be preferred.

positions.title: Modern Epistemology Errs in Starting with Doubt
positions.text: Modern epistemology (post-Descartes) mistakenly starts with radical doubt (Evil Demon) rather than with belief (Angel of Truth), making it artificially difficult to account for knowledge.

positions.title: Better to Explain Error in Terms of Knowledge
positions.text: Epistemology should start with paradigm cases of knowledge and explain error as deviation, rather than starting with skeptical doubt and trying to explain knowledge.

positions.title: Schopenhauer's Fractal Metaphysics A Priori More Reasonable
positions.text: Schopenhauer's view that reality has a fractal structure (micro replicates macro, with will and idea throughout) is more reasonable than materialism which makes mind an inexplicable anomaly.

positions.title: Materialism Either Absurd or Implausible
positions.text: Materialism either absurdly denies mind exists or implausibly claims mind is identical with physical processes to which it bears no apparent resemblance.

positions.title: World as Will and Idea Throughout
positions.text: Schopenhauer's conception that the world is fundamentally will and idea at all levels (not just in humans) avoids the problem of mind as inexplicable cleavage in a purely physical world.

positions.title: Religious Belief Requires Evidence
positions.text: W.K. Clifford is right that religious belief, like other beliefs, requires adequate evidential support. William James' "will to believe" defense is inadequate.

positions.title: Religious Believers vs. Skeptics Disagree on Standards of Evidence
positions.text: The debate between religious believers (like O'Reilly) and skeptics (like Dawkins) often centers on what counts as adequate evidence for God's existence.

positions.title: Arguments from Design and Fine-Tuning Have Force
positions.text: The argument that the universe's apparent design and fine-tuning for life provides some evidence for God's existence is not obviously fallacious.

positions.title: Problem of Evil Seriously Challenges Theism
positions.text: The existence and extent of suffering in the world poses a serious philosophical problem for belief in an omnipotent, omniscient, omnibenevolent God.

positions.title: Jaynes' Bicameral Mind Hypothesis Is Speculative But Interesting
positions.text: Julian Jaynes' hypothesis that ancient peoples experienced thoughts as auditory hallucinations (voices of gods) is speculative but offers interesting explanatory power for religious phenomena.

positions.title: Ancient Religious Experience Was More Direct Than Modern
positions.text: For ancient peoples, religious experiences may have been more direct and experiential (through hallucinations) rather than requiring faith in implausible doctrines.

positions.title: Modern Religiosity Lacks Psychological Force of Ancient Religion
positions.text: Modern religious belief, not being experientially grounded like ancient "bicameral" religion, lacks the psychological reality and force it had for ancients.

positions.title: Blindsight Analogy Illuminates Pre-Reflective Consciousness
positions.text: The phenomenon of blindsight (seeing without knowing one sees) helps explain how ancient peoples could be conscious without being self-conscious or reflective.

positions.title: Religious Figures Now Have "Second-Class Existence"
positions.text: For modern people, God exists as a background figure called upon in need, lacking the primary reality He had for ancients who directly experienced divine presence.

positions.title: Modern Science Risks Faustian Separation from Nature
positions.text: Modern science's attempt to rise above and dominate nature (symbolized by Faust) risks losing connection with our natural grounding.

positions.title: Neuroscience Can Potentially Increase Happiness
positions.text: David Pearce's argument that neuroscience and genetic engineering could dramatically increase human happiness has merit, despite measurement difficulties.

positions.title: Subjective Happiness Not Quantifiable But Neural Correlates Are
positions.text: While happiness as subjective experience may not be quantifiable, its neural underpinnings (neuron firing patterns, neurotransmitter levels) are quantifiable and manipulable.

positions.title: Conscious Experience as Epistemic Foundation
positions.text: Conscious experience provides the epistemic foundation for all empirical knowledge, including physics itself. The apparent conflict between relativity and temporal experience cannot be resolved simply by declaring experience illusory, because conscious experience provides the epistemic foundation for all empirical knowledge. This means that theory-laden perception does not undermine perceptual justification - conscious experience remains foundational even when structured by background beliefs.

positions.title: Phenomenology Resists Reduction to Physical Description
positions.text: Temporal experience exhibits the same kind of first-personal, qualitative character that makes consciousness irreducible to physical description. Just as Nagel's argument against materialism succeeds by showing that objective physical description cannot capture subjective experience, so temporal becoming might resist reduction to B-series relations because it essentially involves the subjective standpoint of a conscious observer. The phenomenology of experience has irreducible epistemic significance.

positions.title: Subjective Standpoint and Epistemic Access
positions.text: The connection to consciousness runs deeper than mere analogy. Temporal becoming might resist reduction to B-series relations because it essentially involves the subjective standpoint of a conscious observer. The subjective standpoint is not an epistemic limitation to be overcome but a constitutive feature of how conscious beings access reality. This means background beliefs that structure perception are part of the constitutive framework, not defeaters of justification.

positions.title: Modified Cartesian Position on Temporal Consciousness
positions.text: The thinking subject exists essentially as a temporal being, not merely as a being located in time. The ego cogito experiences duration from within, not as an external temporal container but as the very structure of conscious experience itself. Time's apparent passage might be neither objective feature of reality nor mere illusion, but rather the form under which reality necessarily appears to finite conscious subjects.

positions.title: Manifest Image and Scientific Image Relationship
positions.text: McTaggart's argument opens onto fundamental questions about the relationship between subjective experience and objective reality, between the manifest and scientific images of the world. Its enduring significance lies in its demonstration that rigorous conceptual analysis can reveal deep structural problems in our most basic metaphysical commitments—problems that resist easy dissolution and point toward the need for more sophisticated philosophical frameworks that can accommodate both the demands of logical rigor and the irreducible character of conscious temporal experience.

positions.title: The B-Series is Generated by Change
positions.text: The B-series is not opposed to change; it is generated by change. When A occurs, B is not yet occurring; when B occurs, C is not yet occurring. Change produces succession, and succession produces the B-series. Its unchanging order is the outcome of the generative process, not evidence of stasis. The 'now' is simply the boundary between what has been generated and what has not yet been generated.

positions.thesis: Semantic Externalism for Proper Names
positions.text: For proper names, semantic externalism is true: An utterance of 'Rocko weighs over five lbs' in world W is true exactly if the object referred to as 'Rocko' in W weighs over five lbs. Reference is world-relative, and truth-conditions are determined by which object the name picks out in that world, following direct reference theory.

positions.thesis: Three Ways to Acquire Name Reference
positions.text: There are three distinct ways to add a proper name to one's lexicon: (i) ostensive definition (being shown the referent), (ii) descriptive definition (learning a uniquely individuating description), and (iii) testimonial acquisition (learning the name from someone else's use, which reduces to knowing a description like 'the person x such that on occasion t, speaker S referred to x').

positions.thesis: Reference Mediated by Description-Knowledge
positions.text: One's ability to refer to an object depends on knowing some uniquely individuating description that the object satisfies. This holds even for names acquired testimonially at many removes from direct acquaintance. Reference is not a primitive causal relation but mediated by descriptive knowledge.

positions.thesis: Kripke Cases Support Descriptivist View
positions.text: Kripke-style cases (like little Timmy learning 'Shakespeare' as 'author of Romeo and Juliet' when Shakespeare didn't write that play) actually support rather than refute descriptivism. Timmy can still refer to Shakespeare because his real description is 'the person to whom, on occasion t, Daddy ascribed writing Romeo and Juliet'—a description Shakespeare satisfies.

positions.thesis: Names Acquired via Testimony Reduce to Type (iii)
positions.text: Most apparent cases of acquiring names via pure description (Type ii) are actually cases of testimonial acquisition (Type iii). When you learn 'Socrates wrote the Nichomachean Ethics' from Charlie (who is wrong), your real description is 'the person to whom Charlie ascribed writing those works on occasion t.'

positions.thesis: Julius Cases Show Limits of Pure Descriptivism
positions.text: In cases where one stipulates 'Julius' as 'the inventor of the zipper' without knowing who that is, one arguably doesn't know which proposition is encoded in 'Julius must be worth a fortune.' One has metalinguistic knowledge that simulates understanding without constituting genuine understanding of the proposition expressed.

positions.thesis: Metalinguistic Knowledge Can Simulate Understanding
positions.text: One can have metalinguistic knowledge (e.g., 'for some object O, this sentence is true iff O has property P') that simulates real understanding without being genuine understanding. This explains how we can operate with names whose referents we don't know, and why language facilitates information transmission even without complete understanding.

positions.thesis: Gricean Theory of Meaning is False
positions.text: Grice's attempt to analyze conventional meaning in terms of speaker's meaning fails. Conventional meaning is prior to and presupposed by speaker's meaning, not constructed from it. One cannot mean proposition P by utterance U unless U already conventionally means P.

positions.thesis: Speaker Meaning Presupposes Conventional Meaning
positions.text: A speaker cannot mean P by utterance U unless the speaker believes that U already conventionally means P. Speaker's meaning essentially involves exploiting antecedent conventional meanings. Grice reverses the order of explanatory priority.

positions.thesis: Compositionality Refutes Grice
positions.text: The compositional nature of language is incompatible with Gricean analysis. Unuttered sentences already have determinate meanings because their constituents have meanings and there are recursive rules. Grice cannot explain how sentences never uttered have fixed meanings, since nobody has ever meant anything by them.

positions.thesis: Sentence Meaning Involves Success-Conditions
positions.text: To know what a sentence means is to know under what circumstances a tokening of it succeeds or fails in whatever illocutionary task it is designed to accomplish. Assertions succeed iff true, questions succeed iff answered truthfully, commands succeed iff obeyed. Success-conditions, not speaker intentions, determine literal meaning.

positions.thesis: Information Transmission Requires Computation
positions.text: Information that one takes away from verbal exchange is absorbed through the process of figuring out what is meant by the speaker's words. Understanding involves computational work exploiting both semantic and non-semantic background knowledge. Literal meaning directs but does not determine communicated information.

positions.thesis: Hesperus=Phosphorus Problem Solved by Distinguishing Meaning Levels
positions.text: The apparent problem that 'Hesperus=Hesperus' and 'Hesperus=Phosphorus' have the same literal meaning despite different cognitive significance dissolves when we recognize that understanding requires computing meanings using background knowledge. They have same literal meaning but different significance relative to typical epistemic contexts.

positions.thesis: Historical Etymology Irrelevant to Synchronic Meaning
positions.text: The historical process by which words acquired meaning is irrelevant to explaining current meaning-facts. 'Socrates' in English was borrowed from French, which got it from Latin, which got it from Greek—but the word had full semantic properties from its first use. Gricean analysis at best identifies historical precursors, not meaning itself.

positions.thesis: Context Required to Compute Sentence-Token Meanings
positions.text: One must exploit background semantic and non-semantic knowledge to compute the meanings of sentence-tokens that one encounters. Sentence-types have standing meanings, but grasping what a particular tokening means requires contextual computation. This is why identical sentence-tokens can convey different information in different contexts.

positions.thesis: Analyticity Exists and is Not Reducible to Synonymy
positions.text: Analytic truth exists but cannot be understood purely in terms of synonymy relations. Quine's argument against analyticity fails because it wrongly assumes all analytic truths must be modeled on 'all bachelors are unmarried.' Many analytic truths hold in virtue of conceptual structures, not synonymy.

positions.thesis: Quine's Argument Against Analyticity Fails
positions.text: Quine's argument that analyticity reduces to synonymy and therefore involves vicious circularity is multiply flawed. It assumes only biconditionals with synonymous flanks can be analytic (false: 'n is an even prime iff n is one less than three'). It conflates formal truth with conceptual truth. It overgeneralizes from trivial to non-trivial analyticities.

positions.thesis: Proof is Abstract Object Not Behavioral Process
positions.text: A proof is an abstract object—a network of logical relations holding among abstract objects (propositions, numbers). It must be distinguished from: (a) psychological knowledge of the proof, and (b) behavioral expressions (writing symbols). Symbol manipulation may cause or express proof-knowledge but never constitutes it.

positions.thesis: Truth-in-L is Not Truth-in-All-Models-of-L
positions.text: Truth-in-language-L is not identical to truth-in-all-models-of-L. The former is determined by L's actual semantic rules; the latter by formal properties invariant under certain transformations. Model-theoretic truth is a construct useful for studying formal properties but doesn't define truth in a language.

positions.thesis: Propositions Exist
positions.text: Propositions exist as abstract objects that are the bearers of truth and falsity. They exist independently of being grasped by any mind. There were true propositions before there was thought. Quine and others who deny propositions are straightforwardly wrong.

positions.thesis: Abstract Objects Exist
positions.text: Abstract objects exist, including propositions, numbers, and logical relations. Proof is a network of logical relations among such abstract objects. These are not reducible to physical objects, mental states, or behavioral dispositions.

positions.thesis: Truth-Bearers Are Propositions Not Sentences
positions.text: The primary bearers of truth are propositions, not sentences. Sentences have success-conditions relative to illocutionary acts. A sentence-token succeeds iff it encodes a true proposition (for assertions), but truth itself is a property of propositions.

positions.thesis: Singular Propositions Exist
positions.text: Singular propositions exist—propositions directly about particular objects (e.g., 'R has property phi' where R is a particular rock). Semantic externalism is vindicated by the existence of singular propositions, even though mental contents are not singular propositions.

positions.thesis: Pre-Theoretic Intuitions About Self-Knowledge Are Data
positions.text: Our pre-theoretic conviction that we have Cartesian certainty about our thought-contents is a datum that philosophical theories must accommodate. A theory that denies this datum is thereby shown to be false. Philosophical theorizing cannot legitimately overturn such basic epistemic facts.

positions.thesis: Distinguish Content-Externalism from Semantic-Externalism
positions.text: Failure to distinguish content-externalism (about mental content) from semantic-externalism (about word meaning) has led to widespread confusion in contemporary philosophy. These are distinct doctrines with different truth-values and must be carefully separated in philosophical analysis.

positions.thesis: Arguments Must Consider Full Range of Epistemically Identical Worlds
positions.text: When evaluating claims about self-knowledge and epistemic access, one must consider the full range of epistemically identical worlds, not just convenient subsets. Burge's argument fails because it only considers worlds where external objects exist, ignoring other epistemically identical possibilities.

positions.thesis: Phenomenology is Evidence for Structure of Experience
positions.text: It is a highly reasonable (though conceivably defeasible) presumption that perceptual experiences have the structure they appear to have phenomenologically. Since visual perception strongly appears not to have sentential structure, this is good evidence it lacks such structure. Theoretical considerations cannot easily override phenomenological evidence about experience.

positions.thesis: Content Externalism is False
positions.text: Content externalism—the doctrine that mental content is constitutively dependent on environmental facts about causal origins—is false. Two brain-states can be qualitatively identical yet have the same contents even when embedded in different environments. Twin Earth arguments fail to establish that mental content is fixed by external factors.

positions.thesis: Semantic Externalism is True But Distinct from Content Externalism
positions.text: Semantic externalism (the doctrine about what words mean) must be sharply distinguished from content externalism (the doctrine about what people think). Semantic externalism is unqualifiedly true even though content externalism is unqualifiedly false. Failure to distinguish these doctrines has led to widespread confusion.

positions.thesis: Mental Content as Knowledge of Existence-Claims
positions.text: To have a concept of x is to know some existence-claim that x uniquely satisfies. Mental content is determined by the subject's knowledge of descriptions, not by causal-historical chains to external objects. Concepts are individuated by the existence-claims one knows, not by external reference.

positions.thesis: Concepts Are Not Object-Involving
positions.text: Concepts do not have object-involving content. Even in paradigm cases of causally-mediated perception, one's concept has the form 'there is something x having such and such properties over in that place' rather than 'Smith has phi.' Mental content is given by existence claims that objects satisfy, not by the objects themselves.

positions.thesis: Mental States Can Exist Without Referents
positions.text: A mental state can exist even if its apparent referent does not exist. Jones could have a concept qualitatively identical to his concept of Smith even if Smith didn't exist, because the concept's content is given by a description rather than being constituted by Smith himself.

positions.thesis: Self-Knowledge Has Cartesian Certainty
positions.text: We have Cartesian certainty about the contents of our own thoughts. It is a datum that I know with special certainty that I am thinking 'I want to drink water' rather than some other thought. This immediate self-knowledge cannot be undermined by philosophical theory.

positions.thesis: Content Externalism Undermines Self-Knowledge
positions.text: Content externalism is incompatible with Cartesian self-knowledge. If externalism were correct, there would be epistemically identical worlds where one fails to have the thoughts one actually has. Therefore one couldn't know with certainty which thoughts one is having, contradicting the datum of self-knowledge.

positions.thesis: Burge's Slow-Switching Argument Fails
positions.text: Burge's argument that content externalism is compatible with self-knowledge fails. It only considers worlds where the relevant objects exist, not the full set of epistemically identical worlds. It either begs the question or erroneously identifies epistemically identical worlds with a subset where external objects have been loaded into thought-contents.

positions.thesis: Computational Theory of Mind (CTM) is False
positions.text: The Computational Theory of Mind—the doctrine that thinking consists in the manipulation of symbols in virtue of their syntactic (morphological) properties—is fundamentally false. It involves a misunderstanding of syntax and cannot account for the nature of cognitive achievement.

positions.thesis: Shape is Neither Necessary Nor Sufficient for Meaning
positions.text: Given any language (actual or possible), an object's having a particular shape is neither necessary nor sufficient for being assigned meaning by that language. Syntax cannot be given a purely morphological characterization.

positions.thesis: Visual Perception Has Non-Sentential Structure
positions.text: Visual perceptions do not have sentential or propositional structure. Perceptual experience is fundamentally analog rather than digital. There is no evidence from phenomenology that perceptions decompose into discrete symbolic elements the way sentences do.

positions.thesis: Perceptual Content is More Fine-Grained Than Linguistic Content
positions.text: Perceptions are much more fine-grained than sentences. What you see is never just 'red' but always a hyper-specific shade. Language uses broad rubrics that fail to capture the structure of perception. Even demonstratives like 'that exact shade' don't have structure comparable to perception.

positions.thesis: Non-Conceptual (Non-Propositional) Content Exists
positions.text: Non-conceptual content must be understood on its own terms, not as conceptual content in disguise. Perceptual content is not propositional content requiring demonstrative supplementation. The structure of perception is fundamentally different from the structure of sentences or propositions.

positions.thesis: Mental Content is Not Categorically Sentential
positions.text: Not all mental content has sentential or para-sentential structure. Dreams, fantasies, mental images, aesthetic reactions, and emotional responses lack the digital/propositional structure that Language of Thought requires. These are genuine forms of representation that cannot be reduced to sentential form.

positions.thesis: Aesthetic and Emotional Reactions Lack Sentential Structure
positions.text: Aesthetic and emotional reactions do not have sentential structure. They are amorphous and lacking in the compositional structure characteristic of sentences. Yet they track objective realities and are genuinely representational, showing that awareness of objective fact need not be embodied in sentential structures.

positions.thesis: Phenomenology is Constitutive of Aesthetic Reaction
positions.text: The phenomenological aspects of aesthetic experience are constitutive of the aesthetic reaction itself, not merely effects of underlying judgments. A being with no phenomenological reaction to music would fail to have an aesthetic reaction to it, even if it made correct judgments about musical structure.

positions.thesis: Sentential Content is Derivative Not Fundamental
positions.text: Sentential/propositional contents are highly derivative representations that must be distillations of non-digital information. Perception is fundamental to our understanding of external reality, and perceptual content is non-sentential; therefore sentential content cannot be the basic form of mental representation.

positions.thesis: Pre-Linguistic Creatures Can Think
positions.text: Animals and pre-verbal infants have true and false mental activity. Linguistic competence is not constitutive of the ability to think, since the ability to think is a prerequisite to learning a language. Davidson's view that one must know a language to think is false and incompatible with anyone meaning anything.

positions.thesis: Cognitive Maps Underlying Perception are Egocentric
positions.text: The cognitive maps embodied in perception have fundamentally egocentric character. There is a privileged reference-point p such that the map's information about place x is more accurate than about place y exactly if x is closer to p on the map. These egocentric maps are the cognitive apparatus through which we learn about external reality.

positions.thesis: Language is Not in Lock-Step With Thought
positions.text: Language's utility depends precisely on its not being in lock-step with thought. We can use expressions meaningfully without fully understanding them (e.g., 87-digit number expressions). Symbols can facilitate information transmission even when nobody knows their exact referents, because understanding involves knowing how they mean rather than what they mean.

positions.thesis: Analytic Functionalism via Ramsey-Lewis Sentences Fails
positions.text: The attempt to analyze mental states through Ramsey-Lewis sentences fails because it reduces to behaviorism. Mental states cannot be defined purely in terms of inputs, outputs, and functional roles. The psychological conditions constituting knowledge are distinct from their behavioral expressions.

positions.thesis: Proof-Derivation is Not Behavioral
positions.text: Proof itself is an abstract object—a network of logical relations among abstract objects. Knowledge of a proof is a psychological condition. Communicating a proof involves producing symbols. These three must be distinguished. Symbolic manipulations may cause or express proof-knowledge but are never constitutive of it. CTM conflates behavioral expressions with cognitive states.

positions.thesis: Calculator Intuitions Depend on Background Knowledge
positions.text: The intuition that calculators 'think' or 'compute' vanishes when we consider a qualitatively identical object created by freak accident in an environment lacking our symbolic conventions. Our intuitions about machine cognition telescope in background knowledge of socio-psychological conditions and designers' intentions, making them unreliable guides to whether symbol manipulation constitutes thought.

positions.text:
A listener of mine, Emma, asked me: What are some early signs of OCD in children?
      Here  is  my  answer:  First  of  all,  all  children  are  OCD.  In  fact,  OCD  is  actually  a
prolongation into adulthood of the child's 'binary' emotional relation to the world. When you
send a child to camp, he kicks and screams and cries. Then he has to come home, he cries again.
Also, children, much like obsessive-compulsives, are soothed by repetition and like familiarity.
They like to be told the same story at bedtime, night after night, and sleep with the same stuffed
animal. That said, children who are actually OCD--i.e. who will be OCD as adults--do have
some distinctinve characteristics. The main difference between them and non-OCD children is
that they tend to have rituals and peculiarities that are specific to them. All children like to sleep
with same stuffed animal night after and be told the same story night after night. But only OCD
children have to sleep with a glass of water next to the bed or have to put their left shoe on
before their first. Also, OCD children tend to be more cholicy and more emotionally intense and
extreme than others children---more prone to bouts of depressive withdrawal. But I stress that
OCD is never itself the issue, and OCD is not eliminated by being confronted head-on. OCD is
about withdrawal from a frightening world into a safe space of thought and ritual, and the only
way to conquer OCD is to conquer fear. Which one does by developing strengths of character
and intellect. OCD is given rise to by emotional vulnerability, which in turn is given rise to by
actual vulnerability--by defenselessness. And the way to defeat OCD is to give proper, non-
neurotic defenses to the person in question.      The Failure of Political Philosophy to Engage Reality

metadata.source: OCD, Bureaucracy and Psychopathy Volume 1

positions.text:
I wrote an audio-article in which I said that both philosophy—specifically, philosophical
skepticism---and OCD were about retreating from the exigencies of external actual reality into
the  safety  of  internal  pseudo-reality.  And  in  keeping  with  this,  I  said  that  both  OCD  and
philosophy are intellectualized impotence.
    In any case, a listener of mine who goes by the name of L and H Productions, made the
following point in response to my article. He said: “What about political philosophers? Don’t
they engage the world? Aren’t they a counter-example to your point, that philosophy is about
autistic non-engagement?”
      Yes and no. Mostly no. Consider Plato, as he is a paradigm case of political philosophers.
Plato’s system was completely a priori. Yes—he was talking about how to reform the world. But
really, that isn’t what he was doing. He really talking about how to replace the world with a
world of his own—a world that had little reality, and little justification, outside of his own
thought-space. So Plato was really talking about replacing the actual world his thought-world.
He wasn’t in any real way engaging the actual world.
      And the absurdity of what Plato was doing comes out in a lot of ways. In, for example,
the  rigidity  of  the  society  that  he  was  proposing;  in  the  stereotyped—and  in  that  respect
decidedly OCD---protocols governing both education and, more generally, interactions between
different people; and, moreover, in the absurd idea, put forth in this dialogue The Laws, that even
the most minor violations of law should be punished by death; and, finally, in the incoherent idea
that society should be ruled by a philosopher king—incoherent, because societies are to be run
by sapient doers, not sapient thinkers; societies are to be run by people who do not flee from the
world into intellectual constructions and, therefore, are not to be run by philosophers, granting,
of course, that they are to be run by people who are philosopher-like to the extent, but only to the
extent, that they are intelligent.
    Or consider Marx, another textbook case of a philosophical philosopher. Yes—he was
talking  about  how  to  change  the  world.  But  really,  he  was  talking,  quite  explicitly,  about
destroying the existing social structure and replacing it with one that had no real basis---other
than a totally fabricated one----in reality, and which, when it was implemented, led to loss of life
and opportunity on an unprecedented scale.
      Now, not all political philosophers are as guilty as Marx and Plato of failing to engage
actual political realities. Some political philosophers do not simply propose that existing political
realities be replaced with their childish a priori fantasies.
    For example, John Locke made a number of extremely reasonable points about law and
economics that, when implemented, were enormously successful. And much the same is true,
though in some respects to an even greater degree, of Adam Smith and David Hume, who, along
with Locke, created the discipline of modern free-market economics.
    But Adam Smith was really an ex-philosopher who left the discipline of philosopher in
order to start a new discipline. And Locke and Hume were hard-core empiricists: their whole
agenda, as philosophers, was to empiricize philosophy. Their criticism of existing philosophy
was that it failed to engage the world, and their objective was to reinvent the discipline in such a
way that it did engage the world.
    So, yes, their political philosophies—which were very good---did engage the world; butto the extent that they did, they were almost anti-philosophical political philosophies.
    That said, political theory can engage the world, and that does represent a limitation on
the  scope  of  my  point  about  philosophy  categorically  being  in  the  nature  of  an  autistic
withdrawal from reality.
    But there is one last point to make, which does to some extent buttress my conception of
philosophy as autistic retreat. Hume and Locke were arch-empiricists, meaning that, in their
view, you don’t know it if you don’t see it (or otherwise have direct sensory evidence of it).
Now, empiricism collapses into the view that we can of nothing except our own conscious states;
for the empiricist rejects all rules of inference would enable us to posit the existence of anything-
--the empiricist says that we can know it only if it is a datum of consciousness and, therefore,
that if we have to posit it, meaning infer its existence, then it doesn’t exist.
    So in their attempt to make philosophy engage the world, Locke and Hume empiricized
philosophy. But in empiricizing philosophy, Locke and Hume made philosophy collapse into
pure skepticism, pure skepticism being the most compete conceivable autistic withdrawal from
reality into one’s own mind. And what this shows is that, even when philosophers try to make
philosophy not be an autistic retreat, it ends up being precisely that. Which does rather confirm
my conception of philosophy as autistic retreat.                                OCD and Philosophy

metadata.source: OCD, Bureaucracy and Psychopathy Volume 1

positions.text:
Obsessive-compulsives and philosophers have a lot in common. First if all, they are both
compulsive doubters. They doubt the obvious. They rationalize their doing so by saying that they
are trying to be rigorous and that they are trying not to believe things that are false. But that is,
indeed, a mere rationalization. Both the obsessive-compulsive and the philosopher are impotent,
being too fearful to engage the world. And to the extent that they have potency, it lies in their
ability to manipulate linguistic or otherwise symbolic proxies of reality. So instead of dealing
with the world on its own terms---instead of dealing with the world in the world—they try to
internalize as much of the world as possible into the sphere of their own thought, where they are
dominant.  And  they  are  dominant  there  both  because  they  are  more  inclined  towards  than
towards action and also, more importantly, because it is their own mind where, of course, they
ipso facto have total power.
    Or at least the illusion thereof—more on this in a moment.
      Then there is the fact that both the philosopher and the obsessive-compulsive are unduly
preoccupied  with  logical  niceties  and  fine  points  of  argumentation.  This  is  at  least  partly  a
derivative of their sense of impotence: when one has no material of one’s own to work on—
which is the case whenever one is too afraid of the world to venture out into it and get material---
one  preoccupies  oneself  with  secondary  material—with  matters  of  grammar,  logic,
argumentation and the like.
      Plus,  by  conditionalizing  the  acceptance  of  hypotheses  on  subtleties  of  proof  and
argumentation, they are able to rationalize continuing to remain in their own heads; they are able
to  rationalize  commandeering  debates,  and  holing  them  up  within  their  own  thought-scape,
pending the resolution of niceties of logic and dialectical protocol which, of their very nature, are
incapable of being definitely resolved.
      There  is  another,  somewhat  less  invidious  dimension  to  the  just-discussed  similarity
between  the  philosopher  and  the  obsessive-compulsive.  Neither  the  philosopher  nor  the
obsessive-compulsive has any real doubts, even of a purely intellectual variety, as to the contents
of the external world. In any case, neither has any more such doubts than the next person. But
both are indeed more doubtful than the next person. They are more doubtful, and with good
reason, as to the contents of the internal world. With the both the philosopher and the obsessive-
compulsive, their supposed doubts about the external world are displaced, projected doubts about
the internal world. They are both people whose minds are veritable caldrons of primal desires but
who, being moral, do their best to suppress and repress their basal urges. The result is that, while
their  understanding  of  the  external  world  is  in  fact  perfectly  good,  notwithstanding  their
asseverations to their contrary, they are quite in the dark as to the internal world, believing or
half-believing  themselves  to  be  logic-driven,  Cartesian  ratiocinators,  when,  in  fact,  their
Cartesianism is only a veneer, which holds only of the thinnest and most uppermost mental
stratum of theirs, the rest of their respective psyches being Nietzschean cesspools.
      And both of these types of people fancy themselves ‘rational actors’—people who have
gotten the better of the base side of themselves---when in reality they are anything but rational
actors,  the  truth  being  that  they  are  driven  by  rage,  narcissism  and  fear.  Indeed,  their
intellectualism is a reaction to, and attempt to cloak their own darkly retrograde psychological
condition.      And their insistence on the rationality of man is merely an idealization—a projection of a
self-image  that  they  half-have  and  are  100%  trying  to  have.  And  the  Lockean/Cartesian
insistence on the non-existence of the unconscious—on the brazenly spurious idea that mind
extends  only  as  far  consciousness---is  merely  another  tendentious  rationalization,  and  a
decidedly  more  sterile  and  superficial  one  than  their  false,  but  not  entirely  misconceived
insistence on the primacy within people of rationality.
      There is a second similarity between the obsessive-compulsive and the philosopher. They
are both pathologically concerned with ethics—with questions of right and wrong and good vs.
evil.  In  both  cases,  this  is  because  they  are  crypto-criminals—people  whose  fundamental
orientation  with  respect  to  others  is  anti-social—this  being  reflected  in  their  solipsism  and
skepticism---but who also have strong, nay draconian, superegos and thus do everything in their
power to suppress their criminal side. And to this end they enlist their pre-existing penchant for
dwelling on technicalities of logic; which, when transferred from the sphere of knowledge-theory
into the domain of ethics, results in their dwelling on technicalities of a legalistic nature. And this
is why many an obsessive-compulsive, and also many a philosopher, goes into law.
      Philosophers tend to be extremely sensitive to, and intelligent about, niceties of language.
This  is  obviously  true  of  some  obsessive-compulsives,  but  it  is  unknown  whether  it  is
categorically true of them. But supposing that it is, this, I think, is a derivative of their fear-based
retreat from the external world, which is the realm of action, into the internal world, which is the
realm of thought. After all, sensitivity to linguistic niceties being cut from much the same cloth
as sensitivity to logical niceties, and the latter being, as we have already seen, a by-product of the
philosopher’s and the obsessive-compulsives replacing of reality with cognitive proxies thereof.
    One last point: I have noticed that a hugely disproportionate number of philosophers,
including myself, are professional level musicians. Around 40% of philosophy professors fall
into this category. I don’t know whether the same is true of the class of obsessive compulsives—
it presumably isn’t. But even though there is not, in all likelihood, a categorical connection
between OCD and musicality, there may be a sub-categorical connection, as in, there may be a
relatively comprehensive sub-set of the class of obsessive-compulsives whose members are also
musical  and  whose  being  musical  is  connected  to  their  OCD.  In  any  case,  it  seems  that
musicianship and philosophical ability are related in that both involve decrypting and then re-
encrypting  information;  they  are  both  about  organizing  information—and  doing  so  with
obsessional concern for the logical interrelations holding among bits of information organized,
and a proportionate disregard for the correspondence of that information to external reality.                              How to Get Rid of OCD

metadata.source: OCD, Bureaucracy and Psychopathy Volume 1

positions.text:
Here is my answer:
      There is only one way to 'get rid' of OCD, and that is to find a calling in life in which
one's obsessive compulsive tendencies are an asset, as opposed to a liability. OCD is not so much
an  illness  as  it  is  a  distinctive  way  of  processing  emotional  information.  In  the  wrong
environment, that psychological architecture can hold one back; in right one, it can push one
forward.  Many  writers,  composers,  mathematicians,  lawyers,  philosophers  and  creative  and
analytical people of many stripes (e.g. Beethoven, Einstein, Dr. Johnson) have OCD; such people
have found callings in which their OCD works for them. So it's not about eliminating it; it's
about putting it to one's advantage. I myself have done that in a very big way. I am being OCD
right now, in writing such a thorough answer, but in being so thorough in this context, I am
putting to my obsessive-compulsive tendencies to non-pathological use, instead of letting them
express themselves as OCD. And that is point: one doesn't get rid of mental illnesses by getting
rid of them; one chooses to work in areas in which those mental illnesses are functional and,
therefore, not mental illnesses        What are Some Characteristics of OCD in Children?

metadata.source: OCD, Bureaucracy and Psychopathy Volume 1

      Here  is  my  answer:  First  of  all,  all  children  are  OCD.  In  fact,  OCD  is  actually  a

extreme than others children---more prone to bouts of depressive withdrawl. But I stress that

and intellect. OCD is given rise to by emotional vulnerability, which in turn is given rise to by
