positions.text:
A philosopher is somebody who thinks but does not act. He is someone who withdraws
into his own mind. Philosophical skepticism is simply an excuse for the philosopher to remain
holed up in his own mind.
            Skepticism is the view that we cannot know to what extent, if any, our sense-
perceptions are accurate.
          The skeptic’s position collapses into the absurdity it is no less rational to believe
that our sense-perceptions are uncaused and therefore inexplicable than it is to believe that they
are caused and therefore explicable.
          So the fact that people are drawn to skepticism cannot be understood in terms its
merits, given that it has none, and is to be understood in terms of its emotional appeal.      And its
emotional appeal is that it allows people to crawl into a safe space inside their heads. It allows
them to deny the reality of threats with which the external world is teeming.
            A  consequence  is  that  the  skeptic  doesn’t  have  to  act.  If  there  is  no  external
reality, and there are no external threats, then it isn’t necessary to do anything. If all there is to
life is what is inside the impregnable fortress of one’s own consciousness, then there is nothing
to do: all there is to do is watch.
            But there is a big problem with skeptic’s retreat from reality into thought. Thought
inevitably refers one back to the external world. And the only way to prevent it from doing as
much  as  to  stop  engaging  in  it.  So  if  the  skeptic  is  to  remain  in  his  safe  haven  of  pure
consciousness, he must keep retreating: it is not enough for him to hole himself up inside his
consciousness. For if he is holed up there but he lets himself think then he will start chipping
away  at  the  walls  of  his  self-isolation.  So  he  must  put  himself  within  a  fortress  inside  that
fortress, and so on. Which means that he has to paralyze his own thought-processes, since, when
left to follow their own course, they push him back into the external world.
            Notice that skeptic’s main weapon—his main way of preserving his aloofness from
reality—is doubt. And this is the main weapon of the obsessive-compulsive. Whenever it comes
time to act, the obsessive compulsive is afflicted by action-crippling doubt.
    He consciously experiences this doubt as unpleasant. But that is neither here nor there,
since it has no effect on the effect of that doubt, which is to keep the obsessive-compulsive inside
his safe haven of thought—of thought which doesn’t engage the world and eventually, for that
very reason, collapses in on itself.
      And the reason the obsessive-compulsive obsesses is that he has nothing else to do. He
has  to  obsess  since  he  has  prevented  himself  from  acting.  He  is  holed  up  inside  his
consciousness, and the only objects on which he can act are ideas; and the only way left to him to
act on them is to pour his consciousness on them—after all, consciousness, along with this ideas,
are all he has left. And what is to obsess except to stream one’s consciousness onto some idea?
          As for his compulsions: these are his mind’s way of pushing him back out into the
world. The sane part of the obsessive-compulsive’s mind wants him to rejoin the living and thus
to get out of his own head.  The only way to do that is to find a way to get his obsessiveness to
be  converted  into  action.  After  all,  obsessiveness  is  all  he  has.  And  compulsiveness  is
obsessiveness about behavior.
          So  the  obsessive-compulsive’s  compulsiveness  represents  the  sane  side  of  him.True—the compulsions themselves are insane. But they are insane only because, as an insane
person, he needs an insane pretext to act sanely. The sane part of the obsessive-compulsive—the
part of him that wants him to act---is like a parent who is trying to feed a toddler who refuses to
eat, and the insane part of the obsessive-compulsive—the part of him that doesn’t want to act---is
like the toddler. And the sane part of the obsessive-compulsive is, as it were, pretending that the
spoon in his hand is an airplane and that the peas on the spoon are little passengers;  and, in this
way, by speaking the toddler’s language—his delusion-embodying language---the parent gets the
toddler to do something good for himself.
          So there it is. OCD is about refusal to act. It is about defensively holing oneself up
inside  consciousness.  And  getting  the  obsessive-compulsive  to  act---and  thus  to  stop  being
obsessive-compulsive—is like a taking a cat out of a tree. It takes a lot of time and a lot of
sensitivity. And the way to do is by exerting a firm and steady, but gentle pressure.
      There is good news, thought. This cat in a tree that the obsessive-compulsive is---is sane.
He knows that he is on a tree; he knows, deep down, that he has to get out of the tree. But he’s a
bit gun-shy.
          The obsessive-compulsive cat in a tree is not like the schizophrenic cat in a tree.
The latter doesn’t know that he is in a tree or that he is a cat. And getting him to come down
from three is dramatically more difficult, since, precisely because he doesn’t grasp reality as it is,
there is no language in common between him and the person who is trying to talk him down.
            But obsessive-compulsive sees things as they are. His problem is not deludedness;
it is fearfulness. And since he does see reality as it is, one can use reason on him, as long as one
does so gently, so as to not intensify his fears.          The Obsessive-compulsive Must Accept his Own

metadata.source: OCD, Bureaucracy and Psychopathy Volume 1

positions.text:
An essential part of the treatment of the obsessive-compulsive is getting him to accept his
own sexuality, which invariably has an extremely sadistic and/or masochistic character. If he
does not accept his sexuality, there will be a continuation of the alienatedness of thought from
feeling that is responsible for OCD. And he does accepts his sexuality, this will effectuate an
OCD-eliminate reconciliation of thought and feeling.
      Let me put these points into context.
    All  mental  illness  involves  some  kind  of  withdrawal  from  reality.  But  there  are  two
different ways of withdrawing from reality and thus two different kind of mental illness. There
are mental illnesses that effectuate this withdrawal by destroying the coherence of one’s ideation:
these are known as psychoses.
      And there are mental illnesses that effectuate this withdrawal not by diminishing the
coherence of one’s thinking but by paralyzing one’s will. These are known as neuroses. Neuroses
do not destroy the coherence of one’s thought.
    Or  rather---they  do  not  the  coherence  of  one’s  intellection,  that  is,  of  one’s  mental
activity so far as it is concerned with the discerning of logical relations. But there is a respect in
which  they  diminish  the  coherence  of  one’s  mentation:  they  estrange  intellection  from  its
ordinary emotional concomitants. So the neurotic can think, and he can also feel, but his thoughts
are alienated from his feelings, the result being that his will is weakened. And the reason for this
weakening of will is that, as philosophers have long known, action is not solely about thought,
nor  solely  about  feeling,  but  about  the  two  operating  jointly  and  symbiotically;  and  in  the
neurotic, this symbiosis has been disrupted.
    In the psychotic, this symbiosis has not been disrupted; but the psychotic’s intellection is
incoherent, so that, even though his will is intact, it is led on so many fool’s errands by his
compromised powers of reasoning.
      The obsessive-compulsive’s intellection is coherent. Nay, hyper-coherent. Indeed, it is
made hyper-coherent by the fact that it is estranged from the usual emotional concomitants of
thought and can thus pursue its own course without being weighed down by the usual emotional
resistances. But the obsessive-compulsive’s will is as atrophied as his intellect is hypertrophied--
-and  for  the  very  same  reason,  this  being  the  just-mentioned  alienatedness  of  thought  from
feeling.  And  the  restoration  of  the  obsessive-compulsive’s  personality  consists  entirely  in
restoring this thought-feeling balance.
      And this is done by two doing two things. First, by finding a profession for the obsessive-
compulsive that is congenial to him and that enables him to flourish without having to perform
the impossible the impossible feat of changing his own psychological architecture.
      Second, by reconciling him to his own sexuality, which, precisely because his affect, and
therewith his sexuality, is so alienated from, and thus not tempered by, his judgement, assumes
particularly  extreme  forms  and,  consequently,  sadistic  or  masochistic  forms.  And  the
reintegration of his sexuality is accomplished by reconciling him to urges and ceasing to regard
them as an abomination.
      And the truth is that his sexuality is no more abominable than anyone else’s; it onlyseems that way because, in him, the sadistic and masochistic undercurrents of human sexuality
are exposed, owing to the fact that his sexuality has not been absorbed into the usual narratives
and rationalizations. And the reason for this non-absorption is, of course, that the thought-feeling
balance responsible for these narratives and rationalizations has been disrupted.
    Be  it  noted  that  the  obsessive-compulsive,  having  as  he  does  a  strong  and  intact
conscience, never actually carries out acts that are actually, as opposed to symbolically sadistic
or masochistic; so there is no danger in reconciling him to his own sadism and/or masochism.                              Institutional Psychopathy

metadata.source: OCD, Bureaucracy and Psychopathy Volume 1

positions.text:
A psychopath is someone who has no conscience. There is a phenomenon known as
'institutional psychopathization', this being the process whereby an entire institution becomes
psychopathic, in the sense that the institution as a whole is without principle and also in the sense
that the people composing it are without principle. Once an institution becomes just slightly
psychopathized,  it  quickly  succumbs  to  wholesale  psychopathization.  And  a  number  of
institutions and professions have become, or are in the process of becoming, psychopathic. This
main reason for this is that, owing to technological advances, many institutions are not only
obsolete and therefore serve no positive function, but actually thwart progress and prevent people
doing  what  those  same  institutions  are  supposed  to  do.  As  a  result,  those  institutions  are
fraudulent, and therefore psychopathic, and the same is therefore true of the people composing
them,  who  have  no  choice  but  to  being  stone-psychopaths  themselves  if  they  want  to  be
employed by those institutions. Many university-departments and academic disciplines are not
completely and utterly psychopathic. This is glaringly where the humanities and social sciences
are concerned. I believe that some branches of psychotherapy have become psychopathized, this
being why psychiatrists are proverbially likely to make their patients’ problems even worse.
Because psychopathy is now being spread by psychopathic institutions and professions, it is
dramatically more widespread than it used to be. Consequently, the old saw that only 1%-3% of
the  population  consists  of  psychopaths  is  now  pathetically  outdated  and  also  presupposes  a
narrow as well as antiquated conception of what a psychopath is. Most psychopaths in our time
are not slick con-men but are rather bureaucrats, who discharge their psychopathy not by running
Madoff-style swindles but by misdeploying educational and professional resources. I am hosting
an ‘Ask me anything’ session on psychopathy, where I will answer any questions that you have
on psychopathy. Please go to www.amafeed.com and type ‘psychopathy’ in the search bar. I’m
the first result that pops up.                      The Psychology of the Bureaucrat

metadata.source: OCD, Bureaucracy and Psychopathy Volume 1

positions.text:
People are internally conflicted only when they are autonomous—only, in other words,
when they decide for themselves how to act and what to think. To be internally conflicted is to
have two incompatible decision-making protocols. And if one has no such protocols—in other
words,  if  one  does  not  decide  for  oneself  what  to  think  or  do—then  one  cannot  have  two
incompatible such protocols and thus cannot be internally conflicted.
    A bureaucrat is somebody who has surrendered all of his decision-making responsibilities
to the bureaucracy that employs him. So long as a given person’s decision-making protocol is
capable of being in conflict with the bureaucracy that hosts him, that person is not a bureaucrat.
And  so  long  as  the  condition  obtains,  he  can  be  internally  conflicted,  since,  under  that
circumstance, he has, or at least can have, two decision-making protocols, one corresponding to
the bureaucracy in question and one that is his own.
    It  is  only  when  those  two  protocols  merge,  and  it  is  therefore  only  when  his  own
decision-protocol is absorbed into and therefore eliminated in favor, the institution’s decision-
protocol, that the person in question becomes a bureaucrat.
    One is either completely a bureaucrat or completely not a bureaucrat. If somebody is only
half a bureaucrat, then he can to some extent make decisions for himself and therefore is not a
bureaucrat at all. What can happen is that a person may be weak and, without crossing the line,
so to speak, that separates non-bureaucrats from bureaucrats, may in contexts like a bureaucrat
and  may,  by  virtue  of  being  obviously  weak,  be  tolerated  by  bureaucrats.  But  even  this
arrangement is unstable and will work only if the non-bureaucrat in question is an utter ‘bump on
a log.’
      Given that bureaucrats are not internally conflicted, they are not neurotic or otherwise
psychodynamic.  They  can  be—and  indeed  categorically  are---broken,  and  the  breaks  within
them may be in located along the psychical fault lines that, in a psychodynamic person, would be
the meeting-points of two opposed emotional forces. In someone who is psychodynamic, these
fault lines corresponding to mind-internal tensions; but in the bureaucrat, they correspond to
veritable schisms---to rents or cracks. In a psychodynamic person, these fault-lines are places
where  there  is  too  much  psychical  activity—where  there  is  a  plurality  of  mutually  opposed
psychical forces; and because these fault-lines are still psychical hot-spots in the psychodynamic
person---because intra-psychical communications, as it were, pass through them and along them
—their existence is not an existential threat to the integratedness of the psyche in question.
    But in the bureaucrat, these fault-lines are indeed schisms---they are crevasse-like gaps or
canyons---which are to no degree populated by psychical forces, and their existence therefore
does pose an existential threat to the psyche in question.
      And when a bureaucrat is made consciously aware of these fault-lines within his own
psyche, he responds, not with psychological resistance, but with abject fear. Bureaucrats do not
have resistance-reactions. Resistances are about internal conflict: one part of the person’s mind
accepts  what  he  is  being  told  about  himself,  but  another  opposed  part  refuses  to  accept  it.
Bureaucrats  are  not  internally  conflicted  and  therefore  do  not  have  resistance-reactions.  A
bureaucrat  will hiss and seethe like a wounded viper when a spot-light is shined on the cracks in
his psyche: but this hissing and seething is not about resistance: it is about pure, non-conflicted,
utter hatred of the person who is shining that light.      The  bureaucrat’s  psychological  architecture  is  not  identical  with,  and  is  not  easily
understood in terms of, that of a psychodynamic person . The bureaucrat’s architecture is to be
understood in terms of that of the psychopath. The psychopath, like the bureaucrat, is incapable
of internal conflict, since internal conflicts are mediated by conscience, and the psychopath has
no conscience. The difference between the bureaucrat and the psychopath is that because the
bureaucrat was once a person---because he was a person before his own decision-protocol was
replaced by that of the corresponding institution---his psychological architecture, though non-
psychodynamic  and  therefore  effectively  dead,  has  a  formal  similarity  to  that  of  the
psychodynamic person: the bureaucrat’s psyche is the skeleton of a once existent but now dead
and gone psychodynamic psyche. By contrast, the psychopath’s psyche does not even have a
skeletal or formal similarity to that of a normal person.
      There is no one way that bureaucrats look or sound. But they often have a distinctively
nasal sound---a distinctly monotonic nasal sound. It sounds like this
