   Paragraph 15: Final thoughts (Type: Conclusion, Topic: Implications of the solution)

8. SENTENCE INDEX:
   1. For any possible universe U, there is some distance D such that nothing in U can move at all without covering a distance of at least D. (Declarative, Complexity: 10, Key: Mills' solution)
   2. Explanation: Zeno has established that if any given motion presupposes another motion, then nothing can even begin to leave the place that it is occupying at a given time. (Declarative, Complexity: 15, Key: Zeno's paradox)
   3. Therefore, (i) must be rejected. (Declarative, Complexity: 3, Key: Rejection of premise)
   4. Assuming that (i) is false, it is not possible for there to no minimum, short of nullity, to the amount by which a given object moves. (Declarative, Complexity: 12, Key: Assumption of minimum distance)
   5. Therefore, in any world where motion is possible, there is some finite distance such that anything that moves in that world moves by at least that distance. (Declarative, Complexity: 13, Key: Finite distance for motion)
   6. Problems with Mills' solution If Mills is right, then any given universe U consists of discrete cells such that, for any occupant x of U, x moves by vanishing from one cell and then appearing in another. (Declarative, Complexity: 18, Key: Critique of Mills' solution)
   7. x cannot move from one cell to the next, since there would in that case not be a minimum distance that x had to travel in order to travel at all. (Declarative, Complexity: 15, Key: Minimum distance problem)
   8. A consequence of Mills' position is that nothing moves. (Declarative, Complexity: 6, Key: Consequence of Mills' solution)
   9. The assertion that (i) x spontaneously appears in one cell and spontaneously reappears in an adjacent cell is equivalent with the assertion that (ii) x spontaneously ceases to exist and an x-like object that is not identical with x spontaneously appears in a cell adjacent to that occupied by x. (Declarative, Complexity: 25, Key: Equivalence of assertions)
   10. If you are obliterated and some person who is exactly like you appears a moment later right next to where you were standing, that person isn't you. (Declarative, Complexity: 18, Key: Identity problem)
   11. Let you2 be that person. (Imperative, Complexity: 3, Key: Definition of you2)
   12. Given that you2 came into existence spontaneously, there are no lines of transmission linking him to the past and thus no lines of transmission linking you2 to you. (Declarative, Complexity: 15, Key: Lack of transmission)
   13. Therefore, you2 is not you; and youn (n>2) isn't identical with either you or with youi (1=i<n). (Declarative, Complexity: 12, Key: Non-identity)
   14. If n is a sufficiently high number and there is a sufficiently small delay between youi's disappearance and youi+1's appearance, it will certainly appear that you had moved from one place to another. (Declarative, Complexity: 18, Key: Appearance of motion)
   15. But that is obviously irrelevant. (Declarative, Complexity: 5, Key: Irrelevance of appearance)
   16. In a Mills-universe, nothing has a past and, therefore, nothing has a future. (Declarative, Complexity: 10, Key: Lack of past and future)
   17. Everything that exists, exists for an instant and then vanishes. (Declarative, Complexity: 8, Key: Instantaneous existence)
   18. There is no possibility of mutual causal influence among the occupants of a Mills-universe. (Declarative, Complexity: 10, Key: Lack of causality)
   19. Such a universe is no universe at all, since any given occupant of such a universe is in the very condition that it would be in if it were in a universe all by itself. (Declarative, Complexity: 18, Key: Non-universe)
   20. Therefore, a Mills-universe is a non-universe. (Declarative, Complexity: 5, Key: Conclusion on Mills-universe)
   21. Also, relative spatial and temporal position are to be understood in causal terms. (Declarative, Complexity: 8, Key: Causal terms for position)
   22. Events are simultaneous if neither can affect the other; otherwise non-simultaneous. (Declarative, Complexity: 10, Key: Simultaneity)
   23. Since there is no causation in a given Mill-universe U, no U-occupant x can have either a spatial or a temporal position relative to any other U-occupant y. (Declarative, Complexity: 15, Key: Lack of relative position)
   24. Thus, a Mills-universe is one in which there are neither places nor times. (Declarative, Complexity: 10, Key: Lack of places and times)
   25. In conclusion, Mills has given us a heap of absurdities, not a solution. (Declarative, Complexity: 8, Key: Conclusion on Mills' solution)

9. ENTITY RELATIONSHIPS:
   - Eugene Mills -> Zeno's paradox (proposes solution)
   - Zeno -> Motion (questions nature of)
   - Bertrand Russell -> Zeno's paradox (proposes solution)
   - Mao Tse Tung -> Motion (quoted on difficulty of first step)
   - x -> Universe U (occupant of)
   - you -> you2 (not identical)
   - youi -> youn (not identical)
   - x1 -> x2 (relative position)

10. SEMANTIC ANALYSIS:
   Semantic fields:
   - Motion: move, distance, travel, vanish, appear
   - Space: universe, cell, position, place
   - Time: instant, past, future, delay
   - Mathematics: infinite, series, sum, finite
   Word families:
   - Move: move, motion, moves
   - Position: position, positions
   - Universe: universe, universes

positions.title: Heed My Wisdom

positions.text: Universities sell degrees, not instruction.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: People pay universities for degrees and for nothing besides (with minor qualifications).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If a university stopped issuing degrees but improved instruction and lowered tuition, its enrollment would plummet (possibly to zero).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: A degree functions like a union card—it's a prerequisite for professional entry regardless of actual competence.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: People with degrees have options; people without degrees don't.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: People without degrees are stuck with blue-collar work, hustling, or long-shot entrepreneurship.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: The entrepreneurial success rate is approximately 1%, and most of that 1% have college degrees and degree-based professional experience.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Retention rates and graduation rates track each other but are not identical.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Retention rates are necessarily at least as high as graduation rates.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities have cogent financial reasons to want high retention rates (continued business).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities have less strong reasons to want high graduation rates (students who never graduate pay more over time).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities with extremely low retention and graduation rates almost always stay in business.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: There is no documented case of a university failing due to low retention rates.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: What matters financially is enrollment rates, not retention rates.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Low retention rates do not necessarily indicate institutional failure—they may indicate the institution serves students on the cusp of being able to complete degrees.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Soon-to-be dropouts are profitable: they pay for time they're not there and make minimal demands on university resources.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities with low retention rates scale down services and set prices accordingly.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If a university made systemic changes to improve retention, it would lose most of its existing customer base.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Why Universities Cannot/Will Not Improve

positions.text: Universities would lose far more customers than they would gain by making courses financially more worth doing.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Why Universities Cannot/Will Not Improve

positions.text: Universities are extremely reluctant to change.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Why Universities Cannot/Will Not Improve

positions.text: Universities don't have to change.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Why Universities Cannot/Will Not Improve

positions.text: Universities would alienate both customers and employees if they changed—especially if changes involved making them better at teaching or producing employable graduates.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Why Universities Cannot/Will Not Improve

positions.text: Universities cannot significantly improve or alter the quality of their educational services without imperiling their very existences.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Why Universities Cannot/Will Not Improve

positions.text: Universities couldn't improve the quality of their services even if they wanted to.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Why Universities Cannot/Will Not Improve

positions.text: Apart from a few religions, no institutions are as unwilling to change and resistant to being tinkered with as universities.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Why Universities Cannot/Will Not Improve

positions.text: OMCs sell products and services that actually have to be good; there's no other reason to buy them.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On the University Business Model vs. Open Market Companies (OMCs)

positions.text: Universities sell prestige backed by degrees, not quality instruction.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On the University Business Model vs. Open Market Companies (OMCs)

positions.text: A degree from Harvard obtained entirely through cheating (without getting caught) is still enormously powerful social capital.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On the University Business Model vs. Open Market Companies (OMCs)

positions.text: OMCs can turn on a dime—replace labor forces, change products, fire CEOs. Universities cannot.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On the University Business Model vs. Open Market Companies (OMCs)

positions.text: OMCs must be flexible because they must meet changing consumer demands; universities need not be.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On the University Business Model vs. Open Market Companies (OMCs)

positions.text: When averaged over time and subjects, instruction at universities must rise above a certain threshold—but only just above it.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Instruction Quality and Student Demand

positions.text: A university won't come to grief if it always provides execrable instruction in some subjects or sometimes provides execrable instruction in all subjects.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Instruction Quality and Student Demand

positions.text: Most of a university's business depends on providing instruction that is on average lackluster.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Instruction Quality and Student Demand

positions.text: Most students are just buying a degree; only a few are really working for it.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Instruction Quality and Student Demand

positions.text: The degree has prestige because of the efforts of those few.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Instruction Quality and Student Demand

positions.text: The ideal situation for a university is when its degrees have prestige and as few people as possible are responsible for giving them that prestige.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Instruction Quality and Student Demand

positions.text: Setting aside ~15% of students who major in engineering, nursing, etc., students don't want particularly high-quality instruction.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Instruction Quality and Student Demand

positions.text: High-quality instruction is demanding; since students are paying for degrees, they want minimal hoops.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Instruction Quality and Student Demand

positions.text: Students do want to believe their degrees have meaning—they're willing to jump through some hoops.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Instruction Quality and Student Demand

positions.text: If universities made instruction more demanding, they would attract more savants, truth-seekers, and aspiring doctors/engineers—but for each such student gained, they'd lose ten existing customers.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century
positions.section: On Instruction Quality and Student Demand

positions.text: People want to be employed, but they don't necessarily want to be employable (i.e., useful).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If someone is employed because they're useful, they're employed only as long as they're useful—job security is contingent.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: A life of usefulness is not easy (e.g., 60-year-old nurses cleaning infected wounds).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: What most college students want is to be relatively useless and employed.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If someone useless has stable employment, their employment isn't contingent on being useful.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Nobody expects redundant bureaucrats and middle managers to be useful—that's not why they're employed.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Jobs serving well-defined practical functions are easily lost; jobs with ill-defined functions are secure.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If one is employed only because useful, earnings wax and wane with degree of usefulness.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: People who make their living by being useful tend not to have inflated salaries.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: People with ill-defined functions may have enormously inflated salaries.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Most college graduates end up as well-paid pencil-pushers in corporations or government agencies—well-paid for doing little more than showing up and not making waves.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Political science majors know their major doesn't make them useful—that's part of why they do it.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: A "successful" political science major tends to end up with a well-paying job without well-defined functions—therefore relatively secure.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If colleges made students more "employable" by making them more "useful," they would alienate most students.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: In a micro-economy (100 people), everyone must be useful and pull their weight.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: In economies of scale with enormous division of labor, only a small percentage of the population actually has to produce.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: The more advanced an economy, the greater the percentage of people who can be useless.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: In the U.S. in 2019, most jobs are useless—because they can be, since the productive minority is so productive.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Until the useful percentage drops too close to zero, it is not irrational for people to do easy majors and bide time until landing a well-paid sinecure.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: The "tragedy" of high dropout rates is not really a tragedy.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: When people drop out, it is usually for good reasons.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: It isn't successful engineering or nursing majors who drop out—it's people barely getting by in political science or criminal justice.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: When people drop out, it's usually because they stand to lose more by staying than by dropping out—and they're usually right.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Dropping out is often part of a hedging strategy—people who drop out can usually go back.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: People who drop out often become plumbers, electricians, police officers, etc., and may find these careers more fulfilling and equally lucrative as the white-collar alternatives.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Dropping out is not a tragedy for the institution either—they adjust services and prices accordingly.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Knowledge Management (KM) explains how institutions must be organized to maximize the likelihood of necessary knowledge exchanges.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: KM can help when poor internal communications prevent an institution from accomplishing what it wants.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: KM cannot help universities because there isn't much of an issue, and insofar as there is one, poor internal communications aren't responsible.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Unlike OMCs, universities actually depend on poor internal communications.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: University departments are highly isolated and fiercely independent—cooperation happens rarely, at specific junctures, in circumscribed ways, after lengthy negotiations.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: An OMC would fail if its departments were comparably isolated.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: OMCs are about providing optimal products/services; universities are about maintaining prestige.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Prestige is harder to maintain when information flows freely than when sequestered.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If departments cooperated whenever students would benefit, professors would face scrutiny from students not under their thumb—they'd have to step up their game considerably.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: The kind of professor who would thrive under cross-departmental scrutiny is high-energy and demanding—most students prefer professors of the second kind (comfortable in their niche, demanding little).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities thrive on low communication between departments—on a certain lack of knowledge management.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Low retention/graduation rates at universities do not represent a business opportunity for anyone.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Low retention/graduation rates do not represent an opportunity to do real good—making universities better would lower rates further.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Low retention/graduation rates do not represent an opportunity to apply KM principles—universities are positively dependent on poor internal communications.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: The way to improve education is not to try to "fix" existing universities—they are happy as they are and both unwilling and unable to be fixed.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: The idea that one could march into a university and fix it (and be paid to do so) is the very definition of absurdity.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If one wants to improve education and make money doing so, there is currently an enormous opportunity—but it involves creating something new, not fixing what exists.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO is a hypothetical online university where students pay for results, not services.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: A degree from DMO must be proof of competence—DMO sells competence, not prestige.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO is not meant to replace brick-and-mortar institutions but to provide maximally efficient instruction in every area that can be taught digitally.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO will not teach piloting, surgery, plumbing, or other skills requiring physical presence.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO will not provide accreditations in areas (like history or religious studies) where a degree cannot constitute proof of competence.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: What DMO offers in instruction will covary in real time with what the market needs people to know.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO will offer instruction in a dramatically wider range of subjects than any brick-and-mortar institution.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO will offer many different levels of accreditation (beyond just bachelor's, master's, doctoral).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Nobody who graduates from DMO will be useless—they will already be employable.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO's objective is to produce competent people, not to exclude incompetent people and take undeserved credit for graduates' merits.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO will not exclude people—unintelligent people seeking education are like sick people going to a hospital: they're there to improve.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Knowledge management is possible when an organization has definite objectives and difficult when objectives are vague.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: When an organization sells results, objectives are well-defined—positive consequences for KM.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: When an organization sells services, objectives tend to be vaguely defined—negative consequences for KM.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: When an organization sells results, prices tend to drop; when it sells services, prices tend to rise.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: When results are sold, there's no money in services and services must be streamlined.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: When services are sold, there's no money in results and services must be inflated.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Results-providers compete on efficiency; prices bid down. Service-providers compete on inefficiency; prices bid up.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Service organizations depend on poor KM—they make money by multiplying and drawing out services.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Management in service organizations profits from inefficiencies that good communication eliminates.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Internal communications tend to be bad when employment is guaranteed and good when it isn't.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Internal communications tend to be good when salaries are performance-dependent and bad when they aren't.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: It's hard to know who's pulling their weight unless clients hire employees directly.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: When clients cannot hire talent directly, projects go to the wrong people; talent is misdeployed and underdeployed.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Organization-internal communications are worthless unless based on solid information about how to engage the market.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: The best way for an organization to have such information is for employees to offer services directly to clients.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: When a business is legitimate, it does not inflate costs.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Legitimate businesses make more money by keeping quality high and costs low.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Businesses are forced to pad and gouge when they don't make money by providing what they say they provide.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: A business that thrives on inefficiency cannot give customers what they're paying for all at once—legitimate services must be dragged out and interspersed with extraneous ones.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Such businesses are concerned with managing expectations, not increasing efficiency.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If such a business is large, most employees must partially accept the company's propaganda—otherwise recruitment and motivation suffer.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Such companies' internal communications tend to be inefficient because it isn't clear what the company does.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: When education can be digitized, it must be digitized.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Digitization saves time and money and allows purchasing custom education from the best instructors at the lowest price.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If a university chooses not to digitize services that can be digitized, it's because it doesn't primarily make money by providing instruction.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: At DMO, instructors do not issue grades—there are no grades, only accreditations.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Accreditations are attained by taking examinations.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Examinations are graded anonymously (by computer whenever possible) and designed/administered by independent third parties.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Questions on examinations have determinate answers—little or no expert disagreement on correct answers.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Instructors have access to past exams but not forthcoming ones.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: There are many subjects (history, religious studies) that DMO simply will not teach.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Instructors at DMO are instructors, not gatekeepers—it doesn't matter if the instructor likes you or what they think of your work.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: The instructor's one job is to get you to pass the accreditation exam.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO's primary solution for humanities-inclined students is to offer "lite" science degrees: lite-physics, lite-math, math for computer scientists, lite biology.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Many people in the humanities (especially philosophy) are actually highly intelligent but find details annoying.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Physics and all sciences are largely about grinding out solutions using thorny mathematics—some "big ideas" but mostly grinding out facts/theorems.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Because humanities people are "big idea" people, they resent the labor involved in STEM.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: College majors being what they are, big-idea people flee from STEM (including economics) into political science, history, etc.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: These people, despite considerable intellectual gifts, end up completely useless—no matter how advanced in political science, you're not employable (except bureaucratically).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Someone whose college career consists of learning the big ideas of science plus enough applications to nail down understanding is extremely useful.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO will offer "general studies in the sciences" with variants (applied sciences, biological sciences, information sciences, health sciences).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Existing universities are discriminatory—they don't service all comers.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: OMCs (Nike, Amazon, Microsoft) don't "exclude" customers.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: The difference: OMCs sell products; universities sell prestige.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If universities really sold instruction (made money by making people leave more competent than they came), they'd want as many customers as possible.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities are selective because their product is weak—they're not that good at instructing people.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities are reasonably good at taking already-gifted people and pointing them in the right direction.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO has the first business model (smart-making pill for everyone); other universities have the second (vet customers because product is weak).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: An A in a given class at a given university tends not to mean anything.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Even when an A means something, its exact meaning is deeply unclear—it means different things at MIT vs. VCU.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Because individual classes have either no significance or no determinate significance, accreditations can only be awarded for hulkingly large pluralities of classes.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: This is a hedging strategy—Smith must have some merit if he passes 100 classes, even if many had no meaning.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: The less guarantee a class has merit, the larger the number of classes needed for a degree taken as proof of merit.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: This limits the number of degree levels a university can issue.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If individual courses had guaranteed and determinate meanings, passing even a single class would be definite proof of a definite kind of merit.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: At DMO, there's no such thing as a grade that isn't definitive proof of well-defined merit.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Therefore, a DMO student can prove high, definite merit by passing relatively few classes.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Therefore, DMO can credibly issue many different levels of degrees.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Any given course at DMO is itself a mini-degree.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Students can earn credible degrees from DMO in a fraction of the time and expense of conventional institutions.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Students have many options intermediate between no degree and spending 4-8 years accumulating debt for a degree of little significance.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO must always offer students the ability to "test out"—get accreditation just by passing the exam without class time.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: This is necessary: as long as test-out is possible, students only pay instructors for actual instruction.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: When testing out isn't possible, instructors tend to function as gatekeepers—there to open gates as they see fit, not to instruct.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If instructors are paid to be gatekeepers, there's no guarantee they're good instructors.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: At DMO, students hire instructors only if they believe the instructor can help them pass an exam the instructor doesn't grade.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Students will want to work with instructors who succeed at getting students to pass and avoid those who fail.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Students will pay more for instructors likely to get them to pass.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Students and instructors must negotiate prices directly.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: If students pay DMO and DMO supplies instructors, students can't choose competent instructors and avoid incompetent ones.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Incompetent instructors would not pay for incompetence; competent ones wouldn't be rewarded.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Payments must go straight to the instructor; instructor gives a percentage to DMO for platform, volume, credibility, materials.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: There is no permanent employment at DMO, let alone tenure—only contract work.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Instructor revenue is determined entirely by instructor competence.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: At conventional universities, money flows down (students → university → instructors)—result: expensive and bad education.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: At DMO, money flows up (students → instructors, who give ~20% to DMO for overhead).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: This makes DMO lean and functional.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Because students know where money goes, DMO cannot overcharge or mis-charge.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Since management works within that ~20%, bureaucracy is impossible.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Management has strong incentives to hire and retain instructors who can service large market sectors.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO must be accredited—students must receive recognized degrees.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Instruction must be largely (though not entirely) individualized.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: There must be clear, universally recognized performance benchmarks for students in all academic areas.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: There must be similar benchmarks for instructors.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Students hire instructors directly.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Instructor rates are determined by student and instructor, not by DMO.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Instructor statistics must always be available to prospective students.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Satisfaction of benchmarks is never decided by professors—no "grades" per se, only accreditation exams.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Questions on accreditation exams admit of clear, unambiguous, universally recognizable answers.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Instructors are minimally policed—can teach what and how they want.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Instructors are encouraged to take performance tests and publicly display scores.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Termination is reserved for: failure to take on available students at agreed rates, and gross misconduct.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: There must be a wider variety of disciplines and degrees than current institutions.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: The student's situation should not be binary (degree/no degree)—there should be a score corresponding to objective accomplishments.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Disciplines with no practical value should be jettisoned or modified to have practical value.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Disciplines that don't admit objective assessments should be jettisoned or modified to admit them.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universally applicable tests of intelligence and critical reasoning should be administered (free) to prove students are becoming better thinkers.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: DMO will have past copies of all tests, but DMO instructors won't create them—outside parties generate tests not revealed to instructors until after administration.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Student evaluations should be minimized or eliminated—instructor merit determined by student performance, not opinion/sentiment.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Students should be able to switch instructors easily to prevent personal conflicts from jamming education.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Curricula contents should be publicly accessible so students can start working before enrolling.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Hirings based exclusively on proven or probable success helping students pass exams—publications and letters of reference are irrelevant.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Mini-majors should be offered.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Existing universities are failing: most degrees have no objective significance and fail to provide definitive proof of merit.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities are expensive and slow.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities are discriminatory—they prevent the people who need their services from receiving them.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities turn away people who want to study (forgivable given limited resources).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities turn their own students away from courses of study that would benefit them (far less forgivable).
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities offer very few practical courses and load those with so many stumbling blocks that capable people are exiled into useless soft majors.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Universities wall out people who need instruction, opening gates only to people who can self-teach their way through.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: There is little hope of reforming universities—they don't want to be reformed and don't stand to gain by being reformed.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: This doesn't matter because there's no need to reform them—DMO can be created instead.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Once in existence, DMO will be the very embodiment of optimal knowledge management.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: The right way to provide better instruction is not to make universities better but to create a new results-based educational service whose revenue is tied to pedagogical efficacy.
positions.title: Economics of Higher Education in the 21st Century

positions.text: Losers hyper-value hierarchies that oppress them in order to prevent others from escaping
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Automation cannot itself be automated

positions.text: "Heterological" when applied to itself contains an undefined pronoun/free variable and is neither true nor false
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: The Liar Paradox: propositions, not words, bear truth values; "I am lying" is simply false when stated perspicuously
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Berry's Paradox dissolves by distinguishing canonical from non-canonical naming

positions.text: Wittgenstein's rule-following paradox collapses into the trivial point that erroneous hypotheses may fit restricted datasets

positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Sorites paradoxes are solved by replacing binary predicates with comparatives plus contextually-supplied benchmarks
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: A single grain of millet does make a sound—the "no sound" claim is like saying a stereo makes no sound because a deaf person can't hear it
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: To be at rest requires failing to move during a non-null interval; instantaneous position does not constitute rest
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: If irresistible forces exist, unmovable objects cannot, and vice versa (Asimov's solution)
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Unions prevent job creation, then claim credit for protecting the jobs that remain
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: People with modest responsibilities deliberately underperform to inflate their importance
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Professors withhold information to artificially inflate its perceived value
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Zeno's paradox dissolves because any finite distance can be represented as an infinite series sum
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Places are constituents, not occupants, of space-time and therefore don't themselves have places
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: The probability of a coin landing in any exact region is infinitesimally small but not zero
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: High self-awareness correlates with both well-being and neurotic distress because self-criticism enables adaptation but causes suffering
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Trying not to think of something initiates a cognitive process without letting it run its course
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Schizophrenics respond better to treatment in developing countries because restoring self-esteem is easier where expectations are lower
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Anti-trust laws hurt consumers when applied to firms with large market share due to superior competition
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Anti-trust laws help consumers when applied to firms that restrict competition
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Tenure eliminates economic incentives to do good work or hire threatening talent
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Vindictive leaders are less likely to be overthrown than benevolent ones
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Conceptual analyses can be informative because information-load depends on how something is said, not just what is said
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Fitch's Paradox is not a paradox but a proof that not all truths can be known
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: People at the bottom of hierarchies enforce those hierarchies against peers trying to rise
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Grassroots movements require leaders, but leaders need pre-existing power to gain followers—making true grassroots movements impossible
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Incompetence has survival value because hyper-competence provokes resentment
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Incompetent people get over-promoted (seen as controllable); hyper-competent people get under-promoted (seen as threatening)
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: School psychologists must blame children rather than institutions to keep their jobs
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Useless laws give states incentive to criminalize populations
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Defense lawyers' livelihoods depend on laws remaining punitive
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Superabundant scholarship creates vested interests in suppressing genuine insights
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Omniscience is incoherent because knowledge of one kind may exclude knowledge of another kind
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: An omniscient being has a frozen belief-set and therefore cannot decide anything
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: The Heavy Rock Paradox fails because an unliftable rock for an omnipotent being is a logical impossibility like a four-sided triangle
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Problem-solving institutions have incentives to perpetuate problems when solving them would eliminate the institution
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Hospitals solve problems because new patients always exist; philosophy departments suppress solutions because their practitioners cannot handle replacement problems
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Race-studies departments exacerbate racial tensions
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Authors don't believe everything they write—much is written because it seems warranted, not because it's believed true
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Bhartrhari's Paradox is a logical blunder: "some things cannot be described" ascribes no property to any particular object
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: "Some things cannot be described" is true (given any language L, non-denumerably many objects cannot be described in L) and false (for any object, some possible language can describe it)
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: The Barber Paradox proves such a barber cannot exist
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Russell's reasoning about the set of all non-self-containing sets is wrong; "doesn't self-contain" when not elliptical for a non-reflexive statement contains an undefined pronoun
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: The Axiom of Comprehension is correct
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: The Unexpected Hanging Paradox dissolves because "surprise" is relative to time—Smith lacks information until the day of hanging
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Ross's Paradox: imperatival logic has world-to-word direction of fit, so validity flows from general to specific (opposite of statement logic)
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: The Lottery Paradox: high probability is not certainty; acceptance is pragmatic, belief is alethic
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Beliefs are not justified on strictly statistical grounds but by explanatory superiority

positions.text: Knowledge of the future is based on continuities, not regularities/repetitions

positions.text: Causation is continuation, not constant conjunction
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: Regularities tell us where to look for continuities but never constitute causal knowledge

positions.text: We can know singular causal claims without knowing of any similar cases

positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: The ability to learn presupposes constitutive knowledge that isn't acquired

positions.text: Content externalism has absurd consequences (content has no psychological effect; one cannot know what one is thinking about)

positions.text: The more legal right a nation has to secede, the less moral right it has (legitimate laws reduce legitimacy of secession)
positions.title: Ninety Paradoxes of Philosophy and Psychology

positions.text: The class of all recursive, truth-preserving logics is not recursively enumerable.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: The set of total recursive functions is not recursively enumerable.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Formalism cannot formalize its own boundaries.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Recursivity cannot recursively characterize its own extent.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: There is no recursive definition of "recursive definition."
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: There is no recursive procedure that generates all recursive procedures.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Formal truth is a relational concept—a statement is formally true if and only if there exists a truth-preserving recursive logic such that the statement belongs to that logic.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: The predicate "formally true" is not recursively enumerable.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: The apparent special status of classical logical constants reflects merely that we discovered workable recursive systems for them early.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: There is no intrinsic syntactic mark of "logicality"—only the pragmatic fact that some expressions admit readily discoverable recursive axiomatizations.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: No recursive meta-procedure can generate all recursive logical systems.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: The space of formal systems itself cannot be recursively surveyed.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Formality requires meta-formal judgment.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: The space of logical systems is irreducibly plural.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Natural languages are not single recursive expression-classes but hierarchies of such classes stratified by self-reference levels.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Syntax can run ahead of semantics—syntactically valid strings can await semantic grounding at a higher level.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: The metalinguistic series (E₀, E₁, E₂, ...) is not recursive.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Any attempt to give a complete account of a language hierarchy requires stepping outside that hierarchy.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Logicism failed due to conceptual incoherence about what "logic" meant, not just technical failure.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Syntax is necessarily recursive; semantics is not recursive.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Semantic compositionality fails for metalinguistic and self-referential expressions.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Linguistic competence equals Universal Grammar (recursive) plus X (non-recursive meta-semantic capacity).
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: The meta-semantic capacity (X) belongs to competence, not mere performance.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Ostensive/demonstrative reference, naming capacity, concept formation, and metalinguistic awareness are non-recursive elements of linguistic competence.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Complete recursive semantics is impossible—a principled impossibility, not a practical limitation.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Neural networks are not recursive systems and therefore are not constrained by recursion-theoretic limitations.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Neural networks succeed precisely because they are non-recursive, allowing them to approximate non-recursive rational capacities.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: GOFAI (Good Old-Fashioned AI) failed because it was limited to recursive operations.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: The equation "Rational = Recursive" is false and has infected philosophy, linguistics, cognitive science, and AI for over a century.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Rationality is broader than recursivity—there are two modes of rational thought: recursive and non-recursive.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Scientific discovery is rational but non-recursive.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Popper was wrong to conclude that because discovery isn't algorithmic, it must be irrational.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Hume's problem of induction dissolves once we recognize that induction is pattern recognition, which is basic and self-justifying.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Pattern recognition is the fundamental mode of rationality; deduction is a special case.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Rationality is the skilled capacity to recognize truth-relevant patterns and respond appropriately to them.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Pattern recognition is trainable, systematic, and productive—but not algorithmizable.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Pattern recognition mediates both data-modeling and theoretical leaps in scientific discovery.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Pattern recognition recovers all functions traditionally attributed to deduction: truth-preservation, consistency-checking, prediction, justification, rigor, knowledge-transmission, and conceptual clarification.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Formalization (Euclid, Chomsky) is secondary—humans recognized patterns before they codified them into formal rules.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Wittgenstein's rule-following paradox is refuted because rule-application is pattern instantiation, which is direct and non-regressive.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Knowledge does not require recursive foundations—perceptual knowledge is direct pattern recognition.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Justification can be non-recursive; coherence is rational without being algorithmic.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Thinking includes non-computational capacities; pattern recognition is physical but non-algorithmic.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: The mind is not purely computational.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Moral reasoning can be rational without universal rules—particularism and virtue ethics are vindicated.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Mathematical intuition is rational; understanding proofs requires seeing why steps follow, not just checking symbols.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Rationality cannot recursively characterize its own scope—just as formalism cannot formalize its own boundaries.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: The tradition mistook a special case (recursive/deductive reasoning) for the whole of rationality.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Rationality is seeing, not calculating.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: The incompleteness of deductive logic is not a limitation but a liberation—it reveals the full breadth of rational human capacities including non-recursive ones.
positions.title: Epistemic Engineering

positions.text: Intentions are judgments as to how to consolidate one's psychological architecture.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Values are beliefs as to what, in general, is conducive to the integrity of one's psychological architecture.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Freedom is a property of actions and also of desires, contrary to Locke's claim that it applies only to actions.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Second-order desires do not collapse into first-order desires; the distinction is meaningful and important.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Persons, unlike non-persons, subject their own desires and mental states to critical rational scrutiny.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Rational conduct is not identical with intelligent conduct; an act can be highly intelligent yet irrational.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: An irrational act is one that does not embody due regard for one's various interests, regardless of its intelligence.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: For an act to be free, one's first-order desires must be consistent with the second-order desires driving that act.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Higher-order desires can become mere vehicles for first-order desires through psychological warping.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Truly free acts are driven by values, not merely by higher-order desires.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Values are not desires, though values may lead to desires and desires may lead to values.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Where there are no values, there are no selves, though there may be minds.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: There is no agency of any kind where there are no selves.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Estrangement from one's own actions amounts to estrangement from all of one's own desires.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Being free consists primarily in being oneself, and secondarily in being able to do what one wants.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Many analyses that philosophers put forth seem not to embody genuine attempts to find the truth; Frankfurt's analysis of freedom does embody such an attempt.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Kant's two categorical imperatives (universalizability and respect for autonomy) are not equivalent.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Acts of con-artistry would not necessarily be self-defeating in a world of con-artists, refuting Kant's CI1.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Morally good forms of conduct can be ones that, if engaged in universally, could not be successfully carried out.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: A moral principle outweighed is not a moral principle extinguished.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: A categorical moral obligation can be outweighed by some other, stronger obligation without ceasing to exist.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Kant's failure to distinguish the good from the praiseworthy is a fundamental blunder in his ethical theory.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Many things that are morally good are not commendable; Brown's happiness is good but not praiseworthy.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Intelligence, wealth, and health have inherent moral value, contrary to Kant's claim that only good will has moral worth.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Wittgenstein's central claims about meaning and mind are demonstrably false.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Norman Malcolm's denial that people dream was a consequence of following Wittgenstein's false views about mental processes.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Ryle's claim that the difference between voluntary and involuntary actions concerns cultural acceptability rather than mental states is obviously false.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: First-class philosophical thinkers are more likely to use their intelligence to defend orthodoxies than to look for truth.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Students and professors at first-tier philosophy institutions are not more likely to produce better work than their counterparts at bottom-tier universities.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Strawson's ascriptivist analysis of moral responsibility is false because it makes intentions irrelevant to responsibility.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Strawson's expressivist claim that "true" is redundant is false; "everything Plato said was true" cannot be stated without "true" or a synonym.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: "It is true that snow is white" and "snow is white" are not synonymous; the former concerns a proposition while the latter concerns snow directly.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Wittgenstein's claim that "I am in pain" is merely expressive behavior like animal howling is demonstrably false.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Austin's performative utterance theory is false; utterances like "I promise" do affirm propositions.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Embedding a sentence P in "if P, then Q" does not change P's meaning; this is necessary for indirect arguments to work.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: A priori knowledge is knowledge that does not depend on sensory observation for its justification.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: All mathematical truths are analytic truths.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Not all analytic truths are mathematical truths; statements about mathematics are analytic but not themselves mathematical.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: No analytic truths are tautologies, since tautologies are definitional truths that hold in virtue of contingent facts about word usage.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: The statement "a bachelor is an unmarried man" is an empirical truth about language, not an analytic truth.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: It is incoherent to deny analytic truths, but one can coherently (though incorrectly) deny definitional truths.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Gödel proved that the truths of arithmetic are analytically true but not formally true.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: What Gödel proved is that there is no recursive definition of the class of truths of arithmetic, not that mathematical truths are non-analytic.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Incompleteness is the property of a statement-set having no recursive definition.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: Most scientifically important statement-sets are incomplete; statement-sets with real scope or explanatory power cannot be axiomatized.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: There is no recursive definition of the class of statements of the form "x is a real number."
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: A logic (system of logic) is a recursively defined statement-set.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: The class of all recursively defined statement-sets is not itself recursively definable.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: There is no recursive characterization of the concept of logical truth.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works

positions.text: The incompleteness of logic is more general, and perhaps more significant, than the incompleteness of arithmetic.
positions.title: A Priori Knowledge and Other Philosophical Works
