positions.text: Human beings have always unconsciously known the theory of evolution, repressing this knowledge due to its threat to self-esteem.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The anatomical similarities between humans and other animals are so obvious that recognition of common ancestry is virtually inevitable.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: When confronted with similar things, humans instinctively seek a common cause; the ability to accept coincidence marks non-instinctive thought.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Human self-conception as morally superior to animals is a defensive mechanism against the humiliating truth of our animal nature.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The biological basis of love and sex, involving excretory organs, reveals our fundamental animality despite idealized conceptions.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Sexual intercourse has always been regarded as indecent except under hyper-specific circumstances, proving awareness of our animal nature.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A proposition is the non-linguistic entity meant by a complete sentence, not the sentence itself.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Emotions are beliefs or are at least belief-like in that they can be true or false, rational or irrational.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Emotions answer to canons of rationality, which only beliefs can do; therefore emotions must be beliefs.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Moral statements express emotions, and since emotions are beliefs capable of truth and falsity, moral statements can be true or false.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Emotivism, properly understood, is a form of moral realism rather than anti-realism.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Awareness of norms cannot be acquired through behavioral conditioning; it must exist ab initio or not at all.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A society of normless individuals cannot collectively become aware of norms; norms cannot emerge from non-normative ingredients.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Understanding complex sentences presupposes grasping concepts; there is no way to reduce sentence comprehension to precognitive reactions.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: To understand a complex sentence, one must grasp not just the concepts of its constituents but also the rules governing their composition.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A creature with purely de re awareness cannot make the leap to de dicto awareness through conditioning alone.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Lexical items do not come bundled the way concepts do; therefore lexical items cannot be our concepts, and we do not think in any language.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: There is no such thing as Mentalese; we do not think in a language or symbolic code.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Constitutive relations among concepts are not containment relations among symbols.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The thought "that is a sphere" is different from, though constitutively connected with, the thought "that is a closed figure of uniform curvature."
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Conceptual analysis and semantic analysis are not the same thing; a single semantic analysis may correspond to multiple conceptual analyses.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Color concepts are not mutually definable, yet they are constitutively interconnected; one cannot grasp one color without grasping others.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Any concept has constitutive connections to other concepts; conceptual atomism is false.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: One cannot represent a single property to oneself without being able to represent other properties; representational capacities come in networks.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: To grasp a specific shade of color, one must be able to distinguish it from other colors and thus grasp multiple colors.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: If a creature could represent only one shade of color, its concept of that shade would collapse into a concept of color in general.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Properties are individuated by their relations to other properties; grasping a property requires grasping its place in a space of properties.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Degree properties like "weighing 1000 lbs" obviously require grasping related properties; this point generalizes to all properties.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A language must comprise basic expressions; otherwise knowledge of any expression would presuppose knowledge of an infinitely large lexicon.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Proper names are not definite descriptions, as Kripke decisively showed.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: What we think does not, by itself, establish what our words and sentences mean; semantic externalism is correct.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Twin Earth cases show that the meanings of natural kind terms are not wholly determined by internal psychological states.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Content internalism is compatible with semantic externalism; linguistic meaning and cognitive content are distinct.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Reference-fixing should be distinguished from meaning-giving; this distinction dissolves many puzzles about concepts.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: To have a concept of a thing is to know some proposition that describes that thing.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Knowledge of an existential proposition that uniquely describes x suffices for the ability to think about x.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Conception is sustained by knowledge of uniquely individuating descriptions, which may include indexical elements.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Recognition consists in seeing that one object satisfies two existence claims previously thought to be satisfied by different objects.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Phenomenal content does not supervene on propositional content; intentionalism is false.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Phenomenal content does not supervene on non-propositional representational content either.

positions.text: Intentionalism over-predicts the amount of information borne by sensations.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A tickle or twinge is too simple and homogeneous to bear fine-grained propositions about anatomical structures.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Creatures can experience phenomenal contents like woozy feelings without possessing vast networks of representational capacities.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: If intentionalism were true, a twinge would deliver vastly more information than such sensations actually deliver.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Phenomenal content is something altogether different from representational content—not identical with or supervenient on it.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Even restricted intentionalism (within sensory modalities) collapses into non-intentionalism upon examination.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: There is no absolute correspondence between phenomenal and representational content.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Different phenomenal contents, even within the same modality, can bear the same representational content.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The relationship between phenomenal contents and their physical causes is like a "rule of projection" that could be different.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The Computational Theory of Mind (CTM) is false because there is no purely morphological characterization of syntax.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: There is no purely syntactic characterization of logical truth; adequate characterizations must be partially semantic.

positions.text: The only way to sensitize a thing to logical form is to sensitize it to semantics.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Turing machines do not compute in the relevant sense because they do not respond to semantics.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Adding numbers is never a purely mechanical, cognition-free operation even when done by the "quick and dirty" method.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A representationally innocent entity generating patterns of shapes does not constitute genuine computation.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Computing involves being sensitive to logical form, and logical form is buried in semantics.

positions.text: The Ramsey-Lewis sentence method for defining theoretical terms is useless because functionalism cannot be articulated without vicious circularity.

positions.text: Functionalism fails because it cannot non-circularly specify the mental states in terms of which functional roles are defined.

positions.text: Content externalism strips mental content of causal efficacy; if externalism is true, content cannot explain behavior.

positions.text: Mental causation requires that mental content be causally relevant in a way externalism cannot accommodate.

positions.text: Davidson's anomalous monism leads to epiphenomenalism about the mental.

positions.text: Jerry Fodor's computationalism is pseudo-computationalism because he cannot adequately account for how syntax tracks semantics.

positions.text: Fodor's main argument for conceptual atomism fails because constitutive connections are not containment relations.

positions.text: Fodor confuses the claim that concepts are not decomposable with the claim that concepts lack constitutive connections to others.

positions.text: Non-atomism does not require inferential role semantics (IRS); the inference from one to the other is fallacious.

positions.text: Inferential role semantics faces the "error objection": if inferential roles define concepts, misapplication becomes impossible.

positions.text: Inferential role semantics leads to an untenable holism where no two people can grasp the same proposition.

positions.text: The bundle theory of personal identity is defensible when properly understood.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Propositions are sets of properties; truth is the instantiation of all members of that set.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A proposition like "Smith punches Jones" is a set containing properties corresponding to Smith, Jones, and the punching relation.

positions.text: For a proposition to be true is for all of its component properties to be instantiated.

positions.text: Propositions have decompositional structure; they are not merely specified by truth-conditions.

positions.text: Analytically equivalent propositions can convey very different information.

positions.text: Analytic propositions do typically convey information, contrary to Lewis's model.

positions.text: Hempel's paradox is solved by recognizing that accidental generalizations do not receive support from their instances.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Only principled (nomic) generalizations receive confirmational support from their instances.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: "If x is heated metal, x expands" and "If x isn't expanding, x isn't heated metal" are not confirmationally equivalent when read nomically.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Negative facts (like not being heated metal) are not fit to stand in nomic relations because they are not events or constituted by events.

positions.text: A thing's not having a property is not nomically connected to anything, unlike a thing's having a property.

positions.text: Inductions by enumeration are actually crypto-inferences to the best explanation.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Formal truth is truth by virtue of being an instance of a sentence-schema all of whose instances are true.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Formal truth is not identical with syntactic truth; there is no purely morphological characterization of logical form.

positions.text: The equation of formal truth with syntactic truth is viciously circular.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Not all logical truths are formal truths; logic cannot be identified with the study of formal truth.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The most fundamental truths of logic must consist of non-formal, analytic truths.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The linguistic theory of logical truth (that logical truths are true by convention) is false.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Logical truths are not true merely in virtue of the meanings of words or linguistic conventions.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Conceivability sometimes entails possibility, providing a neo-Cartesian argument for dualism.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: If mental properties are not identical with physical properties, and identity is necessary, then dualism follows.

positions.text: Time travel is an incoherent concept.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Probabilistic causation is a genuine form of causation distinct from deterministic causation.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Simulated thought is not actual thought; genuine mental processes cannot be simulated by non-mental processes.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Davidson's views on rationality embody fundamental misconceptions.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Foundationalism about knowledge is compatible with non-atomism about concepts.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Intentionalism (the view that phenomenal properties are representational properties) fails both in restricted and unrestricted forms.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The veil of perception problem is exacerbated by externalism, not solved by it.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Representationalism about perception faces serious objections from the nature of phenomenal experience.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Understanding a sentence requires knowing multiple existence claims and their connections.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Identification is relative to background knowledge; being able to identify an object means connecting it to background knowledge.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A parrot cannot really say "it's getting hotter" because it lacks grasp of concepts and semantic rules.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: For a sentence to have meaning for a speaker, it must have internal structure connected to concepts.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The meaning of complex sentences is a resultant, not a mere heaping together, of component meanings.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The word "fire" in isolation has the same semantics as in complex sentences; meaning operates through concepts uniformly.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Sense perceptions have existential content; the content of a perception is given by some bit of existential information.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Introspection, while limited, is reasonably reliable for apprehending the nature of phenomenal contents.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: If phenomenal contents deliver information, our conscious minds must retrieve this information, suggesting we know our phenomenal contents well.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Kant's argument for synthetic a priori truths is spurious, but his important claims about causality, personal identity, and rationality are correct when de-psychologized.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Kant's premises actually entail the opposite of his thesis: there are virtually no a priori truths, making Kant more opposed to a priori truth than John Stuart Mill.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Kant confuses two different epistemological roles of mental imagery: as a vehicle for conceptual analysis and as an object of empirical study.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Kant's reasoning must be de-psychologized—his statements about psychology must be reinterpreted as statements about logic.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Conceptual necessity is non-psychological necessity that holds entirely in virtue of facts about concepts in the platonic sense.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The proposition "triangles have three sides" would still be true even if there were no sentient beings.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Representational necessity is a different category where propositions are necessarily true because of facts about our minds.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Wittgenstein's regress argument shows that grasping a norm is not a cognitive affair in the traditional sense of having an inner representation.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Grasping a norm consists in having certain dispositions, not in possessing some mental icon corresponding to that norm.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Knowing-how is not cognitive in the sense of involving a grasp of propositions; it is a kind of competence or sensitivity.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: For Wittgenstein, to know what a sentence means is to know how to use it, which is to be coordinated with other people's usage.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Knowledge-how does ultimately reduce to knowledge-that; knowing how to do something consists in internally representing rules.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Brandom's account of how individuals acquire intentionality is circular: one cannot give and ask for reasons without first grasping reasons.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Locke, Kant, and classical philosophers were right that speaking is a kind of externalized thinking, not vice versa.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The idea that thinking is internalized speaking (Wittgenstein, Brandom) is false.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Given a group of creatures oblivious to norms, no amount of interaction will make any of them aware of norms.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The "buck cannot be passed onto society": a society of normless individuals will itself be normless.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A proposition is a special kind of norm: a standard against which reality is compared.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: To expect dinner tomorrow, unlike expecting dinner now, requires de dicto awareness and grasp of concepts.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Ordinary stimuli have no internal structure, but sentences are articulate—their parts stand in significant relations.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Through Pavlovian training you can make a dog react to stimuli, but you cannot make it understand compound sentences.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Understanding "if there's smoke, there's fire" requires grasping the concepts of smoke and fire, not just particular instances.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Identity holds necessarily: if A can be distinct from B, then A must be distinct from B.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Logical possibility sometimes indicates actual possibility, but not always—the relevant difference involves object-dependent vs. object-independent concepts.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Whenever there is a disparity between logical and metaphysical possibility, it is underwritten by facts about object-dependent concepts.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Necessities underwritten wholly by object-independent concepts have logical possibility coinciding with metaphysical possibility.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Object-dependent concepts have actual spatiotemporal objects as constituents; object-independent concepts do not.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: To grasp an object-involving proposition, one must have causal liaisons to the object; purely existential propositions can be grasped without such liaisons.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A brain in a vat could grasp purely existential propositions but not object-involving propositions about spatiotemporal objects.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: There is no purely morphological characterization of syntax; syntax is buried in semantics.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Any expression could mean anything; given any entity, there is some language in which "172" denotes that entity.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: There can be no algorithm deciding truth-value wholly on the basis of shape; truth-value requires morphology plus semantic background.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: An algorithm is given by a conditional proposition: if such and such semantic system holds, then there is a morphological characterization of truth.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: To apply an algorithm involves recognizing the truth of a multiply conditional proposition about semantics and shapes.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The manipulation of shapes is at most a causal consequence of actual computation—almost an epiphenomenon.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Even if there are algorithms for various domains, thought in those domains cannot be "mechanized."
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: There are two components to applying an algorithm: recognition of an operative semantic system (not mechanical) and patterns of shapes (can be mechanical).
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Inference requires recognition that premises stand in a bearing relation (entailment or probabilification) to the conclusion.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: To have a rational thought-process, symbols must follow one another in a way that embodies awareness of bearing-relations among them.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A computer's symbols follow one another in the right order, but this doesn't involve recognition of bearing relations—so computers don't really think.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A computation is a purely formal, syntax-driven inference where consideration of semantics plays no part.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Fodor's "solution" to how physics-driven brain activity can be logic-consistent involves a massive confusion.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The "tracking problem"—how purely descriptive brain activity embodies sensitivity to prescriptive norms—is not solved by CTM.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Nobody knows Mentalese; nobody understands its expressions; it isn't by being understood that Mentalese tokens facilitate thought.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Mentalese, if it existed, would differ radically from real languages because its expressions need not be understood to function.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The very concept of a "language of thought" is incoherent because languages require understanding to function.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Brains are governed entirely by the laws of physics, which are purely descriptive, not prescriptive.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Our mental lives are entirely realized by brain activity, yet our mental activity conforms to canons of good reasoning.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Laws of physics are not laws of rationality, so laws of rationality seemingly play no part in our thinking—yet clearly they do.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: What we think largely determines what our expressions mean, but there is also an important non-psychological dimension to meaning.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Given psychological and behavioral indistinguishability, it does not follow that utterances have the same meanings.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Twin Earth cases show that two utterances can differ in meaning despite arising from psychologically identical states.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The word "water" refers to H2O even for speakers in the 1600s who knew nothing of chemistry.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: If a substance were discovered to be XYZ rather than H2O, chemists would say it is extremely water-like, not that water is sometimes XYZ.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Semantic externalism is true: meaning is not wholly determined by internal psychological states.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Content internalism is also true: what we are thinking is wholly determined by our internal states.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The distinction between reference-fixing and meaning-giving dissolves the apparent tension between externalism about meaning and internalism about content.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The representational content of visual perception can be put into words and embodied in propositions.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: All representational content is essentially propositional; there is no genuinely non-propositional representational content.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Functionalism gets the order of explanation wrong: you stop because you see a stop-sign, not vice versa.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: For functionalism, a perception has its content in virtue of causing certain behavior—but content is what causes behavior.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: If x exists in virtue of y's existing, then x cannot cause y to exist; so functionalism makes content causally inert.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Functionalism says you see a growling dog because you flee, but obviously you flee because you see a growling dog.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Whenever one adds a referring term to one's lexicon, it involves knowing some uniquely individuating description of the referent.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The semantics of a name are not existential-descriptive, but the act of learning the semantics is existential-descriptive.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Names are just labels semantically, but one learns which thing a name labels through existential-descriptive claims.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: To avoid running afoul of Kripke's points, we must give operators like "refers to" narrow scope relative to descriptive content.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Recognition of an individual consists in seeing that one thing uniquely satisfies each of two existence claims.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Conception is sustained by knowledge of a series of interlocking existence claims; any given claim may be dropped and replaced.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: At any juncture of having a conception of a thing, that conception involves knowledge of an existence claim.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The cognitive difference between "Hesperus is lovely" and "Phosphorous is lovely" is explained by different existence claims.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Intentionalism is guilty of what Sellars called "the myth of the given."
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Visual sensations do not by themselves tell you what color an object is; background knowledge about environmental conditions is required.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Sensations coupled with background knowledge about conditions of perception tell you what properties objects have.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: What makes a bit of phenomenal content have a given representational content is precisely the addition of background knowledge.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: If Sellars is right about the myth of the given, then intentionalism must be completely wrong.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Perceivable properties require contextualizing information to be represented; they are not intrinsically associated with phenomenal contents.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Tickles and twinges bear only highly generic messages like "disturbance of some kind in such and such part of my body."
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Sensations do not constitute high-resolution apprehensions of bodily conditions; they are fairly opaque and uninformative.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Intentionalism demands that sensations be very non-opaque and high-resolution, which they plainly are not.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Even if sensations bore non-propositional information, they still would not bear enough information to vindicate intentionalism.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Non-propositional content, if it exists, presupposes the same vast networks of representational capacities as propositional content.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The ability to have any kind of representation presupposes the ability to represent things as having properties.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A morphinated feeling of euphoria has no felt bodily location—no spatial representations seem involved.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Some phenomenal contents are "generalized" feelings not felt in any particular body part.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Knowing what is distinctive about a color involves knowing how it differs from other colors.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Grasping the specific character of a property involves grasping what distinguishes it from other properties.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: One can grasp a specific shape-property only if one has a grasp of a whole "space" of shape properties.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Direct reference theory does not show that names express no concepts whatsoever.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Freud held that thoughts must be clothed in words to become conscious—a view with some merit.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Hume's ethical anti-realism is connected to his view that emotions are incapable of being true or false.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Emotions can be justified or unjustified, rational or irrational—properties that only beliefs can have.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: People try to justify their emotions, both negative and positive, suggesting emotions answer to rational standards.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: When someone narcotizes an irrational emotion, this presupposes that emotions can be evaluated as rational or irrational.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Moral statements embody emotions; since emotions are beliefs capable of truth and falsity, moral realism follows.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Speech-act semantics and tractarian semantics are not really opposed on the relation between sentences and propositions.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: If a sentence bears a proposition, then ipso facto it enables one to express the corresponding belief.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Hempel's paradox arises only if we conflate accidental generalizations with principled (nomic) generalizations.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Accidental generalizations like "all heated metal expands" read as mere de facto concomitances don't receive support from instances.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Only nomic generalizations—those alleging causal or lawlike connections—receive confirmational support from their instances.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Nomic relations hold only among spatiotemporal entities; negative facts like "not being heated metal" are not spatiotemporal.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A thing's not having a property is like not destroying Jupiter—it is not an event and doesn't stand in nomic relations.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The operator "there is a nomic connection between..." doesn't permit intersubstitution of logically equivalent sentences.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Logically equivalent sentences can be truth-preservingly intersubstituted in some contexts but not in contexts governed by nomic operators.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Given that "P iff P" is formally true, the statement "all statements of the form P iff P are formally true" is itself not formal.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: It is always on the basis of knowledge of non-formal truths that we know a given truth to be formal.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: It is an empirical fact that logical connectives have the syntactic and semantic properties they have.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: It is non-empirically true that, given their properties, certain connectives are truth-functional—this is analytic but non-formal.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Those non-formal, analytic truths are themselves part of logic and constitute its essence more than formal truths do.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Formal logic is to pure logic as engineering is to physics—formal logic is applied logic.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Logicians who dedicate themselves exclusively to formal work are applied logicians, not logicians in the truest sense.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Bradley's Logic and Mill's Treatise on Logic, despite containing no formal reasoning, are genuine works of logic.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A sentence is formally true if it is an instance of an open-sentence true for all values of its free variables.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A proposition is a property of a world; for a proposition to be true is for that property to be instantiated.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Truth can be identified with instantiatedness: a proposition is true exactly if that property is instantiated in the actual world.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Lewis's identification of propositions with properties of worlds is informative but viciously circular if taken as an analysis.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Propositions have decompositional structure that mere sets of worlds or functions from worlds to truth-values do not capture.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Analytic propositions do convey information, contrary to views that make them disappear as uninformative.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A true proposition is always true in virtue of something about the world—mass-energy distribution, ripples in the quantum.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The truth of "Plato punched Socrates" supervenes on facts about atomic movements describable without mentioning Plato or Socrates.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The property of having the right "rippling" of mass-energy is more basic than the proposition it makes true.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: There is no circularity in analyzing propositions in terms of properties of worlds when those properties are more basic.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Different propositions can share constituents: "Smith punches Jones" and "Plato punches Jones" share the "punches Jones" component.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A proposition has components corresponding to incomplete properties like "x punches Jones" and "Smith punches x."
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Frege's analysis that propositions are built from relations and functions captures the structure of propositions correctly.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: A proposition is a set of properties; it is true when all its members are instantiated.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Each discrete constituent of a proposition corresponds to one property in the set that constitutes it.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Fodor's argument for atomism fails because constitutive connections among concepts are not containment relations.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Fodor confuses the claim that concepts resist decomposition with the claim that they lack constitutive connections.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: One's pink-concept is not composed of other color-concepts, yet one cannot have it without having other color-concepts.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Color-symbols are not mutually definable; color concepts are not decomposable into other color concepts.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: If color concepts literally contained other color concepts, any color thought would consist of infinitely many color thoughts—absurd.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Any attempt to block the infinite regress through looping definitions makes concept-possession presuppose itself.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: One's concept of 1,143 presupposes concepts of 1,142, 1,141, etc., but 1,143-thoughts are not cobbled together from these.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Fodor is right that most concepts resist decomposition, but wrong that this means they lack constitutive connections.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Inferential role semantics makes it impossible for two people to grasp the same proposition.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: For IRS, differences in inferential role mean differences in content; but no two cognitive states have identical inferential roles.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: IRS faces the "error objection": if inferential roles define concepts, misapplication of concepts becomes impossible.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: If IRS is right, wrong applications of a concept collapse into correct applications of some other concept—but plainly this is false.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The distinction between reference-fixing and meaning-giving saves non-atomism from the objections Fodor raises.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: To have a concept of a thing is to know some proposition that describes (uniquely individuates) that thing.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Knowledge of a proposition describing x suffices for ability to think about x—it qualifies as a concept of x.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Once you know a proposition describing a thing, you can attribute any property you grasp to that thing.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Your concept of the number two is your belief in a proposition like "there is some number n such that n=1+1."
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Changes in what else you believe about the number two do not change your concept of it.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: The error objection is sidestepped: wrong beliefs about two are still about two because they attribute properties to what satisfies n=1+1.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Grasping a thing consists in grasping a proposition about that thing; one cannot just think a thing without thinking something about it.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Whatever is thought—whatever is grasped—is some truth or falsity, never just a bare object.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Knowing who a name refers to is contextual: relative to some existence claims you may know it, relative to others not.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: Understanding a sentence-token is contextual because knowing what a term refers to is contextual.
positions.title: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers

positions.text: It is widely held that one can sometimes rationally accept propositions of the form P and not-P.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: There are necessary a posteriori truths.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Kripke's arguments for these views appear probative only so long as one fails to distinguish between semantics and presemantics.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The distinction between semantics and presemantics is between the literal meanings of sentences and the information on the basis of which one identifies those literal meanings.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The failure to distinguish between semantics and presemantics underlies the popular thesis that intersubstituting co-referring terms sometimes turns true sentences into false ones and vice versa.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The thesis that intersubstituting co-referring terms sometimes changes truth values has counterintuitive consequences.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: One counterintuitive consequence of the thesis is that the occurrence of 'snow' in 'it is true that snow is white' doesn't refer to snow.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Understanding the distinction between semantics and presemantics suggests a way to develop a semantic system without the counterintuitive consequences.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The semantic system suggested can reconcile our intuitions concerning cognitive content with some powerfully argued theses of contemporary philosophy of language.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Some of the paper's main contentions are anticipated by Andrzej Boguslawski in his 1994 paper 'Sentential Complementation and Truth.'
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: In at least some contexts, replacing a referring term with a co-referring term preserves truth-value.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The sentence 'Hesperus is closer to the Sun than the Earth' is true.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: 'Phosphorous' and 'Hesperus' refer to the same thing.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The sentence 'Phosphorous is closer to the Sun than the Earth' is also true.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: If a context tolerates intersubstitutions of co-referring terms, that context is 'extensional.'
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: If an expression occurs in an extensional context, it occurs 'extensionally.'
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: There seem to be cases where replacing a referring term with a co-referring term leads from truth to falsehood or vice versa.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Not all contexts are extensional.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: If a context is not extensional, it is 'intensional' and the expressions occurring in it do so 'intensionally.'
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: All contexts are extensional.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The truth of 'I am tired' applies to all sentence-types with indexical or context-sensitive components.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Most, if not all, sentence-types of natural language contain tense-markers.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The identity of the proposition meant by a token of a sentence-type with a tense-marker depends on the time of production.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Kaplan and Soames elaborate and defend the view that sentence-types with tense-markers depend on the time of production for their meaning.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The function meant by a sentence-type can fail to assign a proposition to a particular token.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: An utterance about a non-existent pink elephant fails to encode a proposition.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Strawson, Kaplan, Salmon, Soames, and others, including the author, believe that sentence-tokens typically have propositions for their literal meanings.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Some philosophers deny that sentence-tokens typically have truth-evaluable entities for their literal meanings.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Even after setting aside cases of presupposition-failure, sentence-tokens do not have truth-evaluable entities for their meanings according to some philosophers.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Contextualists believe that sentence-tokens typically communicate propositions but require contextual facts to fill in semantic gaps.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Recanati and Travis are proponents of contextualism or radical pragmatics.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The meaning of 'Charles is ready' depends on knowing what Charles is ready for.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The literal meaning of 'that is red' leaves open what has been affirmed about the object's redness.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The standard of redness intended can vary based on the context, affecting the truth of the statement 'that is red'.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Contextualism holds that these points about the need for context apply to all sentence-tokens, with few exceptions.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Cappelen and Lepore provide a thorough discussion of contextualism.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Pinker provides a brief but effective discussion of contextualism.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Russell's Theory of Descriptions states that a sentence with the form 'the phi' has a meaning that is a quantified generalization.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: According to Russell's Theory of Descriptions, 'the king of France is bald' means a quantified generalization about the king of France being bald.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: If Russell's Theory of Descriptions is correct, 'the king of France' is not a referring term but a quantifier.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The conception of meaning advocated by Quine, Davidson, Dummett, Chomsky, Kaplan, and Soames is not confined to one tradition.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Cappelen and Lepore have made a strong case against contextualism.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Pinker has also made a strong case against contextualism.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: One can hold that sentence-tokens as a rule do have truth-evaluable entities for their literal meanings without being guilty of sheer dogmatism.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Searle does not deny that many sentence-tokens typically have complete propositions for their literal meanings.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Searle denies the view that for every sentence the literal meaning can be construed as the meaning it has independently of any context.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Searle holds that the notion of literal meaning only has application relative to a set of contextual or background assumptions.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Some tokens of 'the cat is on the mat' do have truth-evaluable entities for their literal meanings, while others do not.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: An utterance of 'the cat is on the mat' made in somebody's living room will probably have a determinately true or false proposition for its meaning.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The same utterance of 'the cat is on the mat' made in outer space may not have a determinately true or false proposition for its meaning.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Searle's point is that sentence-tokens have truth-evaluable literal meanings only relative to certain background assumptions.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Searle is not denying that sentences have literal meanings, but that literal meaning is relative to background assumptions.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The argument in the paper is consistent with Searle's thesis.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The question of whether or to what extent Searle's thesis is correct lies outside the scope of the present paper.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The viewpoints of bona fide contextualists and authors with contextualist leanings have roots in the work of Wittgenstein and Austin.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Proper names are mere labels.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: There is no difficulty accounting for the dramatic communicative differences between sentences like 'Hesperus is identical with Phosphorous' and 'Hesperus is identical with Hesperus' if proper names are mere labels.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The differences in communication between sentences with different proper names are not due to differences in literal meaning.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The differences in communication between sentences with different proper names are explained by how people ascertain the literal meanings of sentences.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: For any predicate phi, there is no difficulty accounting for the fact that what is communicated by 'Hesperus has phi' may differ dramatically from what is communicated by 'Phosphorous has phi' if proper names are mere labels.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: What was said about 'Hesperus' and 'Phosphorous' is true for any two co-referring proper names.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Many apparent threats to extensionality prove to be illusory given the truth of the contentions about proper names being mere labels.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The remaining threats to extensionality will be dealt with in subsequent sections.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The proposition literally meant by 'Smith is a great racquetball player' is 'O is a great racquetball player' if proper names are mere labels.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The proposition literally meant by 'Bob is a great racquetball player' is the same as the proposition meant by 'Smith is a great racquetball player' if 'Bob' and 'Smith' are mere labels for the same thing.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Sentences 'Smith is a great racquetball player' and 'Bob is a great racquetball player' tell different things under the circumstances described.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: What is learned from 'Smith is a great racquetball player' does not entail, and is not entailed by, what is learned from 'Bob is a great racquetball player'.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: It is not possible for 'Smith is a great racquetball player' and 'Bob is a great racquetball player' to tell different things if they have the same literal meaning.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The supposed paradox that 'Smith is a great racquetball player' and 'Bob is a great racquetball player' do and do not coincide in what they tell about the world is based on a fallacy.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: What a sentence communicates to somebody isn't always confined to its literal meaning.

positions.text: Semantically, 'Bob' is a name.

positions.text: Under certain circumstances, sentences containing 'Bob' communicate propositions of the form 'there is a person x such that x uniquely has thus and such characteristics and 'Bob' is x's name'.

positions.text: Under certain circumstances, 'Bob' acts as a certain kind of existential quantifier.

positions.text: The quantifier 'Bob' can be given either wide-scope or narrow-scope with respect to 'Fred believes'.

positions.text: The sentence 'Fred believes that Bob is a great racquetball player' is ambiguous between two propositions regarding the scope of the quantifier 'Bob'.

positions.text: The expression 'for some king of France x, x is a king of France and such that, for any king of France y, y=x' is a quantifier.

positions.text: The expression 'for any object king of France z, z is bald' is a quantifier.

positions.text: Definite descriptions are quantifiers or quantifier-like, according to Russell's theory.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: If Russell's theory is correct, there is much to be said for the view that definite descriptions are quantifiers.

positions.text: The concept of indirect-reference will be discussed in Part III.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: If definite descriptions are not referring terms, the difference in truth-value between examples (5) and (6) is irrelevant to the question of extensionality.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: It cannot be taken for granted that definite descriptions are not referring terms.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: To establish extensionality, one must establish either that definite descriptions are not referring terms or that intersubstituting co-referring definite descriptions does not change truth-value.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: If contention (B) is correct, then the question of whether contention (A) is correct becomes irrelevant to the question of whether extensionality is correct.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The author will argue for contention (B).
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Even if (5) and (6) have the same literal meaning, there is no difficulty accounting for the fact that they may communicate propositions with different truth-values.

positions.text: There are two kinds of referring terms: those that refer directly and those that refer indirectly.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: According to Kaplan, when addressing Fred with 'you are tall', the literal meaning is the singular proposition that Fred is tall.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Kaplan's position generalized states that a token of an indexical expression that refers to an object O has a literal meaning that includes O itself as a constituent.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: When using 'you' in a sentence, the proposition encoded includes the person being addressed as a constituent, not a concept under which they fall.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The concept 'person to whom I am speaking' does not enter into the proposition meant by the utterance of 'you'.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The utterance of 'you' is directly referential.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The concept 'person to whom I am speaking' plays an important semantic role but does not itself enter into the proposition.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: When pointing to a car and saying 'that must have cost a fortune', the literal meaning is that the car must have cost a fortune.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The concept 'ostended object' is not part of the literal meaning of the utterance about the car.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: If definite descriptions are directly referential, intersubstituting them never changes the truth-value of what is literally meant by sentences.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The term 'direct referentialism' refers to the position that definite descriptions are devices of direct reference.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The term 'Russellianism' refers to the position that definite descriptions are not referring terms and are instead quantifiers or quantifier-like expressions as described by Russell.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Direct referentialists agree almost entirely with Russellians.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Both Russellians and direct referentialists agree that 'the phi' is true if and only if something x uniquely has phi and x satisfies the rest of the sentence.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Russellians and direct referentialists disagree about the semantics of 'the phi'.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: For Russellians, 'the phi' means there is exactly one object O that has phi and O satisfies the rest of the sentence.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: For direct referentialists, if there is exactly one object O that has phi, then 'the phi' means O satisfies the rest of the sentence.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: If nothing uniquely has phi, then for direct referentialists, 'the phi' does not bear any proposition and is neither true nor false.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: According to the direct referentialist, 'the inventor of bifocals snored' does not encode any proposition if nobody uniquely invented bifocals.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: If exactly one person O invented bifocals, then for the direct referentialist, 'the inventor of bifocals snored' means O snored.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Given that Benjamin Franklin uniquely invented bifocals, the direct referentialist says that 'the inventor of bifocals snored' encodes the proposition that Benjamin Franklin snored.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: According to the Russellian, the proposition encoded in 'the inventor of bifocals snored' is equivalent to 'exactly one object x invented bifocals; moreover, x snored'.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: The direct-referentialist's position and the Russellian's position differ significantly because the propositions they encode do not entail each other.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: A consequence of the direct-referentialist's position is that 'Fred believes that the inventor of bifocals snored' encodes the proposition 'Fred believes that O snored' for some object O identical with Benjamin Franklin.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: For the direct referentialist, 'Fred believes that the first postmaster general snored' also has the meaning 'Fred believes that O snored'.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: Another consequence of the direct-referentialist's position is that 'Little Timmy believes that the whole number that comes immediately after one is less than three' has the literal meaning 'Little Timmy believes that O is one less than three' for some O identical with the number two.
positions.title: Intensionality and the Semantics/Presemantics Distinction

positions.text: For the direct referentialist, 'Little Timmy believes that the number n such that n is a unique even prime if arithmetic is incomplete and is otherwise a unique positive square root of 71 is less than three' also has the meaning 'Little Timmy believes that O is one less than three'.

positions.text: Empiricism is fundamentally incompatible with a scientific psychology.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: There exists subpersonal thought that underlies conscious, preconscious, and repressed ideation.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Subpersonal mentation is inherently intelligent, whereas personal/conscious mentation is inherently unintelligent.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Consciousness qua consciousness is capable only of iconic (pictorial) representation.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Concepts are not constituents of consciousness; they are access-conscious, not consciousness-constitutive.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Berkeley conflated images with concepts, using "idea" to refer to both.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Symbol-manipulations facilitate ratiocinative operations, but anything one can think with symbols can be thought without them.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The meaning of a complex expression cannot be directly determined by convention.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: There is a fundamental distinction between object-awareness and truth-awareness (proposition-awareness).
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Creatures can be merely object-aware (MOCs) or proposition-aware (PACs); all truth-aware creatures are object-aware, but not vice versa.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Desires are identical with judgments concerning what would bring pleasure or gratification.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Intentions are judgments, not desires.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Davidson's identification of intentions with one's strongest desires is false.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Higher-order desires as described by Frankfurt are often actually judgments, not desires.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Agency is identical with rationality—specifically, applied rationality deployed in accordance with one's values.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Rational agents are fundamentally different from mere reflex machines or reagents.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The self is a rationality-constituted superstructure whose original purpose was to ensure the organism's survival but whose developed purpose is self-honing and expanding its powers.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Physiological properties are multiply realizable with respect to microphysical properties.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
