source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The Deductive-Nomological (DN) model of scientific explanation is false.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Psychological explanation works through rationalization, identifying causal mechanisms in rational motivations.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: A cause can be operative even if its effect was not guaranteed—causation can be probabilistic.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Laws need not be exceptionless regularities to have genuine explanatory value.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Macro-level properties cannot be reduced to microphysical properties.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Perception requires conceptual articulation; it is not mere sensation but articulated sensation.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Introspection is an act of conceptualization, not theoretically innocent reportage.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Husserl's phenomenological bracketing fails because one cannot strip perceptions of their conceptual articulations.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The Dennett-Churchland eliminativist position on mind is untenable.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Perceptual data is as theory-laden as psychological data.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Russell and other logicians were wrong that self-referential sentences are meaningless; such expressions are either false or sentence-schemata.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The unconscious mind exists and cannot be understood in strictly empiricist terms.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Repression and rationalization are concepts inherently incompatible with empiricism.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Psychosis involves partial cognitive regression where infantile and adult thought pathologically commingle.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Neurotic people perceive external reality accurately but are blind to their own feelings.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: There is a crucial distinction between access-consciousness and consciousness-proper.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Personal mentation becomes ratiocinative only when it becomes rule-governed through linguistic mediation.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Music transfers subpersonal intelligence to the conscious level, making otherwise imperceptible thought-processes perceptible.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Aesthetic beauty is relational—a property of relations between minds and sounds/objects, not intrinsic to sounds/objects themselves.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Sociopathy is psychological suicide: the destruction of one's value-based psychological architecture.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: One can change oneself but cannot change one's self; identity is predetermined, and experience determines only how one is who one is.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Pathological insecurity and pathological grandiosity are two sides of the same coin.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Shame at being wrong is morbid and regressive, as making mistakes is inseparable from the human condition.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Folk-psychology enables rationalization of conscious states but has inherent limitations in explaining psychopathology.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Dreams involve relaxation of maturation-based inhibitions, liberating infantile mental content from the unconscious.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Propositional attitudes cannot be constituents of consciousness and are knowable only to the extent they are phenomenologized.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: It is viciously regressive to suppose that the self carries out the cognitive operations underlying sense-perception.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Science is not about sticking to facts but about organizing facts by positing hidden structures to restore continuities that observation strips away.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The empiricist's injunction to "stick to the facts" is either a triviality or an absurdity.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Perception cannot self-justify; justification for perception must come from non-empirical, analytic principles.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Rationalizations are moralizations: attempts to make the immoral seem moral.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Sociopaths do not have friends because they do not have values, and a friend is somebody one values.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Propositions are non-spatiotemporal entities that consist of properties.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Properties are non-spatiotemporal; they cannot be identified with spatiotemporal entities.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The relata of logical relations are propositions, not sentences.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Logical relations (entailment, confirmation, disconfirmation, incompatibility, neutrality) hold among propositions, not among spatiotemporal entities.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Empiricists must deny the existence of propositions because propositions are non-spatiotemporal.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Psychological propositions do not present facts as being in the same spatial coordinate system as physical propositions.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The relationship between psychology and physiology is even more remote than the relationship between physiology and microphysics.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Hume's skepticism about perception and induction follows correctly from empiricism, which should lead us to reject empiricism rather than accept skepticism.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Scientific thought is not the same as empirical thought; to the extent thought mediates between observations and beliefs, beliefs are not directly supported by observation.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Hume's analysis of causation as mere constant conjunction is false.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: We perceive instances of causation directly when we perceive instances of persistence and occupancy.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Any instance of persistence or occupancy is an instance of causation.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: A Hume-world (a world with the same events but no causation) is incoherent because persistence itself requires causal ties.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Space-time position is constituted by causal relations; there is no non-relational way of understanding space-time position.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Events are not constituents of the external world but nominalizations of truths.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Facts and truths are distinct; truths hold in virtue of facts but are not identical with facts.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The distinction between explanatory generalizations of different disciplines lies in the concepts composing them, not in the underlying reality.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Wittgenstein's private language argument fails because the lone speaker of a private language is in no different epistemic position than the English speaker relying on memory.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Wittgenstein is a hard-core empiricist whose rejection of non-spatiotemporal entities like propositions and concepts follows from his empiricism.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Groups of zombies cannot collectively think; if every member of a group is a zombie, the group itself is without consciousness.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The Argument from Analogy for other minds is valid, contrary to Wittgenstein's objections.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Quine's thesis of the indeterminacy of translation is a desperate measure resulting from his empiricist commitments.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Translation between languages requires reliance on introspection-based knowledge of one's own mental states.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Thinking is not a psychological process in Wittgenstein's sense; propositional attitudes are not constituents of consciousness.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Mental imagery can be thought-constitutive, contrary to Wittgenstein's view, when understood in relation to its ratiocinative context.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The deployment of propositional attitudes in problem-solving is not constitutive of consciousness; consciousness is to thinking what a driver is to events under the car's hood.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Dispositionalism about mental states fails to model the data; mental states must be functioning as such even between bouts of generating consciousness.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Your belief that 1+1=2 is not identical with your dispositions to make certain judgments; it must be actively functioning to explain those dispositions.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Cognitive processes are realized by physiological processes, but psychological relations between physiological events cannot be understood in physiological terms.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Searle's view that mentality is identical with consciousness (MC) is false.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Propositional attitudes are not and cannot be constituents of consciousness; they are access-conscious at most.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: There is no a priori reason why propositional attitudes must be access-conscious.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Having meta-cognitive wherewithal is required to know what one's propositional attitudes are.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Russell's paradox does not establish that there are meaningful sentences that are neither true nor false; the relevant expressions are either false or sentence-schemata.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The expression "the property of being the set of all sets that aren't members of themselves" picks nothing out.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Tarski's and Russell's type-theory response to the semantic paradoxes is self-defeating.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Anaphoric expressions like "itself" function as bound variables and can be eliminable in extensional contexts.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Mach was wrong to hold that molecules are useful fictions; Einstein showed their existence is required to explain Brownian motion.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Newton's public claims to be purely empirical likely misrepresented his actual methodology for rhetorical reasons.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Every person is to some degree a schizoid-affective psychopath (SAP); differences between the mentally ill and healthy are matters of degree.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: To outgrow a belief or desire is not to cease to have it but for it to cease to be operational.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: People are organized, non-heaps of thought; thoughts are organized both vertically (hierarchically) and horizontally (laterally).
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The mind of the psychologically healthy person is stratified; psychopathology manifests when this stratification breaks down.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: A delusion is not a wrong belief but a belief that is right in the wrong way—the vehicle for a correct belief is an infantile and therefore false belief.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Pleasant affects alert us to the presence of certain structural features in music, art, and architecture; unpleasant affects alert us to their absence.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Phenomenologically consecutive mental images are likely to be ratiocinatively disjoint when considered in isolation from their underlying thought-processes.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The survival instinct is transferred from one's body to one's self as psychological development proceeds.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Initially the psychological superstructure exists to keep the body alive; eventually the body exists to keep the superstructure (self) alive.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: To value something is to see it as conducive to the consolidation or enlargement of one's agency.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Hedonistic pleasure is value-free pleasure; accomplishment-based pleasure is not hedonistic.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The object of desire is gratification, but intentions do not aim at gratification—they are judgments about what one must do to have a life embodying certain values.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Athletic ability is cognitive, not somatic in nature; being coordinated consists in one's mind being intelligent in governing one's body.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Moral nihilism and aesthetic nihilism are both false.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Moral nihilism and moral relativism are equivalent doctrines.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The Moore-Hume thesis that you cannot derive an "ought" from an "is" is false.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Moral truths hold in virtue of the same facts as descriptive truths; we can experience facts either as morally antiseptic or as moral truths.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Morally good acts invariably conduce to psychological, biological, or psychobiological welfare.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Emotions are beliefs, though they are not discursive beliefs; they are fundamentally egocentric in nature.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Hume's emotivism—the view that moral ideation is non-judicative sentiment—is wrong.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: There is no moral difference without a descriptive difference; the moral supervenes on the descriptive.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Moral intuitions track biological facts about welfare and harm.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: One's personhood depends on having values (norms, though not necessarily moral norms).
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Psychopathy is a failure to distinguish consequence of excessive rationalization; it involves a collapse of the distinction between self and other.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The acceptance of empiricism is incompatible with the very existence of any attempt to study anything.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: If empiricism is correct, nothing or next to nothing can be known about either the external or internal world.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Empiricism leaves the mental as nothing but a series of disjoint ex nihilo obtrusions into an otherwise integrated world.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Paraperception is phenomenology-mediated, real-time conception—the mode by which we perceive language, music, and art.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Categories like rock, tree, and chair are individuated by the counterfactual properties of their instances.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: To be a copper bar is to have certain causal properties (conductivity, durability, malleability).
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: One's survival instinct comes to be transferred from one's body to one's self.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Animals are reflex machines; rational agents are not.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Something that merely reacts is sub-agential; we may call such things "reagents."
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Only proposition-aware creatures can have intentions, because intentions are identical with decisions.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Deliberation is nothing other than arriving at a decision.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: When an intention is not preceded by deliberation, relevant deliberations have already occurred in the past.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Aristotle and Kant were right that agency is practical rationality and decisions are conclusions of non-theoretical thought-processes.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: There is no intrinsic difference between practical rationality (agency) and theoretical rationality.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Practical ratiocinations are more likely to be corrupted by prejudices and emotional factors than theoretical ones.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Phenomenology can represent a given thing only by resembling it.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Dogs are not irrational but sub-rational; irrationality is corrupted rationality and is thus a prerogative of rational beings.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Dogs' being sub-rational consists in their minds being incapable of personal thought, not thought tout court.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: For ideation to be transferred from the subpersonal to the personal domain, it must be phenomenologized.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The consciousness of music is the delight we take in acquiring knowledge; it is a form of our survival instinct applied to self-expansion.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Personal mentation becomes ratiocinative only when governed by meta-customs that convert customs into rules.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Repressed propositional attitudes are not intrinsically different from unrepressed ones; they differ only in location within the mind.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The conscious knowledge described by Chomsky and Marr is a conceptualization of subpersonal knowledge, not a duplicate.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Subpersonal processes are not strictly physiological or purely reflexive, contrary to Searle.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Understanding a sentence requires implicit knowledge of extremely complex semantic rules that operate too quickly for conscious, discursive application.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Linguistic competence involves subpersonal mastery of rules that are not access-conscious.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: There is no physiological characterization of linguistic competence; the rules are inherently semantic, not physiological.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Given obvious facts and principles, Chomsky's position that there is subpersonal mentation is de rigueur.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Hume's introspections were contaminated with non-empirical ideation.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Goodman's "grue" paradox shows that any body of data can provide support for incompatible hypotheses without constraints beyond empirical observation.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: There is a transcendental argument for causation: without causal ties, the genidentity of one's mind across time would be unintelligible.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The concept of complying with laws must be distinguished from the concept of being governed by them.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Everything complies with microphysical laws, but macro-level events are governed by macro-level laws implemented by varying microphysical mechanisms.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Smoking causes cancer even though less than 30% of smokers get lung cancer; causes need not operate with 100% efficacy.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Salmon's probabilistic modification of the DN model (DN*) is no better than DN itself.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Explanation can be valid even when the explanandum was less than 50% likely given the explanans.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The psychoanalyst's goal is to implant conscious duplicates of repressed beliefs, which are qualitatively identical with their repressed counterparts.
positions.title: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology
source: Empiricism and the Foundations of Psychology (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The expression "the intentions of the framers" denotes an idealization, not an empirical actuality, since the various framers had numerically and qualitatively different minds.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: When the word "they" is part of an expression meant to denote a collective attitude, it denotes an idealization rather than an empirical reality.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Originalism is exceedingly dubious if the term "intentions" is understood in purely individualistic, psychological terms.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: If originalism is to hold water, "the intentions of the framers" must refer to the intentions of some kind of collective entity.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The intentions of the framers cannot be understood strictly in terms of the psychologies of the framers taken individually.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The originalist position collapses into circularity: Constitutional interpretations are to be evaluated in terms of the intentions of the framers, but the intentions of the framers are to be understood in terms of the historical and social role of Constitutional interpretation.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: An idealization is by definition something that has no precise counterpart in the spatiotemporal world.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: When discussing what Jefferson would have said about constitutional issues, we are not making an empirical statement about what the actual Jefferson would have said, but rather an a priori proposition about what he should have said given his values.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The originalist is caught in a vicious circle: he must use a counterfactual to identify the values on which his conception of Constitutionality is based, but he cannot run the counterfactual until he has identified those values.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Jefferson's values are definable independently of any views of Constitutional interpretation, but the expression "Jefferson's values" itself denotes an idealization as opposed to a psychological reality.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Like many complex human beings, Jefferson had many values, and while some may have been fair-minded and egalitarian, others may have been retrograde and misogynist.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: When we say that Jefferson was not being "true to his values," the word "his" doesn't denote Thomas Jefferson the human being but rather an idealization of Jefferson purged of his 18th century prejudices.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Even the finest minds are marred by ugly biases.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: When we talk about what Jefferson would have said, we are talking about what some idealization of Jefferson would have said—one who didn't suffer from the cultural limitations and random neuroses that mar even the best of minds.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Once we start dealing with an idealization of Jefferson's psyche, as opposed to his actual psyche, we have replaced empirical propositions about psychology with a priori propositions about values.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: People with borderline personality disorder have two distinct, simultaneously operative psychological "operating systems"—one appropriately adult and sane, the other infantile in nature.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: One part of the BPD person's mind remained "stuck" or "fixated" at an early phase of life, usually very early childhood, while the other part advanced to maturity.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The fixation in BPD typically results from a trauma, usually sexual molestation.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: People with BPD oscillate between their adult and infantile psychological integrations.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The rapid oscillation between two modalities makes BPD individuals appear to be lying or "playing games," when in fact they are shifting between two distinct and incongruent worldviews.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: People with BPD are not psychopaths, despite sometimes acting in entitled, limitlessly self-serving narcissistic ways.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The difference between BPD and psychopathy: the psychopath is simply a phony who sometimes puts on a mask of sanity and decency, while the BPD person is genuinely two-sided, with neither side being a mask.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The preferred psychological defense mechanism in BPD is splitting rather than repression.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The BPD personality-structure is itself the result of splitting.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Repression involves having a single psychological architecture and shoving incompatible conceits into the unconscious, while splitting involves shifting between one's adult side and one's infantile side.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The normal person's psyche keeps its infantilism in the abyss of the unconscious, while the BPD person's psyche keeps infantilisms in a concurrently operative sphere of mental activity.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The essence of borderline personality disorder is that splitting takes the place of repression.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: When the psychopath acts non-psychopathically, it is because he is just acting; when the BPD person acts non-psychopathically, it is because she is being true to one side of her personality.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: When the BPD person acts psychopathically, it is because she is regressing and her infantile side is dominating, not because a mask has fallen.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Most people, including most psychologists, wrongly assume that group psychology is to be understood in terms of individual psychology.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The standard view that individuals have self-directed drives that are somehow diverted in group contexts cannot be reconciled with the incredibly high degree of conformism exhibited by people.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The deference given by people to convention, pre-existing custom, and fleeting but powerful fads cannot be explained in terms of fear of others alone.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Given how unintelligent, ignorant, and lacking in moral fiber most individuals are, yet how much intelligence, knowledge, and moral rigor is embodied in the behavior of collectives, it is hard to believe that collectives are merely the result of individuals pursuing their respective paths.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The collective is the primary thing, biologically speaking, and there is an illusion of individual choice and indeed of individualism per se.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Individual cells can be better understood if we recognize that they individually try to survive, flourish, and reproduce only to the extent that their doing so is necessary for the collectives formed thereby to survive, flourish, and reproduce.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Cell-individualism can be explained in terms of cell collectivism, but not so much vice versa.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Person-individualism can be explained in terms of person-collectivism, but not so much vice versa.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Bona fide individuals—geniuses who think for themselves—can be seen as limiting cases of collectives, as collectives of one, or as rogue actors with an unusual office within a collective.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The intense drives that individuals have to survive and experience gratification can be seen as necessary for, and therefore explainable in terms of, the collectives to which they belong.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: It is very hard to explain group behavior on the assumption that collectives represent averages of the behaviors of so many self-contained existentialist autonomous deciders.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Outside of contexts involving immediate threats to physical well-being or prospects of crude gratification, people give far too much weight to existing beliefs and mores to be accounted for by the hypothesis that collectives are indeed collectives of bona fide individuals.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Before the 1929 Stock Market Crash, the nation's financial system was highly unregulated, leading to improprieties that caused the Crash.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The Fed responded to the Stock Market Crash by tightening monetary policy, which exacerbated the situation and led to a full-blown economic depression.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Glass-Steagall prohibited commercial banks from functioning as investment banks, thereby preventing banks from using customer funds to finance their own ventures.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The repeal of Glass-Steagall in 1999 and the deregulation of the derivatives market in 2004 eliminated the firewalls put in place after the 1929 Crash.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The 2001 Dot Bomb Crash resulted from banks investing in substandard tech startups, and the 2008 Housing Crash resulted from banks investing in worthless derivatives contracts.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: A derivative is a contract whose value is a function of the value of some other security, and most derivatives are highly leveraged, highly risky, and difficult to value.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The price of a home tends to stay close to its value, but with securities there is very little short-term relationship between price and value—the two can diverge without limit instantaneously.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: When real-estate debts were bundled into financial securities like MBS's, the market could make them far more valuable than the underlying assets, and could do so in hours.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Once mortgages were securitized, investment banks had enormous incentives to drive up the number of home loans issued, regardless of the quality of those loans.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Bubbles can only last so long; eventually prices and values converge when demand fails to keep pace with supply.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The 2008 crisis was really two distinct crises: a housing crisis that decreased wealth and a banking crisis that diminished credit.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Economies depend on credit as much as they do on wealth; when businesses cannot borrow, they fail and the economy fails.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The Volcker Rule does not categorically prohibit banks from engaging in proprietary trading but requires that they match customer funds with their own funds when doing so.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The Volcker Rule prohibits banks from partnering with hedge funds and private equity firms to limit the extent to which large banks can endanger themselves and the market.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The Fed's role as a regulatory agency is ambiguous, since unlike every other financial regulatory agency, it is partly made up of the very entities it is supposed to regulate.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Given that three banks own over 75% of the Fed's shares, the Fed has at best a serious conflict of interest and at worst has no existence separate from the banks that own it.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Financial regulation has done a great deal of good and very little harm; financial deregulation has done a great deal of harm and very little good.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The Fed is in the practice of managing self-generated crises.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: We cannot expect banks to self-regulate, and we therefore cannot expect the Fed to self-regulate to the extent that it is owned by banks.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: In the United States, financial regulations have decentralized access to capital, keeping the economy going, while financial deregulation has hindered the economy by centralizing access to capital.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Even by the lights of a radical free marketeer, financial regulation has been a success because it has decentralized access to capital.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The Sorites paradox can be solved while holding on to all the laws of classical logic, contrary to most attempts that reject the law of bivalence.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Given any predicate that generates a Sorites-paradox, significant use of that predicate is actually elliptical for a relational statement.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: A significant token of "Bob is poor" means "Bob is poor compared to x" for some value of x.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Once a value of x is supplied, a definite cutoff line between having and not having the paradox-generating predicate is supplied, which neutralizes the inductive step.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Not all properties generate Sorites-type arguments; only properties that can be had to varying degrees generate them.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Binary properties—those that do not admit of comparatives—do not generate Sorites arguments.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Sorites-sentences are context-sensitive; the sentence "Bob is fast" makes a false statement if Bob is a cheetah with a top speed of 30 mph but a true statement if Bob is a turtle with a top speed of 2 mph.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Bob cannot be wealthy simpliciter; he must be wealthy compared to some standard or class.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Different tokens of "x is smart" involve comparison to different standards and thus express what are, strictly speaking, different propositions.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The term "tall" has the same meaning in "Bob is tall" as it has in "Bob is tall compared to Fred," and therefore "tall" is really elliptical for "tall compared to __."
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The meaning of a predicate is intimately bound up with the number of argument places it has; predicates of different degrees ipso facto have different meanings.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Being "absolutely non-wealthy" involves just as much reference to some standard of comparison as being "comparatively non-wealthy"—it means being non-wealthy relative to every standard.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Relativization to a standard is the essence of measurement; to measure is to compare with something taken as a standard.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Degree-statements, even highly generic ones like "x is acidic to some positive degree," always presuppose some benchmark.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Libet's experiment shows that the conscious events people believe to be their decisions are not the operative intentions—consciousness of intention follows rather than precedes the intention itself.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The readiness potential (RP) is obviously the proximal psychological cause of the action; therefore W (the conscious awareness) is not the intention but an awareness of the intention.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: By the time W happens, the RP is almost complete; if W is part of the intention, it is a subordinate part with at most a facilitating function.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: W is not a mere epiphenomenon because consciousness of one's intentions necessarily affects subsequent thought and behavior.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The evidence suggests W is not a part of the operative intention but rather an awareness of it; W happens very late in the process.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: If W were the decision responsible for the finger-movement, the graph would inflect upwards after W, not fail to inflect or inflect negatively.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: W cannot possibly be an intention, let alone a decision, since such things are by their nature decisive causes, and W is not decisive.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Libet's veto experiment provides evidence that W can at least put the brakes on an already-initiated action, even if it cannot initiate actions.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Libet's experiment concerns a subset of actions—real-time sensory-motor engagements with one's environment—but these are actions where consciousness is presumably most heavily involved.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Standing intentions (enduring psychological structures like "my intention to create a trading platform") demonstrably cannot be constituents of consciousness; one can be conscious of them, but they are not themselves constituents of consciousness.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Libet's experiment shows that we do not have "conscious free will"; our decisions, even those governing real-time responses to the environment, are not constituents of consciousness.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The .2 second delay between intention and awareness is very possibly not mere "tape-delay" but reflects W's function as an action-monitor rather than action-instigator.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Follow-up studies using fMRI show that relevant RPs began much earlier than Libet thought, sometimes preceding W by as much as 10 seconds.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: What we believe to be choices initiated in consciousness are not initiated in consciousness; they are initiated prior to any conscious awareness of them.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: It is unclear how Libet's results bear on "whether we have free will," but that is because the concept of free will is unclear, not because the experimental findings are unclear.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Philosophers have responded very conservatively and defensively to Libet's findings, doing more to obfuscate than to illuminate their significance.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Indeterminism threatens freedom; determinism seems to be a prerequisite for freedom.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Thoughts that totally lacked a causal basis in a person's pre-existing psychological structures would be mere intrusions, not exercises of agency.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: We only have freedom to the extent that the world, including ourselves, is deterministic.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Neuroscience does not merely show us how intentions and beliefs are physiologically implemented; it gives us counterintuitive information about them.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Libet's experimental data cannot be dismissed as margin-of-error; the subject established the time of W by coordinating it with a clock making one full rotation every 500 ms.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The distinction between proximal and distal intentions is valid, but distal intentions are extremely unlikely to be more consciousness-based than proximal intentions.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The actions studied in Libet's experiment are textbook cases of bona fide actions—non-automatic, non-reflexive, "freely made" actions—not quasi-reflexes.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The RP obviously causes the finger-movement; either that or it is temporally coincident with whatever the cause is—either way, W is not the operative intention.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The idea that we "just know" we have free will is straightforwardly question-begging; free will, if it exists, is a form of causation, and what causes what is always an open empirical question.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Compatibilism says you are free even though determined, but Libet's experiment shows not so much that you are determined as that you are on the sidelines—by the time you appear, the decision has been made.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: What is ominous about the Libet Experiment is not that it shows your choices to be predetermined but that it shows them not to be yours—they are yours to behold but not to make.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Libet's results are even more radical than Freud's: Freud regarded consciousness as a weak but genuine central authority; Libet shows it to be a mere spectator.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: All developed philosophical positions on human agency assume that the events we regard as our conscious choices are indeed our conscious choices; Libet's experiment shows this to be false.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: One purpose of consciousness may be to integrate information about the external world with information about the internal world.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: W, though not the cause of a particular intention, may implicitly green-light future similar intentions—telling the relevant part of the mind to carry on apace.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The "illusion of free choice"—experiencing decision-approval as decision-making—may be a small price to pay for gains in cognitive efficiency.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Wilfred Sellars destroyed "the myth of the given" and began a revolution in epistemology, but Berkeley made essentially the same points two hundred years earlier.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The foundationalist picture holds that no empirical background knowledge is needed to obtain knowledge of external reality—a given perception can do that on its own.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Sellars is not denying that external realities are made known to us through sense-perceptions, but attacking the idea that perception does so without the cooperation of preexisting empirical knowledge.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: There are two senses in which knowledge is not "given": its existence presupposes a background of empirical knowledge, and the sensory experience considered apart from background knowledge does not constitute knowledge of anything.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The epistemic significance of a quale is not fixed or "given"; it is a variable function of background empirical knowledge.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Berkeley's argument for anti-foundationalism is far more lucid and developed than Sellars' version.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: What ultimately endows visual sensations with information regarding distances is that different kinds of visual sensations have different kinds of kinesthetic and tactile concomitants.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Your visual experience by itself doesn't apprise you of the size or distance of an object; what does that is your visual experience plus various pieces of background knowledge.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: One's visual experience, taken by itself and stripped of contextualization, doesn't tell one whether one is seeing a tree-size pencil a half-mile off or a tiny pencil nearby.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: It is only "past experience" that teaches us that a particular appearance betokens a square object rather than a diamond-shaped one.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Berkeley has a definite answer to Molyneux's question: a man born blind given sight would not know from visual perceptions alone whether he was seeing a round, oval, or square object.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: When you hear somebody talk, you feel as though you directly grasp their meaning, but your auditory experience apprises you of significance only with the help of background knowledge.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Your visual experiences apprise you of the squareness of objects only in the sense in which your auditory perceptions apprise you of the meaning of a spoken sentence.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: When a person's face reddens in a particular way indicating shame, your visual experience by itself doesn't apprise you of the shame; background knowledge of how emotions are given bodily expression is at work.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: No sensory experience by itself tells one anything; any such experience must be put in the context of background knowledge to bear any determinate message about the external world.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: No sensory experience is foundational; empirical knowledge is obtained by putting current sensory experiences into the context of other sensory experiences and previously obtained empirical knowledge.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Sellars only gives one example of how sensory experiences need background knowledge (perception of color under non-standard conditions), while Berkeley's argument directly applies to virtually every perceptible property under standard conditions.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Sellars doesn't really explain how representational mental entities (perceptions) are forged out of non-representational mental entities (raw feels, qualia, sensations), but Berkeley goes to great lengths to show this.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Berkeley provides a non-metaphorical way of interpreting the metaphors that dominate contemporary anti-foundationalism (the "arc" or "web" of knowledge).
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Two problems arise on Berkeley-Sellars anti-foundationalism: how any belief manages to be justified, and how anything representational arises out of what is not representational.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The problem of how perceptions arise out of non-representational sensations is more fundamental than the problem of justification, because until we have perceptions, the problem of justification doesn't arise.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Standard conditions of perception are an idealization; no conditions are really standard—if we could set up a distortion-free situation, it would not be typical.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: What Sellars says about Bob's intake of color-facts in a tie-shop with unusual lighting is true of our intake of color-facts in general, and of shape-facts, size-facts, distance-facts, and so on.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: A ten-foot stop-sign looked at from a hundred feet away gives off the same appearance as a 1,000-foot stop-sign looked at from 10,000 feet away; one knows the approximate height only through background information.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: In the absence of background empirical knowledge, our sensory episodes become indeterminate to the point of meaninglessness—to the point of having no representational content.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The appearance given off by a receding object is not different from the appearance given off by a shrinking object; contextual information (knowing that people don't shrink) disambiguates the visual experience.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The concept of "free will" is so unclear that difficulties in applying experimental findings to it reflect problems with the concept, not with the findings.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Philosophers seldom ask "What does the Libet experiment show?" but instead ask "What does the experiment show about free will?"—the second question concedes too much validity to assumptions that the experiment calls into question.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Nothing is gained, and much is lost, by trying to press experimental results into the service of a pre-existing philosophical agenda.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The position that pre-W brain activity is merely an "urge" that W converts into a genuine intention is question-begging; W does not have a decisive role in causing the finger-movement.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: If W happened at the very beginning or peak of the readiness potential slope, it could be a decision; but W happens far too late to launch the process and too early to transform it.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The comparison between knowledge of free will and the cogito is misconceived; the cogito registers an analytic truth whose negation is incoherent, but the assertion of free will is not trivial.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Actions studied in Libet's experiment were set up to maximize the role of consciousness; the subject was wide awake, given explicit instructions, and could push the button whenever he wanted.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The more deliberation and "rational reflection" are involved in an action, the less likely the operative intention is to be fully conscious.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Philosophers have done little beyond playing skeptic with Libet's data, using standards of proof by which there would be no reason to believe that smoking causes cancer.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: We stand to learn about the mind by investigating models consistent with Libet's data; we do not stand to learn by insisting that Libet "must" be wrong.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Libet's experiment represents an extremely valuable but missed opportunity for philosophy.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: If it turns out that Libet's data is spurious, so be it; in the meantime, we should accept it and try to model it rather than reflexively dismissing it.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: The experiments in question do not merely support but establish that consciousness of intention follows rather than precedes the intention itself.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Consciousness may serve to approve or disapprove of decisions already made rather than to make decisions, with disapproval having a directive role for future intentions.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: There is no experimental data to support the claim that Libet's results only apply to "low-level" actions that fail to engage rationality or agency.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Any episode of taking in information about the external world—any perception of height, speed, shape, or color—involves the cooperation of contextual or background information.
positions.title: Free Will and Other Papers (Collected)

positions.text: Content-externalism is false, but semantic externalism is true.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Content-externalism and semantic externalism are distinct doctrines that have been conflated in contemporary philosophy of mind.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Two brain-states can be qualitatively identical while having different semantic contents, but this applies only to linguistic meaning, not to mental content.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Mental content is not constitutively dependent on facts about one's environment in the way content-externalists claim.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Semantic externalism correctly holds that the truth-conditions of utterances containing proper names depend on facts about the referents of those names.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The apparent problems with semantic externalism (e.g., Frege puzzles about identity statements) vanish when we properly distinguish between semantic and conceptual analysis.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: If content-externalism were true, then mental content would be without psychological significance, which contradicts the truism that beliefs, thoughts, and doubts are psychologically relevant.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The differences between Max and Twin-Max in thought experiments are like the differences in Poincaré's universe-expansion scenario—distinctions without genuine differences.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: One's knowing what an expression refers to always involves knowing some description that uniquely singles out its referent.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Ostensive definitions work through knowledge of uniquely individuating descriptions, not through direct acquaintance with referents.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: There are three ways a proper name can be added to one's lexicon: ostensive definition, descriptive definition, and "picking up" the use—but all three collapse into the descriptive.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Causal connections between subjects and referents are embedded in knowledge of uniquely individuating descriptions containing context-sensitive expressions.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Russell's theory of descriptions is demonstrably false as a semantic analysis, though the distinction between semantic and conceptual analysis is crucial.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Content-externalism is incompatible with the Cartesian certainty of self-knowledge regarding one's own mental states.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: If content-externalism were correct, one could mistake a non-thought for an actual thought, contradicting the immediacy of introspective access.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Burge's attempt to reconcile content-externalism with privileged access to one's own thoughts is fallacious because it restricts attention only to worlds where the relevant referent exists.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Functionalism cannot accommodate the causal potency of the mental because functional roles are not spatio-temporally located entities.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The Jackson-Pettit distinction between program causes and effective causes fails to save functionalism because program explanations of mental causation involve logical, not causal, guarantees.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: A brain-state's having a certain functional role cannot be causally potent because functional role descriptions denote abstract relationships, not states of affairs.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: A symbol-token is not purely morphological—the psychological underpinnings of symbol production are constitutively involved in the tokening.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: When a symbol-type is genuinely instantiated, a constituent of that instance includes aspects of the mind of the person producing the token.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Psychology cannot single-handedly transform an otherwise dead sound into a living symbol-token; both morphological and psychological aspects are necessary.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: CTM's appeal to Ramsey-sentences to characterize mental states still fails because it reduces to a form of functionalism with the same fatal problems.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: If content is causal role, then the same content cannot have different causal roles, but beliefs manifestly can have different causal roles in different contexts.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Conceptual atomism does not follow from conceptual-object atomism; even if concepts-as-objects lack structure, our mental representations of them may be structured.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Fodor's arguments against non-atomism fail because they conflate the information needed to lock onto a concept with information ascribed to that concept after lock-on.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Quine's arguments against the analytic-synthetic distinction are not cogent; analyticity can be understood without vicious circularity.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Inferential Role Semantics (IRA) is obviously false because inferences presuppose content—nothing can have content in virtue of its inferential role.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Non-atomism is not equivalent to inferential role semantics and does not presuppose it.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Transitional Role Analysis (TRA) does not have the defects Fodor attributes to non-atomism, but it is still a form of functionalism and therefore false.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The systematicity of thought does not require that thought be conducted in a language; it can be explained without positing mental sentences.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The productivity of language is an aspect of its systematicity and does not support the Language of Thought hypothesis.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The conceptual/non-conceptual content distinction is genuine; perceptual content is fundamentally different from propositional content.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: McDowell's arguments against the conceptual/non-conceptual distinction are fallacious; being able to refer to what one sees does not show that perception is structurally similar to language.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Sentences and perceptions have fundamentally different structures; demonstratives referring to specific perceptual features do not show that perception has sentential structure.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Peacocke's transitional role analysis of concepts fails because there is no non-arbitrary way to distinguish concept-constitutive transitions from merely habitual ones.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Any analysis of concepts in purely relational terms (concepts as nodes in networks of other concepts) cannot account for the fact that there are right and wrong ways to conceptualize experience.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Whether a certain kind of experience would confirm a certain empirical judgment is not ultimately an empirical matter but an analytic one.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Davidson's view that one must know a language to think is incompatible with the fact that linguistic competence must be mediated by cognitive processes.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Knowledge of language facilitates thought but cannot be constitutive of the ability to think, since thinking is a prerequisite to learning language.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Pre-linguistic creatures can have true and false mental contents; denying this is not a reasonable position.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Speaker's meaning presupposes conventional meaning; one cannot mean proposition P by producing noise N unless one believes N already means P.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Grice's theory of meaning fails because it is incompatible with the compositional character of sentence-meaning.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The differences between literal and communicated meaning can be explained pragmatically without adopting Russell's quantificational analysis of definite descriptions.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Propositions existed and had truth-values before there was thought; nobody grasped them before thought, but that is irrelevant to their existence.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind
