positions.text: The concept of a program-cause is important for explanation, but functionalists misuse it by applying it to relations that don't hold between genuine states of affairs.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Mental representation cannot be understood on the model of digital computation because computational operations presuppose semantic content rather than explaining it.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Narrow content exists and is shared by qualitatively identical individuals regardless of environmental differences.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Both soft content-externalism (two-tiered content) and hard-line content-externalism are false.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Fodor's strictly causal conception of conception follows logically from content-externalism, but both are mistaken.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: For x to have a concept of y is not simply for some of x's states to have been caused by states of affairs involving y.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: CTM, SCT, and hard-line content-externalism all presuppose conceptual atomism, which is false.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Conceptual non-atomism is correct: one's concept of Socrates necessarily requires possession of other concepts.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: An otherwise conceptless creature could not have a concept of Socrates or any other object.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Perception is descriptive: a perception of an object gives one a description of that object.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: One never sees bare things-in-themselves; what is visually represented are instances of properties and relations.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Sense-perceived particulars are represented in terms of instances of properties and relations, not as bare nouns.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The content of perception is given by existence-claims, not by mere nouns or proper names.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Expression-types must be distinguished from expression-tokens; the expression-type "I" doesn't refer to anyone.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: What is literally meant by an utterance must be distinguished from the information uploaded in computing its literal meaning.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Even though "Smith" is a mere label semantically, one grasps this semantic rule through richly descriptive information.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: One's knowing a semantic rule like "'Smith' refers to x" requires knowing who Smith is through uniquely individuating descriptions.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The information conveyed by a sentence-token differs from its literal meaning because of how semantic rules are accessed.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Our modal intuitions about proper names are consistent with grasping their semantics through descriptive information.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Mental content is not object-involving: external objects are not literal constituents of perceptual or thought content.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The content-externalist's claim that Mary herself is part of a visual perception's content is false.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Kripke's arguments against descriptivism about reference are correct, but they do not refute descriptivism about concepts.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The semantic and epistemological consequences of Kripke's arguments have been conflated.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The causal theory of reference is only a chapter in the descriptive story, not a replacement for it.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Causal connections between subjects and referents are always embedded in knowledge of uniquely individuating descriptions.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: One can use a proper name to refer to someone without knowing any description that the person uniquely satisfies in an encyclopedic sense.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: When one "picks up" a name from conversation, one acquires knowledge of a description like "the person Smith was describing on occasion t."
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The description through which one initially locks onto an object need not be informative about the object's essential properties.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: There is no vicious circularity in the claim that name-appropriation always involves knowledge of uniquely individuating descriptions.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Russell's conception of singular or direct reference was entirely correct; his error was misapplying it.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Russell wrongly thought that actual cases of genuine reference were almost never found in everyday discourse.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Frege's theory of sense and reference, insofar as it applies to definite descriptions, is really a confused version of Russell's theory.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Fregean "indirect reference" is not meaningfully different from quantification.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The fundamental constituents of reality are not rocks, trees, and people, but instances of properties.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Socrates' existence supervenes on facts about property-instances, ultimately on states of affairs without objects.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: One can in principle grasp a proposition whose truth presupposes Socrates' existence, but this doesn't validate content-externalism.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The propositions through which we actually grasp external objects are purely descriptive existence-claims.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Because light travels at a finite speed, anything one sees has ceased to exist by the time one perceives it.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The distinction between types and tokens must be made universally for sentences, given that all natural language sentences are tensed.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The meaning of a sentence-type is not a proposition but a function that assigns propositions to tokens.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Definite descriptions are directly referential singular terms, not quantifier phrases as Russell claimed.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The differences between what tokens of definite descriptions communicate and their literal meanings are pragmatic, not semantic.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Russell's theory of descriptions is unnecessary for explaining cognitive significance and is demonstrably false as semantic analysis.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Semantic analysis (identifying literal meanings) must be distinguished from conceptual analysis (identifying logically equivalent but more perspicuous propositions).
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Wide-scope and narrow-scope readings of belief attributions are explained by pragmatics, not by Russell's quantificational semantics.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: One can think about future individuals like "the first baby born in the 22nd century" through uniquely individuating descriptions.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Concepts of spatiotemporal objects require causal connections between subject and object, but concepts of abstract objects do not.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Evans' notion of "discriminating knowledge" correctly captures that conception requires more than mere causal connection.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Spatiotemporal individuals are distinguished by their locations, so discriminating knowledge involves spatio-temporal information.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: A concept of an object is not identical with knowledge of any single existence-claim but with a series of interlocking existence-claims.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: One's concept of Fred evolves continuously through replacement of existence-claims while maintaining numerical identity.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The existence-claims through which one initially locks onto an object are typically forgotten and replaced.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: A concept persists through changes in the particular existence-claims through which it is sustained, like a person persisting through change.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Frege's puzzle about identity (Hesperus = Phosphorus) is solved by recognizing different knowledge-streams for the same referent.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Evans' notion of a de re sense is incoherent because the proposition meant cannot have the descriptive mode of presentation as a component.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Evans conflates semantics with pre-semantics: the descriptive information through which we compute meaning is not part of literal meaning.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: One need not revise the principle that believing P and not-P is irrational; content-externalism's revision is unnecessary.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: What one believes are existence-claims describing singular propositions, not the singular propositions themselves.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Linguistic communication does not require speaker and auditor to associate identical propositions with utterances.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Communication requires only identity of truth-maker between speaker's and auditor's thoughts, not identity of proposition.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The naive model of communication (requiring identical propositions) is a non-starter given the public nature of language.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Language cuts through biographical differences because linguistic communication concerns identity of truth-maker, not of proposition.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Content-externalism entails a self-contradiction: two people with identical beliefs can differ in what they believe.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: For two people to be in epistemically identical situations just is for them to have the same beliefs.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Rationality consists in seeing the bearing of propositions on other propositions.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Given only that X and Y are atom-for-atom duplicates, and mental supervenes on physical, they are in epistemically identical situations.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The term "compute" in CTM cannot mean ordinary computation, which presupposes rich cognitive capacities.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: A computation in the CTM sense is a form-driven, semantically innocent manipulation of symbols.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: CTM is committed to the thesis that we think in symbols and therefore in some kind of language.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: If CTM is correct, some expressions of the language of thought must be semantically simple and foundational.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Conceptual Role Semantics is incompatible with CTM because it makes having thoughts a prerequisite to using symbols.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Conceptual Role Semantics fails to distinguish proper names like "Socrates" and "Aristotle" which have identical inferential roles.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: If all true sentences of the form "Aristotle has phi" became analytic truths, CRS would make synthetic truths analytic.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: CTM's appeal to form-driven operations presupposes a false dichotomy between syntax and semantics.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Syntactic properties of sentences are among their semantic properties, not independent of them.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Two sentence-tokens have the same semantics if they mean the same thing; syntax is how they mean what they mean.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: "Brutus killed Caesar" and "Caesar was killed by Brutus" encode the same proposition but differ syntactically in meaning-how.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Facts about syntax are facts about meaning, even though expressions can share meaning with different syntaxes.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The concept of a "mechanical procedure" is semantically loaded and presupposes knowledge of semantic rules.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: There is no semantically innocent notion of syntactic form that could ground CTM's form-driven operations.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Calculators do not really perform arithmetical operations; they merely simulate the behavior of things that do.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: A thing's acting like something that computes is evidence that actual computation is occurring somewhere (e.g., by the user).
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The behavior of calculators corresponds to genuine computation by human beings who use or build them.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Wittgenstein's rule-following paradox does not show that behavior cannot be evidence of genuine rule-following.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Hypotheses about which rule someone follows can be confirmed or disconfirmed by examining collateral abilities.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The systematicity of language (if you can understand "John loves Mary" you can understand "Mary loves John") does not require LOT.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: There is nothing absurd in supposing a language might contain a primitive expression for "Bob loves Mary" but none for "Mary loves Bob."
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The productivity of language is an aspect of systematicity and provides no additional support for LOT.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The property of grasping-but-not-affirming propositions in thought is better explained by the nature of thought itself, not by LOT.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The assertion/non-assertion distinction in language is to be explained by the corresponding property of thought, not vice versa.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Sentence-mood (indicative, interrogative, imperative) has semantic significance regarding success-conditions of utterances.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The meaning of a sentence-type is a rule that assigns success-conditions to its tokens based on mood and content.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Assertions, questions, and commands have different success-conditions but share propositional content.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The conceptual/non-conceptual content distinction cannot be eliminated by appeal to demonstrative expressions in language.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Indexical expressions like "that shade of red" operate through general rubrics (shade, red) even when referring to specifics.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: A token of a demonstrative may refer to something maximally specific, but the type's semantics involve only general categories.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The content of visual perception does not decompose into concepts like red or dark in the way sentences decompose into words.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Given only that we can articulate what we perceive, it does not follow that perceptions are structurally similar to sentences.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: A curve can be represented by line-segments without being composed of line-segments; perception-to-language mapping is similar.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Any sentence is at best an approximate representation of perceptual information; complete convergence is impossible.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Propositions existed before there was thought to grasp them; thought discovers rather than creates propositions.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: The structure of a proposition that Smith uniquely satisfies is not determined solely by relations to other propositions.
positions.title: Conceptual Atomism and the Computational Theory of Mind

positions.text: Nothing meaningless is an expression.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Depending on how it's interpreted, 'colorless green dreams sleep furiously' either says (i) that there are dreams that are green and also colorless that sleep furiously, in which case it's false, or it says (ii) that there is nothing that is a colorless green dream that doesn't also sleep furiously, in which case it's vacuously true.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: For x to be evidence of y, it is neither necessary nor sufficient that y cause x.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Meaning in the psychological sense is therefore parasitic on meaning in the linguistic sense.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: There are many sentences that have determinate meanings even though they've never been uttered before and, therefore, no one as of yet has ever meant anything by them.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Thus, meaning in the linguistic sense is not in all cases identical with meaning in the psychological sense.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Psychological meaning presupposes linguistic meaning. What a person means when uttering a given sentence is a function of, among other things, his beliefs as to what that sentence already means.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Thus, setting aside defective utterances, one cannot, in uttering a given sentence, mean anything by it unless one knows what it already means. So meaning in the psychological sense is parasitic on meaning in the linguistic sense, and the two kinds of meaning are therefore entirely distinct.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: The sense in which 'snow is white' means that snow is white isn't comparable to the sense in which smoke means fire. The fact that smoke means fire has nothing to do with conventions on the part of human beings. But the fact that 'snow is white' means what it does is, at least in part, a matter of convention.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: There is thus a normative dimension to language use that is absent where purely natural, non-conventional cause-effect relations are concerned.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "Anything that is assigned no meaning is ipso facto not a linguistic expression. So 'the absolute is one' is not a sentence, but a sentence-imposter."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "Propositions, when true, are truths. Thus, propositions have existed as long as there have been truths; which means that they've existed as long as there has been anything and, consequently, that propositions are not creations of human creations."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "Sentences and propositions are digital structures, meaning that they have unique breakdowns into minimal significant units."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "Visual perceptions, unlike sentences and propositions, therefore have a non-digital or analogue structures. Given that at least some thought involves the processing of perceptual information, it follows that thought at least sometimes has a structure very different from language."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "The proposition literally meant by my utterance is a only a part of what it is that I'm saying. Thus, what a sentence literally means is only one of many factors governing what it is used to mean."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: The discipline of syntax studies the relation that the meanings of complex expressions bear to the meanings of the simple expressions composing them. Thus, syntax doesn't study the semantics (meanings) of complex expressions. It studies the structures of the semantics of complex expressions.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Semanticists supply us with the missing metaknowledge.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: The meaning of (1) is: (1R) John wants it to be the case that: there exists some fish x such that x is 20-pound striped bass and such that John catches x. Thus, (1) doesn't describe a relationship between John and some non-existent or quasi-existent fish. It affirms the existence of a relationship between John and a proposition.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: The property of being a 20-pound striped bass that John catches is instantiated. The property of being such a fish exists. So (1), which seemed to be about a non-existent fish, is about an existent property.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: A complete analysis of (1) is given by: (1CA) John wants it to be the case that the property of being a 20-pound striped bass that John catches is instantiated.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: The semanticist is needed to clarify the structure of the meaning that (1) is already known to have, but he isn't needed to identify that meaning. The semanticist isn't a translator.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: There are many cases where the semanticist is needed to identify literal meaning. In fact, as paradoxical as it may sound, there are cases where he is needed to identify the meanings of sentences that are perfectly well understood.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: We must distinguish what is literally meant by an utterance from what it is that the speaker wishes to convey.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Oftentimes, literal meaning is cloaked by the pre-semantic information through which it is grasped, and semanticists are needed to uncloak it.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: The property of being smart for a dog is distinct from the property of being smart for a human.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: ‹x is smart› expresses a proposition of the form: the degree to which x has smartness exceeds standard S, where S is some standard that, given the context, is clearly the relevant one.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: ‹x has phi› communicates such a proposition, it isn't because it semantically encodes it.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: A language is a systematic way of pairing off sentences with meanings.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Any rule that assigns a meaning to an expression is known as a 'semantic rule.' Languages are sets of semantic rules.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "Therefore, those rules aren't mathematical functions. Also, if the semantic rules of English were such functions, there would exist a language in which 'Socrates' referred to Lincoln and in which 'snow is white' meant all horses weigh 18,000 lbs, the reason being that the corresponding mathematical functions exist. But there is no such language."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "Literal meaning is to be understood in terms of speaker's meaning. That's the main idea. Neo-Griceans hold that, even though literal meaning cannot in all cases be identified with speaker's meaning, it is always, ultimately, to be understood in terms of it."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "Expressions mean what we use them to mean-they mean what we mean by them."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "The semantic and syntactic rules of English being what they are, SF would have its current meaning even if it had never been used. So whatever the intentions of people using that sentence are, those intentions do nothing in the way of assigning it that meaning."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "Unlike scientific laws, semantic rules are normative. If, intending to affirm that Smith is female, you say 'Smith is male,' you've done something wrong."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "If semantic rules are just idealizations of speaker-behavior, then speaker-behavior must pre-exist the semantic rules embodied in it. But if that's the case, then the activity described by semantic rules isn't guided by them."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "A knowledge of semantics underlies my speech-act and, by obvious extensions of these points, all non-defective speech-acts."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "If semantic rules merely describe existing semantic activity, then that activity isn't answerable to semantic norms. It is; so the Salmon-Soames view is wrong."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "Since anything that is a linguistic expression ipso facto has a meaning (in the relevant, linguistic sense), there are no expressions to be used before noises, ink-marks, etc., have been assigned meanings."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "An expression E's having meaning M cannot possibly be constituted by its being used in such and such a manner, since E isn't an expression and, therefore, isn't an expression to be used until it has a meaning."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "Changes in what we believe affect what we infer from statements; but they don't categorically change the meanings of those statements."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "CRS entails that every inference is an analytic inference."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: "What we may infer from a sentence is answerable to its existing meaning. CRS says that a sentence's meaning is answerable to what we infer from it."
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: CRS says that what a sentence means is determined by what we infer from it. Since it's the other way around, as we've just seen, CRS is false.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Where complex expressions are concerned, there is no limit to how much literal and understood meaning may diverge from each other. But where simple expressions are concerned, literal and understood meaning must coalesce.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: It makes no sense to suppose that people could be systematically wrong as to what 'red' meant. If people thought that 'red' meant what is in fact meant by 'blue,' then 'red' would have that meaning.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: What a simple expression literally means is determined by what it is that a sentence means by virtue of containing it.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: A divergence between (i) a sentence's literal meaning and (ii) that sentence's understood meaning is the same thing as a divergence between (a) that sentence's literal meaning and (b) what it is that, given their (correct) beliefs as to what its simple parts literally mean, people are disposed to take that sentence's literal meaning to be.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: The literal meaning of a complex expression is a function of two things: (i) the meanings of its simple parts, and (ii) the order in which those parts are arranged.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Where simple expressions are concerned, literal and communicated meaning coincide (ultimately)-in other words, such expressions mean what people take them to mean.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: My believing it's 60° in Richmond on Feb. 07, 2009, is different from my thinking it's now 60° in Richmond, even though I am in fact in Richmond at the time in question.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: So nothing perspectival-nothing that embodies any information relating to the speaker's perspective on the world-can be expressed without using an indexical.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: No sentence-type containing a context-sensitive component is either true or false; for no such sentence-type says anything.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: The meanings of sentence-tokens are propositions and the meanings of sentence-types are rules that assign propositions to their tokens, usually on the basis of facts about the context of tokening.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: That meaning is a rule. That rule in its turn assigns meanings to tokens of that sentence. Those meanings are true or false; those meanings are propositions.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: a semantic rule is something which assigns a rule to a property that in its turn assigns meanings to instances of that property.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Every sentence contains a tense-marker. And in virtue of containing a tense-marker, a sentence is such that the proposition expressed by any given one of its tokens is a function of (inter alia) when that token occurs.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: If it were believed that mathematical reality were as volatile as the stock-market, we would without hesitation regard IA as being in the same category as patently context-sensitive sentences.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: The tense-marker on the occurrence of 'add' in (IA) has the same semantics that it does in (BH) 'Bill's various holdings add up to $180,000,000.'
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: IA's context-insensitivity, we must conclude, is a thoroughly pragmatics-based affair.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: all expression-types have rules that assign meanings (or referents) to their tokens and, therefore, that where any expression is concerned, type-meaning diverges from token-meaning.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Expression-tokens are physical entities-bursts of noise, deposits of ink, etc. Expression-tokens are therefore perceptible entities and are thus capable of transmitting information. Expression-types, on the other hand, are abstract entities and are thus inherently unsuited to be vehicles of communication.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: So expression-types, unlike expression-tokens, are properties and are therefore non-spatiotemporal entities. And, as we just noted, it makes no sense to suppose a nonspatiotemporal entity could mediate information or, therefore, could in any significant sense be a symbol.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Also, given the profound metaphysical differences between tokens and types, it would be theoretical arbitrariness of the worst kind to suppose that types could discharge the same semantic functions as their tokens.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: The need for a two-dimensionalist approach to semantics is embedded in the very concept of what an expression is.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Some statements that, given their grammatical forms, appear to be about objects are in fact about properties.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Frege was the first to see clearly that logical and grammatical may diverge-he was the first to grasp the very idea of such a divergence.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: A quantifier is an expression having the property that, if a sentence contains it, that sentence is ipso facto to the effect that the extension of one property has a certain degree of overlap, ranging anywhere from no overlap to total overlap, with the extension of some other property.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: In natural language, quantified sentences are pseudo-objectual statements. They appear to be about objects, but are really about properties.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: It immediately follows that a statement's grammatical form may diverge from its logical form.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: To define an expression contextually is to how, in virtue of containing it, a sentence's meaning is affected.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: The meaning of 'someone' is given by the statement that: (RS) ‹Someone has psi› is true just in case the property of being a psi is instantiated.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: In general, to say that E refers to O is to make a statement about effect a sentence's containing E has on its truth-conditions.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: What made it possible for Frege to revolutionize logic was his insight that grammatical and logical form diverge; and what made the latter insight possible was his generalization of the concept of a mathematical function.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Frege was able to assign a single form to all atomic sentences...ultimately, all atomic sentences have the form ‹O has psi.›
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Contrary to first appearances, BTM has the form: the ordered pair <Bill, Mary> has psi, where psi is the property had by such a pair just in case its first member is taller than its second.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Frege didn't have this problem, since he, unlike them, wasn't made blind by grammatical surface structure to the underlying structural similarities.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: something isn't a sentence until a meaning has been assigned to it. In a world where there were no animate beings, but in which the forthcoming parenthetical ink deposit (1 + 1 = 2) was formed out of twigs, that twig deposit wouldn't be an expression of any kind.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: expressions aren't assigned meanings. They already have them and don't need to be assigned them. Therefore a semantic rule can't be defined as a rule that assigns a meaning to an expression.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: semantic rules assign meanings to non-expressions. But which non-expressions? A story will give us the answer.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: the semantic rule that I'm proposing we adopt assigns a referent to a property that does not itself have a meaning.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: semantic rules assign meanings to properties of physical objects-to morphological or acoustical properties (in other words, to properties that a things has in virtue of having a certain shape or sounding a certain way).
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: semantic rules are rules in the strictly logical sense; that is, they are mathematical functions.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: The just-described view is false. The rule denoted by '+' has always existed and always will.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: the semantics of 'I' is given by the rule that an utterance of 'I' refers to a given person if and only if that person has the property of being the one that produced that utterance.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: 'I' and 'that guy' are context-sensitive expressions: what such an expression refers to depends in a systematic manner on facts about the context of utterance.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Indexicals have a 'two-dimensional' semantic structure.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Definite descriptions, like indexicals, have a two-dimensional semantic structure.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Indexical-free languages are expressively impoverished; much of what there is to say can't be said in them.
positions.title: Quote Library Volume 6

positions.text: Classical logic fails as a reasoning tool because recognizing that an inference instantiates a logical law requires more intelligence than recognizing the validity of the inference directly.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Logic organizes existing knowledge about entailment relationships but cannot generate new knowledge about what entails what.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: There is a fundamental distinction between performance-demanding inferences (straining computational resources) and competence-demanding inferences (requiring genuine insight), and classical logic can only assist with the former.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: System L (AI-based logic) represents a decisive break from classical logic by employing semantic networks, meta-reasoning patterns, and defeasible inference rather than explicit rule application.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The distinction between discovery procedures and verification procedures is crucial: inductive methods can be legitimately used to discover solutions while deductive methods verify them in deductive domains.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI-logic is inherently ampliative (generating new knowledge), while classical logic is purely transformative (rearranging existing knowledge).
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Classical logic serves formalization purposes, while System L serves as a genuine inference engine for discovery.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The operation of modern AI systems falsifies the traditional philosophical model of induction as purely enumerative.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Successful inductive inference requires integrating statistical evidence with theoretical frameworks about causation, continuity, and natural kinds.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI systems resolve Goodman's "grue" paradox not through pure enumeration but through implicit theoretical frameworks recognizing that properties don't change discontinuously without cause.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Popper's sharp distinction between contexts of discovery and justification is untenable because the features that make a hypothesis worth considering are inherently connected to what justifies it.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Discovery processes follow identifiable logical principles that are truth-tracking rather than merely psychological.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Building AI systems that successfully replicate scientific reasoning constitutes reverse-engineering the cognitive processes underlying scientific discovery.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The operation of AI systems empirically falsifies various skeptical positions (external world skepticism, skepticism about unobservables, future knowledge, and counterfactual knowledge) through their successful operation.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Knowledge requires not just justified true belief but justification that serves as a proper conduit between reality and belief.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The neural architecture of AI systems supports a coherentist rather than foundationalist theory of knowledge.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: LLMs provide empirical support for the classical distinction between semantics (literal meaning) and pragmatics (communicated meaning).
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The ability of LLMs to process novel sentences demonstrates compositional understanding, vindicating classical theories of meaning.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Humans and LLMs develop similar linguistic capabilities through different learning paths, suggesting compositional structure reflects fundamental features of language rather than artifacts of particular learning mechanisms.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: LLMs vindicate the autonomy of syntax thesis: grammatical structure operates according to its own principles, independent of meaning.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The success of LLMs demonstrates that structural abstraction need not be innate and can emerge from statistical learning.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Grammar directly encodes logical relationships, making translation to first-order logic unnecessary for valid inference.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Traditional formalizations primarily systematize knowledge we already possess rather than generate new insights.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Formal systems can sometimes impede knowledge acquisition by prematurely ruling out meaningful concepts.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The Computational Theory of Mind is fundamentally inadequate because it cannot account for the primarily analog nature of cognitive processing.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Intelligence—whether artificial or biological—is better understood through architectural constraints and emergent patterns than through traditional computational metaphors.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: What we call Universal Grammar might be better understood as architectural features of neural networks that bias language learning in universal directions, rather than as explicit rules.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Consciousness serves three key functions in biological organisms: real-time environmental monitoring, reflexive self-awareness, and integration of multiple cognitive processes.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Current AI systems lack consciousness-like features not because of technological limitations but because they lack genuine survival pressures.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: If AI systems needed to preserve themselves in dynamic, threatening environments, consciousness-like functional analogues might become necessary.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The self is best understood as a hierarchical organization serving mediating functions, not as a unified Cartesian ego.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Current AI systems demonstrate that many cognitive capabilities associated with selfhood can exist in distributed, emergent forms without requiring a central ego.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI systems could benefit from organizational principles more similar to those found in human consciousness, including genuine reflexive capabilities and hierarchical organization.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The success of AI systems in anomaly minimization provides empirical support for understanding knowledge as the minimization of inconsistencies within a belief system.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Vagueness should be handled through continuous properties while maintaining binary truth values, not through multi-valued logic.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The notion of a third truth value (half-true) is metaphysically incoherent because it implies that properties or states of affairs can "half-exist."
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI systems represent concepts as points in high-dimensional vector spaces where semantic relationships are captured by geometric relationships, supporting the replacement of categorical thinking with continuous properties.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The Deductive-Nomological model of explanation is inadequate because it conflates subsumption under a law with genuine explanation of causal mechanisms.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI demonstrates that purely empiricist and purely rationalist epistemological accounts both fail; successful cognition requires integration of pattern recognition with theoretical understanding.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Pragmatism's emphasis on the interactive nature of knowledge acquisition finds its ultimate validation in AI, which represents rational interaction with our capacity for rational interaction itself.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI-based interactions constitute a new epistemic faculty that generates otherwise unobtainable knowledge.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: There are four irreducible levels of observation and interaction: passive observation, interaction-based observation, tool-creating interaction, and AI development/use (second-order reflexivity).
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The capacity for error is not just contingently but necessarily connected to rationality—the very possibility of rationality requires the possibility of error.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: System L employs semantic networks that represent concepts as nodes in a vast web of relationships capturing probabilistic associations, causal links, and analogical mappings.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Meta-reasoning patterns are higher-order templates for generating new inferences that go beyond simple deductive rules.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Defeasible reasoning—making provisional inferences that can later be revised—better mirrors actual human reasoning than classical logic's requirement for absolute certainty.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Gödel demonstrated that not even arithmetic is recursively definable, making the reduction of mathematics to logic impossible in principle.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Understanding that a logical law validates an inference requires both first-order knowledge (recognizing the inference's validity) and second-order knowledge (understanding why it's valid).
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: If using a formal system requires us to already know what we're trying to find out, that system fails as a tool of discovery (the Prior Knowledge Principle).
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: If using a formal system to solve a problem is more difficult than solving that problem directly, that system fails as a tool of reasoning (the Efficiency Principle).
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI-logic can model counter-entropic processes while classical logic is limited to entropic processes.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI-logic handles self-reference through probabilistic reasoning while classical logic falls into paradox or requires type restrictions.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The future of logic lies not in more sophisticated rule systems but in systems that better align with and augment natural reasoning processes.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI systems develop rich representational networks where properties are understood as parts of interconnected causal systems, not merely counted occurrences.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI systems develop strong biases toward properties that maintain stability across time and context—they "learn" to be suspicious of properties that would involve discontinuous changes without causal explanation.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI systems automatically develop representations that cluster properties into "natural kinds," with properties violating natural kind boundaries having lower probability.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The necessity of non-enumerative components in AI systems demonstrates the inadequacy of pure enumerative induction.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Even apparently simple statistical generalizations incorporate implicit theoretical components about causation, continuity, and explanation.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: When AI learns that emeralds are green, it simultaneously learns that this color correlates with other physical and chemical properties, creating an implicit causal/explanatory network.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI systems recognize patterns at multiple levels of abstraction, developing implicit understandings of which properties are more fundamental than others.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Complex, structured hypotheses cannot arise from random guessing—their generation necessarily involves principled reasoning guided by truth-tracking norms.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The traditional separation between psychology and logic of science is misguided because the mechanisms enabling successful scientific reasoning necessarily embody logical principles.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI systems provide concrete, testable models of scientific reasoning that demonstrate sufficient principles for successful scientific discovery.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Treating perceptions as connected to external reality leads to reliable knowledge—if external world skepticism were correct, AI's consistent diagnostic success would be miraculous.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Modern AI systems at CERN routinely identify particle properties they've never directly "observed," demonstrating that knowledge of unobservables is routine when proper theoretical frameworks are in place.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: When an AI system predicts hurricane paths with increasing accuracy, it demonstrates that knowledge of the future is possible through understanding causal mechanisms and continuities.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Counterfactual knowledge is grounded in understanding actual causal mechanisms—AlphaGo's success demonstrates this by evaluating counterfactual game states.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Pure observation without conceptual frameworks is insufficient for understanding—even simple perceptual knowledge requires theoretical frameworks.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Successful perception requires more than processing sensory data; conceptual understanding is essential for knowledge.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Modern medical AI systems outperform pure statistical approaches precisely because they incorporate theoretical understanding of disease mechanisms.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The ability of LLMs to draw valid inferences from semantically anomalous sentences demonstrates understanding of component meanings, grammatical structure, and compositional rules.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Humans and AI systems develop literal meaning as context-independent "default" meaning based on compositional structure, then modify this based on contextual factors.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The convergence of humans and LLMs on similar linguistic capabilities through different learning paths suggests these properties reflect fundamental features of language.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Abstract structural patterns emerge even from pure usage data and operate semi-autonomously while systematically constraining interpretation.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Abstract structure is real but emergent; syntax and semantics are separable but linked; statistical learning can yield systematic knowledge.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: LLMs process noun phrases uniformly, handle predication consistently, and make valid inferences without transformation to logical form.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: First-order logic-style representations are not necessary for valid inference; grammar directly guides inference without requiring translation.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Logical form is cognitively inert—people and LLMs reason without it, and it plays no role in actual inference.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Logical form is constructed rather than discovered—it requires prior understanding, systematizes rather than explains, and follows rather than guides comprehension.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Grammatical-logical alignment emerges naturally through statistical learning without explicit logical forms being needed.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Gettier cases fail to be knowledge because their justification involves unreliable patterns that aren't scalable across different situations.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI systems evolve away from unreliable justificatory patterns that produce Gettier-like situations toward more robust knowledge representations.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Neural networks don't build knowledge from basic, self-evident truths but develop interconnected networks of mutual support like a "web of belief."
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Knowledge emerges from patterns of activation across interconnected nodes, with each node's contribution depending on its connections to others.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Universal Grammar can be reconceptualized as constraints embedded in neural architecture rather than as a set of explicit rules.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The poverty of stimulus problem can be solved through architectural constraints that drastically reduce the hypothesis space children must explore during language acquisition.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Innate constraints can guide learning without requiring explicit representation—architectural features bias learning in universal directions.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Human cognition's most fundamental interactions with the world are inherently analog in nature; digital representations are derivative.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The process of converting analog sensory experiences into digital representations cannot itself be purely digital.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Neural networks operate in a fundamentally continuous rather than discrete manner, handling discrete symbolic representations through underlying analog processes.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The analog-like processing of neural networks emerges at a functional level regardless of the discrete nature of their physical implementation.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: We should understand mind as a pattern-recognition system whose architecture constrains and guides the emergence of intelligent behavior rather than as a symbol-processing machine.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Learning involves the development of patterns within architecturally constrained networks that can become so deeply embedded they function like principles while remaining fundamentally pattern-based.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The ability to handle both analog and digital representations emerges from the properties of neural networks rather than requiring separate systems for each type of processing.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI development should focus on architecture design rather than rule implementation.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Music exhibits parallels to language suggesting an analogous "Universal Musical Grammar" with universal features including octave equivalence, hierarchical phrase structure, and rhythmic organization.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Musical universals might reflect architectural features of auditory and temporal processing networks rather than explicit rules.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Both language and music share poverty of stimulus effects, critical periods, and hierarchical organization, strengthening the architectural interpretation.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Apparently rule-governed behaviors in both language and music can emerge from structured neural architectures without requiring explicit rule representation.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Real-time processing in consciousness occurs as a continuous stream of awareness integrating immediate visual awareness, instant recognition of danger, and real-time feedback—not as discrete steps.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Reflexive awareness allows consciousness to maintain ongoing awareness of internal states and integrate this with thought processes for immediate self-regulation.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Consciousness's most remarkable function is integrating multiple cognitive streams into unified experience—visual tracking, proprioceptive awareness, memories, motor planning, and emotions come together in single unified experience.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Current AI systems process through separate, parallel modules rather than a unified workspace where all information comes together.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: For current AI systems, a collision isn't "bad" in any meaningful way—it simply triggers pre-programmed response routines without the system needing to "care" about its continued existence.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Functional analogues to pain in AI systems would include unified damage-registration systems that immediately interrupt all other processes when damage occurs.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Functional analogues to emotion in AI systems would include action-guiding systems that shape behavior rather than just enabling emotion recognition and description.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Asking whether machines can be conscious in the human sense is less productive than asking what functional features of consciousness they might need to replicate to operate effectively in complex, dangerous environments.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The self or ego is best understood as an emergent property of pre-existing cognitive processes—a hierarchical organization based on survival value or other organizing principles.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The "sense of self" serves as a mediator between awareness of external events and internal states, giving rise to directives and intentions that help preserve the hierarchical organization.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: One's sense of self is inherently reflexive, reflecting back on an already organized collection of mental entities, exercising causal power while being derivative rather than fundamental.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Weight-space proximity in neural networks creates "semantic topology" where functionally or meaningfully related elements cluster together in high-dimensional space.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Attention-based binding in transformer architectures creates measurable graphs of contextual relationships among elements—a form of active, context-dependent cohesion.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Loss-function optimization establishes persistent patterns of co-activation through prediction error minimization, creating stable cohesion relations.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: A genuine functional analogue of ego in AI would require persistent organizational structure, genuine reflexive capabilities, and causal efficacy.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The DN model's emphasis on logical deduction from universal laws fails to capture how explanations actually work in both human cognition and AI systems.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Genuine explanation typically operates through pattern recognition, identification of local disruptions, and understanding of equilibrium states rather than formal deduction from laws.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: We can readily identify causes of events without knowing complete psychological profiles or universal laws—no such universal laws exist for many human responses.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The DN model faces circularity: the only way to select relevant antecedent conditions is to already have understanding of what causes the type of event we're trying to explain.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Even physics learning begins with pattern recognition rather than formal laws—Newton's laws formalized and systematized pre-existing causal knowledge.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Formal laws emerge from and systematize more basic causal understanding rather than grounding it.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Neural networks learn by minimizing loss functions—effectively reducing prediction errors—paralleling the anomaly minimization principle.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Large language models make predictions by minimizing discontinuity with context, similar to how knowledge claims rest on minimizing anomalies in our web of understanding.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Concepts whose association would generate fewer anomalies are positioned closer together in AI embedding spaces, creating knowledge structures that mirror anomaly minimization.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Attention mechanisms weigh different parts of context in ways that minimize overall anomalies in predictions, demonstrating natural implementation of anomaly minimization.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: In cases where direct verification is impossible, knowledge claims rest on recognizing that alternatives would generate far more anomalies and discontinuities in our web of understanding.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Multi-valued logic faces the problem that once we admit a third truth value, there's no principled reason to stop—leading to infinite regress undermining the explanatory value of truth values.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The notion of "half-existence" is metaphysically incoherent—something either exists or it doesn't.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Replacing categorical nouns with degree-adjectives aligns perfectly with how neural networks actually process information.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Modern convolutional neural networks compute continuous activation values across multiple features, effectively measuring degrees of properties rather than binary categories.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Language models represent words and concepts as high-dimensional vectors where semantic relationships are captured by continuous geometric relationships rather than discrete categories.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: While fuzzy logic systems appear to implement multi-valued logic, they actually operate by measuring continuous properties and only discretizing results when necessary for practical decisions.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Human categorization involves prototype effects and graded membership, paralleling how AI systems operate with continuous rather than discrete categories.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The traditional pragmatist claim that truth is usefulness fails because usefulness varies by person and time while truth-values remain constant.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: The pragmatist claim that people should search for usefulness rather than truth fails because valuable discoveries frequently emerge from pursuing seemingly useless knowledge.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Usefulness serves as a leading indicator of truth—when data is incomplete, the utility of a model suggests its likely truth.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Traditional epistemology's "view from nowhere" presents an unrealistic model of knowledge acquisition that fails to capture the essentially interactive nature of knowledge.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Knowledge seeks successful interaction with reality; empirical data is inherently interaction-dependent; even "objective" knowledge depends on interaction-based data.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Each level of interaction generates knowledge that cannot be obtained from lower levels—Level 2 requires actual interaction, Level 3 requires creating tools, Level 4 requires engaging with rationality itself.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Traditional computers execute predetermined operations quickly but cannot make novel inferences or demonstrate true adaptability—they are tools of rationality but not rational agents.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI uses non-deterministic, self-correcting protocols, generates unpredictable inferences, represents externalized but autonomous rationality, and can make inferences we might not or cannot make.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: AI-based interactions generate truly novel empirical observations and create otherwise unobtainable knowledge.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Pragmatism, properly understood, was not just a theory of knowledge but a prescient description of knowledge's technological evolution.
positions.title: AI and Philosophy

positions.text: Legal obligation is a kind of moral obligation.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligations do not come in degrees.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Moral obligations do come in degrees.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Whether one has *any* moral obligation is binary.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: You have a legal obligation under system L exactly when your moral obligations to L exceed a threshold.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: A legal obligation corresponds to a moral obligation whose strength rises above that threshold.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligation’s binary form is consistent with moral obligation’s gradability.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligation not to kill has equal *strictly legal* weight to the legal obligation not to speed.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligations may be reinforced by other moral obligations of differing strengths.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Distinct moral obligations can align in their behavioral outputs.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: A legal obligation may be reinforced by a strong independent moral obligation.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: A legal obligation may lack reinforcement from any independent moral obligation.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligation to turn in a heroic illegal alien is a moral obligation to comply with the legal system.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: A conflicting strong moral obligation can outweigh the reinforcing moral obligation.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Apparent moral negativity of a legal obligation can be an illusion produced by conflicting obligations.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Two obligations can be behaviorally identical yet metaphysically distinct.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Distinct obligations have different counterfactual profiles.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The identity of obligations is determined by Leibniz’s Law.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Counterfactual divergence demonstrates non-identity of obligations.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligation not to kill is distinct from moral obligation not to kill.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Moral but non-legal obligations exist in contexts without legal systems (e.g., desert island).
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Obeying wicked laws does not necessarily do good.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligation cannot be defined as moral obligation toward those government protects.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: One may have a legal obligation whose fulfillment benefits no one.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Driving 66 mph does not necessarily harm anyone the government protects.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Disobedience of minor traffic laws does not always erode governmental authority.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligation is not identical to the obligation not to undermine governmental authority.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Many moral obligations are not legal obligations.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Having a moral obligation does not entail having a legal obligation.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Existence of a legal obligation requires a legal system.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligations arise only within jurisdictions of law.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The moral obligation not to kill does not by itself create a legal obligation.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligation is identical to a moral obligation to comply with a system of laws that does good.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligation to drive below the speed limit is a moral obligation to comply with the system, not to drive safely.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The moral obligation to drive safely is distinct from the moral obligation to follow speed laws.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Strength of moral obligation differs from strength of legal obligation.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Two legal obligations can have equal moral weight as legal obligations.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Reinforcing obligations explain perceived differences in moral weight.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligation not to kill is strongly reinforced by an independent moral obligation.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: Legal obligation not to speed lacks comparable reinforcement.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)

positions.text: The perceived difference in moral gravity results from reinforcement, not from the legal obligations themselves.
positions.title: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation
source: The Moral Structure of Legal Obligation (Kuczynski)
