positions.text: Semantics is essential for understanding the structure of meaning in language, particularly in distinguishing literal from non-literal meaning. - **Dependency**: The conclusion depends on all four claims, as it synthesizes the arguments about the structure of meaning, the need for semantics, and the distinction between literal and non-literal meaning.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: The literal meaning of utterances can be obscured by pre-semantic information, necessitating the intervention of professional semanticists to uncover the true meaning.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: The text concludes by reiterating the need for professional semanticists to uncover the true literal meanings of sentences, given the complexity and obscurity of these meanings.

positions.text: There would be no languages if there were no expressions (words, phrases, sentences, etc.). Nothing meaningless is an expression.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: But it isn't much use to be told that words and sentences 'have meanings,' since the word 'meaning' has three different meanings, and only one of these directly relates to the nature of language.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Chomsky famously said that 'colorless green dreams sleep furiously' is both meaningless and is a bona fide sentence, and therefore expression, of English. This reasoning is no good.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Meaning #1: The evidential meaning of 'meaning' In some cases, to say that x 'means' y is to say that x is evidence of y-that x and y are causally interrelated in such a way that, given x, it can reasonably be inferred that y.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Meaning #2: The psychological meaning of 'meaning' When we use sentences, we mean things by them. Meaning in this sense is a psychological notion.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Meaning #3: The linguistic meaning of 'meaning' The sentence 'snow is white' says something about something; it attributes the property of being white to snow. Therefore, it means that snow is white.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Meaning in the psychological sense involves, but does not coincide with, meaning in the linguistic sense.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: My meaning that Sally is devious is parasitic on my utterance's meaning that things aren't always as they seem.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Some philosophers and linguists have held that for: (1) 'snow is white' to mean that snow is white is for it to be the case that, in uttering 'snow is white,' what people mean is that snow is white.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: The sentence: (2) 'the cube root of three is Sir Lawrence Olivier's favorite irrational number between one and four' has a determinate meaning, even though that sentence has never been uttered before and, therefore, no one as of yet has ever meant anything by it.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Let's say that P1 and P2 are the propositions meant by (1) and (2), respectively. In saying that (1) means P1 and that (2) means P2, we are not using the word 'means' equivocally.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: You must believe that 'snow is white' means snow is white if, intending to speak sincerely and literally, you say 'snow is white' with the intention of getting it across that snow is white.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: If you think that 'snow is white' means grass is green, you cannot, if your intention is to speak sincerely and literally, believe that 'snow is white' means snow is white.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: It's a matter of convention that 'snow' doesn't refer to grass and, therefore, that 'snow is white' doesn't mean that grass is white.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Utterances of 'snow is white' embody judgments about the color of snow and about how, linguistic conventions being what they are, one can report the color of snow.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Thus, propositions have existed as long as there have been truths; which means that they've existed as long as there has been anything and, consequently, that propositions are not creations of human creations.

positions.text: Thus semantic rules assign meanings to properties of physical objects-to morphological or acoustical properties (in other words, to properties that a things has in virtue of having a certain shape or sounding a certain way).

positions.text: Thus, semantic rules assign meanings to non-expressions. But which non-expressions? A story will give us the answer.

positions.text: A semantic rule is therefore something which assigns a meaning to a property, and language is a set of such rules.

positions.text: Many believe that linguistic meaning is to be understood in function-theoretic terms-that, in other words, semantic rules are mathematical functions.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Let us start by defining the word 'function.' Given any pair of whole numbers, the expression '+' assigns exactly one whole number to that pair.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: The semantic rule for 'Socrates' is a function that assigns a certain individual (Socrates) to that word (or to occurrences thereof).
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Since Socrates lived well before the English language came into existence, 'Socrates' (the name, not the person) didn't come into existence until well after its referent went out of it.

positions.text: The thing that, according to my proposal, is henceforth to pick out Larry is the thing of which all and only such bursts of noises (etc.) are instances. That thing, like anything else of which there are instances, is a property.

positions.text: The most natural assumption is that they are idealized descriptions of what people mean when they speak and write (etc.). If this assumption is right, then, given the points just made, the view in question is wrong.

positions.text: Expressions have meanings. A meaningless burst of noise isn't an expression. If I cough or guffaw, the burst of noise I've produced doesn't have the sort of meaning had by bona fide expressions.

positions.text: This view is held by Nathan Salmon (2007) and also by Scott Soames (2002). This view is inconsistent with some obvious facts.

positions.text: If semantic rules are just idealizations of speaker-behavior, then speaker-behavior must pre-exist the semantic rules embodied in it.

positions.text: Another problem with the Salmon-Soames view is that it's inconsistent with the normative character of semantic rules.

positions.text: According to a doctrine known as 'conceptual role semantics' (CRS), whose exponents include Hartry Field (1977) and Robert Brandom (1994), for an expression to have a given meaning is simply for it to be used in a certain way. But this isn't correct as we just saw.

positions.text: If we learn tomorrow that, contrary to what we previously thought, Aristotle wrote several plays, we'll infer 'somebody who wrote several plays wrote the Nichomachean Ethics' from 'Aristotle wrote the Nichomachean Ethics.' But it doesn't follow that 'Aristotle wrote the Nichomachean Ethics' would have undergone some change in its semantic meaning.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: CRS entails that every inference is an analytic inference. Once it's learned that Aristotle wrote plays, it becomes, according to CRS, constitutive of the meaning of 'Aristotle wrote the Nichomachean Ethics' that one can infer from it that a playwright wrote the Nichomachean Ethics.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: But where simple expressions are concerned, literal and understood meaning must coalesce.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: If people thought that 'red' meant what is in fact meant by 'blue,' then 'red' would have that meaning.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Systematic, widespread error is impossible where semantically simple expressions are concerned.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Even though there can be widespread, systematic misinterpretations of sentences, those misinterpretations do not arise as a result of people failing to know what the simple parts of sentences mean.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: They arise as a result of people not knowing how to put those meanings together.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: So to the extent that its meaning is fixed by the fact that it has the form ‹...Socrates...›, people (English speakers) do systematically understand 'Socrates was more wise than Plato, but he was less sharp then Aristotle'; and to the extent that its meaning was determined by its having the form ‹...wise...›, people do understand that sentence; and so on.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: So far as that sentence is systematically misunderstood, it is because people are having trouble putting the meanings of its constituents together-it is because they're having trouble figuring out how those meanings ought to be put together.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Such expressions are known as 'indexicals.'
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Indexicals that fall into this category are known as 'demonstratives.'
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: The meaning of an utterance 'today' is the day it picks out. The meaning of the corresponding word-type is the rule that assigns that referent to that token.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Some definite descriptions appear to be 'incomplete'-that is, they fail to pick out a single object.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: The reason is that much information is perspectival, and nothing perspectival can be stated in a language that doesn't contain indexicals.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: But that utterance wouldn't necessarily express the belief I wanted to express.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: It is tokens of 'I am now tired' that make statements; the corresponding sentence-type does not do so.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: What a token of 'I am tired now' affirms depends on facts about the context of utterance.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Thus the semantic rule that assigns a proposition to such a token does so on the basis of the facts about the context of utterance.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: So context-sensitive sentence-types aren't true or false; they don't, in and of themselves, bear propositions.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: But they have an important semantic role: the identity of the proposition meant by a token of such a type depends on the identity of the type.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Long story short: the meanings of sentence-tokens are propositions and the meanings of sentence-types are rules that assign propositions to their tokens, usually on the basis of facts about the context of tokening.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: are propositions-they are things that are true or false.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: There are thus very different sorts of semantic rules. There are those that assign meanings to properties, and there are those that assign meanings to instances of those properties.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: The meaning that was assigned to the property we were just discussing is itself a semantic rule.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Consider the following ink deposit: 'Barack Obama.' There is a semantic rule that assigns a meaning to the property of having a morphology similar to that ink deposit.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: The right definition is this: a semantic rule is something which assigns a rule to a property that in its turn assigns meanings to instances of that property.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: If you say 'the U.S. economy is fairly stable,' whether you are speaking the truth or not depends on when you say it.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: For all intents and purposes, any two tokens of (IA) 'the interior angles of a Euclidean triangle add up to 180°' express the same proposition.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Nonetheless, many semanticists hold that, at the level of literal meaning, IA is context-insensitive.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: concerns properties. In natural language, quantified sentences are pseudo-objectual statements. They appear to be about objects, but are really about properties. That is why they must be reparsed if their logical forms are to be exposed.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Frege discovered both facts and, therewith, created modern analytic philosophy.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: The fact that grammatical and logical form sometimes diverge is related to the fact some expressions are to be defined contextually.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: So, for example, 'someone' doesn't refer to anyone. It doesn't refer to John or Sally or Jane.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Thus, the meaning of 'someone' isn't given by some rule that pairs it off with this or that individual.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Rather, the meaning of 'someone' is given by the statement that: (RS) ‹Someone has psi› is true just in case the property of being a psi is instantiated.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Nonetheless, it would be an overstatement to say that whenever a sentence contains an expression that must be defined contextually, it's grammatical form pulls apart from its logical form.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: How can it be said that all definitions are contextual definitions? Aren't there also denotative definitions?
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Whenever an expression can be defined denotatively, a sentence's logical form will not, by virtue of that sentence's containing that expression, diverge from its logical form.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: As we'll see in Chapter 7, what made it possible for Frege to revolutionize logic was his insight that grammatical and logical form diverge; and what made the latter insight possible was his generalization of the concept of a mathematical function.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: understanding language requires a focus on linguistic meaning and its distinction from other types of meaning.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: the notion of meaningless expressions must be rejected, and all expressions must be seen as having some form of meaning.
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: then the role of context in determining meaning is crucial, and speakers need to be aware of the contextual factors that influence the meaning of their utterances.

positions.text: it would require a rethinking of theories of meaning that rely on speaker's intentions, such as Grice's theory. 1.2. It would also have implications for the understanding of semantic rules, emphasizing their normative and compositional nature.

positions.text: theories of meaning would need to account for the normative and compositional nature of semantic rules. 3.2. It would also mean that the psychological approach to meaning, as exemplified by Grice's theory, would need to be revised or abandoned.

positions.text: semantic rules are not merely descriptive but guide and normatively constrain speaker behavior, affecting how we understand and analyze language use.

positions.text: then there are cognitive constraints on how people interpret language, particularly in how they combine the meanings of simple expressions to understand complex ones. - This follows from the text's discussion of how systematic misinterpretations arise from difficulty in combining the meanings of simple expressions.

positions.text: then indexicals play a crucial role in language, enabling the expression of perspectival information that cannot be conveyed in indexical-free languages.

positions.text: then context-sensitive expressions are essential for expressing perspectival thoughts and beliefs, and an indexical-free language would be severely impoverished.

positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 4

positions.text: Philosophy is the discipline that delineates the structures of the categories in terms of which we think about the world. — "Philosophy studies these categories. It delineates their structures. This is its sole function. Thus, philosophy is the discipline that delineates the structures of the categories in terms of which we think about the world."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: We understand the world through categories (event, cause, thing, mind, perception, belief, knowledge, language, truth, value-judgment, law, justice, etc.). — "We understand the world in terms of certain categories (e.g., person, statement, fact, impossibility, existence)."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: These categories are seldom objects of thought, even though we cannot think without them. — "Whatever we know, we know it through these categories (and others like them—the list is not complete). But even though we cannot think without them, they are seldom the objects of thought."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: We are adept at using categories but have difficulty making explicit the conditions that govern them. — "The result is that, although we're adept at using them, we know little about them."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Self-understanding is not the mind's primary function. — "In general, it's hard to identify the principles that guide our thoughts. Self-understanding isn't the mind's primary function."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The idea of a mind that thinks about nothing other than itself is incoherent. — "Nor could it be. The idea of a mind that thinks about nothing other than itself is an incoherent one."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Any case of self-awareness is necessarily derivative of, and of lesser quality than, some more fundamental sort of understanding. — "Thus, any case of self-awareness, and therefore of self-understanding, is necessarily derivative of, and for that reason of lesser quality than, some other more fundamental sort of understanding."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Philosophy is the analysis of the preconditions of all knowledge—the analysis of analysis, the logic of logic, the science of science. — "Philosophy is the analysis of the preconditions of all knowledge. It is the analysis of analysis, the logic of logic, the science of science."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Philosophical knowledge is metaknowledge—knowledge about knowledge. — "Philosophical knowledge is metaknowledge—knowledge about knowledge."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The philosopher wants to know the laws governing the laws—what it would even make sense to claim to hold. — "The philosopher is interested in the laws governing the laws. He doesn't want to know what in fact holds. He wants to know what it would even make sense to claim to hold. He wants to know the laws that the laws can't break."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The philosopher is to the physicist what the physicist is to the engineer. — "The philosopher is to the physicist what the physicist is to the engineer and, therefore, what the engineer is to the stranded motorist."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Pre-Fregean philosophers (with some exceptions) mistakenly thought they were studying the most general features of the actual world. — "With some exceptions, pre-Fregean philosophers thought they were studying the most general features of the actual world."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Frege showed that philosophers' interest in the actual is subordinate to their interest in the possible. — "Frege showed that this is wrong... But with philosophers, it's the other way around. Any interest they have in the actual is subordinate to their interest in the possible."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Analytic philosophers figure out what there could be by analyzing statements. — "Unlike non-analytic philosophers, analytic philosophers figure out what there could be by analyzing statements."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Statements that make sense describe possible realities; those that don't, don't. — "Statements that make sense are those that can be true, and statements that don't are those that can't. Therefore, statements that make sense describe possible realities and those that don't don't."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Frege's key insight: logical form ≠ grammatical form. — "2.0 Frege's key insight: Logical form ≠ grammatical form"
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: When people have an obviously correct belief that seems to have an absurd consequence, they often accept the absurdity and develop ad hoc hypotheses rather than questioning whether the absurdity really follows. — "(FL) When people have an obviously correct belief that seems to have an absurd consequence, they should ask themselves whether that absurdity really is a consequence of that belief. But they frequently don't. Instead they accept the absurdity and, in order to make this mistake of theirs work, they develop ad hoc hypotheses as to the nature of reality that undermine the integrity of their own belief system."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Philosophical insight is acquired not by doing parascience or positing entities, but by analyzing meanings and clarifying statements. — "What this shows is that, in at least some cases, philosophical insight is acquired, not by doing parascience, but by analyzing meanings—not by positing entities, but by clarifying statements."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: "Nothing is a square circle" does not attribute a property to a non-entity; it states that the set of squares doesn't overlap with the set of circles. — "Since (i) and (ii) are just different ways of saying what SC says, the latter doesn't make the absurd statement that some non-entity is a circle, and it instead makes the innocuous statement that the set of squares doesn't overlap with the set of circles."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The concept of "non-entities" or "subsistent entities" (Brentano, Meinong) is absurd—such philosophers were ontologizing when they should have been analyzing. — "This entire approach is misguided. Brentano and Meinong were ontologizing when they should have been analyzing."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Hallucinations do not require the existence of non-existent objects; they have for their content an existence-claim that nothing satisfies. — "When we describe a thought or perception as having a 'non-existent object,' what we mean, so far as what we mean is coherent, is that it has for its content an existence-claim that nothing satisfies."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Scientific puzzles involve acquiring new data; philosophical puzzles are purely conceptual and result from confusion, not ignorance. — "The puzzles that science deals with typically involve a failure to know all the facts... Unlike scientific puzzles, philosophical puzzles are not solved by generating new raw data. Philosophical puzzles are purely conceptual in nature and have no strictly factual component."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Scientific breakthroughs have two components: (i) new raw data and (ii) new conceptual modeling of already-known data. — "In general, scientific breakthroughs have two components: (i) a strictly fact-based component (new raw data is acquired), and (ii) a purely conceptual component (already known data is modeled in a new and better way)."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Philosophical puzzles are not solved by generating new raw data—they result from failure to draw the right inferences. — "Philosophical puzzles result, not from a failure to know the facts, but from a failure to draw the right inferences. They result, not from ignorance, but from confusion."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Wittgenstein was wrong to claim that all philosophical puzzles are linguistic in nature. — "But this position is incorrect. There are many philosophical puzzles that don't have anything to do with language, and the solutions to these puzzles cannot be modeled on Frege's solutions to the puzzles discussed a moment ago."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: There are philosophical puzzles that have nothing to do with language. — "There are many philosophical puzzles that don't have anything to do with language."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Propositions are not sentences; propositions are the things meant by sentences. — "Propositions are the things meant by sentences. 'Snow is white,' 'schnee is weiss,' and 'la neige est blanche' all mean the same thing. There is some one proposition that is the meaning of each of them."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Propositions exist independently of human beings; they are not human creations, whereas sentences are. — "It's widely thought that propositions are human creations. This is false... it follows from this that propositions are not human creations. Sentences, on the other hand, are human creations. They wouldn't be around if it weren't for us."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Propositions are properties; truth is instantiatedness. — "Propositions must be identified with properties and a proposition's being true must be identified with its being instantiated. Propositions are properties and truth is instantiatedness."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The world was a certain way before we were around, so propositions (as ways the world is) exist independently of our beliefs. — "The world was a certain way before we were around; it will be a certain way after we're gone; and the way it is while we're around is up to us only to a limited extent. It follows that, independently of our having any beliefs or, indeed, our doing or thinking anything, certain propositions are true."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Analytic philosophy (broadly construed) is philosophy done in the careful, clear-headed, systematic way that Frege approached problems. — "The word 'analytic philosopher' sometimes refers to the sort of philosophy done by people who aspire to approach philosophical problems in the same careful and clear-headed way as Frege."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The claim that one can know something only by being causally affected by it (e.g., Fodor's position regarding knowledge of mathematical truths) is absurd in the extreme. — "I personally regard JK as being absurd in the extreme."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The fact that 1+1=2 is non-spatiotemporal; nothing outside space-time can bear causal relations to anything. — "The fact it describes must therefore be non-spatiotemporal. Nothing outside of space-time can bear any causal relation to anything. Therefore, JK is wrong."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The dispute over causal theories of knowledge is not linguistic—it concerns whether an agreed-upon meaning is true. — "In any case, the dispute between those who accept JK and those who reject it has nothing to do with language. It's agreed what JK means. What isn't agreed is whether the thing that it's agreed to mean is true."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: No philosophical assertions are empirical; observation cannot tell you whether one statement is inconsistent with another. — "No philosophical assertions are empirical. Philosophy analyzes the categories in terms of which we think about the world... Since observation can't tell one whether one statement is inconsistent with another, philosophical assertions are non-empirical."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The question of whether law requires morality (legal positivism vs. anti-positivism) is to be resolved through logical, not empirical, analysis. — "What analytic philosophers of law, such as positivist H.L.A. Hart (1907–1992) and anti-positivist Ronald Dworkin (1931–), do agree about is that LM is to be resolved on the basis of logical analysis."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Logical positivism (LP) holds that meaningful statements are either tautologies or empirical. — "LP says that any meaningful statement is either a tautology or is empirical."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: LP is self-refuting: it is neither a tautology nor empirically verifiable, so by its own criterion it is meaningless. — "Thus, LP is neither an empirical truth nor a tautology. It is thus a counterexample to itself and is therefore false."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: There are non-empirical truths that have nothing to do with anyone's linguistic practices. — "We've seen that, contrary to what LP says, there are non-empirical truths that have nothing to do with anyone's linguistic practices."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Strictly observational grounds cannot support theoretical statements like Newton's physics directly—only in conjunction with specific factual claims. — "By itself NT doesn't make any predictions or otherwise have observable consequences... Thus, taken by itself, NT isn't confirmable. What is confirmable isn't NT, but NT plus statements describing specific matters of fact."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Wittgenstein's acknowledgment that the Tractatus is meaningless by its own standards is self-contradictory; to understand something is to see its meaning. — "So far as they aren't trivial, these magisterial words are false. To understand something is to see its meaning. Therefore, anything that is understood has a meaning. So Wittgenstein's words, if understood, have a meaning, and Wittgenstein is therefore contradicting himself in saying that those who understand his words will see that they're meaningless."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Empiricism is self-refuting: if true, there are no grounds for believing it; if there are grounds, it is false. — "Thus, so far as empiricism is correct, there are no grounds for believing it correct; and so far as there are such grounds, empiricism is false... Therefore, empiricism, if true, is false; therefore it's false."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Russell's argument against naïve realism ("if true, is false; therefore false") rests on spurious reasoning—we do observe external objects, not merely effects upon ourselves. — "Though eloquently stated, this argument consists of spurious reasoning for a false conclusion. In observing the paper-weight on my desk, I'm not observing some effect of the stone upon myself: I'm observing the stone itself."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The greenness, hardness, and coldness of physics are identical with those of daily observation; physics tells us what these properties are. — "This is false. Physics has a lot to say about greenness, hardness, and coldness that commonsense does not. But that's very different from saying that the coldness we feel, the greenness we see, etc., aren't the greenness, coldness, and hardness of physics. Physics tells us what it is for something to have the properties we know them to have through sight, touch, etc."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The concept of confirmation is incoherent unless non-empirical knowledge is granted (Goodman's grue argument). — "But we'll now see that the concept of confirmation is an incoherent one unless it's granted that there is nonempirical knowledge and, therefore, that confirmationalism is incoherent."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Induction does not have a strictly observational basis; there must be non-observational grounds for preferring certain properties over others in inductive inference. — "Contrary to what that argument tacitly assumes, induction does not have strictly observational basis... This means that, if any inductions are better than any others, we must have legitimate but at least partly nonobservational grounds for believing that, when making inductive inferences, certain properties (e.g., green) are relevant and others (e.g., grue) are not."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: It is utterances, not sentences per se, that are tautologies—tautologousness depends on how the auditor learned the meanings of expressions. — "In this section we'll see that it is utterances of sentences, not sentences per se, that are tautologies."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: One knows the meanings of expressions descriptively; two different descriptions can pick out the same thing. — "This story illustrates some deeply important facts that are in the philosophy of language. First, one knows the meanings of expressions descriptively... Two very different descriptions can pick out some one thing (cf. 'the third U.S. President' and 'the President responsible for the Louisiana Purchase')."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: All entailment is not formal entailment (contra Wittgenstein's Tractatus). — "There are some qualifications to this, as we'll see in a moment, but none that redound to the credit of Wittgenstein's point... There are infinitely many informally valid inferences for each formally valid one. So Wittgenstein's allegation that all entailments are formal entailment is not feasible."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: "Brown is a circle" entails "Brown is a two-dimensional figure," but this is not a formal entailment—it is a non-tautologous, non-empirical truth. — "(12) doesn't say anything about the spatiotemporal world. (12) is logically true; its truth is guaranteed by the structures of the concepts composing it. Unlike (11), (12) isn't a tautology. Therefore, it's a non-tautologous, non-empirical truth. Thus, there are non-formal entailments."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The concept of a logically perfect language is incoherent. — "In any case, the concept of a logically perfect language is not a coherent one. It isn't possible for everything about a sentence's meaning to be reflected in its grammar."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: If a logically perfect language existed, it would be expressively inferior to natural languages. — "And if per impossibile there did exist a logically perfect language, it would be expressively inferior to English, Arabic, and every other natural language."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: For each analytic truth a logically perfect language could express, there would be infinitely many it could not express. — "A consequence is that, for each analytic truth that a logically perfect language can express, there are infinitely many that it cannot express."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Knowing that a formal entailment is valid requires knowing that some informal entailment is valid; there are infinitely many informally valid inferences for each formally valid one. — "The only way to know that some formal entailment is valid is to know that some informal entailment is valid... There are, quite literally, infinitely many informally valid inferences for each formally valid one."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: There cannot be strictly observation-based knowledge that an object's length is given by an irrational number (Hempel's argument). — "It follows that, if an object's length is given by an irrational number, that fact cannot be known directly on the basis of measurement. It therefore follows that there is no strictly observation-based way to know that y's length equals x's multiplied by √2."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Modern physics depends on assumptions (that magnitudes can assume irrational values) for which there cannot be strictly observational grounds. — "Thus, it must be assumed that, at certain junctures, the velocities, lengths, masses, etc., of objects are sometimes given by irrational numbers. But there cannot, as we've seen, be strictly observational grounds for believing that a given object's length is √2 meters."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Strict empiricism is inconsistent with the fact that modern physics is a source of knowledge. — "This means that strict empiricism is inconsistent with the obvious fact that modern physics is a source of knowledge."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Euclid's axioms (1)–(5) are statement-forms, not statements proper, and some interpretations of them are false. — "We'll use the expression '(1)-(5)' to refer to the conjunction of (1) and (2) and (3), etc... One would think that (1)-(5) is correct. But this turned out not to be so. It turned out that (1)-(5) is a statement-form, not a statement proper, and that (1)-(5) therefore isn't true or false. It also turned out that some of its instances are false."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The parallel postulate is not true or false of anything—it is a statement-form. — "According to many, this shows that the parallel postulate isn't true of every possible space. This isn't what it shows. The parallel postulate isn't true or false of anything. It's a statement-form, not a statement, and statement-forms aren't true or false of anything."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Arithmetical truth cannot be formalized: any formal characterization of arithmetic is either inconsistent or incomplete. — "It turned out that this is not possible. This means that any formal characterization of arithmetic is either inconsistent (i.e., it entails a contradiction), or incomplete (i.e., there is some arithmetical truth that is not a consequence of it)."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The possible worlds analysis of counterfactuals (Lewis, Kment, Stalnaker) is completely preposterous. — "I find this analysis completely preposterous."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: We don't know anything about other universes; any knowledge of them would be parasitic on knowledge of counterfactual truths. — "We don't know anything about other universes, let alone whether Smith and Kennedy exist in them or, therefore, what their situations there are. Anything we do know about such universes is parasitic on our knowledge of counterfactual truths like K and S."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Counterfactuals are grammatical variants of ordinary causal or logical statements. — "The right analysis of counterfactuals, I would suggest, is to see them as grammatical variants of garden variety causal or logical statements."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: "Someone" is not in the same semantic category as "John" or "Ethel"—it does not refer to an individual. — "What all this shows is that 'someone' isn't in the same category as 'John' and 'Ethel.' It doesn't function in the same way, even though, given its grammatical function, one would expect it to."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: "Someone smokes" does not attribute the property of smoking to a non-specific individual; it states that the property of being a smoker is instantiated. — "What SS is saying, as Frege made clear, is that the characteristic—or as analytic philosophers put it, the property—of being a smoker is instantiated. SS is making a statement, not about some non-specific individual, but about a very specific property, and it's saying of this property that it's instantiated."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: There is no such thing as an "ambiguous person" or "non-specific entity"—to posit such things is to twist reality to conform to grammatical assumptions. — "The wrong way to react to that fact is to twist reality to make it conform to our assumption that 'someone,' being grammatically comparable to 'John,' must refer to something... But then we're then stuck with the absurd thesis that there is some non-specific entity in the world."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The word "someone" is not ambiguous; it has one meaning. — "That's false. 'Someone' isn't ambiguous; it isn't like the word 'dumb.' SS has one meaning."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Analytic philosophers solve problems by clarifying statements, not by ontologizing (positing entities not previously believed to exist). — "As a general rule, analytic philosophers do not try to solve philosophical problems by 'ontologizing'—that is, by positing an entity, or class of entities, not previously believed to exist. They try to solve them by clarifying statements."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: When analytic philosophers do ontologize, they posit only abstract, non-spatiotemporal entities—never spatiotemporal denizens like persons or tables. — "But, whenever this happens, the 'entity' being posted is never a denizen of the spatiotemporal world; the thing that's posited is never a person or a table or a mountain or a monster. It's always an abstract object of some kind."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Properties exist; their existence is validated by obviously true statements (e.g., "Bob and Sally are both intelligent"). — "For example, given the premise that Bob and Sally are both humans who are intelligent, it follows that there exist characteristics—or, to use the word preferred by analytic philosophers, properties—that Bob and Sally have in common, and from this it follows that properties exist."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Properties are not identical with spatiotemporal entities; you will never encounter intelligence per se in space-time. — "But although you may encounter many instances of intelligence, you'll never encounter intelligence per se and it would make no sense to assign any spatiotemporal location to it."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The essence of the mental is intentionality (non-derivative representationality), as Brentano held. — "According to Franz Brentano (1838–1917), the essence of the mental is intentionality... So Brentano's thesis is that for something to be mental is for it to be non-derivatively representational."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Perception is description—seeing Larry involves seeing that various properties are instantiated. — "The solution to this problem lies in the fact that perception is description... Seeing Larry involves seeing a thing having various properties—having a certain color, shape, size, position (relative to you), etc. So seeing Larry involves seeing that various properties are instantiated."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Perceptions represent objects by encoding existence-claims that those objects satisfy. — "So, yes, your perception does represent Larry. But it represents him by way of encoding an existence-claim that he satisfies."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The content of a perception is given by an existence-claim, not by a semantically simple symbol. — "But in no sense-perception of Larry is he represented by some simple, homogenous, non-composite cipher. So far as he is perceived, Larry is represented as having these or those properties... Thus, the content of your perception of Larry is given by some existence-claim."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Hallucinations are representational not because they pick out non-existent objects, but because they encode false existence-claims. — "The important point is that, although it was hallucinatory, your visual experience encoded an existence-claim and, in so doing, gave you a message and was therefore representational. It wasn't representational in the sense that there was some thing that it picked out and, for that reason, represented."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Thoughts about non-existent things (e.g., an even prime greater than two) involve thinking false existence-claims. — "The same thing is true of thoughts about the non-existent. When you think about some non-existent number—for example, an even prime greater than two—there isn't some mathematically impossible entity that you're cognitively locking onto. What's going on is that you're thinking some false existence-claim."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Wittgenstein's view that philosophical problems arise only when sentences are misused is incorrect. — "But in both of them, Wittgenstein insists that that philosophical problems arise when, and only when, sentences are misused... But this position is incorrect."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Wittgenstein's Tractatus holds that philosophical statements violate hidden syntactic rules; the Investigations holds they result from using words in non-standard ways—both positions are wrong. — "In the TLP, Wittgenstein contends that all philosophical problems result from a failure to understand the syntactic rules of the languages we use... In the Philosophical Investigations... he denies that such misuses involve violations of hidden syntactic rules, and instead says that such misuses consist exclusively in one's using words in ways in which they are not ordinarily used."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: One cannot learn about concepts by studying word usage unless one can already grasp the concept independently of the word. — "Given any expression E and any concept C, an obvious extension of this argument shows that one can't learn about C by studying E unless one can grasp C, and can therefore examine it, without E's help. Thus, Wittgenstein is simply wrong to contend that one learns about concepts by learning about how the corresponding words are used."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Wittgenstein's thesis that "meaning is use" is incoherent. — "This contention is incoherent on many levels."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: If two expressions could only differ in meaning by being used differently, then any two sentences that have never been used would have the same meaning—which is absurd. — "Also, given any natural language, there are infinitely many sentences belonging to it that have never been used. The thesis that sentences have the same meaning iff they're used in the same way has the absurd consequence that any two sentences that haven't been used before have the same meaning."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Conceptual role semantics (CRS)—the thesis that two sentences have the same meaning iff used the same way—is indefensible. — "In any case, CRS is indefensible."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Someone who utters a sentence does so because she knows semantic rules assign a certain proposition to those words and she wishes to express that proposition. — "So far as she isn't misspeaking or randomly barking out noises, anyone who says 'it's hot out' or 'hace much calor,' or any other sentence, does so because she knows that existing semantic rules assign a certain proposition to those words and she wishes to put that proposition into words."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Carnap's view that for a lecture to concern triangles is for the word "triangle" to occur in the lecture is false. — "Carnap's view is false. Many a lecture that doesn't contain the word 'triangle' concerns triangles. (Think of all the mathematics lectures given in Japanese, Swedish, and Arabic. How often does the word 'triangle' occur in them?)"
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The very essence of logical positivism is the denial of meaning (propositions). — "The distinction between the word 'triangle' and the corresponding meaning is one that Carnap couldn't countenance without ceasing to be a logical positivist. The very essence of logical positivism is the denial of meaning."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: It is not up to us whether meanings (propositions) are correct, even though it is up to us what our symbols mean. — "We can use words in any way that we like... But whatever meaning we end up assigning to those words, it's not up to us whether that meaning is correct."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Logical truths are never sentences; they are always propositions. — "Thus, it is always propositions, and never sentences, that are logically true."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Propositions are never conventionally true; linguistic conventions are known empirically, but what those conventions express is not itself empirical. — "But no proposition is true by convention. It's up to us what our symbols mean. But it's not up to us whether those meanings are correct."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Logical positivists were unanimous in denying the existence of propositions and meanings generally. — "It should be pointed out that logical positivists were unanimous in denying the existence of propositions and of meanings generally."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Sentences like "the universe is a perfect unity" are not meaningless because they are non-empirical; they are meaningless because key terms are undefined—they are statement-forms, not statements. — "'Statements' such as 'the universe is a perfect unity,' 'the nothing nothings,' and so on, aren't really statements at all. They're statement-forms. (i) is obviously supposed to attribute some property to the universe. But since this property isn't identified, (i) contains an undefined term and therefore says nothing."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Once undefined terms are defined, previously "meaningless" sentences become meaningful and true or false. — "Once that term is defined, a meaningful statement results."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: A sentence is meaningful if it attributes some property to some object; it is meaningless if it does not attribute any property to anything. — "A sentence is meaningful if it attributes some property to some object. A sentence S is meaningful if, for some object x and some property phi, S says that x has phi."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Any non-compound sentence either says of some individual that it has some property or says that two or more individuals are interrelated in a certain way. — "Any non-compound sentence (i.e., any sentence that doesn't consist of other sentences) either says of some individual that it has some property or it says that two more individuals are interrelated in a certain way."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: All sentences (relational, compound, negative, quantified) can be reduced to the form ‹x has phi›. — "We have thus established that any given sentence S is equivalent to some sentence having the form ‹x has phi.›"
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The picture theory of meaning—that sentences are "pictures" of the facts they describe—is either trivially true or incoherent. — "What does he mean? Maybe he means that sentences are picture-like in that they, like pictures, represent facts. But in that case what Wittgenstein is saying is completely and utterly trivial."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: If the picture theory means that sentences share structural features with the facts they describe, it is dubiously coherent. — "Maybe Wittgenstein is saying that sentences and the facts they describe share structural features. But this claim is of dubious coherence."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Wittgenstein's claim that "what can be shown, cannot be said" is obviously false. — "Wittgenstein's assertion that 'what can be shown, cannot be said' is obviously false. I tell you that I can do fifty push-ups. (I say 'I can do fifty push-ups.') You don't believe me. So I show you that I can do fifty push-ups."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Contrary to Wittgenstein, we can identify the logical forms of our own utterances; we do it whenever we put the meanings of words into words. — "In any case, contrary to what Wittgenstein says, we can identify the logical forms of our own utterances. To identify the logical form of a statement is simply to make it clear what it means. We can do this. We do it all the time. We do it whenever we put the meanings of words into words."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: No language can state all of its own semantic rules—there is a proof of this. — "But neither the English language, nor any other, can state all of its own semantic rules. Here's why... We're forced to reject this obvious truth if we grant the supposition that the English language can express each of its own semantic rules. Therefore, the English language cannot state all of its own semantic rules."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The claim that truth is indefinable is false if it means we cannot say what it is for a proposition to be true; it is true if it means no language can state all of its own semantic rules. — "It is often said that truth is indefinable... (a) is false. (See Chapter 3.) But (b) is true."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The sub-disciplines of philosophy (mind, language, epistemology, logic, metaphysics, science, ethics, political philosophy, religion, formal logic) overlap considerably. — "It should be kept in mind that these sub-disciplines overlap a great deal."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The question "do we think in words?" belongs to both the philosophy of mind and the philosophy of language. — "So, for example, the question 'do we think in words?' belongs to the philosophy of mind and to the philosophy of language."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Philosophical logic studies bearing-relations among sentences and propositions. — "This discipline studies bearing-relations holding among sentences and propositions (sentence-meanings)."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Metaphysics studies the nature of possibility, necessity, causal relations, identity, and the conditions something must meet to exist. — "This discipline studies the nature of possibility and necessity, of causal relations between objects. It also studies the nature of identity and the conditions that something must meet in order to exist."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: The philosophy of science studies the logical structure of scientific endeavor and its results. — "This discipline studies the logical structure of scientific endeavor and of its results."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: There may be a fundamental difference between explanations in the physical sciences and explanations in the psychological sciences. — "Is there a fundamental difference between explanations in the physical sciences and explanation in the psychological sciences?"
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Whether a system is deterministic or not may depend on how it is described—determinism may be a logical property of statements, not of events (Nagel's view). — "Determinism is a logical property of statements, in Nagel's view, not of the events they describe. A consequence is that a given domain may be deterministic with respect to one method of describing it, but indeterministic with respect to some other method of describing it."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Formal logic studies formal truth and attempts to formalize informal analytic truth insofar as possible. — "This discipline studies formal truth... Formal logic tries to formalize informal analytic truth, so far as that's possible to do so, and to say when it isn't possible, so far as it isn't."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: A sentence is formally true if every sentence of the same form is true. — "A sentence is formally true if every sentence of the same form is true."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Open-sentences (statement-forms) are neither true nor false; only their instances (when variables are replaced with constants) are true or false. — "An open sentence is a sentence-like expression that contains a free variable and is thus neither true nor false."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Mathematical logicians try to identify open-sentences that are true under all their interpretations, thereby formalizing analytic truth. — "Formal logicians aspire to identify open-sentences that are true under all their interpretations, since it is only to the extent that they can do this that they can formalize analytic truth, which is their main objective."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1

positions.text: Some classes of true statements (e.g., arithmetical statements) that one would expect to be formalizable turn out not to be—arithmetical truth cannot be formalized. — "There are some classes of true statements that one would expect to be formalizable but turn out not to be. The class of arithmetical statements ('1 + 2 = 3,' '2 × 9 = 18,' etc.) is an example. In other words, arithmetical truth cannot be formalized."
positions.title: Analytic Philosophy Chapter 1
