positions.position: A 'correct hallucination' or lucky guess does not constitute knowledge.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Logical truths (like the laws of logic) are not limitations on power but define what is coherent.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: God's inability to have non-causal knowledge is akin to an athlete's inability to score a football touchdown in a soccer game—it's a category error.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Prediction is an application of causal laws to known initial conditions.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Inaccurate knowledge of initial conditions guarantees inaccurate predictions.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Our own mental states and knowledge are part of the causal fabric of the world.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The content of our future knowledge is a future fact that can affect present actions.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Knowing your future knowledge now would change that future knowledge, creating a paradox.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Therefore, the growth of personal knowledge is an inherently unpredictable process.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The 'infallible self-predictor' is a logically incoherent concept.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Psychological attitudes like confidence can be causal factors in bringing about outcomes.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Self-fulfilling prophecies demonstrate that predictions can be part of the causal chain leading to the predicted event.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: This complicates prediction because the prediction itself becomes a new initial condition.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: 'Random' in common parlance means 'unforeseen' or 'unexpected given my knowledge,' not 'acausal.'
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: An event can be fully determined yet perfectly random from a limited epistemic perspective.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Randomness is not in the event but in the relationship between the event and an information set.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Two observers with different knowledge can disagree on whether an event is random.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: In an indeterministic scenario with two equally likely outcomes, the actual outcome is not random relative to the knowledge that one of them would occur.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Indeterminism can exist without events seeming 'random' to a knowledgeable observer.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The verification of universal claims like 'all metals expand' requires inductive inference.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Hume's problem of induction questions the rational justification for such inferences.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: In practice, everyone accepts inductive reasoning as justified.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The difference between a justified belief (I won't sprout wings) and an unjustified one (it will rain gumdrops) is clear in practice.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Arguments against determinism based on the behavior of 'identical' clones fail because perfect microphysical identity is virtually impossible.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Any two physical systems, no matter how similar, will have some differing initial conditions.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Determinism only requires that *identical* initial conditions yield identical outcomes; it doesn't claim we can ever create them.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Causal discovery in science works by observing correlations while striving to control other variables.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: A perfect correlation (100%) in practice is not necessary to infer a deterministic underlying law.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Evidence for a deterministic law is found if the correlation can be made arbitrarily strong by controlling more factors.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Our evidence for deterministic laws is 'imperfect' but of the same type as our evidence for any empirical fact.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Nothing known through sense-perception can be 'definitively established' with absolute certainty.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The existence of your hands is no more certain, in a strict philosophical sense, than the existence of deterministic laws.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The shortcomings of induction are a general problem for empirical knowledge, not a specific problem for determinism.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: We can have strong, reasonable certainty about deterministic laws.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The text presents a sustained defense of 'compatibilism' between determinism and unpredictability.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: It argues against the intuitive link between determinism and complete predictability.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: It also argues against the intuitive link between indeterminism and randomness.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The author positions their view as a 'close approximation' to the truth of determinism for the macro-world.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The narrative example of Smith's death illustrates the core distinctions between determined, random, and foreseeable.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The concept of 'independent causal sequences' is key to understanding common-sense randomness.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The burden of proof is higher for the determinist than for the indeterminist.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Modern physics has made determinism a 'live' philosophical question, not a settled one.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Self-knowledge, specifically knowledge of one's own future states, is presented as particularly problematic for prediction.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The chapter implies that free will debates must account for these predictability limits.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The 'perfect predictor' is often used in thought experiments about free will; this chapter undermines the coherence of that concept.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Information is physically embodied (in light waves, neural impulses).
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Transmission of information takes time, creating a necessary lag.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Decoding information is a physical process in the perceiver.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The 'veil of perception' is a real, physical barrier created by the causal process of perception itself.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The term 'transperceptual' refers to the world as it exists outside of this perceptual process.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Scientific laws are about transperceptual correlations.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Our evidence for these correlations is always 'imperfect' because it's filtered through perception.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The possibility of an 'accidentally correct' representation of the world is acknowledged but deemed irrelevant to knowledge.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Justification requires a reliable link between belief and truth, which causality provides.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The argument against divine foreknowledge is based on epistemic principles, not theological ones.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Much debate about determinism is muddled by conflating the metaphysical (what *is*) with the epistemic (what we can *know*).
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The author is a realist about the external world and causal laws.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: They are also a fallibilist about our knowledge of that world.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The view presented is naturalistic, explaining knowledge and perception within a physical, causal framework.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: It rejects Cartesian-style dualism or direct mental access to the world.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The 'gulf' between perception and reality is not an argument for skepticism but a logical constraint on prediction.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The focus is on the *conditions for the possibility* of prediction, which are found to be logically limited.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The chapter can be seen as an application of broader insights about information theory and observer effects to classic philosophical problems.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Laplace's demon—an infinitely intelligent predictor knowing all initial conditions and laws—is logically impossible, not just physically impossible.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: A deterministic universe is not a 'clockwork' universe knowable in its entirety by any internal observer.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: This is due to the very nature of what it means to be an observing part of that causal system.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The author uses analogies from sports (football/soccer) to illustrate logical incoherence.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The text is pedagogical, addressing common student arguments directly.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: One exercise question references the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle, suggesting it is often mistakenly invoked in these debates.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The author claims their personal experience validates the power of self-fulfilling prophecy (self-confidence).
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: They also speculate that apparent self-doubt might sometimes be a strategic form of self-protection.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Examples are used extensively to ground abstract philosophical points (Smith, the elevator, the metal bar, the cloned cells).
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The chapter aims to clarify common misconceptions in philosophy of science and epistemology.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The ultimate conclusion is that a deterministic universe is not knowable in its entirety by any internal observer, due to the nature of observation itself.
positions.source: Determinism, Randomness, and Unpredictability - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The physical world is a deterministic system.
positions.quote: The physical world is a deterministic system.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: There are both physical and mental entities.
positions.quote: There are both physical and mental entities.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Anything that involves thought or feeling is mental; anything that does not but is spatiotemporal is physical.
positions.quote: Anything that involves thought or feeling is mental; anything that does not (but is spatiotemporal) is physical.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Beliefs, intentions, and regrets are mental; rocks, trees, and explosions are physical.
positions.quote: So beliefs, intentions, and regrets are mental; and rocks, trees, and explosions are physical.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: It is an open and delicate question how the mental and physical are related.
positions.quote: It is an open and delicate question how the mental and physical are related.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Materialism is the position that mental entities are identical with certain physical entities.
positions.quote: Some hold that mental entities are identical with certain physical entities... This position is known as materialism.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Dualism is the position that the mental is non-physical.
positions.quote: Others deny this, saying that the mental is non-physical. This position is known as dualism.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: If materialism is right, then the mental world is just part of the physical world.
positions.quote: If materialism is right, then the mental world is just part of the physical world.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Given the way the world was 10 million years ago, the state of the universe made any experience's occurrence inevitable.
positions.quote: So if E is some experience that you either have had, are having, or will have, then given the way the world was 10 million years ago, the state of the universe 10 million years ago made E's occurrence inevitable.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Whatever you decide to do today is an inevitable consequence of the way the world was a million years ago.
positions.quote: Whatever you decide to do today is an inevitable consequence of the way the world was a million years ago.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Supposing that the physical world is deterministic and materialism is right, it's hard to see how there could be free will.
positions.quote: Supposing that the physical world is deterministic, and supposing also that materialism is right, it's hard to see how there could be free will.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: If the state of the universe a million years ago completely settled what choices I would make today, then I couldn't make any choices other than those that I do make.
positions.quote: If the state of the universe a million years ago completely settled what choices I would make today, then presumably I couldn't make any choices other than those that I do make.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Some believers in free will accept dualism because of the apparent irreconcilability of materialism with the presumption that we are free.
positions.quote: Because of the apparent irreconcilability of materialism with the presumption that we are free, some believers in free will—for example, Jean Paul Sartre and Maurice Merleau-Ponty—accept dualism.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Others accept materialism but reject determinism.
positions.quote: while others, e.g. Peter van Inwagen, accept materialism but reject determinism.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Supposing that there is indeterminism in the world, it doesn't necessarily mean that our mental processes aren't determined.
positions.quote: First of all, supposing that there is indeterminism in the world, it doesn't necessarily mean that our mental processes aren't determined.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: There is some corroboration for the view that decisions are determined by unconscious forces.
positions.quote: In any case, this is one point of view, and it is one for which there is some corroboration.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Choices not fixed by the state of the universe prior to their occurrence would not be free.
positions.quote: For the reasons given earlier, that wouldn't make those choices be free.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Choices not fixed by prior states of the universe would be more like fits or seizures than freely made judgments.
positions.quote: On the contrary, it would make them more like fits or seizures than like considered, freely made judgments.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: A sudden choice to drop out of college and become a drug dealer does not sound like a freely made choice—it sounds more like a compulsion.
positions.quote: Does this sound like a freely made choice? No. It sounds more like a compulsion.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Indeterminism doesn't help freedom and actually undermines it.
positions.quote: Again, we see that indeterminism doesn't help freedom and actually undermines it.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Determinism seems to threaten freedom.
positions.quote: The problem is that, as we saw, determinism also seems to threaten freedom.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: If determinism is right, the past completely determines what choices you're making now.
positions.quote: If determinism is right, then what happened in the past completely determines exactly what's happening in the present and, in particular, completely fixes what choices you're making now.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Under determinism, something over which you have no control is completely responsible for your choices.
positions.quote: So something over which you have no control is completely responsible for the choices that you're making.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: It's hard to see how you could have free will under determinism.
positions.quote: It's hard to see how, under these circumstances, you could be said to have free will.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Compatibilists believe there can be free will in a deterministic world.
positions.quote: Some hold that there can be free will in a deterministic world. Such people are known as compatibilists.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Incompatibilists deny that there can be free will in a deterministic world.
positions.quote: Those who deny it are incompatibilists.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: There are two different forms of compatibilism.
positions.quote: There are ultimately two different forms of compatibilism.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: According to the straightforward version of compatibilism, you are free to the extent that you can do what you want.
positions.quote: According to the straightforward version, you are free to the extent that you can do what you want, and you are unfree to the extent that you cannot.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: According to the other version of compatibilism, being able to do what you want is necessary but not sufficient for freedom.
positions.quote: According to the other version of compatibilism, being able to do what you want, though necessary for freedom, isn't sufficient for it.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: G.E. Moore believes an act is performed freely if the agent would have done something else if he had chosen to do so.
positions.quote: According to G.E. Moore, an act is performed freely if the agent would have done something else if he had chosen to do so.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Free acts aren't those that are uncaused; they're those that are caused by decisions on the part of the people who commit them.
positions.quote: Free acts aren't those that are uncaused; they're those that are caused in a certain way—they're those that are caused by decisions on the part of the people who commit them.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Chisholm objects to Moore's analysis: if every choice I make is inevitable, it's irrelevant that I would have acted differently had I made different choices.
positions.quote: If every choice I make is inevitable, then it's irrelevant that, had I made different choices, I would have acted differently.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Chisholm's point is a good one, but it isn't decisive.
positions.quote: Chisholm's point is a good one, but it isn't decisive.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Freedom is a property of actions, according to Locke.
positions.quote: In Locke's view, freedom is a property of actions.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Only things that one can intend to do are done freely.
positions.quote: Only things that one can intend to do are done freely.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Second-order intentions (intentions to intend) collapse into first-order intentions.
positions.quote: Second-order intentions (intentions to intend) collapse into first-order intentions.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Choices can be made freely according to a defensible form of compatibilism.
positions.quote: according to one, quite defensible form of compatibilism, choices can be made freely
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Freedom is primarily a property of choices and only secondarily of acts.
positions.quote: freedom is primarily a property of choices and only secondarily of acts
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Harry Frankfurt decisively demonstrated the falsity of Moore's position.
positions.quote: Harry Frankfurt decisively demonstrated the falsity of Moore's position
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Somebody can perform an act freely even if they could not have abstained from performing that act.
positions.quote: scenarios in which somebody performs some act freely even though that person could not have abstained from performing that act
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: 'x performs act A freely' is not equivalent with 'x does A and, had x chosen not to do A, x would not have done A.'
positions.quote: 'x performs act A freely' is not equivalent with 'x does A and, had x chosen not to do A, x would not have done A.'
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Moore's analysis is a misguided way of stating the principle that a free act is one that is choice-driven.
positions.quote: it's really just a misguided way of stating the principle that a free act is one that is choice-driven
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The counterfactual analysis of causality is wrong.
positions.quote: CFA is wrong.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: There is causal redundancy in the world.
positions.quote: This is because there is causal redundancy in the world.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Counterfactuals typically have to be hedged with ceteris paribus clauses to come out correct.
positions.quote: It's well known that, if they are to come out correct, counterfactuals typically have to be hedged with ceteris paribus clauses.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: As a rule, counterfactuals are causal claims.
positions.quote: As a rule, counterfactuals are causal claims.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: An act is free if it expresses one's decision to act that way.
positions.quote: an act is free if it expresses one's decision to act that way.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: One chooses to act; one doesn't choose to choose.
positions.quote: One chooses to act, Locke said; one doesn't choose to choose.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: It makes no sense to suppose that one chooses to choose.
positions.quote: It makes no sense to suppose that one chooses to choose.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: One would have to make infinitely many choices to make a single choice if one always chose what to choose.
positions.quote: For if one always chose what to choose, Locke points out, one would have to make infinitely many choices in order to make a single one
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Freedom is freedom to act, not freedom to choose.
positions.quote: So freedom is freedom to act, not freedom to choose.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Freedom is a relationship between choice and act, not between subject and choice.
positions.quote: freedom is a relationship between choice and act, and not between subject and choice.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: It is irrelevant whether one's choices are caused or not, so far as one's freedom is concerned.
positions.quote: so far as one's freedom is concerned, it is irrelevant whether one's choices are caused or not.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: It doesn't matter whether choices are caused for freedom, nor how they're caused.
positions.quote: it doesn't matter whether choices are caused... Nor does it matter how they're caused
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Determinism is not only compatible with freedom but is a prerequisite for it.
positions.quote: if FA is right, determinism is not only compatible with freedom but is a prerequisite for it
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Indeterministic mechanisms can undermine the implementation of choices.
positions.quote: If a degenerative neurological condition has undermined the integrity of the mechanisms linking your brain to your hand, thereby making them indeterministic, your intention to move your hand may be more likely to fail than to succeed
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: To the extent that the mechanisms involved in the implementation of our choices are indeterministic, we are in the position of somebody who has Parkinson's.
positions.quote: To the extent that the mechanisms involved in the implementation of our choices are indeterministic, we are in the grim position of somebody who has Parkinson's
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: There are different kinds of freedom.
positions.quote: This is obviously a kind of freedom—a very important kind. But it isn't the only kind
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Contrary to what Locke says, there is a sense in which choices can be free or unfree.
positions.quote: And, contrary to what Locke says, there is a sense in which choices can be free or unfree
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: A person can have conflicting desires.
positions.quote: He thus has a desire to not take the heroin—even though he simultaneously has a desire to take the heroin
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: A first-order desire is a desire to do something.
positions.quote: Jones' desire to take heroin is a first-order desire
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: A second-order desire is a desire about another desire.
positions.quote: Jones' desire not to take heroin is a desire as to what to do with another desire; it is a desire not to cave in to a desire to take heroin
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Free will is aligned with second-order desires.
positions.quote: Frankfurt says that somebody has a free will to the extent that his conduct aligns with his second-order desires
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Rationality drives the decision not to act on a first-order desire.
positions.quote: When the heroin-addicted writer decides not to take heroin, what is driving him is a rational appraisal of his situation
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Self-consciousness is a key feature of persons.
positions.quote: First of all, we are self-conscious
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Rationality is a key feature of persons.
positions.quote: Second, we are rational
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Persons are rational not only about how to attain their objectives, but also about what those objectives should be.
positions.quote: Third, we are rational not only about how to attain our objectives, but also about what those objectives should be
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: What distinguishes persons from non-persons is that persons subject their own desires to critical scrutiny.
positions.quote: What distinguishes persons from non-persons is that the former, but not the latter, subject their own desires to critical scrutiny
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Self-consciousness enables rational assessment of desires.
positions.quote: And this is obviously made possible by the fact that we are self-conscious
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Whenever someone is driven by a rational assessment of their own desires, they are driven by a desire about a desire.
positions.quote: Whenever someone is driven by a rational assessment of their own desires, they are driven by a desire about a desire
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: There is a clear sense in which rational conduct is free and irrational conduct is not.
positions.quote: There is a clear sense in which rational conduct is free and in which irrational conduct is not
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Your desires must reflect who you are. If they don't, those desires belong to you but aren't of you.
positions.quote: Your desires must reflect who you are. If they don't, those desires belong to you but aren't of you.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Alienation from one's desires is worse than alienation from one's body.
positions.quote: And in that case, you are alienated from your own mental states, in much the way that a person with Parkinson's is alienated from his body. But, in a way, the alienation is even worse.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Involuntary behavior is unintentional behavior.
positions.quote: Involuntary behavior is unintentional behavior.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Intentional behavior is voluntary behavior.
positions.quote: Intentional behavior is voluntary behavior.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Voluntary behavior is desire-driven behavior.
positions.quote: Voluntary behavior is desire-driven behavior.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Epileptic fits are not actions.
positions.quote: Epileptic fits are not actions.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Estrangement from one's actions leads to a lack of freedom.
positions.quote: So estrangement from one's own actions amounts to estrangement from all of one's actions; and it's hard to imagine a condition less free than that.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Freedom requires acting on second-order desires and alignment with first-order desires.
positions.quote: Thus, one is free insofar as two conditions are met: (i) one acts on one's second-order desires, as opposed to one's first-order desires; and (ii) one's second-order desires are in alignment with one's first-order desires.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: All freedom might ultimately be about doing what one wants.
positions.quote: But, despite everything said thus far, a case can be made that at the end of the day all freedom is freedom to do what one wants.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Desires are structures, not feelings.
positions.quote: Desires, even short-lived ones, are structures; they aren't feelings.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: The feelings to which a desire gives rise don't necessarily make it clear how intense that desire really is.
positions.quote: And the feelings to which a desire gives rise don't necessarily make it clear how intense that desire really is.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Many desires are rooted in personality structures.
positions.quote: Many desires are rooted in personality structures.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Addiction doesn't turn people into complete vegetables, at least not right away.
positions.quote: Addiction doesn't turn people into complete vegetables, at least not right away, and usually not ever.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Being free consists primarily in being oneself and secondarily in being able to do what one wants.
positions.quote: So being free consists primarily in being oneself and secondarily in being able to do what one wants.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: To have a free will is to be free to decide what you want. It is not to be free to have what you want.
positions.quote: To have a free will is to be free to decide what you want. It is not to be free to have what you want.
positions.source: Free Will and Compatibilism - J.-M. Kuczynski

positions.position: Events cause other events to occur.
positions.quote: Events cause other events to occur.

positions.position: Some philosophers have denied the existence of causation.
positions.quote: Astonishingly, some philosophers have denied the existence of causation.

positions.position: Empiricism holds that all knowledge is strictly observation based.
positions.quote: According to empiricism, all knowledge is strictly observation based.

positions.position: Empiricists believe we cannot see or otherwise observe causal ties.
positions.quote: According to many empiricists, we cannot see or otherwise observe causal ties.

positions.position: The argument against causal ties involves a major non-sequitur.
positions.quote: But this argument involves a major non-sequitur.

positions.position: We don't observe electrons or quarks, but we have good reason to believe them to exist.
positions.quote: We don't observe electrons or quarks, but we have good reason to believe them to exist.

positions.position: Causal ties, if they exist, are occupants of space-time.
positions.quote: Causal ties, if they exist, are occupants of space-time, like rocks and vases and chairs

positions.position: The concept of persistence is itself a causal notion.
positions.quote: The concept of persistence is itself a causal notion

positions.position: We observe causal sequences whenever we observe anything.
positions.quote: Thus, we observe causal sequences whenever we observe anything.

positions.position: The contention that we don't observe causal ties is of doubtful accuracy and questionable coherence.
positions.quote: Although it has been enormously influential, it's of doubtful accuracy, it's of questionable coherence.

positions.position: The very notion of spatial occupancy is a causal notion.
positions.quote: For example, the very notion of spatial occupancy is a causal notion

positions.position: Malleability and conductivity are causal properties.
positions.quote: Malleability and conductivity are causal properties.

positions.position: To describe an object as made of steel is to say it will react to situations in certain ways.
positions.quote: Thus, to describe a given object as being of made of steel is to say that, other things being equal, it will react to situations in ways in which it wouldn't otherwise react.

positions.position: Anything that says anything about the spatiotemporal world has causal content.
positions.quote: For that reason, anything that says anything about anything in the spatiotemporal world has causal content.

positions.position: Only events can be causes, not objects.
positions.quote: First of all, it is only events that can be causes. Objects are not causes.

positions.position: The rock did not cause the window to break, but an event involving the rock did.
positions.quote: The rock didn't cause the window to break. What did so was some event involving the rock

positions.position: Non-causes may have causal properties.
positions.quote: Non-causes may have causal properties.

positions.position: Not every property of a rock is a cause.
positions.quote: Not every property of the rock is a cause.

positions.position: A condition is a relatively persistent state.
positions.quote: A condition is a relatively persistent state (e.g., the room's having a certain temperature).

positions.position: An event is a change in conditions.
positions.quote: An event is a change in conditions.

positions.position: Conditions can be causes.
positions.quote: But conditions themselves seem to be causes.

positions.position: An event is a change, whereas a condition is the absence of change.
positions.quote: An event is, by definition, a change, whereas a condition is, it would seem, the absence of change.

positions.position: Conditions consist of events.
positions.quote: This argument overlooks the fact that conditions consist of events.

positions.position: The water's having a given temperature consists in its particles having certain kinetic properties.
positions.quote: The water's having a given temperature consists in its containing particles that have certain kinetic properties.

positions.position: There is no heat where there are no events.
positions.quote: There is no heat where there are no events.

positions.position: A person's being healthy consists of metabolic events.
positions.quote: A person's being healthy consists in that person's body doing what it should.

positions.position: There is no life and no health where there are no metabolic events.
positions.quote: There is no life, and therefore no health, where there are no metabolic events.

positions.position: Changes may be internal to conditions of changelessness.
positions.quote: And although events do necessarily constitute changes, those changes may be, and often are, internal to conditions of changelessness.

positions.position: Nothing non-spatiotemporal can be a cause.
positions.quote: Nothing non-spatiotemporal can be a cause.

positions.position: There are causes only where there are changes, and changes happen in time.
positions.quote: There are causes only where there are changes, and changes happen in time.

positions.position: Universals, if they exist, are causally impotent.
positions.quote: So universals, if they exist, are causally impotent.

positions.position: There must be some kind of causal connection between two things if one is to know of the other.
positions.quote: The idea is that there must be some kind of a causal connection between two things if one of them is to know of the other.

positions.position: There is never awareness where there isn't a causal nexus.
positions.quote: One obvious way to generalize this line of reasoning is to say that there is never awareness where there isn't a causal nexus.

positions.position: A causal connection between two things by itself does not mediate awareness of the other.
positions.quote: under no circumstances does a causal connection between two things by itself mediate the one's having an awareness of the other.

positions.position: There is always a non-causal component to awareness, even to sense-perception.
positions.quote: There is always a non-causal component to awareness, even to sense-perception.

positions.position: We do have good reasons to countenance the existence of universals.
positions.quote: we do have good reasons to countenance the existence of universals, and no good reasons not to.

positions.position: The complete cause of anything is very hard to identify.
positions.quote: The complete cause of anything is very hard to identify.

positions.position: Whether anything other than the entire state of the universe can be the complete cause is debatable.
positions.quote: It is a matter of philosophical and scientific debate whether, given an event E happening at t, anything other than the entire state of the universe just prior to t can be the complete cause of E.

positions.position: Aristotle distinguished between four kinds of causality: efficient, final, material, and formal.
positions.quote: Aristotle distinguished between four different kinds of causality: efficient, final, material, and formal.

positions.position: x is the efficient cause of y if x makes y happen.
positions.quote: x is the efficient cause of y if x makes y happen.

positions.position: x is the final cause of y if y is an action and x is the creature's objective in performing y.
positions.quote: x is the final cause of y if y is an action on the part of some creature and x is that creature's objective in performing y.

positions.position: x is the material cause of y if x is the material that y is made of.
positions.quote: x is the material cause of y if x is the material that y is made of.

positions.position: x is the formal cause of y if x is y's form.
positions.quote: x is the formal cause of y if x is y's form.

positions.position: A property is essential to a thing if it cannot lose that property without ceasing to exist.
positions.quote: a property is essential to a given thing if that thing could not lose that property without for that very reason ceasing to exist.

positions.position: The dependence-relation between essences and things is logical or constitutive, not causal.
positions.quote: The dependence-relation in question is not of a causal nature, and is instead of a logical or constitutive nature.

positions.position: Aristotle's belief in essences is incoherent.
positions.quote: Aristotle's belief that things have essences is an incoherent one,

positions.position: Dependence-relations hold between events or statements, not objects.
positions.quote: Contrary to what Aristotle thought, dependence-relations don't hold between objects. They hold between events or between statements.

positions.position: The concept of formal causes is incoherent.
positions.quote: Thus, the concept of a formal cause is an incoherent one.

positions.position: The concept of material causes is incoherent.
positions.quote: So, for the same reason, is the concept of a material cause.

positions.position: The concept of efficient causality is coherent.
positions.quote: The concept of efficient causality clearly is a coherent one.

positions.position: Efficient causality is the most fundamental form of causality.
positions.quote: But it's the most fundamental one.

positions.position: Goals per se are abstract objects and do no causal work.
positions.quote: Goals are abstract objects and therefore do no causal work.

positions.position: A person's having a goal does causal work.
positions.quote: What does do causal work is a given person's having a given goal.

positions.position: Goals per se are propositions and not causes.
positions.quote: In general, goals per se, being propositions, aren't causes at all.

positions.position: A person's having a goal is an efficient cause.
positions.quote: But my having a goal is a garden-variety efficient cause.

positions.position: States of affairs, not objects, have effects.
positions.quote: it is states of affairs (or events), not objects, that have effects;

positions.position: Propositions, not objects, stand in dependence-relations.
positions.quote: it is propositions, not objects, that stand in dependence-relations.

positions.position: Functionalism has no plausibility for sensations or anything with phenomenal content.
positions.quote: Functionalism has no plausibility when it comes to sensations or, more generally, anything that has phenomenal content.

positions.position: Functionalism cannot accommodate the fact that mental states cause behavior.
positions.quote: But functionalism cannot accommodate this obvious fact

positions.position: According to functionalism, causal relations fix the content of mental states.
positions.quote: According to functionalism, it is in virtue of what x causes and what causes it that x is a belief

positions.position: Functionalism implies that mental states have no causal powers.
positions.quote: if functionalism is correct, then the mental does nothing; it has no causal powers at all

positions.position: Functionalism denies the very existence of minds.
positions.quote: functionalism denies the very existence of minds

positions.position: Content-externalism posits that two identical people can have thoughts with different contents due to different causal histories.
positions.quote: two people, Smith and Twin-Smith, who are exactly the same, can have thoughts with different contents

positions.position: Content-externalism is incoherent.
positions.quote: This view is incoherent

positions.position: If Smith and Twin-Smith switched places, they would not behave or think differently.
positions.quote: If Smith and Twin-Smith were to switch places, neither would behave or think differently from how he would otherwise do so

positions.position: Content-externalism embodies a failure to appreciate the concept 'in virtue of'.
positions.quote: Content-externalism embodies a failure to appreciate the concept meant by the phrase 'in virtue of'

positions.position: Tyler Burge's revision of causality to accommodate content-externalism is ad hoc and unnecessary.
positions.quote: This position borders on incoherence. It's brazenly ad hoc. And it's unnecessary, since the data is easily modeled without it

positions.position: Two worlds cannot differ with respect to a single event without differing in all causal antecedents.
positions.quote: Two worlds cannot differ with respect to a single event unless they differ with respect to all of the causal antecedents of those events.

positions.position: Counterfactuals are identical with causal statements.
positions.quote: If we're right, counterfactuals are identical with causal statements.

positions.position: According to Hume, nothing makes anything happen.
positions.quote: According to David Hume, nothing makes anything happen.

positions.position: If Hume is right, it's hard to see how anything could cause anything to happen.
positions.quote: If Hume is right, it's hard to see how anything could, in any real sense, cause anything to happen.

positions.position: Lewis accepts Hume's analysis.
positions.quote: David Lewis accepts Hume's analysis.

positions.position: Lewis and Hume are hardcore empiricists and reject the idea of forces.
positions.quote: like Hume, Lewis is a hardcore empiricist and rejects the idea that there are forces.

positions.position: Lewis thinks denying causality goes too far.
positions.quote: But Lewis rightly thinks that denying the existence of causality is going too far.

positions.position: We can't sense-perceive what is going on in other possible worlds.
positions.quote: We obviously can't see or otherwise sense-perceive what is going on in other worlds.

positions.position: Our knowledge of counterfactual truths is not based on other worlds.
positions.quote: Any knowledge that we have of what is going on in them is parasitic on our knowledge of counterfactual truths.

positions.position: Other worlds have nothing to do with counterfactual truth.
positions.quote: other worlds have nothing to do with counterfactual truth.

positions.position: Lewis' analysis (LA) is false.
positions.quote: Which shows that LA is false.

positions.position: Statements about the past confirm statements about the future.
positions.quote: I believe that statements about the past confirm statements about the future.

positions.position: It is hard to reconcile confirmation with Humeanism.
positions.quote: It is hard to reconcile this belief with Humeanism.

positions.position: Lewis' analysis (LA) is clever but false.
positions.quote: LA is obviously a clever analysis. But it's false, and it's clear why.

positions.position: Knowledge of other worlds is based on knowledge of our own world.
positions.quote: any knowledge that we have of what happens after t in other worlds will be based on our knowledge of what happens after t in our world.

positions.position: LA assumes knowledge of our own world's future, which is question-begging.
positions.quote: So LA goes through only if it's assumed, question-beggingly, that we know what will happen in our own world's future.

positions.position: The unknown cannot be assumed to resemble the known without question-begging.
positions.quote: Given only that such and such is true of the known, it can't be assumed that it also holds of the unknown—unless it's assumed, question-beggingly, that the unknown resembles the known.

positions.position: LA assumes that inductive inference works, which is question-begging.
positions.quote: So LA goes through only if it's assumed, question-beggingly, that inductive inference works.

positions.position: There is no knowledge without certainty.
positions.quote: In general, there's no knowledge where there isn't certainty.

positions.position: If there is any chance that P isn't the case, then you don't know P.
positions.quote: If, given the information at your disposal, there is any chance, no matter how small, that P isn't the case, then you don't know P.

positions.position: The merely probable is not known.
positions.quote: The merely probable is ipso facto not known.

positions.position: To know something is 99.9999% likely is to not know it.
positions.quote: To know it to be 99.9999% likely that unsupported objects will continue to fall is to not know that they'll fall.

positions.position: Statistical improbability is innocuous.
positions.quote: Statistical improbability is innocuous.

positions.position: It's explanatory improbability that you have to worry about.
positions.quote: It's explanatory improbability that you have to worry about.

positions.position: Every event is improbable in the statistical sense.
positions.quote: Every event is improbable in the statistical sense.

positions.position: Winning the lottery is improbable in the statistical sense but not in the explanatory sense.
positions.quote: My winning the lottery is improbable in the statistical sense, but not in the explanatory sense.

positions.position: Something is grue if it is green before Jan. 1, 2010, or blue thereafter.
positions.quote: Something is grue if it is green before Jan. 1, 2010, or blue thereafter.

positions.position: All emeralds examined before 2010 are grue.
positions.quote: the fact that all emeralds examined before 2010 are green means that all emeralds examined before 2010 are grue.

positions.position: The degree to which a fact is statistically improbable depends on how it is described.
positions.quote: The degree to which a given fact is statistically improbable depends on how it is described.

positions.position: The supposition that objects don't change color is deeply embedded in scientific theory.
positions.quote: The supposition that objects don't change color is deeply embedded in scientific theory and also in the commonsense underpinning of scientific theory.

positions.position: If the supposition that objects don't change color is wrong, then science is in serious trouble.
positions.quote: If it's wrong, then science is in serious trouble, and so is human knowledge in general.

positions.position: Natural selection preserves structures that accurately represent the world.
positions.quote: Natural selection preserves those cognitive structures that accurately represent the world.

positions.position: Our hardwired belief in universal causation was selected for because it's true.
positions.quote: our hardwired belief in universal causation was selected for because it's true.

positions.position: Inborn expectations about uniform causal regularities presuppose those very regularities.
positions.quote: inborn expectations about uniform causal regularities presuppose the very regularities that we're using those expectations to explain.

positions.position: Probability judgments are rational when they merely redescribe statistics.
positions.quote: Probability judgments are rational when they merely redescribe statistics

positions.position: Probability judgments are irrational within a Humean framework when they involve extrapolations.
positions.quote: Probability judgments are irrational, given a Humean framework, when they involve extrapolations.

positions.position: De facto generalizations don't receive support from their instances.
positions.quote: de facto generalizations don't receive support from their instances

positions.position: Accidental generalizations do not receive support from their instances.
positions.quote: accidental generalizations don't receive support from their instances

positions.position: For Hempel's paradox to arise, at least one statement must be taken as more than a de facto concomitance.
positions.quote: if Hempel's paradox is to arise, at least one of MH and NH must be taken as doing more than affirming a de facto concomitance

positions.position: Hempel's paradox does not arise if HM has no nomic content.
positions.quote: Hempel's paradox doesn't arise if we don't take HM to have nomic content.

positions.position: A nomic connection between having phi and psi does not imply a nomic connection between not having psi and not having phi.
positions.quote: Given only that there is a nomic connection between a thing's having phi and its having psi, it doesn't follow that there is a nomic connection between a thing's not having psi and its not having phi

positions.position: Nomic relations hold only among occupants of the space-time manifold.
positions.quote: it is pretty clear they hold only among occupants of the space-time manifold.

positions.position: Nomic relations hold among events.
positions.quote: They clearly hold among events (my pounding the table causes it to break).

positions.position: Nothing non-spatiotemporal stands in nomic relations.
positions.quote: it is clear that nothing non-spatiotemporal stands in them.

positions.position: Seeing a million black ravens does not guarantee the next raven will be black without a causal connection.
positions.quote: Maybe you've seen a million ravens, all of them black. It doesn't matter. Unless you have reason to believe that a thing's being a raven is connected somehow to its being black

positions.position: A black raven can only support 'All ravens are black' if there is a causal or nomic connection between ravenhood and blackness.
positions.quote: If the existence of a black is raven is to provide any legitimate support for (1), there must be a causal or nomic connection between ravenhood and blackness.

positions.position: When read as affirming de facto concomitances, MH and HM are confirmationally identical.
positions.quote: If read as doing nothing more than affirming de facto concomitances: (MH) and (HM) don't receive support from their instances and are therefore vacuously confirmationally identical.

positions.position: When read as alleging nomic connections, MH and HM are not logically equivalent.
positions.quote: If they are read as alleging some kind of nomic connection, they don't even appear logically equivalent.

positions.position: Hume's argument concerning induction would invalidate certain arguments about knowledge.
positions.quote: If Hume's argument concerning induction goes through, then the following argument fails to go through

positions.position: According to the counterfactual analysis of causation, if the cause hadn't occurred, the effect wouldn't have occurred.
positions.quote: if the cause of the doorbell's ringing is the button's being pushed, then the doorbell wouldn't have rung if the button hadn't been pushed.

positions.position: If AC is right, then causal connections are not known empirically but through logical analysis.
positions.quote: If AC is right, then causal connections are not to be known empirically, and must instead be known through logical analysis.

positions.position: If AC is right, we can't see or otherwise sense-perceive causal connections.
positions.quote: If AC is right, then we can't see, or otherwise sense-perceive, causal connections.

positions.position: The only way to know that E1 caused E2 is to know of some general regularity.
positions.quote: The only way to know that E1 caused E2 is to know of some general regularity of which the sequence of events consisting of E1 and E2 is an instance.

positions.position: There is a problem with Hume's analysis of causality due to causal redundancy.
positions.quote: One problem with the analysis of causality is that, because there is a certain amount of causal redundancy in the world, there are cases where a given event would have occurred even if the event that in fact caused it had not happened.

positions.position: A miracle is needed to validate counterfactuals according to Lewis.
positions.quote: Lewis is saying that, if a world validates LASK, there is some miracle in it.

positions.position: A theory supported only by doctored data is a bad theory.
positions.quote: A theory that only doctored data supports is a bad theory, and Lewis' analysis of counterfactuals is untenable.

positions.position: Smith's falling off of a building is an event that causes other events.
positions.quote: Smith's falling off of X is an event. It is something that makes things happen.

positions.position: Brown's not falling off a building is not an event.
positions.quote: Whereas Smith's falling off of X is an event, Brown's not falling off of X is not an event,

positions.position: Brown's not falling off a building is not constituted by events.
positions.quote: Brown's not falling off of X isn't identical with an event and isn't constituted by events.

positions.position: A chair consists of events (various miniscule displacements of mass-energy).
positions.quote: A chair consists of events (various miniscule displacements of mass-energy),

positions.position: A corporation consists of events (various actions on the part of sentient beings).
positions.quote: and does a corporation (various actions on the part of sentient beings);

positions.position: Existentialism is more of an attitude than a doctrine.
positions.quote: At its core, existentialism is therefore less of a doctrine than it is an attitude.

positions.position: A doctrine is a system of interconnected propositions.
positions.quote: A doctrine is a system of interconnected propositions.

positions.position: The viability of a doctrine has nothing to do with how anyone feels about anything.
positions.quote: The viability of a doctrine has nothing to do with how anyone feels about anything.

positions.position: Doctrines are to be judged according to logical or empirical benchmarks.
positions.quote: They are to be judged according to how well they measure up to the relevant logical or empirical benchmarks.

positions.position: Attitudes cannot be judged as right or wrong.
positions.quote: Attitudes cannot be understood in these terms.

positions.position: To the extent that it is an attitude, existentialism cannot be judged to be wrong.
positions.quote: So to the extent that it is an attitude, existentialism cannot be judged to be wrong.

positions.position: Choosing values leads to more reflection on what values should be.
positions.quote: If you come into the world believing that values are to be chosen, as opposed to passively accepted, you are probably more likely than you would otherwise be to reflect on what your values should be.

positions.position: Choosing values is psychologically beneficial.
positions.quote: First, if you choose your values, instead of simply accepting the values of others, you are more likely to live according to values that work for you psychologically.

positions.position: Even choosing the wrong values affirms rationality and freedom.
positions.quote: Second, even if you choose the wrong values, the mere fact that you chose your values would seem to be an affirmation of your rationality and freedom.

positions.position: Some values involve a diminishment of self.
positions.quote: A number of values seem to involve a diminishment of self.

positions.position: Society's values are good for society but not necessarily for the individual.
positions.quote: One possible answer is that the values that are endorsed by society are those that are good for society, and therefore aren't necessarily those that are good for the individual.

positions.position: Society requires the abridgment of individual freedoms.
positions.quote: As Freud emphasized in Civilization and its Discontents, society is possible only if the aspirations of individuals are abridged.

positions.position: Society's values embody much of the antagonism people have for one another.
positions.quote: the values that society asks the individual to accept embody much of the antagonism that people have for one another.

positions.position: Choosing values protects against moralistic propaganda.
positions.quote: If you see values as yours to accept or reject, you are less likely to be duped by moralistic propaganda into reducing yourself.

positions.position: Existentialist philosophers have produced doctrines to validate the existentialist attitude.
positions.quote: existentialist philosophers have spent a great deal of time producing doctrines that validate the attitude in question.

positions.position: The doctrines produced by existentialist philosophers may not be correct or coherent.
positions.quote: Even though that attitude may be salutary, it doesn't follow that those doctrines are correct or even coherent.

positions.position: The values other people tell us to accept may not always be the right ones.
positions.quote: The values that other people tell us to accept may not always be the right ones.

positions.position: It doesn't follow that there are no values.
positions.quote: But it doesn't follow that there are no values

positions.position: Rejecting conventional values can be to uphold other values.
positions.quote: Sometimes when people reject conventional values, it is because they feel that they must do so to uphold other values.

positions.position: Fruitful rebellion against conventional values involves acceptance of deeper values.
positions.quote: It would be hard to think of a single case where a fruitful rebellion against conventional values did not involve an acceptance of a deeper and more legitimate set of values.

positions.position: Attitudes are not themselves true or false.
positions.quote: We said earlier that attitudes are not themselves true or false.

positions.position: Attitudes involve presuppositions that are true or false.
positions.quote: But, despite this fact, attitudes often involve presuppositions that are true or false.

positions.position: Emotional attitudes are grounded in beliefs about the world.
positions.quote: In general, our emotional attitudes are grounded in beliefs about the world, and those beliefs are true or false.

positions.position: The thesis that there are no universally valid moral truths is controversial.
positions.quote: there are no universally valid moral truths.

positions.position: Feelings can have an objective basis.
