positions.position: Our feelings track our feelings about rightness and wrongness.
positions.quote: Our feelings seem to track our feelings about rightness and wrongness.

positions.position: Our feelings are not always appropriate.
positions.quote: This is not to say that our feelings are always appropriate.

positions.position: It doesn't follow that feelings are categorically without an objective basis.
positions.quote: it doesn't follow that they are categorically without an objective basis.

positions.position: Our perceptions aren't always completely accurate, but that doesn't mean they're always wrong.
positions.quote: Our perceptions aren't always completely accurate. But that doesn't mean that they are always completely wrong.

positions.position: There is a strong pre-theoretic presumption that there are objective moral truths.
positions.quote: there is a strong pre-theoretic presumption to the effect that there are objective moral truths.

positions.position: The word 'value' is ambiguous with at least three meanings.
positions.quote: the word 'value' is ambiguous as it has (at least) three meanings.

positions.position: A 'value' can be something valued or something one ought to value.
positions.quote: A 'value' can be something that is in fact valued, or it can be something that one ought to value.

positions.position: Moral truths would not be false in a world of sociopaths.
positions.quote: In a world of mathematical morons, nobody would know that 2 + 2 = 4; but the statement '2 + 2 = 4' would not on that account be false.

positions.position: People often come to believe they have been abiding by the wrong values.
positions.quote: People often come to believe that they have been abiding by the wrong values, and must therefore adopt new ones.

positions.position: Tolstoy's The Death of Ivan Ilyich illustrates the point about changing values.
positions.quote: Tolstoy's novel The Death of Ivan Ilyich illustrates this point.

positions.position: Emotions track value-judgments.
positions.quote: In general, it's hard to believe that emotions don't track value-judgments.

positions.position: Emotions embody value-judgments.
positions.quote: Supposing that our emotions embody value-judgments, it seems to follow that we cannot choose any value-system without jeopardizing our own happiness.

positions.position: Emotions presuppose certain ethical views.
positions.quote: It thus seems that your emotions presuppose certain ethical views.

positions.position: Changing value system requires changing emotions.
positions.quote: a necessary condition for really accepting a new set of values would be that you re-wire, so to speak, your emotions.

positions.position: Innate psychological structure limits happiness.
positions.quote: Your innate psychological structure obviously puts limits on what you can do and still be happy.

positions.position: Emotional state tracks views on moral fitness.
positions.quote: Your emotional state tracks your views as to the moral fitness of facts about your life.

positions.position: Certain value systems are internal to emotional architecture.
positions.quote: certain value systems are internal to his/her emotional architecture.

positions.position: The range of admissible value systems is limited by psychology and biology.
positions.quote: the range of admissible value systems is limited by immutable facts about our psychology (and biology).

positions.position: Chomsky showed humanly learnable languages satisfy narrowly defined formal constraints.
positions.quote: Chomsky showed that any humanly learnable language satisfies rather narrowly defined formal constraints.

positions.position: One cannot change value system except in trivial respects.
positions.quote: you cannot (except in trivial respects) change your value system at all.

positions.position: One can comply with a value system that doesn't make one happy.
positions.quote: Of course, you can, in terms of your overt behavior, comply with a value system that doesn't make you happy.

positions.position: True acceptance of a value system requires emotional alignment.
positions.quote: But if the view we've been defending is right, you don't really accept such a value system.

positions.position: Emotions reflect actual values.
positions.quote: If your emotions reflect your actual values, then you don't really accept a value system that brings you misery.

positions.position: Choosing one's own ethics may embody a contradiction.
positions.quote: Does it make sense to say that it is ethically permissible to choose one's own ethics? Doesn't that question embody a contradiction?

positions.position: If there is no God, there are no moral laws.
positions.quote: In other words, if there is no God, there are no moral laws.

positions.position: The so-called philosophy of religion doesn't concern religion itself.
positions.quote: The so-called philosophy of religion doesn't concern religion.

positions.position: Philosophy of religion concerns logical puzzles, not religion.
positions.quote: It concerns puzzles that can be illustrated in connection with religious questions but are really of a strictly logical nature.

positions.position: The Omnipotence Paradox is about logical subtleties, not God.
positions.quote: OG doesn't have anything to do with God. It has to do with subtleties of logic.

positions.position: Philosophy of religion often veers off into irrelevant logical minutiae.
positions.quote: its efforts to do this quickly veer off course and end up bogging down in logical minutiae that are of no relevance to religion at all.

positions.position: St. Thomas Aquinas's argument (AG1) is flawed.
positions.quote: So Aquinas has simply assumed that there was a first event, and he thus hasn't established it at all.

positions.position: The first sentence of AG1 is ambiguous.
positions.quote: The first sentence of AG1, which we'll refer to as (a), is ambiguous.

positions.position: Assuming a first event does not prove God's existence.
positions.quote: But supposing, if only for argument's sake, that there was a first event, it doesn't follow, at least not for any obvious reason, that it was an act of God.

positions.position: Aquinas's second argument (AG2) for God's existence is flawed.
positions.quote: AG2 is a failure.

positions.position: Comparatives do not require absolutes.
positions.quote: Contrary to what Aquinas believes, there don't have to be absolutes for there to be comparatives.

positions.position: Pre-modern philosophers were only dimly aware of syntactic ambiguity.
positions.quote: Pre-modern philosophers, such as Aquinas, were only dimly aware of the phenomenon of syntactic ambiguity.

positions.position: Studying Aquinas's arguments does not teach about God or religion.
positions.quote: But we didn't learn anything about God or religion.

positions.position: It is futile to provide rational arguments for or against religious belief.
positions.quote: The futility of trying to provide rational arguments for or against religious belief

positions.position: Religion is not science or logic.
positions.quote: Religion is not science or logic.

positions.position: A scientifically minded person's belief in God is contingent on available evidence.
positions.quote: Smith's belief in God goes only as far as the available evidence permits.

positions.position: A person who believes in God isn't religious if their belief is contingent on data consistency.
positions.quote: A person who believes in God isn't religious if that person's acceptance of that position is contingent on its being consistent with the data.

positions.position: It is silly to try to undermine religious belief by arguing it doesn't square with the facts.
positions.quote: This is why it's silly to try to undermine religious belief by arguing that it doesn't square with the facts.

positions.position: Religious belief is not subject to views on what inferences the available data warrants.
positions.quote: What distinguishes religious from scientific belief is precisely that the former, unlike the latter, isn't subject to one's views as to what inferences the available data warrants.

positions.position: Religious people are right to be unmoved by anti-religious arguments.
positions.quote: And they're right to be unmoved.

positions.position: A religious attitude is not marked by subservience to data and logic.
positions.quote: a religious attitude is precisely one that isn't marked by the subservience to data and logic characteristic of the scientific view.

positions.position: Attacking religion on scientific or logical grounds is misguided.
positions.quote: those who attack religion on scientific or logical grounds are misguidedly trying to understand religious sentiment in epistemological terms

positions.position: Religious sentiment should be understood in psychoanalytic terms.
positions.quote: when in fact it is to be understood in psychoanalytic terms

positions.position: The philosophy of religion tends to be sterile.
positions.quote: And this, I believe, is part of the reason why the philosophy of religion tends to be so sterile.

positions.position: Saying 'God is love' is not the view of somebody who believes in God.
positions.quote: But it isn't the view of somebody who believes in God.

positions.position: Attempts to identify God with reason or truth or love are dishonest and incoherent.
positions.quote: Attempts to identify God with reason or truth or love are dishonest and incoherent

positions.position: Religious people have no obligation to defend their views.
positions.quote: religious people have no obligation to defend their views.

positions.position: A view that is only as good as the logic and evidence underlying it is a non-religious view.
positions.quote: a view that is only as good as the logic and evidence underlying it is ipso facto a non-religious view.

positions.position: Defending a religious view is almost the same as apologizing for it.
positions.quote: Defending a religious view is the same as explaining why one holds it, and explaining why one holds it is almost the same as apologizing for it.

positions.position: Many self-described atheists are actually crypto-believers.
positions.quote: many self-described atheists are actually crypto-believers.

positions.position: Acceptance of logical or ethical standards can be a distorted expression of belief in God.
positions.quote: Acceptance of such rarefied and depersonalized arbiters of right and wrong is sometimes a distorted and oblique expression of a belief in God.

positions.position: There is no relationship between the degree of religiosity and intelligence.
positions.quote: Is there a relationship between the degree to which one is religious and the extent to which one is intelligent? No.

positions.position: Religious belief isn't about facts or logic.
positions.quote: religious belief isn't about facts or logic.

positions.position: Philosophy has nothing to teach us about religion, with one exception.
positions.quote: There is only one qualification to my previous statement that philosophy has nothing to teach us about religion.

positions.position: Plato's Euthyphro argument is cogent.
positions.quote: I consider that argument to be cogent.

positions.position: Some acts are inherently good and others are inherently bad.
positions.quote: Some acts are good and others are bad.

positions.position: The goodness of an act is not determined by God's liking it.
positions.quote: If an act is good, that is because God likes it. In other words, God's liking it is what causes it to be good... Is G correct? No.

positions.position: Rape would remain bad even if God liked it.
positions.quote: What if God liked rape? Would that make it good? No. Rape would be bad even if God liked it, and God would be bad for liking it.

positions.position: Acts of kindness would remain good even if God disliked them.
positions.quote: What if God disliked acts of kindness? Would that make such acts bad? No. They would still be good, and God would be bad for disliking them.

positions.position: God has a reason for disliking rape, based on its inherent wrongness.

positions.position: Morality is independent of God's views.
positions.quote: Conclusion: Morality is independent of God's views.

positions.position: God cannot make rape morally good.
positions.quote: Could God make rape to be morally good? No. Even God cannot make rape be good.

positions.position: Even if God speaks to individuals, it is not because of His commands that we believe certain acts are wrong.
positions.quote: This shows that even if God speaks to certain individuals, it isn't because of what He told them that we believe that rape, theft, and so on, are wrong.

positions.position: The Divine Command Theory cannot legitimate existing religious practices or beliefs.
positions.quote: the Divine Command Theory can do nothing in the way of legitimating existing religious practices or beliefs

positions.position: The existence of bad things suggests either no God or a limited God.
positions.quote: This suggests that there is no God or that, if there is a God, He isn't all-powerful, all-good, and all-knowing

positions.position: Theodicies are attempts to show that an all-powerful, all-good, all-knowing God is coherent despite evil.
positions.quote: Attempts to show that (d) is coherent are known as theodicies

positions.position: A world with free will is better than a world without it.
positions.quote: A world where nothing has free will wouldn't be a very good world.

positions.position: A world of robots who do no wrong is not as good as a world of free creatures that do some wrong.
positions.quote: A world of robots who do no wrong isn't as good as a world of free creatures that do some wrong

positions.position: God gave humans free will to avoid them being soulless robots.
positions.quote: Not wanting us to be soulless robots, God gave us free will

positions.position: Preventing all disasters would prevent human development of character.
positions.quote: we, as a species, would never develop any character.

positions.position: Life without challenges would be meaningless.
positions.quote: if it weren't a challenge, it wouldn't be meaningful.

positions.position: A certain amount of strife enriches life, but too much impoverishes it.
positions.quote: A certain amount of strife enriches life. But too much impoverishes it.

positions.position: There is too much strife for the theodicy to work.
positions.quote: There's too much strife for this theodicy to work.

positions.position: God cannot make 1 + 1 = 3.
positions.quote: She is unable to make 1 + 1 = 3.

positions.position: Something is impossible if the idea of doing it is incoherent.
positions.quote: For something to be impossible is not for it to be very hard to do: it is for the very idea of doing that thing to be an incoherent one.

positions.position: Something preexisting itself is logically impossible.
positions.quote: It seems impossible that something should come into existence before it comes into existence.

positions.position: Impossibilities are expressed by self-undermining statements.

positions.position: God cannot create a square circle.

positions.position: God's inability to create a square circle does not limit Her powers.
positions.quote: this does not show that Her powers are in any way limited.

positions.position: A square circle is an impossibility because it involves contradictory definitions.
positions.quote: A square is by definition something that has four straight sides. A circle is by definition something that has no straight sides.

positions.position: Impossibility is often mistakenly thought of as a property of things.
positions.quote: We pre-theoretically tend to think of impossibility as a property of things.

positions.position: Impossibility is a property of statements, not things.
positions.quote: Impossibility is a property, not of things, but of statements.

positions.position: A statement is impossible if it is incoherent.

positions.position: Most incoherent statements contradict themselves implicitly, not explicitly.
positions.quote: We must bear in mind that most incoherent statements contradict themselves implicitly, not explicitly.

positions.quote: To say that God cannot do the impossible is therefore not to say that there exist certain things that God cannot do.

positions.position: There are no impossible objects.
positions.quote: There exist no impossible objects.

positions.position: For a statement to be meaningless is for it to have no meaning.
positions.quote: For a statement to be meaningless is for it to have no meaning.

positions.position: For a statement to be incoherent is for it to have two or more opposed meanings.
positions.quote: For a statement to be incoherent is for it to have two (or more) opposed meanings.

positions.position: Incoherent statements ultimately fail to say anything.
positions.quote: Therefore, my previous statement—that incoherent statements ultimately fail to say anything—has turned out to be true.

positions.position: The Ontological Argument (AG) is a spurious argument.
positions.quote: AG is a spurious argument.

positions.position: God does not exist in the mind—if God exists, She is in the world.
positions.quote: If God exists, She is out there, in the world, not in my mind.

positions.position: No idea of mine is God.
positions.quote: No idea of mine is God.

positions.position: The only things that exist in the mind are thoughts, feelings, etc.
positions.quote: The only things that exist in my mind are thoughts, feelings, etc.

positions.position: Having knowledge of what conditions a thing must fulfill does not imply its existence.

positions.position: Existence is an excellence.

positions.position: If you don't exist, you are a complete and total zero.

positions.position: 'If God exists, She exists' is all the ontological argument proves.

positions.position: The ontological argument proves nothing.

positions.position: SOA presupposes that failing to exist makes a thing imperfect.

positions.position: It's incoherent to say that failing to exist makes you a lesser person.

positions.position: If you didn't exist, there would be no you to begin with.

positions.position: It is not possible for something to fail to exist.

positions.position: 'Square circles don't exist' means no object can be both a square and a circle.

positions.quote: Thus, SC and ZE attribute properties to properties. They don't attribute non-existence to objects.

positions.position: It is not possible to attribute non-existence to a non-existent object.
positions.quote: It isn't even possible to attribute non-existence to a non-existent object.

positions.position: It is not possible to attribute existence to a non-existent object.
positions.quote: Nor is it possible to attribute existence to a non-existent object.

positions.position: A precondition for a thing's being described as existent or non-existent is its existing.
positions.quote: It follows that a precondition for a thing's being described as existent or non-existent is its existing.

positions.position: In attributing existence to a given thing, one isn't affirming anything.
positions.quote: Since any given proposition can be negated, it follows that, in attributing existence to a given thing, one isn't affirming anything.

positions.position: It is meaningless to say of specific individuals that they exist or don't exist.
positions.quote: It is meaningless to say of specific individuals that they exist or don't exist.

positions.position: Claiming ethical judgments are by-products of upbringing denies their objectivity.
positions.quote: if you say that our ethical judgments are by-products of our upbringing, and therefore aren't hewed to anything objective

positions.quote: then you are ipso facto denying that your judgments are any good, in which case you forfeit your right to assert anything

positions.position: St. Thomas Aquinas argued against DCT.

positions.position: To the extent Aquinas rejected DCT, he wasn't religious.

positions.position: To the extent Aquinas was religious, he actually did accept DCT.

positions.position: Religious intellectuals are masters of compartmentalization.

positions.position: Free will theodicy presupposes there is such a thing as free will.
positions.quote: One of the two theodicies that we discussed presupposes that there is such a thing as free will

positions.position: It is questionable whether there is such a thing as free will.

positions.quote: Also say why, supposing that there is free will, it wouldn't necessarily help the theodicy in question

positions.position: God is omniscient.

positions.position: God's omniscience implies knowledge of all actions of every person.
positions.quote: Given that God is omniscient, He/She has always known exactly what each person would do at each moment of his/her life

positions.position: God's omniscience could imply a lack of free will.

positions.position: God's knowledge of future actions challenges the concept of free will.

positions.quote: The Free Will defense thus seems to have the absurd consequence that people both do, and do not, have free will

positions.position: Descartes believed an omnipotent God can make 1 + 1 = 3.

positions.position: Wittgenstein believed even an omnipotent God cannot make 1 + 1 = 3.
positions.quote: According to Wittgenstein, even if God is omnipotent, He/She cannot make 1 + 1 = 3

positions.position: Modal statements describe what could or must be the case.
positions.quote: A modal statement is one that says what could be the case or what must be the case.

positions.position: Necessity and possibility are logical relations between propositions.
positions.quote: For any proposition P, it's necessarily the case that P iff it's not possible that not-P.

positions.position: There are no impossible objects.
positions.quote: There are no impossible objects—no objects that couldn't possibly exist.

positions.position: Possibility is a property of propositions, not objects.
positions.quote: Possibility and impossibility are properties of propositions, not of objects.

positions.position: Statements of non-existence are about propositions, not objects.
positions.quote: Statements that, given their surface-structures, appear to attribute non-existence to objects in fact attribute falsity to propositions.

positions.position: Fictional characters are false existence claims.
positions.quote: The cartoons that brought Fred Flintstone and Bart Simpson into our homes can be thought of as making false statements.

positions.position: Necessity does not come in degrees.
positions.quote: There are only two degrees of necessity: having it completely and lacking it completely.

positions.position: Highly necessary statements hold in more circumstances.
positions.quote: Some statements need less help from others to be true—those are the 'highly necessary' ones.

positions.position: Kripke argued for non-analytic necessary truths.
positions.quote: In 1969, Saul Kripke gave a now famous series of lectures in which he argued that there are non-analytic necessary truths.

positions.position: Proper names are rigid designators.
positions.quote: In general, proper names are rigid designators.

positions.position: 'Hesperus is Phosphorus' expresses a necessary truth.
positions.quote: 'Hesperus is Phosphorous' expresses a non-analytic necessary truth.

positions.position: Kripke's argument confuses semantics with pre-semantics.
positions.quote: In each case, semantics is being confused with pre-semantics.

positions.position: Modal concepts reduce to necessity.
positions.quote: The main modal concepts are necessity, possibility, impossibility, and contingency. All of these can be understood in terms of the concept of necessity.

positions.position: Probability is a modal property.
positions.quote: I believe that the property of being probably true is a modal property.

positions.position: Necessity is analyticity.
positions.quote: Necessary truth is analytic truth; necessity is analyticity.

positions.position: Analyticity is a property of propositions, not sentences.
positions.quote: We will use the word 'analytic' to denote a property of propositions, not sentences.

positions.position: Sentence-analyticity diverges from proposition-analyticity.
positions.quote: Sentence-analyticity diverges from proposition-analyticity.

positions.position: Analytic truths are non-empirical.
positions.quote: Analytic truths are true no matter what.

positions.position: Analytic truth is high-resolution structural truth.
positions.quote: Analytic truth is truth in virtue of structure along with substructure.

positions.position: Language mirrors propositional structure.
positions.quote: Sentential structure mirrors propositional structure.

positions.position: Analytic truths are true in virtue of micro-structure.
positions.quote: For a proposition to be analytically true is for it to be true in virtue of its structural and its substructural properties.

positions.position: Quine denied informal analytic truth.
positions.quote: W.V.O. Quine says that, with a few trivial exceptions, there is no such thing as sentential analytic truth.

positions.position: Quine argued analyticity is circularly defined.
positions.quote: Quine concludes, the concept of analyticity is viciously circular and therefore incoherent.

positions.position: Quine was wrong about synonymy as the basis of analyticity.
positions.quote: Quine is wrong to hold that analyticity is to be understood in terms of synonymy.

positions.position: Analytic truth is non-empirical truth.
positions.quote: Analytic truth is non-empirical truth.

positions.position: Quine's position is self-defeating.
positions.quote: The view that there are no analytic statements is self-defeating.

positions.position: Without analytic truth, nothing confirms anything.
positions.quote: If all truth were empirical, then nothing could confirm anything.

positions.position: Some truths are contingent.
positions.quote: It seems clear that some truths are contingent.

positions.position: The denial of necessary truth is self-undermining.
positions.quote: The assumption that no propositions are necessarily true is self-undermining.

positions.position: Knowledge of necessary truth is needed for contingent knowledge.
positions.quote: Knowledge of necessary truths is a prerequisite for knowledge of contingent truth.

positions.position: Quine's empiricism led him to reject analytic truth.
positions.quote: Quine's real reason for denying the existence of analytic truth lay in his staunch empiricism.

positions.position: Empiricism collapses without non-observational knowledge.
positions.quote: There is necessarily a non-observational component to the acquisition of scientific knowledge.

positions.position: Recognizing resemblances requires necessary truths.
positions.quote: Knowledge of necessary truths is a precondition for being able to recognize resemblances between things.

positions.position: Proof requires necessity.
positions.quote: There are no proofs if all truth is contingent.

positions.position: Analytic truth is not formal truth.
positions.quote: Formal truth isn't analytic truth.

positions.position: Analytic statements have incoherent negations.
positions.quote: A statement is analytically true if its negation is incoherent.

positions.position: Non-analytic truths are empirical.
positions.quote: Non-analytic truths are empirical truths.

positions.position: Kant was wrong about synthetic a priori truths.
positions.quote: Kant infamously claimed that there are non-analytic truths that can be learned in a non-observational manner. He was wrong.

positions.position: Sense-perception may trigger ratiocination without transmitting empirical content.
positions.quote: Sense-perception may trigger ratiocination without transmitting empirical content.

positions.position: Analytic knowledge is justified by insight, not observation.
positions.quote: Your acceptance of DN can be justified only by insight into its meaning.

positions.position: Sentence meaning is empirical; propositional content may be analytic.
positions.quote: It's an empirical truth that the sentence 'squares have four sides' means what it does… but it's an analytic fact that squares have four sides.

positions.position: There are non-trivial analytic truths.
positions.quote: There are non-trivial analytic truths.

positions.position: No synthetic non-empirical truths exist.
positions.quote: Any non-analytic truth is empirical.

positions.position: Formal truth is syntactic truth.
positions.quote: Formal truth is syntactic truth. A sentence is 'syntactically true' if, given only its syntactic structure, it cannot fail to be true.

positions.position: Quine defined formal truth via logical constants.
positions.quote: A sentence S is formally true if, given any expression in S that isn't a logical constant, it isn't possible to produce a false sentence by replacing that expression.

positions.position: Quine's list of logical constants is arbitrary.
positions.quote: Quine's analysis is vitiated by his failing to say what conditions an expression must satisfy to be a 'logical constant.'

positions.position: Formal truth is not topic-neutral truth.
positions.quote: Formal truth ≠ topic-neutral truth.

positions.position: Formal truth is instance of universally true open-sentence.
positions.quote: A sentence is formally true if it's an instance of a universally true open-sentence.

positions.position: Logical constants are expressions in pure universally true open-sentences.
positions.quote: A 'logical constant' is any expression other than a variable that occurs in a pure, universally true open-sentence.

positions.position: Not all analytic truth is formal truth.
positions.quote: Not all analytic truth is formal truth.

positions.position: Wittgenstein was wrong: analytic truth ≠ formal truth.
positions.quote: Wittgenstein's thesis that all analytic truth is formal truth is not only wrong but incoherent.

positions.position: No conceptual analysis is formally true.
positions.quote: No conceptual analysis is formally true.

positions.position: Analytic statements are universal generalizations or instances.
positions.quote: Conceptual analyses are expressed by analytic statements, and analytic statements either coincide with informal analytic universal generalizations or are instances of such generalizations.

positions.position: Pre-semantic information affects conveyed meaning.
positions.quote: Pre-semantic information can have an incalculably profound effect on what is conveyed by utterances.

positions.position: Kripke's examples confuse semantic and pre-semantic content.
positions.quote: Kripke and, after him, many others have professed to find many examples of non-analytic necessary truths; but in each case… semantics is being confused with pre-semantics.

positions.position: Necessity is the central modal concept.
positions.quote: Necessity is the most important modal concept.

positions.position: Quine's empiricism is self-refuting.
positions.quote: The view that there is no analytic truth entails that there is analytic truth, and is therefore self-refuting.

positions.position: The supposition that all truth is empirical is viciously regressive.
positions.quote: The supposition that all truth is empirical is viciously regressive.

positions.position: Behaviorism is false.
positions.quote: Behaviorism is false.

positions.position: Chomsky's innatism opposes Quine's behaviorism.
positions.quote: Chomsky proposed an alternative theory, according to which inborn cognitive structures are largely determinative of how we learn and use language.

positions.position: Empiricism is inconsistent with empirical evidence.
positions.quote: Empiricism is therefore inconsistent with empirical evidence of the most blatant and widespread kind.

positions.position: All denial of analytic truth stems from empiricism.
positions.quote: All philosophers, and indeed non-philosophers, who deny the existence of analytic truth do so because they are staunch empiricists.

positions.position: Observation alone cannot yield interpretation.
positions.quote: It is totally incoherent to suppose that one could know on the basis of observation alone how to interpret the data of observation.

positions.position: Abstract resemblance requires necessary knowledge.
positions.quote: One cannot discern any abstract similarities between any two concrete situations unless one knows… what conditions must be fulfilled.

positions.position: To prove something is to show it must be the case.
positions.quote: To prove conclusion C on the basis of premises P is to show it to be necessarily true that if P, then C.

positions.position: Empirical truths require observational justification.
positions.quote: If, in order to justify acceptance of S, you must cite empirical data, it's empirical.

positions.position: Sense-perception can trigger non-empirical knowledge.
positions.quote: Sense-perception may well have been needed to initiate or trigger the reflections on the basis of which you learned DN.

positions.position: Sentence meaning is contingent; propositional content may be necessary.
positions.quote: It's an empirical fact that the sentence 'squares have four sides' means what it does… but it's an analytic fact that squares have four sides.

positions.position: Quine's synonymy account of analyticity fails.
positions.quote: Quine is wrong to hold that analyticity is to be understood in terms of synonymy.

positions.position: Kripke's argument is a massive non-sequitur.
positions.quote: Kripke's argument involves a massive non-sequitur.

positions.position: Rigid designators refer to the same object in all possible worlds.
positions.quote: Rigid designators refer to the same object in all possible worlds.

positions.position: Negative existentials are metalinguistic.
positions.quote: Statements of non-existence attribute falsity to propositions.

positions.position: Quine's linguistic behaviorism is incoherent.
positions.quote: Quine's linguistic behaviorism is incoherent and inconsistent with evidence.

positions.position: Innate structures explain language acquisition.
positions.quote: Chomsky's innatism is supported by empirical evidence.

positions.position: Proof establishes necessary consequence.
positions.quote: Proof shows that a conclusion necessarily follows from premises.

positions.position: Proper names refer directly without descriptive content.
positions.quote: Proper names refer directly without descriptive content.

positions.position: Modal operators apply to propositions.
positions.quote: Necessity and possibility are operators on propositions.

positions.position: Existence statements assess whether any object satisfies a description.
positions.quote: Existence statements assess whether any object satisfies a description.

positions.position: Kripke mistakes epistemology for modal status.
positions.quote: Kripke mistakes how we come to know an identity for its modal status.

positions.position: Human cognition requires innate conceptual structures.
positions.quote: Human cognition requires innate conceptual structures.

positions.position: Scientific reasoning depends on necessary truths not derived from observation.
positions.quote: Scientific reasoning depends on necessary truths not derived from observation.

positions.position: Identifying properties requires knowledge of necessary conditions.
positions.quote: Identifying properties requires knowledge of necessary conditions.

positions.position: Proof shows a conclusion is necessarily true given premises.
positions.quote: Proof shows that a conclusion is necessarily true given the premises.

positions.position: Analytic truths are known independently of experience.
positions.quote: Analytic truths are known independently of experience.

positions.position: Formal truths are substitution instances of logical laws.
positions.quote: Formal truths are substitution instances of logical laws.

positions.position: Quine's list of logical constants lacks a theoretical basis.
positions.quote: Quine's list of logical constants lacks a theoretical basis.

positions.position: Conceptual analyses are expressed by analytic sentences that are not formal truths.
positions.quote: Conceptual analyses are expressed by analytic sentences that are not formal truths.

positions.position: There are two kinds of statements: normative and descriptive.
positions.quote: There are two kinds of statements: normative and descriptive.

positions.position: Descriptive statements do not express value-judgments.
positions.quote: Descriptive statements don't express value-judgments.

positions.position: Normative statements express value-judgments.
positions.quote: A normative statement is one that does express a value-judgment.

positions.position: Ethics is the discipline that attempts to clarify the structure of normative concepts.
positions.quote: Ethics is the discipline that attempts to clarify the structures of these concepts.

positions.position: The term 'good' is ambiguous.
positions.quote: For each of these terms is ambiguous.

positions.position: Instrumental goodness refers to acts that benefit the agent.
positions.quote: To say that an act is instrumentally good is to say that it has consequences that are desired by, or to the advantage of, the agent.

positions.position: Ethics is concerned with intrinsic, not instrumental, goodness.
positions.quote: Ethics is concerned with intrinsic goodness.

positions.position: Ethics asks: 'Which things are intrinsically good?'
positions.quote: Ethics attempts to answer the question 'which things are intrinsically good?'

positions.position: Happiness, intelligence, benevolence, honesty are examples of intrinsic goods.
positions.quote: happiness, intelligence, benevolence, honesty… are intrinsically good.

positions.position: There are different kinds of freedom: physical, emotional, intellectual.
positions.quote: there is freedom from external, physical coercion… emotional freedom… intellectual freedom.

positions.position: Some things are both instrumentally and intrinsically good.
positions.quote: Having a sharp intellect is both an intrinsic good and an instrumental good.

positions.position: The expression 'intrinsic good' is itself ambiguous.
positions.quote: the expression 'intrinsic good' is itself ambiguous.

positions.position: Some intrinsic goods are praiseworthy; others are not.
positions.quote: Some things that are intrinsically good are commendable—that is, they deserve praise. And some things that are intrinsically good do not deserve praise.

positions.position: Happiness is intrinsically good but not necessarily praiseworthy.
positions.quote: happiness is non-instrumentally good, while at the same time not being commendable.

positions.position: Natural talent is intrinsically good but not praiseworthy.
positions.quote: Smith's musical talent is intrinsically good… But Smith doesn't deserve any praise for happening to have been blessed with this talent.

positions.position: There are two kinds of intrinsic goods: commendable and non-commendable.
positions.quote: there are two kinds of intrinsic goods—those which are commendable and those which are not.

positions.position: There are two kinds of intrinsic badness: condemnable and non-condemnable.
positions.quote: Just as there are two kinds of intrinsic goodness, so there are two kinds of intrinsic badness.

positions.position: Unhappiness is intrinsically bad but not always condemnable.
positions.quote: It's obviously bad to be unhappy. And the badness in question is clearly of a non-instrumental, and therefore intrinsic, nature.

positions.position: Causing unnecessary suffering is both intrinsically bad and condemnable.
positions.quote: Causing somebody else to suffer, for no good reason, is intrinsically bad and it is condemnable.

positions.position: 'Intrinsically bad' is not always identical with 'worthy of condemnation.'
positions.quote: 'intrinsically bad' is not, at least not always, identical with 'worthy of condemnation.'

positions.position: A single act can be both intrinsically good and intrinsically bad in different respects.
positions.quote: A single act or state of affairs can be intrinsically good in one respect and intrinsically bad in some other respect.

positions.position: A given act can be both condemnable and commendable.
positions.quote: A given act can be both condemnable and commendable.

positions.position: Moral properties are not privative; they are positive properties.
positions.quote: Neither property is 'privative'; they are both 'positive' properties.

positions.position: Badness is not merely the absence of goodness.
positions.quote: badness, like goodness, is the presence of something.

positions.position: People mistakenly think good and bad cannot coexist because they see them as opposites.
positions.quote: people tend to think that good and bad cannot co-exist in the same person, or the same act, because they wrongly think that good is the absence of bad.

positions.position: People think in binary terms about morality due to cognitive ease.
positions.quote: People tend, especially where moral and emotional issues are concerned, to think in an unrealistically binary way.

positions.position: Moral obligations are not binary; they come in degrees.
positions.quote: Moral obligations are not 'binary'; they come in degrees.

positions.position: Some obligations are stronger than others.
positions.quote: some obligations are therefore stronger than others.

positions.position: There are degrees of wrongness.
positions.quote: A related point is that there are degrees of wrongness.

positions.position: Conflicting obligations can coexist.
positions.quote: Oftentimes, we have conflicting obligations.

positions.position: An overridden obligation still exists.
positions.quote: obligations that are outweighed by other, more powerful obligations do not on that account cease to exist.

positions.position: The right action is the least bad option available.
positions.quote: what we mean by 'the right course of action' is the least bad course of action that the circumstances permit.

positions.position: A wrong action is not necessarily a bad action; it is the least good possible option.
positions.quote: For a course of action to be wrong is for it to be the least good possible course of action.

positions.position: One has an obligation only if one can fulfill it.
positions.quote: It is a generally accepted principle of ethics that one has an obligation to do something only if one can do it.

positions.position: The principle 'ought implies can' must be qualified in cases of conflicting obligations.
positions.quote: We saw that there are circumstances where one has multiple conflicting obligations… where it isn't strictly correct to say that 'ought' implies 'can.'

positions.position: Legal acts can be immoral.
positions.quote: There are, or at least can be, acts which are legal, even legally required, which are immoral.

positions.position: Legality and morality sometimes overlap.
positions.quote: At the same time, legality and morality often overlap.

positions.position: Knowing a fact does not entitle one to assert it without evidence.
positions.quote: Given only that some object in fact weighs 200 pounds, I don't necessarily know, or therefore have the right to say, that it has that weight.

positions.position: It can be wrong to condemn what is in fact condemnable if one lacks sufficient evidence.
positions.quote: it can be condemnable to condemn the condemnable.

positions.position: Praise depends on intention, not outcome.
positions.quote: Whether somebody deserves to be commended for an act depends, not on whether it actually was the right act, but on whether it was the agent's intention to perform the right act.

positions.position: Two agents with identical intentions deserve the same moral evaluation, regardless of outcome.
positions.quote: Mary's intentions are exactly like Kathy's… they are equally worthy of praise.

positions.position: An agent who intends evil but brings about good still deserves condemnation.
positions.quote: Betty clearly deserves condemnation of the most severe kind… because, even though she ended up doing good, it was not her intention to do so.

positions.position: Failure to commit a crime may indicate lack of resolve or moral reservation.
positions.quote: failure to carry a plan out successfully oftentimes… suggests that the person's heart wasn't really in it.

positions.position: Hitler's intentions were not good.
positions.quote: it pretty clearly was not Hitler's intention to do good.

positions.position: Self-destructive behavior can be immoral.
positions.quote: One can sell oneself short; one can act self-destructively.

positions.position: Self-harm is not morally equivalent to harming others.
positions.quote: This is not to say that people who hurt themselves are ethically in the same category as people who hurt others.

positions.position: Principle-driven acts are not necessarily good.
positions.quote: Given only that an act is principle-driven, it doesn't follow that it isn't wrong or even evil.

positions.position: Punishing self-harm may be ethically worse than the act itself.
positions.quote: by punishing such a person, we'd be violating their autonomy… which might be ethically worse than what they did.

positions.position: Metaethics studies the meaning of ethical statements.
positions.quote: Metaethics is the discipline that says what, if anything, ethical statements mean.

positions.position: The principle that you cannot derive 'ought' from 'is' may be false.
positions.quote: This principle may well be false.

positions.position: If certain moral doctrines are correct, 'ought' can be inferred from 'is.'
positions.quote: If either of these doctrines… is on target, then an 'ought' can be inferred from 'is.'

positions.position: Moore argued that moral truths are not reducible to descriptive truths.
positions.quote: Ethical truths are not identical with, or otherwise 'reducible to,' factual statements.

positions.position: Moore's premise is deeply implausible.
positions.quote: Premise 1 is deeply implausible.

positions.position: Moore's argument fails due to a misunderstanding of entailment.
positions.quote: Moore didn't have a very good grasp of the concept of entailment.

positions.position: The principle 'ought implies can' is at least approximately correct.
positions.quote: Unlike (1) and (2), (3) is at least partly correct—maybe even 100% correct.

positions.position: There are counterexamples where obligation persists despite inability.
positions.quote: Suppose that you give me $10,000 to move some rocks for you… It still seems that I have an obligation…

positions.position: The origin of a belief is irrelevant to its truth.
positions.quote: Whether an idea is correct has to do solely with whether it corresponds to the facts.

positions.position: The genetic fallacy occurs when we judge a belief by its origin.
positions.quote: In making such a judgment, one commits the genetic fallacy.

positions.position: Moral statements can be used for propaganda or evil purposes.
positions.quote: many moral statements are pure propaganda.

positions.position: The misuse of a true moral principle does not negate its truth.
positions.quote: given that alleged physical laws aren't always actual physical laws, it doesn't follow that there are no physical laws.

positions.position: Moral principles are not social inventions.
positions.quote: Moral principles are not social rules.

positions.position: Social enforcement of morality is human-made, but moral truths are not.
positions.quote: The social institutions that implement moral principles… obviously came into existence at some point in time… But it doesn't follow that moral principles themselves came into existence at that point.

positions.position: Disagreement in ethics does not prove ethical non-realism.
positions.quote: There appears to be less agreement in ethics than in other disciplines. From this it is concluded that ethical statements merely express 'opinions'… But there are at least four reasons why this is not good reasoning.

positions.position: Factual statements can also be disputed without being mere opinion.
positions.quote: Consider the statement: If Kennedy hadn't been assassinated… It is exceedingly difficult to know whether KV is an accurate statement or not… But surely it isn't just a 'matter of opinion.'

positions.position: If a statement can be rationally disputed, it likely has an objective basis.
positions.quote: any position that can be rationally disputed is likely to have an objective basis.

positions.position: Ethical debates often turn on factual questions.
positions.quote: whether gay marriage is right or wrong depends largely on purely factual issues.

positions.position: Many ethical questions have clear, agreed-upon answers.
positions.quote: Is it morally okay to torture babies for the sheer fun of it? No.

positions.position: The illusion of disagreement arises because settled issues are no longer debated.
positions.quote: The illusion that there is no moral agreement… is that an ethical issue isn't worth discussing anymore if there is universal agreement about it.

positions.position: Debates over the death penalty depend on factual questions.
positions.quote: The question 'how many innocent people are executed for crimes they didn't commit?' is a strictly factual question.

positions.position: The abortion debate often turns on factual questions about fetal consciousness.
positions.quote: Does the fetus have a mind? Can it feel pain?… These are factual questions about the fetus's psychological state.

positions.position: Ethical assertions may be disguised factual assertions.
positions.quote: ethical assertions are factual assertions, albeit disguised ones.

positions.position: Utilitarianism holds that good acts are those that maximize happiness.
positions.quote: According to one version of it, which is known as 'utilitarianism,' good things are those that bring about happiness.

positions.position: Another view holds that good acts promote human flourishing.
positions.quote: for something to be good is for it to be identical with, or logically required by, a practice on which human flourishing depends.

positions.position: If these views are correct, moral questions are factual questions.
positions.quote: if the just-described moral view is correct, moral questions are factual questions.

positions.position: Normative categories include good, bad, just, unjust, etc.
positions.quote: Examples of such categories are good, bad, just, unjust, valiant, noble, wicked, depraved, commendable, and condemnable.

positions.position: Ethics aims to state the conditions for falling under normative categories.
positions.quote: ethics attempts to make explicit exactly what conditions an act must satisfy to be praiseworthy.

positions.position: Intrinsic goodness is independent of consequences.
positions.quote: Ethics is interested in identifying those courses of action… that are good even if their consequences are disregarded.

positions.position: Helping others without self-interest is intrinsically good.
positions.quote: Helping a person in need, in cases where doing so is not to one's own practical advantage, is intrinsically good.

positions.position: Honesty when inconvenient is intrinsically good.
positions.quote: Being honest with people, when it would be more convenient to lie, is intrinsically good.

positions.position: Intelligence can be both instrumentally and intrinsically good.
positions.quote: Having intelligence is an instrumental good… But the goodness of being intelligent exceeds any practical advantage.

positions.position: A rabbit's happiness is good but not praiseworthy.
positions.quote: the happiness of a rabbit is a good thing. But… rabbits are not capable of acting in a praiseworthy manner.

positions.position: Grieving a loss is not condemnable.
positions.quote: Somebody who is grieving the loss of a loved one doesn't deserve to be reprimanded.

positions.position: Being cold is a privative property (lack of heat).
positions.quote: For something to be cold is for it to lack heat.

positions.position: Being smart is a positive property.
positions.quote: for something to be hot or smart or rich is not for it to lack anything.

positions.position: Condemnability is a positive property.
positions.quote: The property of being condemnable is not privative; it is a positive property.

positions.position: Rocks are neither good nor bad.
positions.quote: A rock lacks goodness. But rocks aren't bad. They're neither good nor bad.

positions.position: Hitler was bad because he had ill-will.
positions.quote: Hitler was bad. And he was bad because he had something that rocks don't have—he had ill-will.

positions.position: Binary moral thinking is emotionally easier.
positions.quote: It's emotionally easier to see the world as divided into saints and ghouls.

positions.position: Conflicting obligations can create moral dilemmas.
positions.quote: I have a choice: steal the money or let my children die.

positions.position: The right action is the lesser of two evils.
positions.quote: the 'right' thing to do is to steal my friend's money… it is clearly the lesser of two evils.

positions.position: Saving fewer lives when more could be saved is wrong.
positions.quote: If I save the lives of M16–M17, I am obviously doing a good thing… But I'm still doing the wrong thing.

positions.position: 'Ought implies can' is generally accepted but has exceptions.
positions.quote: There is much truth in the principle that 'ought' implies 'can.' But… this principle must be taken with a grain of salt.

positions.position: Legal acts can be deeply immoral (e.g., slavery).
positions.quote: it was legal to have slaves, and it was illegal to set another person's slaves free. But it is exceedingly immoral to have slaves.

positions.position: One may not have the right to assert a truth without evidence.
positions.quote: Given only that some statement is correct, I don't necessarily have the right to make it.

positions.position: It is wrong to convict without sufficient evidence, even if the person is guilty.
positions.quote: It is wrong to convict a person that one doesn't know to be guilty, even if that person is guilty.

positions.position: Attempted murder is punished less severely because it may indicate lack of intent.
positions.quote: failure to carry a plan out successfully oftentimes… suggests that the person's heart wasn't really in it.

positions.position: One can be immoral toward oneself.
positions.quote: One can sell oneself short; one can act self-destructively.

positions.position: Self-harm is often driven by hyper-principled reasoning.
positions.quote: People who hurt themselves are often… hyper-principled.

positions.position: Punishment is not always warranted for wrongdoing.
positions.quote: Whether somebody deserves punishment is not a function solely of whether they've done wrong.

positions.position: Punishing self-harm may be redundant and harmful.
positions.quote: self-destructive behavior is its own punishment; so an externally-imposed punishment would be redundant.

positions.position: The is-ought gap may be bridgeable.
positions.quote: if there is any truth to the thesis that acts are good to the extent that they maximize happiness… then an 'ought' can be inferred from 'is.'

positions.position: Moore's open question argument is flawed.
positions.quote: Moore's argument is no good.

positions.position: Entailment can be ampliative (increase knowledge).
positions.quote: Entailment is ampliative.

positions.position: The genetic fallacy confuses origin with validity.
positions.quote: The fact that Newton's hypothesis had such a random and ignominious origin is irrelevant to whether it is correct or not.

positions.position: Moral truths can exist independently of their social implementation.
positions.quote: Moral principles are not social rules.

positions.position: Ethical disagreement often rests on unresolved factual issues.
positions.quote: the seemingly unending nature of this ethical debate has to do… with there being relevant facts that aren't yet in evidence.

positions.position: Utilitarianism ties goodness to happiness maximization.
positions.quote: good things are those that bring about happiness and bad things are those that diminish happiness.

positions.position: Human flourishing involves actualizing innate abilities.
positions.quote: By 'human flourishing' is meant the actualization of human abilities.

positions.position: Utilitarianism cannot be completely wrong.
positions.quote: It's hard to believe that something that made everybody miserable would be good.

positions.position: Ethical statements, when sincere, inherently express feelings or emotions.
positions.quote: According to some, it is inherent in the nature of ethical pronouncements that, when made sincerely, they express feelings or emotions of some kind.

positions.position: A purely factual statement does not reveal the speaker's feelings.
positions.quote: Given only that you've sincerely said 'that rock weighs over 100 pounds,' nothing can be concluded about how you feel about the rock's weight.

positions.position: A sincere moral judgment must embody some feeling about its subject.
positions.quote: if you say 'Larry is a bad man,' your statement, if sincere, must embody feelings of some kind concerning Larry's deeds or character.

positions.position: To make a moral statement without feeling is insincere.
positions.quote: If you feel nothing either way, you're guilty of insincerity in uttering that sentence…

positions.position: Emotivism suggests moral 'judgments' are merely expressions of sentiment.
positions.quote: these points suggest that moral 'judgments' are nothing more than expressions of sentiment.

positions.position: Saying 'Larry is a bad man' is just venting negative feelings.
positions.quote: If you say 'Larry is a bad man,' you're just venting your negative feelings about Larry…

positions.position: Moral statements are no more true or false than facial expressions.
positions.quote: you're no more saying something true or false than you would be if you scowled at Larry.

positions.position: 'Stealing is wrong' is equivalent to saying 'boo stealing!'
positions.quote: 'Stealing is wrong' is just a way of saying 'boo stealing!'…

positions.position: If emotivism is true, there are no moral truths or falsehoods.
positions.quote: Consequently, there are no moral truths and no moral falsehoods.

positions.position: It is false that 'x is good' means 'hurray x!'
positions.quote: It's simply false that saying 'x is good' is tantamount to saying 'hurray x!'

positions.position: One can sincerely make a moral judgment without personal emotion.
positions.quote: a competent speaker of English can sincerely say 'Cotton Mather was a good man' without personally having any emotion…

positions.position: People can coolly make moral judgments.
positions.quote: It's clear that people can coolly make moral judgments.

positions.position: Emotion-driven judgment is not an unbiased ethical judgment.
positions.quote: an emotion-driven judgment is, to that extent, not an ethical judgment—or, in any case, not an unbiased one.

positions.position: One can make positive moral judgments about displeasing things.
positions.quote: One often makes positive moral judgments about people and things that bring them displeasure.

positions.position: One can judge an act as good while personally disliking it.
positions.quote: There are people who I dislike whose noble acts make me want to wretch. But… I do judge that those acts are good.

positions.position: Hume provided a compelling answer on reconciling moral judgment with emotion.
positions.quote: David Hume… provided a compelling answer to this question…

positions.position: Artistic goodness is often linked to giving pleasure.
positions.quote: In general, people regard artistic works that bring them pleasure as 'good'…

positions.position: 'x is good' in art might seem to mean 'x gives me pleasure.'
positions.quote: …when said of a work of art, 'x is good' means 'x gives me pleasure'…

positions.position: The pleasure analysis of artistic goodness cannot be right.
positions.quote: But this analysis cannot quite be right.

positions.position: One can judge a pleasure-less work as good.
positions.quote: People can, without self-contradiction, judge that works that don't bring them pleasure are 'good'…

positions.position: Irrelevant personal factors can inhibit pleasure from a good work.
positions.quote: This happens when people know that they would like a given work were it not for the inhibiting influence of some plainly irrelevant factor.

positions.position: One can know a work is good even if it brings personal torture.
positions.quote: So you know that it's good, even though… it doesn't bring you pleasure.

positions.position: 'Good' in art means it brings pleasure setting aside irrelevancies.
positions.quote: when people describe a work of art as 'good,' they mean that, setting aside irrelevancies… it brings people pleasure.

positions.position: The same principle applies mutatis mutandis in the moral sphere.
positions.quote: The same thing mutatis mutandis holds in the moral sphere.

positions.position: People can regard pleasure-less acts as moral.
positions.quote: people sometimes regard things that bring them no pleasure, or even displeasure, as 'moral'…

positions.position: People can regard pleasure-bringing acts as immoral.
positions.quote: they sometimes regard things that bring them acute pleasure as 'immoral.'

positions.position: A criminal may commend his own punishment.
positions.quote: Many a person has committed heinous crimes and then commended the government for putting him in jail.

positions.position: 'x is good' means 'x makes people happy barring idiosyncratic factors.'
positions.quote: 'x is good' means 'x makes people happy to the extent so far as individual-specific, non-transferable facts about them aren't a factor.'

positions.position: Hume's argument shows emotivism must be changed to be credible.
positions.quote: Hume's powerful argument shows that, if it is to be credible, emotivism must be changed…

positions.position: One can judge something good even if it brings misery.
positions.quote: a person can judge that something is good even if that thing brings him misery…

positions.position: Therefore, 'x is good' cannot be expressing happiness about x.
positions.quote: …saying 'x is good' cannot possibly be the same thing as expressing one's happiness about x.

positions.position: 'x is good' may express belief that x is happiness-conducive.
positions.quote: But 'x is good' is plausibly seen as expressing the speaker's belief that x is happiness-conducive…

positions.position: Thus modified, emotivism is blatantly false.
positions.quote: But, thus modified, emotivism is blatantly false.

positions.position: If 'x is good' expresses a belief, it is true or false.
positions.quote: If it expresses any belief, 'x is good' is either true or false.

positions.position: The essence of emotivism is that moral statements express feelings, not judgments.
positions.quote: The essence of emotivism is that 'x is good' expresses a feeling, as opposed to a judgment…

positions.position: Modified emotivism collapses into its opposite.
positions.quote: So emotivism collapses into its opposite if it's modified along the lines suggested by Hume.

positions.position: Modified emotivism coincides with utilitarianism.
positions.quote: thus modified, emotivism coincides with utilitarianism.

positions.position: Hume's analysis (HA) is viciously circular.
positions.quote: What is more, HA is viciously circular.

positions.position: One must distinguish between enjoying something and being caused pleasure by it.
positions.quote: It's crucial to distinguish between enjoying something, on the one hand, and being caused by it to experience pleasure, on the other.

positions.position: Being caused joy by X is not the same as enjoying X.
positions.quote: Here the G-minor ballad is causing me joy; but I'm not enjoying it.

positions.position: When this distinction is recognized, emotivism collapses.
positions.quote: When this distinction is given its due, emotivism collapses…

positions.position: Value-nihilism also collapses with this distinction.
positions.quote: …and so does every form of value-nihilism.

positions.position: Value-nihilism: 'x is good' means nothing or means x is liked.
positions.quote: By 'value-nihilism' I mean any doctrine to the effect that 'x is good' either means nothing or means that x is liked or otherwise looked upon favorably…

positions.position: Not all reasons for feeling good are aesthetic.
positions.quote: Not all of those reasons are of an aesthetic nature.

positions.position: A genuine aesthetic reaction is driven by a thing's artistic merits.
positions.quote: …what makes Jones feel good is X's having certain artistic merits.

positions.position: If there are aesthetic reactions, there are aesthetic facts.
positions.quote: So to the extent that there are aesthetic reactions, there are aesthetic facts…

positions.position: If there are aesthetic facts, we can be wrong about them.
positions.quote: If there are aesthetic facts, we can be wrong about them, just as we can be wrong about anything else.

positions.position: If there are aesthetic facts, value-nihilism is false regarding aesthetics.
positions.quote: So if there are aesthetic facts, value-nihilism is false, at least as far as aesthetic properties are concerned.

positions.position: The saying 'there's no disputing taste' is invoked against aesthetic facts.
positions.quote: Everyone knows the old saying: 'there's no disputing taste.'

positions.position: Aesthetic facts don't imply all works are comparable.
positions.quote: …it doesn't follow that, given any two works of musical works… they are capable of being meaningfully compared.

positions.position: Works can be good in non-comparable ways.
positions.quote: they are good in non-comparable ways.

positions.position: Their goodness may be 'incommensurable.'
positions.quote: The goodness of any one of them is 'incommensurable'… with the goodness of either of the others.

positions.position: Not every musical work is as good as every other.
positions.quote: But that doesn't mean that every musical work is as good as every other musical work.

positions.position: It's obvious some composers are better than others.
positions.quote: In any case, it's patently obvious that some composers are better than others.

positions.position: No musician can coherently believe all works are of equal merit.
positions.quote: And no such musician can coherently believe that no musical work is better than any other.

positions.position: Moral nihilism fails for similar reasons as aesthetic nihilism.
positions.quote: Moral nihilism fails for much the same reasons that aesthetic nihilism fails.

positions.position: A moral reaction requires S's moral properties cause the joy.
positions.quote: It's only if S's having such and such moral properties is what brings me joy that my liking S is a moral reaction to it.

positions.position: There are moral reactions only if there are moral facts.
positions.quote: In general, there are moral reactions only to the extent that there are moral facts.

positions.position: If there are moral facts, emotivism collapses.
positions.quote: If there are moral facts, our feelings may or may not do them justice; and emotivism completely collapses.

positions.position: Sense-perceptions represent the world as being a certain way.
positions.quote: Our sense-perceptions represent the world as being a certain way.

positions.position: Sense-perceptions have truth-conditions.
positions.quote: …they have 'truth-conditions': they're accurate iff the world satisfies certain conditions.

positions.position: The common view: emotions are neither true nor false.
positions.quote: Emotions can be healthy or unhealthy, but they cannot be true or false.

positions.position: Emotions typically involve presuppositions about the world.
positions.quote: Of course, emotions typically involve presuppositions about how the world is.

positions.position: You cannot be angry that Tim stole your bike unless you believe he did.
positions.quote: You cannot be angry that Tim stole your bicycle unless you believe that Tim stole your bicycle.

positions.position: Emotions presuppose beliefs but aren't themselves beliefs.
positions.quote: Emotions presuppose beliefs; but emotions aren't themselves beliefs.

positions.position: This view is attributed to David Hume.
positions.quote: The position just described was advocated by David Hume…

positions.position: Hume's view is totally false.
positions.quote: Hume's view, we will now see, is totally false.

positions.position: Emotions are true or false.
positions.quote: Emotions are true or false.

positions.position: Emotions are beliefs.
positions.quote: The reason is that, counterintuitive though it may seem, emotions are beliefs.

positions.position: We'd see taking a pill to wipe out emotions as self-lobotomizing.
positions.quote: But that is how we'd regard somebody who took a pill that wiped away all his unpleasant emotions.

positions.position: People want circumstances to change so emotions become inappropriate.
positions.quote: What they want is to change their circumstances in such a way that their unpleasant emotions no longer have a basis in them.

positions.position: People don't want to 'zap' emotions; they want the world to change.
positions.quote: People don't want to 'zap' their anger or hate or pessimism. They want the world to change… that those emotions cease to be appropriate.

positions.position: Attitude toward emotions is like attitude toward senses: we want accurate representation.
positions.quote: In this respect, the attitude that people have towards their emotions is similar to the attitude they have towards their senses.

positions.position: People want to feel joy because circumstances warrant it.
positions.quote: And people want to feel joy. But they want to feel it because their circumstances warrant it…

positions.position: Like perceptions, emotions have an object.
positions.quote: Like a sense-perception or thought, an emotion must have an object.

positions.position: You can't just be angry; you're angry at or about something.
positions.quote: You can't just be angry. You're angry at your boss for giving someone else the promotion.

positions.position: Causing an emotion is not the same as being its object.
positions.quote: Given only that x is what caused so and so to have emotion y, it doesn't follow that x is what… so and so is emoting about.

positions.position: Having a negative emotion about X isn't being caused by X to have it.
positions.quote: This confirms that having a negative emotion about X isn't the same thing as being caused by X to have a negative emotion.

positions.position: Happiness is the recognition that one can now gratify a desire.
positions.quote: Ben's being happy that he can play soccer is his recognition that… he can now gratify an otherwise ungratifiable desire.

positions.position: The analysis extends to all emotions.
positions.quote: This analysis is easily extended to apply to all emotions.

positions.position: Fear involves believing a desire is jeopardized.
positions.quote: So, to fear her coming is for you to believe that her coming would frustrate some desire of yours.

positions.position: Emotions are beliefs about the prospects of gratifying desires.
positions.quote: Emotions, then, are beliefs about the prospects of gratifying desires that one has.

positions.position: Positive emotion: belief prospects are good.
positions.quote: If the belief is that the prospects are good, the emotion is a positive one…

positions.position: Negative emotion: belief prospects are bad.
positions.quote: If the belief is that those prospects are bad, it's a negative one…

positions.position: Geach put forth a clever argument against emotivism.
positions.quote: Peter Geach put forth the following, very clever argument against emotivism…

positions.position: Geach's argument substitutes 'boo stealing!' for 'stealing is wrong.'
positions.quote: Suppose that saying 'stealing is wrong' is… no different from saying 'boo stealing!'…

positions.position: Replacing with a synonym shouldn't turn meaning into nonsense.
positions.quote: Replacing an expression with a synonymous expression doesn't turn meaning into nonsense…

positions.position: (1) is meaningful; (2) is nonsense.
positions.quote: But whereas (1) is obviously meaningful… (2) is complete nonsense.

positions.position: Therefore, the expressions are not synonymous; emotivism is wrong.
positions.quote: Thus, the underlined expressions are not synonymous, and the emotivist is wrong to say otherwise.

positions.position: If emotions are beliefs, Geach's argument fails.
positions.quote: Which makes it obvious that Geach's argument fails if emotions are beliefs.

positions.position: Geach's argument makes a strong, unargued, probably false assumption.
positions.quote: Which, in its turn, shows that it makes a strong, unargued, and probably false assumption.

positions.position: Therefore, Geach's argument is spurious.
positions.quote: Which, finally, shows that it's spurious.
